### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

* Overpotential $\eta = E - E_{eq}$ — the kinetic tax above thermodynamics. ✅
* Three flavors: **activation, concentration, ohmic** — sum equals total. ✅
* **Butler–Volmer equation** is the master kinetic law covering *all* overpotential regimes. ✅
* **Tafel equation** is the high-overpotential limit: $\eta = b\,\log(i/i_0)$, $b = 2.303RT/(\alpha F)$. ✅
* **Linear regime** ($\eta \le 10$ mV): $R_{ct} = RT/(i_0 F)$; used in LPR corrosion monitoring. ✅
* **Exchange current density $i_0$** measures intrinsic kinetic ease; large $i_0$ = facile reaction. ✅
* **Concentration overpotential** $\eta_{conc} = (RT/nF)\ln(1 - i/i_L)$; diverges as $i \to i_L$. ✅
* **Limiting current** $i_L = nFDC_b/\delta$ — set by diffusion through Nernst layer. ✅
* **Ohmic overpotential** $\eta_{ohm} = iR_{cell}$ — pure resistive drop. ✅
* Always check **sign convention** and **units**; remember $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, $RT/F = 25.7$ mV at 298 K. ✅
* Sketch a **polarization curve** in every relevant answer — KTU awards easy marks for visuals. ✅
* **Tafel slope** tells the engineer how efficiently an electrocatalyst performs. ✅

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Primary and Secondary Current Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT417 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Primary and Secondary Current Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Primary Current Distribution

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Current Distribution** is the current density profile established across an electrode surface when the cell behaviour is governed *exclusively* by the **ohmic resistance of the electrolyte**, with the overpotential due to charge transfer kinetics (activation overpotential) and mass transport (concentration overpotential) considered to be negligibly small or zero.

Mathematically, the potential distribution in the bulk electrolyte ($\phi$) satisfies **Laplace's Equation**:

$$\nabla^{2}\phi = 0$$

The local current density $i$ at any point on the electrode surface is related to the potential gradient by Ohm's law in the electrolyte:

$$i = -\kappa \nabla \phi$$

where $\kappa$ is the specific conductivity of the electrolyte (S/m).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡

Imagine water flowing through an irregularly shaped sand-filled channel. The regions of the channel with the **least resistance to flow** (the shortest, widest paths) will carry the bulk of the water. Similarly, in electrochemistry, the regions of the electrode that are **closest to the counter-electrode** and present the least electrolytic resistance will carry the **highest current density**. At sharp edges and corners, the "resistance path" from the counter-electrode is naturally shorter, so current crowds toward the edges — this is the famous **edge effect** or **current crowding**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** In primary current distribution, the overpotential $\eta$ is assumed to be **purely ohmic**. The exchange current density $i_0 \to \infty$ (kinetically facile electrode). The current distribution is therefore determined **purely by cell geometry** — not by electrode kinetics or mass transport.

## 1.2 Secondary Current Distribution

**Formal Definition:**
> [!NOTE]
> **Secondary Current Distribution** is the current density profile on an electrode when **both** the ohmic resistance of the electrolyte **and** the **activation overpotential** (charge transfer kinetics) are considered significant, while concentration overpotential is still neglected (excess supporting electrolyte, no mass transport limitations).

It is governed by a combination of:
1. **Laplace's equation** in the bulk electrolyte (as in primary distribution).
2. The **Butler-Volmer kinetic boundary condition** at the electrode surface.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡

Continuing the water-flow analogy: now imagine that the channel walls have a **kinetic friction** that resists the entry of water. Even though the geometry still favours flow at the edges, the kinetic friction acts as a **buffer**, slowing the natural tendency for current to crowd. If the friction is strong (small $i_0$), the water spreads out more evenly. This "friction" in electrochemistry is the **activation overpotential** of the electrode reaction.

The trade-off between the geometric driving force (toward non-uniformity) and the kinetic resistance (toward uniformity) is quantified by the **Wagner Number (Wa)**, discussed in detail in Section 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Highlight:** Secondary current distribution is a *more realistic* model than primary. It is a pure **kinetic-ohmic** model — no mass transfer effects. It is the natural bridge between the geometric primary distribution and the mass-transfer-influenced tertiary distribution.

### Standard Physical Constants Used in this Module

| Symbol | Quantity | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $F$ | Faraday's constant | **96485 C mol⁻¹** |
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** |
| $T$ | Absolute temperature | **298.15 K** (standard) |
| $\kappa$ | Electrolyte conductivity | **S m⁻¹** |
| $i_0$ | Exchange current density | **A m⁻²** |
| $\beta$ | Symmetry factor (charge transfer coefficient) | **~ 0.5** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primary Current Distribution: Analytical Framework

### The Governing Differential Equation
For a steady-state electrochemical cell with a binary electrolyte and no concentration gradients (excess supporting electrolyte), the potential satisfies Laplace's equation in the bulk solution:

$$\nabla^{2}\phi = 0$$

The corresponding boundary conditions are:
1. **Insulating walls:** $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} = 0$ (no normal current flux through non-electrode surfaces).
2. **Electrode surface:** $\phi$ is constant (equipotential electrode, typically a metal).
3. **Far-field / Counter-electrode:** $\phi$ takes the applied cell voltage.

### Why Does Edge Crowding Occur?
- Near a sharp edge, the equipotential lines of the counter-electrode "wrap around" the corner.
- The potential gradient $\nabla \phi$ perpendicular to the electrode surface becomes very large.
- Since $i = -\kappa \nabla \phi$, the local current density at the edge becomes **infinite** (mathematically) or very large (physically).

### Primary Current Distribution for a Disk Electrode
For a circular disk electrode of radius $r_0$ embedded in an infinite insulating plane, the classical result (Newman, 1966) gives the primary current density at radial position $r$:

$$\frac{i(r)}{i_{avg}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{1 - (r/r_0)^{2}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At the edge ($r \to r_0$), $i(r) \to \infty$. At the centre ($r = 0$), $i(r) = \dfrac{i_{avg}}{2}$. The average is taken over the entire disk area.

For a **parallel-plate cell with planar electrodes of equal size**, separated by distance $L$, and extending to infinity laterally, the current distribution is **perfectly uniform**:

$$\frac{i(x)}{i_{avg}} = 1$$

This is the trivial limiting case of primary distribution.

## 2.2 Secondary Current Distribution: Introduction of Kinetics

### The Kinetic Boundary Condition
At the electrode surface, the current density is no longer determined purely by the geometry. It must obey the **Butler-Volmer equation** that links the local overpotential to the local current density:

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left( \frac{(1-\beta) F \eta}{RT} \right) - \exp\left( -\frac{\beta F \eta}{RT} \right) \right]$$

The overpotential at any point on the electrode is:

$$\eta(x) = \phi_{metal} - \phi_{solution}(x) - E_{eq}$$

This makes the problem **non-linear** in general, but linearization around the equilibrium gives the **Wagner Number**.

## 2.3 The Wagner Number (Wa) — The Master Parameter

The **Wagner Number** is the single dimensionless number that characterises secondary current distribution. It is defined as:

$$\boxed{Wa = \frac{\kappa}{L \left( \dfrac{d\eta}{di} \right)_{i \to 0}}}$$

where $L$ is a characteristic length of the electrode geometry.

### Physical Interpretation of Wa
- **$Wa \to 0$:** The electrode is "kinetically sluggish" (large $d\eta/di$). The kinetic resistance dominates, but paradoxically this leads to a **uniform** distribution because every part of the electrode requires almost the same overpotential to push the same current. → Uniform → **Secondary distribution approaches uniformity.**
- **$Wa \to \infty$:** The electrode is "kinetically facile" (small $d\eta/di$). The ohmic resistance dominates, and the distribution becomes identical to the **primary distribution** (geometry-driven, edge-crowded).

### Linearized Form (Cathodic Tafel Slope Approximation)
For large cathodic overpotentials, the Butler-Volmer equation reduces to the Tafel form:

$$\eta = \frac{RT}{\alpha_c F} \ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$

So:

$$\frac{d\eta}{di} = \frac{RT}{\alpha_c F i}$$

Substituting into the Wa definition:

$$\boxed{Wa = \frac{\kappa \alpha_c F i_{avg} L}{RT i_0} \quad \text{(using } i_{avg} \text{ as reference)}}$$

Equivalently, using the local current density in the linear regime:

$$Wa = \frac{\kappa}{L} \cdot \frac{RT}{i_0 F} = \frac{\kappa RT}{L i_0 F}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use | Valid Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ | Laplace's equation in bulk electrolyte | Primary & Secondary |
| 2 | $i = -\kappa \nabla \phi$ | Ohm's law in electrolyte (local) | Primary & Secondary |
| 3 | $\frac{i(r)}{i_{avg}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{1-(r/r_0)^2}}$ | Primary distribution, disk electrode | $i_0 \to \infty$ |
| 4 | $\frac{i(x)}{i_{avg}} = 1$ | Primary distribution, parallel plates | $L \ll$ plate size |
| 5 | $i = i_0 [e^{(1-\beta)F\eta/RT} - e^{-\beta F\eta/RT}]$ | Butler-Volmer equation | Secondary |
| 6 | $\eta = \frac{RT}{\alpha_c F} \ln(i/i_0)$ | Tafel equation (cathodic) | $\vert \eta \vert \gg RT/F$ |
| 7 | $Wa = \frac{\kappa}{L (d\eta/di)_{i \to 0}}$ | Wagner number definition | Secondary |
| 8 | $Wa = \frac{\kappa R T}{L i_0 F}$ | Wagner number, linear kinetics | Small $\eta$, $i_0$ finite |
| 9 | $\frac{i(r)}{i_{avg}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-(r/r_0)^2}} \cdot \frac{1}{1+\text{const}/Wa}$ | Secondary distribution, disk (qualitative) | $Wa$ finite |
| 10 | $\eta = \phi_M - \phi_S - E_{eq}$ | Overpotential definition | All regimes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL MARKDOWN NOTE:** The vertical pipe `\vert` and `\mid` are used in this table to represent absolute value / magnitude, ensuring that the KTU formula sheet renders perfectly in markdown.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Primary distribution analysis** is used in the **design of electrolytic cells** (chlor-alkali, water electrolysis, electrorefining) to identify regions of high current density that may suffer from accelerated corrosion or non-uniform deposition.
- **Secondary distribution analysis** with the Wagner number guides **electrode material selection** — choosing a catalyst with high $i_0$ may worsen uniformity, while a kinetically slower electrode can give surprisingly uniform plating — a critical insight in **electroplating, electrorefining, and battery electrode design**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Primary Current Density for a Disk Electrode

### Problem Setup
A circular disk electrode of radius $r_0$ is embedded in an infinite insulating plane (the $z = 0$ plane) and faces a large counter-electrode at $z = L$ in a binary electrolyte of conductivity $\kappa$. The disk is held at potential $V_0$ and the counter-electrode is at $V = 0$. Find the primary current distribution on the disk.

### Step 1 — Symmetry Recognition
The geometry is axisymmetric about the $z$-axis. We use **cylindrical coordinates** $(r, \theta, z)$. By symmetry, the potential $\phi$ is independent of $\theta$.

The Laplace equation in cylindrical coordinates becomes:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left( r \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r} \right) + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

### Step 2 — Choose a Trial Solution
The geometry suggests a **separation of variables** with a solution of the form:

$$\phi(r, z) = \int_0^\infty A(k) \sin(k z) J_0(k r) \, dk$$

where $J_0$ is the Bessel function of the first kind (order 0), chosen because it is regular at the origin $r = 0$. The function $\sin(kz)$ enforces $\phi = 0$ at the counter-electrode (taking it as $z = 0$ for now) and finite values on the disk plane.

### Step 3 — Apply the Boundary Condition on the Counter-Electrode
We place the disk at $z = L$ and the counter-electrode at $z = 0$. The counter-electrode is held at $\phi = 0$. The plane $z = L$ outside the disk ($r > r_0$) is insulating. Only the disk ($r \le r_0$) is at potential $V_0$.

This mixed boundary-value problem is classical and is solved using the **Hankel transform** of order 0. The detailed solution is given by Newman (1966); we summarise the result.

### Step 4 — Current Density on the Disk
The normal current density at any point on the disk (at $z = L$) is:

$$i(r) = -\kappa \left. \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} \right|_{z=L}$$

After evaluating the Hankel transform with the appropriate boundary conditions, one obtains the **classical Newman result**:

$$\boxed{\frac{i(r)}{i_{avg}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - (r/r_0)^2}}}$$

### Step 5 — Verification of Edge Singularity
As $r \to r_0$:
$$1 - (r/r_0)^2 \to 0^+ \implies \sqrt{1 - (r/r_0)^2} \to 0^+ \implies \frac{i(r)}{i_{avg}} \to \infty$$

This confirms the **edge-crowding** phenomenon — current density becomes infinite at the rim. At the centre ($r = 0$):
$$\frac{i(0)}{i_{avg}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

The current density at the centre is **half the average**, with the rest piling up at the edge.

### Step 6 — Conservation of Current Check
The total current must equal $i_{avg} \times \pi r_0^2$. Integrating:

$$I_{total} = \int_0^{r_0} i(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \int_0^{r_0} \frac{i_{avg}}{2\sqrt{1 - (r/r_0)^2}} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

Let $u = r/r_0$, so $r = u r_0$ and $dr = r_0 \, du$:

$$I_{total} = \pi r_0^2 i_{avg} \int_0^1 \frac{u \, du}{\sqrt{1 - u^2}} = \pi r_0^2 i_{avg} \cdot \left[ -\sqrt{1-u^2} \right]_0^1 = \pi r_0^2 i_{avg} \cdot 1$$

✅ Conservation of current verified.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Wagner Number from the Linearised Butler-Volmer Equation

### Starting Point — The Butler-Volmer Equation
For small overpotentials (linear kinetics regime), we Taylor-expand the exponentials:

$$\exp(x) \approx 1 + x + \frac{x^2}{2} + \cdots$$

Applying this to the Butler-Volmer equation:

$$i = i_0 \left[ \left( 1 + \frac{(1-\beta) F \eta}{RT} \right) - \left( 1 - \frac{\beta F \eta}{RT} \right) \right]$$

### Step-by-Step Simplification
$$i = i_0 \left[ \frac{(1-\beta) F \eta}{RT} + \frac{\beta F \eta}{RT} \right]$$

$$i = i_0 \left[ \frac{F \eta}{RT} \left( (1-\beta) + \beta \right) \right]$$

$$i = i_0 \cdot \frac{F \eta}{RT} \cdot 1$$

$$\boxed{i = \frac{i_0 F}{RT} \eta \quad \text{(linear regime)}}$$

### Derivation of the Polarisation Resistance
The polarisation (charge transfer) resistance per unit area is:

$$R_{ct} = \frac{d\eta}{di} = \frac{RT}{i_0 F}$$

### Derivation of the Wagner Number
The Wagner number is the **ratio of kinetic resistance to ohmic resistance**:

$$Wa = \frac{\text{Polarisation resistance}}{\text{Ohmic resistance of electrolyte}} = \frac{R_{ct}}{\rho L / A} = \frac{R_{ct} \cdot \kappa}{L}$$

For a unit-area electrode:

$$\boxed{Wa = \frac{\kappa R T}{L i_0 F}}$$

### Step — Limiting Cases of Wa
1. **$Wa \to \infty$** (e.g., $i_0 \to \infty$ or $L \to 0$): Polarisation resistance is negligible compared to ohmic resistance. Distribution is governed by geometry → **primary distribution**.
2. **$Wa \to 0$** (e.g., $i_0 \to 0$ or $\kappa \to 0$): Polarisation resistance dominates. Every point on the electrode demands almost the same overpotential, so current is forced to be **uniform**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Secondary Current Distribution on a Strip Electrode

### Problem
A strip electrode of length $L$ embedded in an insulating plane in a cell of width $H$. The far-field is at potential $V_0$. Find the secondary current distribution.

### Governing Equations
Inside the electrolyte: Laplace's equation, $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$.

On the electrode, the **non-linear kinetic condition** applies:

$$i(x) = i_0 \left[ \exp\left( \frac{(1-\beta)F\eta(x)}{RT} \right) - \exp\left( -\frac{\beta F \eta(x)}{RT} \right) \right]$$

with the overpotential $\eta(x) = V_{metal} - \phi(x,0) - E_{eq}$ varying with position $x$ along the strip.

### Linearisation (Small $\eta$)
In the linear regime, the kinetic condition becomes:

$$\kappa \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} \bigg|_{z=0} = \frac{i_0 F}{RT} \left[ V_{metal} - \phi(x,0) - E_{eq} \right]$$

This is a **mixed boundary condition** — Robin type (linear combination of $\phi$ and $\partial \phi / \partial z$).

### Final Solution (Wagner Number Form)
For a strip electrode in a cell of width $H$ ($H \gg L$), the secondary current distribution takes the form:

$$\frac{i(x)}{i_{avg}} = \frac{1}{1 + \text{(geometry term)}/Wa} \cdot (\text{slowly varying function of } x)$$

A key asymptotic result is that, **at the centre of the strip** (far from the edge), the current density is:

$$\frac{i_{centre}}{i_{avg}} \to 1 \quad \text{as} \quad Wa \to 0$$

and:

$$\frac{i_{centre}}{i_{avg}} \to 0.5 \quad \text{as} \quad Wa \to \infty \quad \text{(primary distribution)}$$

This single relation encapsulates the entire physics of secondary current distribution: **as the Wagner number decreases, the distribution flattens**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Choosing a high-$Wa$ cell design (large $\kappa$, small $L$, small $i_0$) makes the cell *less* uniform. To improve uniformity in a practical cell, one can either **reduce** $\kappa$ (use a less conductive electrolyte — but this wastes power), **increase** the gap $L$ (which also wastes power), or **increase** the polarisation resistance (use a kinetically slow electrode — often the cheapest option). The Wagner number encapsulates this design trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Current Distribution Classification

The following Mermaid diagram maps the hierarchical relationship between the three classical current distribution models in electrochemical engineering.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrochemical Cell - Current Distribution Models] --> B[Primary Distribution]
    A --> C[Secondary Distribution]
    A --> D[Tertiary Distribution]
    
    B --> B1[Governed by Ohm's Law Only]
    B --> B2[Laplace Equation: nabla^2 phi = 0]
    B --> B3[Geometry-Driven]
    B --> B4[Edge Crowding at r -> r0]
    B --> B5[Wagner Number Wa -> infinity]
    
    C --> C1[Ohm + Activation Kinetics]
    C --> C2[Linear: nabla^2 phi = 0]
    C --> C3[Non-linear: BV at surface]
    C --> C4[Uniformity improves as Wa decreases]
    C --> C5[Wa = kappa RT over L i0 F]
    
    D --> D1[Ohm + Activation + Mass Transport]
    D --> D2[Concentration Overpotential included]
    D --> D3[Nernst-Planck Equation]
    D --> D4[Limiting Current Effects]
    
    B5 --> C
    C --> D
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Effect of Wagner Number on Distribution Uniformity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K[Kinetic Resistance Rct = RT over i0 F] --> W[Total Cell Resistance]
    O[Ohmic Resistance R_ohm = L over kappa] --> W
    W --> WA[Wagner Number Wa = Rct over R_ohm]
    WA --> CH1{Wa is Large?}
    WA --> CH2{Wa is Small?}
    CH1 --> R1[Geometry Dominates]
    R1 --> R2[Primary Distribution]
    R2 --> R3[Non-uniform with Edge Crowding]
    CH2 --> S1[Kinetics Dominate]
    S1 --> S2[Uniform Secondary Distribution]
    S2 --> S3[Low Plating Variability]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Edge-Crowding on a Disk Electrode (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Disk_Electrode[Disk Electrode r0 at z = 0]
        C1[Centre r=0: i over i_avg = 0.5]
        C2[Mid radius r=0.5 r0: i over i_avg = 0.577]
        C3[Near edge r=0.9 r0: i over i_avg = 1.29]
        C4[Edge r -> r0: i over i_avg -> infinity]
    end
    CE[Counter Electrode at z = L, phi = 0] -->|Equipotential lines| Disk_Electrode
    Disk_Electrode -->|Local current density i = -kappa dphi over dz| Current_Flow[Current Density Profile]
    Current_Flow --> Result1[Strong Non-Uniformity]
    Current_Flow --> Result2[Mathematical Singularity at r0]
    Current_Flow --> Result3[Real Cells: Mass Transport Smooths the Singularity]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Design Decisions Based on Wagner Number

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Design Target: Uniform Plating] --> S1{Choose Strategy}
    S1 --> P1[Decrease Wa]
    S1 --> P2[Increase Wa]
    P1 --> Q1[Increase Polarisation Resistance]
    P1 --> Q2[Decrease Electrolyte Conductivity]
    P1 --> Q3[Increase Electrode Gap L]
    Q1 --> R1[Use Slower Catalyst - Low i0]
    Q2 --> R2[Use Dilute Electrolyte - Power Loss]
    Q3 --> R3[Increase Inter-Electrode Distance - Power Loss]
    P2 --> F1[Use Faster Catalyst - High i0]
    P2 --> F2[Use Conductive Electrolyte]
    P2 --> F3[Small Gap Cell]
    F1 --> OUT1[Edge Crowding Risk]
    F2 --> OUT2[Power Efficient but Less Uniform]
    F3 --> OUT3[Compact Cell Geometry]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** The Mermaid diagrams above provide the block-level functional architecture of the current distribution models and the design trade-offs governed by the Wagner number. For complex physical drawings (e.g., equipotential line maps), the conceptual model is captured at the block level, as per KTU schematic conventions.
