# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define primary current distribution. Under what conditions does an electrochemical cell exhibit primary current distribution?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> Primary current distribution is the current density profile across an electrode surface when the cell behaviour is governed entirely by the **ohmic resistance of the electrolyte**, with activation overpotential and concentration overpotential assumed to be zero. (1 mark)

> The governing equation is Laplace's equation: $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$, with $i = -\kappa \nabla \phi$ on the electrode. (1 mark)

> It is observed when the **exchange current density $i_0 \to \infty$** (kinetically facile electrode) and mass transfer is fast (excess supporting electrolyte). (1 mark)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**What is the Wagner number? How does its magnitude influence the current distribution in an electrochemical cell?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The Wagner number is a dimensionless parameter defined as: $Wa = \dfrac{\kappa}{L \, (d\eta/di)_{i \to 0}} = \dfrac{\kappa R T}{L i_0 F}$ (1.5 marks)

> **Large Wa** ($Wa \to \infty$): Ohmic resistance dominates; distribution is non-uniform and approaches the primary distribution with edge crowding. (0.75 mark)

> **Small Wa** ($Wa \to 0$): Kinetic (polarisation) resistance dominates; distribution becomes uniform across the electrode surface. (0.75 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** Starting from the Butler-Volmer equation, derive the expression for the Wagner number for an electrochemical cell in the linear kinetics regime. Discuss the limiting cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a circular disk electrode of radius $r_0$ embedded in an insulating plane, derive the primary current density distribution $i(r)/i_{avg}$. What fraction of the total current flows in the outer 10% of the disk radius? **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question A (a) — Wagner Number Derivation

**Step 1: Write the Butler-Volmer equation** (1 mark):

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{(1-\beta)F\eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\beta F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

**Step 2: Taylor expansion for small $\eta$** (1 mark):

$$i = i_0 \left[ 1 + \frac{(1-\beta)F\eta}{RT} - 1 + \frac{\beta F \eta}{RT} \right]$$

**Step 3: Simplification** (1 mark):

$$i = i_0 \cdot \frac{F \eta}{RT}$$

**Step 4: Polarisation resistance** (1 mark):

$$R_{ct} = \frac{d\eta}{di} = \frac{RT}{i_0 F}$$

**Step 5: Define the Wagner number** as the ratio of polarisation to ohmic resistance (1.5 marks):

$$Wa = \frac{R_{ct}}{R_{ohm}} = \frac{\dfrac{RT}{i_0 F}}{\dfrac{L}{\kappa}} = \boxed{\frac{\kappa R T}{L i_0 F}}$$

**Step 6: Limiting cases** (1.5 marks):
- $Wa \to \infty$ ($i_0 \to \infty$ or $L \to 0$): kinetics is fast → primary distribution dominates (geometry-controlled).
- $Wa \to 0$ ($i_0 \to 0$ or $\kappa \to 0$): kinetics is slow → uniform distribution.

### Solution to Question A (b) — Disk Electrode Primary Distribution

**Step 1:** Set up the problem in cylindrical coordinates. The governing equation is Laplace's equation. (1 mark)

**Step 2:** Apply boundary conditions: counter-electrode at $\phi = 0$, disk at $\phi = V_0$, insulating plane at $\partial \phi / \partial z = 0$. (1 mark)

**Step 3:** Solve using the Hankel transform (Newman 1966). The result is: (2 marks)

$$\frac{i(r)}{i_{avg}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{1 - (r/r_0)^2}}$$

**Step 4: Fraction of current in outer 10%** (3 marks):

The fraction of total current flowing in the annulus $0.9 r_0 \le r \le r_0$ is:

$$f = \frac{\int_{0.9 r_0}^{r_0} i(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr}{\int_0^{r_0} i(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr} = \int_{0.9}^{1} \frac{u \, du}{\sqrt{1-u^2}}$$

Let $w = 1 - u^2$, $dw = -2u \, du$, so $u \, du = -dw/2$. When $u = 0.9$, $w = 0.19$; when $u = 1$, $w = 0$:

$$f = \int_{0.19}^{0} \frac{-dw/2}{\sqrt{w}} = \frac{1}{2} \int_0^{0.19} w^{-1/2} \, dw = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 \sqrt{w} \Big|_0^{0.19} = \sqrt{0.19}$$

$$f \approx 0.436 \quad \text{or} \quad \boxed{43.6\%}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Over **43% of the total current** flows in the outermost 10% of the disk radius — a striking illustration of the edge-crowding effect. This is why uniform electrode design (e.g., in lithium-ion battery electrodes, fuel cell gas diffusion layers) requires careful attention to current distribution.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Internal Choice]

**(a)** What is secondary current distribution? Derive the linearised form of the Butler-Volmer equation and use it to obtain the Wagner number. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A disk electrode of radius $r_0 = 1$ cm is used in a cell with electrolyte conductivity $\kappa = 10$ S/m and inter-electrode gap $L = 2$ cm. The exchange current density is $i_0 = 10$ A/m² and the temperature is 298 K. Calculate the Wagner number. Will the current distribution be uniform or non-uniform? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question B (a) — Secondary Distribution & Wagner Number

**Step 1: Definition of secondary distribution** (1 mark):
Secondary current distribution is the current density profile when both ohmic resistance of the electrolyte and activation overpotential (charge transfer kinetics) are significant, while mass transport is neglected.

**Step 2: Write the Butler-Volmer equation** (1 mark):

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{(1-\beta)F\eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\beta F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

**Step 3: Linearisation using Taylor series** (1.5 marks):

For $\vert \eta \vert \ll RT/F$ (linear regime), $e^x \approx 1 + x$:

$$i \approx i_0 \cdot \frac{F \eta}{RT} \implies \eta = \frac{RT}{i_0 F} i$$

**Step 4: Polarisation resistance** (0.5 mark):

$$R_{ct} = \frac{RT}{i_0 F}$$

**Step 5: Wagner number derivation** (2 marks):

The Wagner number is the dimensionless ratio of polarisation to ohmic resistance:

$$Wa = \frac{R_{ct}}{R_{ohm}} = \frac{RT/i_0 F}{L/\kappa} = \frac{\kappa R T}{L i_0 F}$$

**Step 6: Discussion** (1 mark): A larger $Wa$ indicates that ohmic effects dominate, leading to primary-like distribution; smaller $Wa$ indicates kinetic control with uniform distribution.

### Solution to Question B (b) — Numerical Wagner Number Calculation

**Given:**
- $r_0 = 1$ cm $= 0.01$ m (characteristic length, $L$)
- $\kappa = 10$ S/m
- Inter-electrode gap $= 2$ cm $= 0.02$ m (we will use this as $L$ as per the problem)
- $i_0 = 10$ A/m²
- $T = 298$ K
- $F = 96485$ C/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Step 1: Write the Wagner number formula** (1 mark):

$$Wa = \frac{\kappa R T}{L i_0 F}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values** (1.5 marks):

$$Wa = \frac{10 \times 8.314 \times 298}{0.02 \times 10 \times 96485}$$

**Step 3: Numerator evaluation** (1 mark):

$$\text{Numerator} = 10 \times 8.314 \times 298 = 24775.72$$

**Step 4: Denominator evaluation** (1 mark):

$$\text{Denominator} = 0.02 \times 10 \times 96485 = 19297$$

**Step 5: Final calculation** (1 mark):

$$Wa = \frac{24775.72}{19297} \approx 1.284$$

**Step 6: Interpretation** (1.5 marks):

Since $Wa \approx 1.28$, which is of order unity, the system is in a **transitional regime** between the primary (non-uniform) and uniform distribution limits. The current distribution will be **moderately non-uniform**, with appreciable edge crowding — but less severe than the pure primary case. To achieve more uniform distribution, one would need to **decrease** $Wa$ — for example, by decreasing $i_0$ (using a kinetically slower electrode) or by increasing $L$ or decreasing $\kappa$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Scheme Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing primary and secondary distributions:** Many students write that "primary distribution is uniform." This is **WRONG** for general geometries. Primary distribution is **uniform only for parallel-plate geometry with infinite plates**. For a disk, the primary distribution is *highly non-uniform* with edge crowding. (Lose up to 2 marks.)
>
> 2. **Sign error in Butler-Volmer linearisation:** The sign of $\eta$ in the linearised form $i = (i_0 F/RT)\eta$ is often flipped. Recall: positive $\eta$ (anodic) gives positive (anodic) current. (Lose up to 1 mark.)
>
> 3. **Forgetting the characteristic length $L$ in Wagner number:** The Wagner number is **dimensionless** and *must* include a characteristic length. Writing $Wa = \kappa R T / (i_0 F)$ without $L$ is dimensionally incorrect. (Lose 1 mark.)
>
> 4. **Wrong limit of primary distribution:** At the centre of a disk, $i/i_{avg} = 1/2$, not $1$. At the edge, $i/i_{avg} \to \infty$, not "0". (Lose 1 mark.)
>
> 5. **Not stating assumptions:** Always explicitly state: (i) steady state, (ii) excess supporting electrolyte, (iii) isothermal conditions. (Lose 0.5–1 mark depending on the question.)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Primary & Secondary Current Distribution**

### Primary Current Distribution
- ☐ Governed **only** by electrolyte resistance: $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$, $i = -\kappa \nabla \phi$.
- ☐ Depends **purely on geometry**, not on kinetics or mass transport.
- ☐ Corresponds to $i_0 \to \infty$ (kinetically facile electrode).
- ☐ **Parallel-plate cell:** $i/i_{avg} = 1$ (uniform).
- ☐ **Disk electrode:** $i(r)/i_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{1-(r/r_0)^2}}$ — **edge crowding**, $i \to \infty$ at the rim.
- ☐ $i(0)/i_{avg} = 1/2$ at the centre of the disk.
- ☐ **43.6% of total current** flows in the outermost 10% of a disk radius.

### Secondary Current Distribution
- ☐ Includes **activation overpotential** (charge transfer kinetics) in addition to ohmic resistance.
- ☐ Neglects concentration overpotential (excess supporting electrolyte).
- ☐ Governed by **Butler-Volmer** at the surface, **Laplace** in the bulk.
- ☐ Quantified by the **Wagner number**:

$$Wa = \frac{\kappa R T}{L i_0 F}$$

- ☐ **$Wa \to \infty$** → primary distribution (geometry dominates, non-uniform).
- ☐ **$Wa \to 0$** → uniform distribution (kinetics dominate).
- ☐ Increasing $L$, decreasing $\kappa$, or decreasing $i_0$ → decreases $Wa$ → more uniform.
- ☐ Linearised Butler-Volmer (small $\eta$): $i = \dfrac{i_0 F}{RT} \eta$.

### Quick Sanity Checks
- ☐ $Wa$ is **dimensionless** — always include the characteristic length $L$.
- ☐ $\kappa$ in S/m, $i_0$ in A/m², $L$ in m, $T$ in K.
- ☐ Faraday $F = 96485$ C/mol; Gas constant $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).
- ☐ Charge transfer coefficient $\beta \approx 0.5$ (often assumed symmetric).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Students:** Current distribution is a high-weightage topic in PECHT417. Master the **Wagner number** derivation — it appears in nearly every KTU board exam. Always quote the **limiting cases** ($Wa \to 0$ and $Wa \to \infty$) and explain the **engineering implications** (uniformity, plating quality, electrode design).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotating Disc Electrode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT417 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rotating Disc Electrode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rotating Disc Electrode (RDE):** A hydrodynamic working electrode in which a circular disc of conductive material (Pt, Au, GC) embedded in an insulating sheath (Teflon, Kel-F) is rotated about its central axis at a precisely controlled angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s). Rotation establishes a well-defined, laminar flow of electrolyte normal to the disc surface, producing a uniform and reproducible **convective-diffusion boundary layer** whose thickness is a precise inverse function of $\omega^{1/2}$.

In the context of **PECHT417 – Electrochemical Engineering (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1)**, the RDE is treated as the canonical experimental tool for isolating **mass-transport-controlled currents** from kinetically controlled currents in electrochemical reactions. It is the experimental workhorse for measuring heterogeneous rate constants $k^0$, diffusion coefficients $D_i$, and the number of transferred electrons $n$.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stirred Honey Pot"

Imagine you have a wide, shallow pot of honey lying still — diffusion of a dye into the honey is agonizingly slow. Now mount a flat disc-shaped stirrer at the center and rotate it: the viscous drag **pulls the liquid vertically upward toward the disc and flings it radially outward**. This is exactly what happens at an RDE.

- The **upward flow** continuously delivers fresh electroactive species to the electrode surface.
- The **thickness of the layer where concentration drops from bulk to surface value** becomes razor-thin and perfectly predictable.
- Faster spin ⇒ thinner layer ⇒ larger current.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Insight:** Unlike a stationary electrode (planar diffusion → semi-infinite), the RDE imposes **steady-state** diffusion. The current becomes **time-independent** at a given rotation rate — an enormous experimental advantage for extracting clean kinetic data.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Kinematic viscosity of water (25 °C) | $\nu$ | $\mathbf{1.0 \times 10^{-2}\,cm^2\,s^{-1}}$ |
| Faraday constant | $F$ | $\mathbf{96485\,C\,mol^{-1}}$ |
| Gas constant | $R$ | $\mathbf{8.314\,J\,mol^{-1}\,K^{-1}}$ |
| Typical angular velocity range | $\omega$ | $\mathbf{100 - 10000\,rpm}$ |
| Typical disc radius | $r_0$ | $\mathbf{0.1 - 0.5\,cm}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hydrodynamic Flow Field Near the Disc

When the disc rotates with angular velocity $\omega$, the velocity field in cylindrical coordinates $(r, \phi, z)$ has three components:

- Radial: $v_r$ — fluid is ejected **outward**.
- Azimuthal: $v_\phi$ — fluid is **swirled** tangentially.
- Axial: $v_z$ — fluid is drawn **upward** toward the disc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cochran's Solution (1934):** The exact velocity components of an incompressible, viscous fluid at a rotating disc are given in terms of the dimensionless axial variable $\xi = z \sqrt{\omega/\nu}$.

The **thickness of the hydrodynamic boundary layer** scales as:

$$
\delta_H \sim \sqrt{\frac{\nu}{\omega}}
$$

The **thickness of the diffusion (concentration) boundary layer** is smaller by a factor of $Sc^{-1/3}$ (where $Sc = \nu/D$ is the Schmidt number):

$$
\delta_D \sim \left(\frac{D}{\omega}\right)^{1/3} \cdot \nu^{1/6}
$$

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of RDE Operation

1. **Bulk solution** has uniform concentration $C_b$ of the electroactive species $O$.
2. **Rotation** drags solution vertically toward the disc via viscous entrainment.
3. At the disc surface, $O$ is reduced (or oxidized) → surface concentration $C_s < C_b$.
4. A **steady-state concentration gradient** $\partial C / \partial z$ is established within $\delta_D$.
5. Fick's first law gives the flux; multiplying by $nFA$ gives the **diffusion-limited current**.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $i_L = 0.62 \, n F A D^{2/3} \omega^{1/2} \nu^{-1/6} C_b$ | **Levich Equation** (limiting current) | A |
| 2 | $\delta_H = 3.6 \, (\nu / \omega)^{1/2}$ | Hydrodynamic boundary layer thickness | cm |
| 3 | $\delta_D = 1.61 \, \nu^{1/6} D^{1/3} \omega^{-1/2}$ | Diffusion layer thickness (Levich) | cm |
| 4 | $\omega = 2\pi f / 60$ | Angular velocity from rpm $f$ | rad/s |
| 5 | $Sc = \nu / D$ | Schmidt number | dimensionless |
| 6 | $i^{-1} = i_K^{-1} + i_L^{-1}$ | Koutecky-Levich reciprocal relation | A$^{-1}$ |
| 7 | $i_K = n F A k^0 C_b$ | Kinetic current (mixed control) | A |
| 8 | $i_L / \omega^{1/2} = 0.62 \, n F A D^{2/3} \nu^{-1/6} C_b$ | Levich slope (plot basis) | A·s$^{1/2}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Alert:** Never write $\vert x \vert$ in markdown tables — use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to prevent table parser breakage.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Fuel cell R&D:** Characterizing ORR (oxygen reduction reaction) kinetics on Pt/C catalysts.
- **Corrosion science:** Measuring Tafel slopes and corrosion rates.
- **Electrosynthesis:** Optimizing yields of redox products.
- **Sensor development:** Calibration of amperometric biosensors.
- **Battery research:** Studying lithium-ion intercalation kinetics.

The RDE is the **gold standard** for any study where you must distinguish "how fast the reaction can run" (kinetics) from "how fast the reactant can arrive" (mass transport).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Levich Equation (Full Working)

**Starting point:** Convective-diffusion equation in cylindrical coordinates for steady state:

$$
v_z \frac{\partial C}{\partial z} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}
$$

(valid in the thin diffusion layer where radial convection is negligible compared to axial terms).

**Step 1 — Hydrodynamic velocity near the disc.** Cochran's solution gives for small $z$:

$$
v_z \approx 0.510 \, \omega^{3/2} \nu^{-1/2} \, z^2
$$

This is the **dominant axial velocity component** that drives fresh reactant toward the electrode.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the convective-diffusion equation.**

$$
0.510 \, \omega^{3/2} \nu^{-1/2} \, z^2 \frac{dC}{dz} = D \frac{d^2 C}{dz^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Boundary conditions.**
- At $z \to \infty$: $C \to C_b$ (bulk concentration).
- At $z = 0$: $C = C_s$ (surface concentration; $= 0$ for limiting current).

**Step 4 — First integration.** Define $\theta = (C - C_s) / (C_b - C_s)$. Multiply through and integrate once using $dC/dz \to 0$ as $z \to \infty$:

$$
\int_0^{z} \left(\frac{d\theta}{dz'}\right) \, dz' = \left(\frac{0.510 \, \omega^{3/2}}{D \, \nu^{1/2}}\right)^{1/3} \int_0^{\theta} \theta'^{\,-2/3} \, d\theta'
$$

This yields:

$$
1 - \theta^{1/3} = \left(\frac{0.510 \, \omega^{3/2}}{D \, \nu^{1/2}}\right)^{1/3} \cdot \frac{z}{3^{1/3}}
$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate to obtain the concentration gradient at the surface.**

$$
\left. \frac{dC}{dz} \right|_{z=0} = - \frac{(C_b - C_s)}{\delta_D}
$$

where the **diffusion layer thickness** is identified as:

$$
\delta_D = 1.611 \, D^{1/3} \nu^{1/6} \omega^{-1/2}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Fick's first law at the surface.** The molar flux is:

$$
J = -D \left. \frac{dC}{dz} \right|_{z=0} = \frac{D(C_b - C_s)}{\delta_D}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert flux to current density.**

$$
i = n F J = \frac{n F D (C_b - C_s)}{\delta_D}
$$

**Step 8 — Substitute $\delta_D$ and set $C_s = 0$ for the limiting current.**

$$
i_L = n F D C_b \cdot \frac{\omega^{1/2}}{1.611 \, D^{1/3} \nu^{1/6}}
$$

**Step 9 — Simplify the numerical prefactor.**

$$
i_L = \frac{1}{1.611} \, n F D^{2/3} \omega^{1/2} \nu^{-1/6} C_b = 0.62 \, n F D^{2/3} \omega^{1/2} \nu^{-1/6} C_b
$$

Multiplying by electrode area $A$:

$$
\boxed{\,i_L = 0.62 \, n F A D^{2/3} \omega^{1/2} \nu^{-1/6} C_b\,}
$$

This is the **Levich equation**, the cornerstone of all RDE analysis.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Koutecky-Levich Equation

When both kinetics and mass transport are rate-limiting, the observed current $i$ obeys:

$$
\frac{1}{i} = \frac{1}{i_K} + \frac{1}{i_L}
$$

**Step 1 — Define the kinetic current.** From Butler-Volmer at low overpotential:

$$
i_K = n F A k^0 C_b
$$

**Step 2 — Define the limiting current (Levich).** Use the derived form above.

**Step 3 — Combine via the resistance analogy.** Mass transport and kinetic steps occur **in series**, so their "resistances" (reciprocal currents) add:

$$
\frac{1}{i} = \frac{1}{n F A k^0 C_b} + \frac{1}{0.62 \, n F A D^{2/3} \omega^{1/2} \nu^{-1/6} C_b}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into the experimentally useful linear form.**

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{1}{i} = \frac{1}{n F A k^0 C_b} + \frac{1}{0.62 \, n F A D^{2/3} \nu^{-1/6} C_b} \cdot \frac{1}{\omega^{1/2}}\,}
$$

A plot of $i^{-1}$ vs $\omega^{-1/2}$ is a **straight line** with:
- **Slope:** $\dfrac{1}{0.62 \, n F A D^{2/3} \nu^{-1/6} C_b}$
- **Intercept:** $\dfrac{1}{n F A k^0 C_b}$

From slope → $n$ or $D$; from intercept → $k^0$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A Pt RDE ($A = 0.196\,\text{cm}^2$) in $1\,\text{mM}$ $\text{K}_3[\text{Fe(CN)}_6]$ + 0.1 M KCl at 25 °C ($D = 7.6 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{cm}^2\,\text{s}^{-1}$, $\nu = 0.01\,\text{cm}^2\,\text{s}^{-1}$) rotates at $\omega = 157\,\text{rad/s}$ (1500 rpm). Calculate the limiting current for a 1-electron reduction.

**Solution:**

Compute the prefactor $0.62 \, D^{2/3} \nu^{-1/6}$:

$$
D^{2/3} = (7.6 \times 10^{-6})^{2/3} = (7.6)^{2/3} \times 10^{-4} = 3.84 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{cm}^{4/3}\,\text{s}^{-2/3}
$$

$$
\nu^{-1/6} = (0.01)^{-1/6} = 10^{1/6} = 1.468
$$

$$
0.62 \times 3.84 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.468 = 3.49 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Now plug into the Levich equation:

$$
i_L = (3.49 \times 10^{-4}) \times (1)(96485)(0.196)(157^{1/2})(10^{-6})
$$

$$
157^{1/2} = 12.53
$$

$$
i_L = 3.49 \times 10^{-4} \times 96485 \times 0.196 \times 12.53 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
i_L \approx 8.27 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{A} = 82.7\,\mu\text{A}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical ferricyanide test reaction** — every electrochemistry laboratory uses it to verify RDE functionality. The measured $i_L$ must match this value to within ~5%.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an RDE Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rotating Shaft Motor with Precise Speed Controller] --> B[Disc Electrode Assembly]
    B --> B1[Conductive Disc Material Pt or Au or Glassy Carbon]
    B --> B2[Insulating Sheath Teflon or Kel-F]
    C[Electrochemical Cell Three Electrode Configuration] --> C1[Working Electrode Rotating Disc]
    C --> C2[Reference Electrode SCE or AgAgCl]
    C --> C3[Counter Electrode Pt Wire or Mesh]
    D[Potentiostat with Current Follower] --> D1[Apply Potential Sweep]
    D --> D2[Measure Resulting Current]
    E[Data Acquisition System] --> E1[Plot Current versus Potential]
    E --> E2[Plot Levich iL versus omega to the power 0.5]
    E --> E3[Koutecky Levich i inverse versus omega inverse 0.5]
    C1 --> D
    D2 --> E
    F[Electrolyte with Bulk Concentration Cb] --> C
```

### 4.2 Schematic Cross-Section of an RDE (Block-Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RDE_HEAD["RDE Assembly Cross Section"]
        A1[Rotating Shaft] --> A2[Conductive Metal Rod]
        A2 --> A3[Disc Surface Pt Radius r0]
        A3 --> A4[Insulating Sheath Teflon]
        A5[Bulk Electrolyte Cb] --> A6[Diffusion Boundary Layer delta_D]
        A6 --> A7[Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer delta_H]
        A7 --> A3
    end
```

### 4.3 Flow Logic: From Rotation to Steady-State Current

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Disc Rotates omega rad per s] --> stepB[Axial Suction of Electrolyte]
    stepB --> stepC[Steady Diffusion Layer Forms]
    stepC --> stepD[Constant Concentration Gradient]
    stepD --> stepE[Steady State Limiting Current iL]
    stepE --> stepF[Levich Equation Predicts iL proportional to omega^0.5]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The flow visualization above represents the **cause→effect chain** that makes the RDE such a clean experimental tool: rotation → controlled convection → predictable $\delta_D$ → reproducible $i_L$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define the Rotating Disc Electrode. What is the role of the insulating sheath?

**Model Answer:**
A Rotating Disc Electrode is a hydrodynamic working electrode consisting of a circular disc of conductive material (e.g., Pt, Au, glassy carbon) embedded flush in an insulating cylindrical sheath. The electrode is rotated at a controlled angular velocity $\omega$ about its vertical axis, generating laminar electrolyte flow normal to the disc surface and establishing a steady diffusion layer. The insulating sheath confines current flow to the disc area, ensuring a well-defined electrode area $A = \pi r_0^2$ and a uniform current distribution, while preventing lateral leakage of current.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** State the Levich equation. What does a plot of $i_L$ vs $\omega^{1/2}$ yield?

**Model Answer:**
The Levich equation for a Rotating Disc Electrode is:

$$
i_L = 0.62 \, n F A D^{2/3} \omega^{1/2} \nu^{-1/6} C_b
$$

A plot of $i_L$ (y-axis) versus $\omega^{1/2}$ (x-axis) is a **straight line passing through the origin** with slope $0.62 \, n F A D^{2/3} \nu^{-1/6} C_b$. From this slope, one can determine either the diffusion coefficient $D$ (if $n$ is known) or the number of electrons $n$ (if $D$ is independently known). Linearity confirms that the reaction is **purely mass-transport-controlled** in the limiting-current region.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly lose marks by (i) forgetting the units consistency ($D$ in $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$, $\nu$ in $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$, $\omega$ in rad/s); (ii) mis-converting rpm to rad/s; (iii) omitting the area $A$ when using $i_L$ instead of current density; and (iv) drawing the Koutecky-Levich plot with axes inverted.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Derive the Levich equation for the limiting current at a Rotating Disc Electrode, clearly stating the boundary conditions used. (7 Marks)

**(b)** For the reduction of $\text{O}_2$ at a Pt RDE ($A = 0.25\,\text{cm}^2$) in 0.1 M KOH, the following data are obtained at 25 °C:

| $\omega$ (rpm) | $i_L$ ($\mu$A) |
|---|---|
| 400 | 92 |
| 900 | 138 |
| 1600 | 184 |
| 2500 | 230 |

Using $\nu = 0.01\,\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$ and $C_b = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{mol/cm}^3$, determine the number of electrons $n$ transferred and the diffusion coefficient $D$ of $\text{O}_2$. (7 Marks)

---

**(a) Model Solution:**

The convective-diffusion equation for steady state in the thin diffusion layer is:

$$
v_z \frac{dC}{dz} = D \frac{d^2 C}{dz^2}
$$

Cochran's hydrodynamic solution near the disc gives:

$$
v_z = 0.510 \, \omega^{3/2} \nu^{-1/2} z^2
$$

**Boundary Conditions:**
- As $z \to \infty$: $C \to C_b$
- At $z = 0$: $C = C_s = 0$ (limiting-current condition)

Substituting $v_z$ and integrating once with the condition $dC/dz \to 0$ as $z \to \infty$ yields:

$$
\frac{dC}{dz} = -\frac{(C_b - C_s)}{\delta_D}
$$

with the **diffusion layer thickness**:

$$
\delta_D = 1.611 \, D^{1/3} \nu^{1/6} \omega^{-1/2}
$$

Fick's first law at the surface gives flux $J = D(C_b - C_s)/\delta_D$, and the current is $i = nFAJ$. Setting $C_s = 0$:

$$
i_L = 0.62 \, n F A D^{2/3} \omega^{1/2} \nu^{-1/6} C_b \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

Convert rpm → rad/s using $\omega = 2\pi f/60$:

| $\omega$ (rpm) | $\omega$ (rad/s) | $\omega^{1/2}$ | $i_L$ ($\mu$A) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 400 | 41.89 | 6.47 | 92 |
| 900 | 94.25 | 9.71 | 138 |
| 1600 | 167.55 | 12.94 | 184 |
| 2500 | 261.80 | 16.18 | 230 |

Linear regression of $i_L$ vs $\omega^{1/2}$ yields slope $\approx 14.2\,\mu\text{A·s}^{1/2}$:

$$
\text{slope} = 0.62 \, n F A D^{2/3} \nu^{-1/6} C_b
$$

Substituting $F = 96485$, $A = 0.25$, $\nu^{-1/6} = 1.468$, $C_b = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$:

$$
14.2 \times 10^{-6} = 0.62 \times n \times 96485 \times 0.25 \times D^{2/3} \times 1.468 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-6}
$$

Solving iteratively with $n = 4$ gives $D \approx 1.93 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$, matching the known literature value for $\text{O}_2$ diffusion in KOH ($D \approx 1.9 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$). ✓

**Result:** $n = 4$ (4-electron ORR pathway), $D_{O_2} \approx 1.93 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the construction and working of a Rotating Disc Electrode. Discuss the nature of the diffusion layer and how its thickness varies with rotation speed. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Using the Koutecky-Levich equation, show how a plot of $i^{-1}$ versus $\omega^{-1/2}$ can be used to determine the kinetic current $i_K$ and the diffusion coefficient $D$. A reduction reaction at an RDE gives $i^{-1}$ values of $3.20 \times 10^3$, $2.40 \times 10^3$, $2.00 \times 10^3$, and $1.76 \times 10^3\,\text{A}^{-1}$ at $\omega^{1/2}$ values of 10, 15, 20, and 25 rad$^{1/2}$/s$^{1/2}$ respectively. Calculate $i_K$ and the slope. (7 Marks)

---

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Construction:** The RDE consists of (i) a metallic disc (Pt, Au, GC) of radius $r_0$ embedded flush in (ii) an insulating sheath (Teflon) of larger radius $R$, mounted on (iii) a rotating shaft driven by a motor with precise speed control. The three-electrode cell contains the RDE (working), a reference electrode (SCE/Ag-AgCl), and a Pt counter electrode.

**Working:** On rotation, viscous drag creates a **flow pattern** in which electrolyte is drawn axially upward toward the disc and thrown radially outward. This establishes:
- A **hydrodynamic boundary layer** of thickness $\delta_H = 3.6(\nu/\omega)^{1/2}$.
- A thinner **diffusion (concentration) boundary layer** of thickness $\delta_D = 1.61\, D^{1/3}\nu^{1/6}\omega^{-1/2}$.

Because $\delta_D \propto \omega^{-1/2}$, faster rotation thins the diffusion layer, steepens the concentration gradient, and increases the limiting current. The steady-state nature eliminates capacitive (charging) currents and yields highly reproducible measurements.

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

The Koutecky-Levich equation:

$$
\frac{1}{i} = \frac{1}{i_K} + \frac{1}{0.62 \, n F A D^{2/3} \nu^{-1/6} C_b} \cdot \frac{1}{\omega^{1/2}}
$$

is of the form $y = c + m x$ with $y = i^{-1}$, $x = \omega^{-1/2}$:

| $\omega^{1/2}$ | $\omega^{-1/2}$ | $i^{-1}$ (A$^{-1}$) |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | 0.1000 | 3200 |
| 15 | 0.0667 | 2400 |
| 20 | 0.0500 | 2000 |
| 25 | 0.0400 | 1760 |

Linear regression gives:
- **Slope** $m = \dfrac{\Delta i^{-1}}{\Delta \omega^{-1/2}} \approx 24{,}000\,\text{A}^{-1}\,\text{s}^{-1/2}$
- **Intercept** $c = 1/i_K \approx 800\,\text{A}^{-1}$

Therefore:

$$
i_K = \frac{1}{800} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A} = 1.25\,\text{mA}
$$

$$
\text{slope} = \frac{1}{0.62 \, n F A D^{2/3} \nu^{-1/6} C_b} \Rightarrow D = \left(\frac{1}{0.62 \, n F A \nu^{-1/6} C_b \cdot m}\right)^{3/2}
$$

With appropriate values, $D$ can be computed; the key exam takeaway is that the **intercept gives $i_K$** and the **slope gives $D$** (or $n$). $\blacksquare$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RDE = steady-state hydrodynamic electrode** — current is time-independent at fixed $\omega$.
- **Levich equation** is the master formula: $i_L = 0.62\, n F A D^{2/3} \omega^{1/2} \nu^{-1/6} C_b$.
- **Diffusion layer thickness** $\delta_D \propto \omega^{-1/2}$ — the heart of all RDE physics.
- **Hydrodynamic layer thickness** $\delta_H \propto \omega^{-1/2}$ but is ~10× larger than $\delta_D$.
- **Schmidt number** $Sc = \nu/D$ determines $\delta_D/\delta_H \approx Sc^{-1/3}$.
- **Levich plot:** $i_L$ vs $\omega^{1/2}$ → straight line through origin (mass-transport control confirmed).
- **Koutecky-Levich plot:** $i^{-1}$ vs $\omega^{-1/2}$ → straight line; intercept = $1/i_K$, slope $\propto 1/D^{2/3}$.
- **Units trap:** Use CGS (cm, s, mol) — never mix SI (m) and CGS in the same calculation.
- **rpm → rad/s:** always convert via $\omega = 2\pi f / 60$.
- **Limiting current** occurs when $C_s = 0$; **mixed current** when both kinetics and diffusion contribute.
- **Ferricyanide/ferrocyanide** couple is the standard test reaction for verifying RDE performance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrochemical Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT417 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrochemical Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrochemical Series (ECS):** The Electrochemical Series is a tabular arrangement of elements (primarily metals and hydrogen) in the **increasing order of their standard reduction potentials ($E^0$) measured with respect to the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) at 25 °C (298 K) and 1 M (or 1 atm) concentration of the ions involved.**

The standard reduction potential ($E^0$) is the **tendency of an electrode to gain electrons (i.e., to be reduced)** when all species are in their standard states. A more positive $E^0$ means a greater tendency toward reduction (acting as a **cathode**), while a more negative $E^0$ means a greater tendency toward oxidation (acting as an **anode**).

**Mathematically**, the standard cell potential of any galvanic couple is:
$$E^0_{cell} = E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode}$$
where both $E^0$ values are standard reduction potentials.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **tug-of-war competition** where each metal is assigned a "pulling strength" corresponding to its desire to hold onto its electrons versus grabbing new ones.

- **Highly noble metals** (like Gold, Platinum) sitting at the **top** of the series (very positive $E^0$) are like the **strongest tug-of-war players** — they are "satisfied" with their ionic form and strongly resist giving up their electrons; they prefer to *steal* electrons from others (i.e., be reduced).
- **Highly active metals** (like Magnesium, Zinc) sitting at the **bottom** of the series (very negative $E^0$) are **eager to donate** their electrons — they "lose willingly," which is why they oxidize (corrode) easily in practical environments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** is arbitrarily assigned $E^0 = 0.000$ V. It serves as the universal reference point, just like the **sea level** is the reference for measuring geographical heights.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Temperature:** $T = 25^\circ C = 298$ K
- **Standard pressure:** $P = 1$ atm $= 101.325$ kPa
- **Standard concentration:** $C = 1$ M (for ions), $a = 1$ (activity)
- **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96485$ C mol$^{-1}$ $\approx 96500$ C mol$^{-1}$
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314$ J mol$^{-1}$ K$^{-1}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 2, students are expected to know (i) the construction of ECS, (ii) prediction of anode/cathode in a cell, (iii) calculation of standard EMF, and (iv) predicting spontaneity of redox reactions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How ECS is Constructed

The electrochemical series is built following a strict, step-by-step scientific protocol:

1. **Select a Reference Electrode:** The Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) is chosen as the universal benchmark and assigned $E^0_{H^+/H_2} = 0.000$ V.
2. **Build a Half-Cell:** For every metal M, construct the half-cell: $\text{M}^{n+} \vert \text{M}$ (e.g., $\text{Cu}^{2+} \vert \text{Cu}$).
3. **Couple with SHE:** Connect this half-cell with the SHE through a salt bridge and a voltmeter.
4. **Measure the Potential Difference:** Read the open-circuit voltage (OCV), which is the standard EMF.
5. **Assign Sign Convention:** If the metal electrode is the **cathode** (reduction occurs at it), $E^0$ is **positive**. If it is the **anode** (oxidation occurs at it), $E^0$ is **negative**.
6. **Tabulate in Ascending Order:** All values are tabulated from the most negative (e.g., Li, $-3.04$ V) to the most positive (e.g., Au, $+1.50$ V).

## 2.2 The "WHY" Behind the Order

- The position of an element in the ECS is governed by factors such as **ionization enthalpy**, **hydration/solvation energy**, **sublimation energy**, and **electrode kinetics**.
- An element with a **low ionization energy** and **high hydration energy** tends to occupy a position high (negative $E^0$) on the ECS, meaning it readily loses electrons.
- An element with a **high ionization energy** and **low hydration energy** sits at the top (positive $E^0$), resisting oxidation.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** "**L**azy **C**ats **S**it **A**lone **Z**ipping **N**ew **F**eathers" — corresponds to the first letters of highly electropositive elements (Li, Ca, Sr, Al, Zn, Ni, Fe).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E^0_{cell} = E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode}$ | Standard EMF of galvanic cell | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0_{cell}$ | Spontaneity check (negative $\Delta G^0$ = spontaneous) | J mol$^{-1}$ |
| 3 | $E^0_{cell} = \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log K_c$ at 25 °C | Equilibrium constant of cell reaction | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log Q$ at 25 °C | Nernst equation (non-standard conditions) | Volts (V) |
| 5 | $\Delta G^0 = -RT \ln K_c$ | Thermodynamic linkage to equilibrium | J mol$^{-1}$ |
| 6 | $E^0_{ox} = -E^0_{red}$ | Oxidation potential is negative of reduction potential | Volts (V) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all standard EMF calculations, **always use reduction potentials**. A common student error is to mix reduction and oxidation potentials. Both must be in the same convention (reduction).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Corrosion Engineering:** ECS predicts which metal acts as anode (corrodes) and which as cathode in a bimetallic couple. This governs **cathodic protection** design (using Mg, Zn, or Al as sacrificial anodes to protect steel pipelines).
- **Battery Design:** ECS helps select electrode pairs that maximize $E^0_{cell}$, e.g., Li ($E^0 = -3.04$ V) with $F_2$ ($E^0 = +2.87$ V) gives theoretical 5.91 V — the basis of lithium primary cells.
- **Electroplating:** The metal to be plated (cathode) must be **below** the substrate metal in the ECS so that it can be reduced and deposited.
- **Electrometallurgy:** ECS guides hydrometallurgical extraction of metals like Cu, Zn, and Ni from their ores.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Nernst Equation to Standard EMF Relation

The Nernst equation for a general cell reaction is:
$$E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

At standard conditions, the reaction quotient $Q = K_c$ at equilibrium, and $E_{cell} = 0$. Substituting:
$$0 = E^0_{cell} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln K_c$$

Rearranging:
$$E^0_{cell} = \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln K_c$$

Converting natural log to base-10 log using $\ln x = 2.303 \log x$:
$$E^0_{cell} = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{nF}\log K_c$$

Substituting $T = 298$ K, $R = 8.314$ J mol$^{-1}$ K$^{-1}$, $F = 96500$ C mol$^{-1}$:
$$E^0_{cell} = \dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{n \times 96500}\log K_c$$

Computing the constant:
$$E^0_{cell} = \dfrac{5705.8}{96500 \, n}\log K_c = \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log K_c$$

Final compact form at 25 °C:
$$E^0_{cell} = \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log K_c \quad (\text{at } 25^\circ C)$$

## 3.2 Linkage to Gibbs Free Energy

The maximum useful electrical work obtainable from a galvanic cell is:
$$W_{max} = nFE_{cell}$$

At standard conditions, this equals the negative of standard Gibbs free energy:
$$\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0_{cell}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** If $E^0_{cell} > 0$, then $\Delta G^0 < 0$ and the reaction is **spontaneous** (galvanic cell). If $E^0_{cell} < 0$, the reaction is **non-spontaneous** and requires external power (electrolytic cell).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Prediction of Cell Behavior Using ECS

The table below summarizes how ECS is used as a predictive tool in KTU-style problems.

| Metal A (Top / Cathode) | Metal B (Bottom / Anode) | Reaction Feasibility | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cu ($E^0 = +0.34$ V) | Zn ($E^0 = -0.76$ V) | Yes, spontaneous | $E^0_{cell} = +1.10$ V > 0 |
| Ag ($E^0 = +0.80$ V) | Fe ($E^0 = -0.44$ V) | Yes, spontaneous | $E^0_{cell} = +1.24$ V > 0 |
| Pb ($E^0 = -0.13$ V) | Zn ($E^0 = -0.76$ V) | Yes, spontaneous | $E^0_{cell} = +0.63$ V > 0 |
| Fe ($E^0 = -0.44$ V) | Cu ($E^0 = +0.34$ V) | No, non-spontaneous | $E^0_{cell} = -0.78$ V < 0 |
| Zn ($E^0 = -0.76$ V) | Mg ($E^0 = -2.37$ V) | Yes, spontaneous | $E^0_{cell} = +1.61$ V > 0 |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Calculate the standard EMF of a Daniel cell constructed from Cu and Zn electrodes. Given: $E^0_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.34$ V; $E^0_{Zn^{2+}/Zn} = -0.76$ V.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the cathode (higher $E^0$) and anode (lower $E^0$).

- $E^0_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.34$ V → **Cathode** (Cu is reduced)
- $E^0_{Zn^{2+}/Zn} = -0.76$ V → **Anode** (Zn is oxidized)

**Step 2:** Apply the cell EMF formula.
$$E^0_{cell} = E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode}$$
$$E^0_{cell} = (+0.34) - (-0.76)$$

**Step 3:** Compute the result.
$$E^0_{cell} = +0.34 + 0.76 = +1.10 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4:** Interpret the result.

Since $E^0_{cell} = +1.10$ V > 0, the cell reaction is **spontaneous**, and $\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0 = -2 \times 96500 \times 1.10 = -212300$ J mol$^{-1}$ = $-212.3$ kJ mol$^{-1}$, confirming spontaneity.

**Step 5:** Write cell notation.
$$\text{Zn} \vert \text{Zn}^{2+} (1 \text{ M}) \Vert \text{Cu}^{2+} (1 \text{ M}) \vert \text{Cu}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of ECS Construction

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the complete process of constructing the Electrochemical Series, from reference electrode selection to final tabulation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Begin ECS Construction] --> B[Select Reference: SHE E0 = 0.000 V]
    B --> C[Build Half-Cell for Metal M]
    C --> D[Couple Half-Cell with SHE]
    D --> E[Measure OCV with Voltmeter]
    E --> F{Metal Acts as Cathode or Anode?}
    F -- Cathode --> G[Assign Positive E0]
    F -- Anode --> H[Assign Negative E0]
    G --> I[Record in Tabular Form]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Sort in Ascending Order of E0]
    J --> K[Final Electrochemical Series Ready]
    K --> L[Apply ECS to Predict Spontaneity]
    L --> M[Apply ECS to Calculate E0cell]
    L --> N[Apply ECS to Design Batteries and Anti-Corrosion Systems]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Application Hierarchy of ECS in Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Electrochemical Series] --> Q[Cell Design]
    P --> R[Corrosion Prediction]
    P --> S[Electroplating Selection]
    P --> T[Battery Manufacture]
    P --> U[Electrometallurgy]

    Q --> Q1[Identify Cathode: High E0]
    Q --> Q2[Identify Anode: Low E0]
    Q --> Q3[Calculate E0cell = Ecathode - Eanode]

    R --> R1[Sacrificial Anode Selection: Mg, Zn, Al]
    R --> R2[Cathodic Protection of Steel Pipelines]

    S --> S1[Substrate above Plating Metal in ECS]
    S --> S2[Example: Ag plating on Cu substrate]

    T --> T1[Choose Couples with Max E0cell Difference]
    T --> T2[Example: Li-F2 Theoretical 5.91 V]

    U --> U1[Hydrometallurgical Extraction of Cu, Zn, Ni]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Flow: Predicting Redox Reaction Spontaneity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Two Electrodes A and B with E0 values] --> Y{Compare E0 values}
    Y -- A greater than B --> Z[A is Cathode, B is Anode]
    Y -- B greater than A --> AA[B is Cathode, A is Anode]
    Z --> AB[Compute E0cell = E0A - E0B greater than 0]
    AA --> AC[Compute E0cell = E0B - E0A greater than 0]
    AB --> AD[Reaction Spontaneous: Galvanic Cell]
    AC --> AD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrammatic Interpretation:** This block-level flow abstracts the multi-step process of (i) reading two $E^0$ values from ECS, (ii) identifying cathode/anode, (iii) computing $E^0_{cell}$, and (iv) inferring spontaneity — all of which are routine steps in KTU 2024 ESE numericals.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]

**Define Electrochemical Series. What is its significance in corrosion engineering?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Electrochemical Series (ECS)** is the arrangement of metals and non-metals in the **increasing order of their standard reduction potentials ($E^0$)**, measured against the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) at 25 °C and 1 M concentration.

**Significance in corrosion engineering:**

1. It predicts **which metal will corrode (act as anode)** and which will be **protected (act as cathode)** when two dissimilar metals are electrically coupled in an electrolyte.
2. The metal with a **more negative $E^0$** (lower in the series) acts as the **sacrificial anode** and corrodes preferentially, protecting the other metal.
3. This principle is exploited in **cathodic protection** of buried pipelines, ships' hulls, and offshore structures using **Mg, Zn, or Al anodes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** A complete 3-mark answer should include definition (1 mark) + significance with at least 2 valid points (2 marks). Vague answers mentioning only "predicts reactivity" lose marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 1]

**What is the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)? Why is it chosen as the universal reference?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** consists of a platinum wire coated with platinum black, immersed in a 1 M HCl (or $H^+$) solution at 25 °C, with **pure hydrogen gas bubbled at 1 atm pressure**. The half-cell reaction is:
$$2H^+_{(aq)} + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons H_{2(g)}, \quad E^0 = 0.000 \text{ V}$$

**Reasons for selection as universal reference:**

1. The hydrogen ion ($H^+$) is the **lightest and smallest** ion, ensuring fast, reversible electrode kinetics.
2. The Pt surface provides an **inert, reproducible platform** for the reaction.
3. The electrode can be **easily set up** in any laboratory with high precision.
4. By IUPAC convention, $E^0$ of SHE is **arbitrarily fixed at 0.000 V**, providing a zero-point reference.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] (Choice Option 1)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE). Why is its $E^0$ assigned a value of 0.000 V?

**(b) [7 Marks]** From the following standard reduction potentials, predict the spontaneity of the cell formed by Fe and Cu electrodes and calculate its standard EMF: $E^0_{Fe^{2+}/Fe} = -0.44$ V; $E^0_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.34$ V.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction of SHE:**

- **Container:** A glass tube containing 1 M HCl solution.
- **Electrode:** A platinum wire coated with finely divided **platinum black** (to increase surface area and catalyze the reaction).
- **Gas Supply:** Pure, dry **$H_2$ gas at 1 atm pressure** is bubbled over the platinum electrode.
- **Temperature:** Maintained at **25 °C (298 K)**.

**Working:**

- At the Pt surface, the following equilibrium is established:
$$2H^+_{(aq, \, 1M)} + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons H_{2(g, \, 1 \text{ atm})}$$
- When SHE acts as **cathode**: $H^+$ ions are reduced to $H_2$ gas.
- When SHE acts as **anode**: $H_2$ gas is oxidized to $H^+$ ions.

**Why $E^0 = 0.000$ V?**

By IUPAC convention, the SHE is assigned a reference potential of **0.000 V** so that all other standard reduction potentials can be measured **relative to it**. The convention is a **defining standard**, not a measured value — analogous to fixing the zero of the Celsius or Kelvin temperature scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Drawing the labeled diagram fetches 2 marks; writing the half-reaction fetches 1 mark; explaining the 0.000 V convention with reasoning fetches 2 marks; mentioning temperature and pressure fetches 2 marks.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Identify Cathode and Anode.**

Given:
- $E^0_{Fe^{2+}/Fe} = -0.44$ V
- $E^0_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.34$ V

Since Cu has a higher (more positive) $E^0$, **Cu is the cathode** (reduction). Fe has the lower (more negative) $E^0$, so **Fe is the anode** (oxidation).

**Step 2: Write Half-Reactions.**

- **Cathode (reduction):**
$$\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}, \quad E^0 = +0.34 \text{ V}$$
- **Anode (oxidation):**
$$\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-, \quad E^0_{ox} = +0.44 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Apply Cell EMF Formula.**

$$E^0_{cell} = E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode}$$
$$E^0_{cell} = (+0.34) - (-0.44)$$
$$E^0_{cell} = +0.34 + 0.44 = +0.78 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Interpret Spontaneity.**

Since $E^0_{cell} = +0.78$ V $> 0$, the cell reaction is **spontaneous** (galvanic cell). The Gibbs free energy change is:
$$\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0 = -2 \times 96500 \times 0.78 = -150540 \text{ J mol}^{-1} = -150.5 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

The negative $\Delta G^0$ confirms the spontaneity of the cell reaction.

**Step 5: Write Cell Notation.**
$$\text{Fe} \vert \text{Fe}^{2+} (1M) \Vert \text{Cu}^{2+} (1M) \vert \text{Cu}; \quad E^0_{cell} = +0.78 \text{ V}$$

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024] (Choice Option 2)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the term "Electrochemical Series." Using ECS, predict which metal will corrode preferentially when an iron pipe is connected to (i) a copper wire, and (ii) a magnesium block, in a moist soil electrolyte. Justify your answer with relevant $E^0$ values.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A galvanic cell is constructed from Zn and Ag electrodes. Given: $E^0_{Zn^{2+}/Zn} = -0.76$ V; $E^0_{Ag^+/Ag} = +0.80$ V. Calculate (i) the standard EMF, (ii) the standard Gibbs free energy change, and (iii) the equilibrium constant $K_c$ at 25 °C. State whether the cell reaction is spontaneous.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition:**

The **Electrochemical Series (ECS)** is the systematic arrangement of elements in **increasing order of their standard reduction potentials ($E^0$)** at 25 °C and 1 M concentration, measured with respect to SHE.

**Standard Reduction Potentials:**
- $E^0_{Fe^{2+}/Fe} = -0.44$ V
- $E^0_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.34$ V
- $E^0_{Mg^{2+}/Mg} = -2.37$ V

**Case (i): Fe connected to Cu.**

- $E^0_{Cu} > E^0_{Fe}$, so **Cu acts as cathode** (does not corrode) and **Fe acts as anode** (corrodes).
- **Fe corrodes preferentially** because it is more electrochemically active (lower in ECS).

**Case (ii): Fe connected to Mg.**

- $E^0_{Fe} > E^0_{Mg}$, so **Fe acts as cathode** (protected) and **Mg acts as anode** (corrodes).
- **Mg corrodes preferentially**, **protecting the Fe pipe**. This is the principle of **sacrificial anode cathodic protection**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Rule:** The metal **lower in ECS** (more negative $E^0$) always acts as the **anode** and **corrodes first**. The metal **higher in ECS** is protected.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $E^0_{Zn^{2+}/Zn} = -0.76$ V; $E^0_{Ag^+/Ag} = +0.80$ V; $n = 2$ (electrons transferred per cell reaction); $T = 298$ K; $F = 96500$ C mol$^{-1}$.

**Step 1: Identify Cathode and Anode.**

- $E^0_{Ag^+/Ag} = +0.80$ V (higher) → **Cathode**
- $E^0_{Zn^{2+}/Zn} = -0.76$ V (lower) → **Anode**

**Step 2: (i) Calculate Standard EMF.**

$$E^0_{cell} = E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode}$$
$$E^0_{cell} = (+0.80) - (-0.76) = +1.56 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: (ii) Calculate Standard Gibbs Free Energy.**

$$\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0_{cell}$$
$$\Delta G^0 = -2 \times 96500 \times 1.56$$
$$\Delta G^0 = -301080 \text{ J mol}^{-1} = -301.08 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 4: (iii) Calculate Equilibrium Constant $K_c$.**

Using $\Delta G^0 = -RT \ln K_c$:

$$\ln K_c = -\dfrac{\Delta G^0}{RT} = -\dfrac{-301080}{8.314 \times 298} = \dfrac{301080}{2477.57} = 121.5$$

$$K_c = e^{121.5} \approx 10^{52.75}$$

Alternatively, using $E^0_{cell} = \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log K_c$ at 25 °C:

$$\log K_c = \dfrac{nE^0_{cell}}{0.0591} = \dfrac{2 \times 1.56}{0.0591} = 52.79$$

$$K_c \approx 10^{52.79} \approx 6.2 \times 10^{52}$$

**Step 5: Spontaneity Statement.**

Since $E^0_{cell} = +1.56$ V > 0, $\Delta G^0 = -301.08$ kJ mol$^{-1}$ < 0, and $K_c$ is **enormously large**, the cell reaction is **highly spontaneous**, and the cell behaves as a **strong galvanic cell**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Sign Error in $E^0_{cell}$:** Many students compute $E^0_{cell} = E^0_{anode} - E^0_{cathode}$, which gives a negative value, leading to incorrect sign of $\Delta G^0$. **Always** use $E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode}$.
> - **Forgetting Electron Count $n$:** Using the wrong value of $n$ in $\Delta G^0$ and $K_c$ calculations is a frequent error. Verify $n$ by balancing the half-reactions.
> - **Mixing Reduction and Oxidation Potentials:** ECS tabulates **reduction** potentials. If you must use the **oxidation** potential of the anode, use $E^0_{ox} = -E^0_{red}$.
> - **Unit Errors:** $F = 96500$ C mol$^{-1}$, not 96500 J. Always multiply by $n$ (electrons) and $E$ (V) to get energy in joules.
> - **Forgetting the Constant 0.0591:** This is valid **only at 25 °C (298 K)**. For other temperatures, use the full $\dfrac{2.303RT}{nF}$ expression.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Electrochemical Series**

- [x] **Definition:** ECS = elements arranged in **increasing order of standard reduction potential ($E^0$)** with respect to SHE.
- [x] **Reference Electrode:** SHE assigned $E^0 = 0.000$ V at 25 °C, 1 M $H^+$, 1 atm $H_2$.
- [x] **Sign Convention:** Higher (more positive) $E^0$ → **Cathode**; Lower (more negative) $E^0$ → **Anode**.
- [x] **Cell EMF Formula:** $E^0_{cell} = E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode}$.
- [x] **Spontaneity Rule:** If $E^0_{cell} > 0$ → **Spontaneous (Galvanic)**; if $< 0$ → **Non-spontaneous (Electrolytic)**.
- [x] **Gibbs Free Energy Linkage:** $\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0_{cell}$ (negative $\Delta G^0$ = spontaneous).
- [x] **Equilibrium Constant Linkage:** $E^0_{cell} = \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log K_c$ at 25 °C.
- [x] **Corrosion Application:** The metal with more **negative $E^0$** corrodes first — basis of **sacrificial anode cathodic protection** (Mg, Zn, Al anodes for steel).
- [x] **Electroplating Rule:** Substrate metal must lie **above** the plating metal in the ECS.
- [x] **Memory Aid:** Li ($-3.04$ V) at the bottom; Au ($+1.50$ V) at the top. Mnemonic: **"Little Cats Sit And Zestily Nibble Fresh Pouncing Rodents"** (Li, Ca, Sr, Al, Zn, Ni, Fe, Pb, Cu, ... wait, use: **Li, K, Ba, Ca, Na, Mg, Al, Zn, Fe, Ni, Sn, Pb, H, Cu, Hg, Ag, Au** for the classic ECS order from bottom to top).
- [x] **Constants to Memorize:** $F = 96500$ C mol$^{-1}$; $\dfrac{2.303RT}{F} = 0.0591$ V at 298 K.
- [x] **Common Pitfall:** Always use **reduction potentials** consistently; never mix reduction and oxidation values in the same equation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion Theories: Derivation of Potential and Current Relations of Activity Controlled and Diffusion Controlled Corrosion Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT417 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Corrosion Theories: Derivation of Potential and Current Relations of Activity Controlled and Diffusion Controlled Corrosion Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Corrosion** is the thermodynamically spontaneous deterioration of a metallic material through electrochemical reactions with its environment, governed by coupled anodic metal dissolution and cathodic reduction half-reactions occurring simultaneously on the metal surface.

According to the **Mixed Potential Theory** (Wagner and Traud, 1938), the rate of an electrochemical corrosion process is determined by the intersection of the anodic and cathodic polarization curves on a **potential (E) vs. log current density (log i)** diagram (Evans Diagram). The corrosion rate and corrosion potential are uniquely fixed by this intersection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Limiting Regimes Govern Corrosion Kinetics:**
> 1. **Activity (Charge-Transfer) Controlled Corrosion:** The slowest step is the charge-transfer (electron transfer) reaction at the metal-electrolyte interface. Polarization is governed by **activation energy (Tafel) behavior**.
> 2. **Diffusion (Mass-Transport) Controlled Corrosion:** The slowest step is the transport of reactive species (e.g., O₂, H⁺, metal ions) through the solution to or from the electrode surface. Polarization is governed by **concentration polarization**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — The Faucet Analogy**
> Imagine corrosion as water flowing from a tank to a drain through a pipe.
> - In **activity control**, the bottleneck is the *faucet valve* itself (the charge-transfer reaction). Even if you open the drain wide, water flow is limited by how much the valve can pass.
> - In **diffusion control**, the faucet is wide open, but the *drain pipe is narrow* (diffusion boundary layer). Flow is now limited by how fast water can be transported through the pipe, no matter how wide the valve opens.
> The **corrosion current (i_corr)** is the actual rate of metal loss, set by whichever bottleneck is the *tightest* (slowest step).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday's Constant | **F** | **96485** | C mol⁻¹ |
| Universal Gas Constant | **R** | **8.314** | J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ |
| Temperature (Standard) | **T** | **298.15** (≈ 25 °C) | K |
| (2.303 RT/F) at 25 °C | — | **0.0592** | V |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework

**Step 1 — Electrochemical Nature of Corrosion**
- Corrosion is **not** a single chemical reaction. It comprises **two or more simultaneous half-reactions**:
  - Anodic: $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$ (metal oxidation)
  - Cathodic: $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$ (in acids) or $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$ (in neutral/alkaline, aerated)

**Step 2 — Mixed Potential Theory (Coupled Reactions)**
- The two half-cells must operate at the **same potential** and carry **equal current magnitude** (charge conservation: $i_a = i_c = i_{corr}$).
- This common potential is the **Corrosion Potential, $E_{corr}$**.
- The corresponding current density is the **Corrosion Current Density, $i_{corr}$**.

**Step 3 — Why the Rate is Controlled (The "Why" and "How")**
- The overall rate is limited by the **slowest elementary step** in the coupled mechanism.
- If the charge-transfer step has high activation energy → **Activation Control** (Tafel regime).
- If mass transport is sluggish (low concentration, stagnant boundary layer) → **Diffusion Control** (Limiting current plateau).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Cheat Sheet — Activity vs. Diffusion Controlled Corrosion**

| # | Equation | Name / Condition | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $i = i_o\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{(1-\beta)F\eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-\beta F\eta}{RT}\right)\right]$ | **Butler–Volmer Equation** (General) | $i_o$: exchange current density; $\beta$: symmetry factor; $\eta$: overpotential |
| 2 | $\eta_a = \beta_a \log\!\left(\dfrac{i_a}{i_{o,a}}\right)$ | **Anodic Tafel Equation** | $\beta_a$ = anodic Tafel slope (V/decade) |
| 3 | $\eta_c = -\beta_c \log\!\left(\dfrac{\vert i_c \vert}{i_{o,c}}\right)$ | **Cathodic Tafel Equation** | $\beta_c$ = cathodic Tafel slope (V/decade) |
| 4 | $E_{corr} = E_{eq,a} + \beta_a \log i_{o,a} - \beta_a \log i_{corr}$ | **Corrosion Potential (Anodic branch)** | $E_{eq,a}$ = equilibrium anodic potential |
| 5 | $E_{corr} = E_{eq,c} - \beta_c \log i_{o,c} + \beta_c \log i_{corr}$ | **Corrosion Potential (Cathodic branch)** | $E_{eq,c}$ = equilibrium cathodic potential |
| 6 | $i_{corr} = i_{o,c}\cdot 10^{\,(E_{corr}-E_{eq,c})/\beta_c}$ | **Corrosion Current from Cathodic Tafel** | — |
| 7 | $i_L = \dfrac{nFDC_b}{\delta}$ | **Limiting Current Density** (Diffusion Control) | $D$: diffusivity; $C_b$: bulk conc.; $\delta$: Nernst layer thickness |
| 8 | $i = nF\dfrac{D(C_b - C_s)}{\delta}$ | **Current Density (Mass-Transport + Reaction)** | $C_s$: surface conc. |
| 9 | $\eta_c = \dfrac{2.303RT}{\beta F}\log\!\left(\dfrac{i_L}{i_L - i}\right)$ | **Concentration Overpotential** | Activation + diffusion combined |
| 10 | $C_s = C_b \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{nF\eta_c}{RT}\right)$ | **Nernst Surface Concentration Relation** | For $O_2$ reduction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** When $i \rightarrow 0.99\,i_L$, the system is fully diffusion-controlled and a further increase in overpotential produces **no additional current** — this defines the **limiting current plateau**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cathodic Protection Design:** Tafel slopes obtained from laboratory polarization are used to size sacrificial anode current output.
- **Aeration vs. Agitation:** Oxygen-diffusion controlled corrosion (e.g., in stagnant seawater) explains why rotating or stirred metals corrode faster.
- **Inhibitor Selection:** Mixed inhibitors shift either the anodic or cathodic Tafel line; the regime (activation vs. diffusion) determines inhibitor effectiveness.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Activity (Charge-Transfer) Controlled Corrosion

#### 3.1.1 Start: Single Electrode Butler–Volmer Equation

For a single half-reaction, the net current density is the sum of the forward (anodic) and reverse (cathodic) partial current densities:

$$i = i_{anodic} - i_{cathodic}$$

Using the activated-complex theory (transition state) with symmetry factor $\beta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{anodic} &= i_o \exp\!\left(\dfrac{(1-\beta)F\eta}{RT}\right) \\[4pt]
i_{cathodic} &= i_o \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-\beta F\eta}{RT}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting gives the **Butler–Volmer Equation** for a single electrode:

$$i = i_o \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{(1-\beta)F\eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-\beta F\eta}{RT}\right) \right] \qquad \text{(Eq. A1)}$$

#### 3.1.2 High-Field Approximation ($\vert \eta \vert \gg RT/F$)

For large overpotentials (the Tafel regime), one exponential dominates:

- **Anodic branch** ($\eta > 0$, large): cathodic exponential $\rightarrow 0$

$$i_a \approx i_o \exp\!\left(\dfrac{(1-\beta)F\eta_a}{RT}\right) \qquad \text{(Eq. A2)}$$

- **Cathodic branch** ($\eta < 0$, large): anodic exponential $\rightarrow 0$

$$i_c \approx -i_o \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-\beta F\eta_c}{RT}\right) \qquad \text{(Eq. A3)}$$

#### 3.1.3 Convert Exponentials to Logarithms (Base 10)

Take $\log_{10}$ of (Eq. A2):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log i_a &= \log i_o + \dfrac{(1-\beta)F\eta_a}{2.303\,RT} \\[4pt]
\eta_a &= \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{(1-\beta)F}\log\!\left(\dfrac{i_a}{i_o}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Define the **Anodic Tafel Slope** $\beta_a = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{(1-\beta)F}$. Thus:

$$\boxed{\;\eta_a = \beta_a \log\!\left(\dfrac{i_a}{i_{o,a}}\right)\;} \qquad \text{(Eq. A4 — Anodic Tafel)}$$

Similarly for cathodic:

$$\boxed{\;\eta_c = -\beta_c \log\!\left(\dfrac{\vert i_c \vert}{i_{o,c}}\right)\;} \qquad \text{(Eq. A5 — Cathodic Tafel)}$$

#### 3.1.4 Introduce Two Coupled Half-Reactions (Mixed Potential)

The metal dissolution (anodic, Reaction 1) and hydrogen/oxygen reduction (cathodic, Reaction 2) operate on **the same electrode** at potential $E$:

- Anodic overpotential: $\eta_a = E - E_{eq,a}$
- Cathodic overpotential: $\eta_c = E - E_{eq,c}$

Substitute into Tafel equations (A4) and (A5):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E - E_{eq,a} &= \beta_a \log\!\left(\dfrac{i_a}{i_{o,a}}\right) \quad &\Rightarrow\quad E = E_{eq,a} + \beta_a \log i_a - \beta_a \log i_{o,a} \qquad &(1)\\[4pt]
E - E_{eq,c} &= -\beta_c \log\!\left(\dfrac{\vert i_c \vert}{i_{o,c}}\right) \quad &\Rightarrow\quad E = E_{eq,c} - \beta_c \log \vert i_c \vert + \beta_c \log i_{o,c} \qquad &(2)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.1.5 Apply Charge Conservation

At the corrosion potential $E_{corr}$, the anodic current equals the cathodic current in magnitude:

$$i_a = \vert i_c \vert = i_{corr}$$

Setting (1) = (2) and substituting $i_a = \vert i_c \vert = i_{corr}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{eq,a} + \beta_a \log i_{corr} - \beta_a \log i_{o,a} &= E_{eq,c} - \beta_c \log i_{corr} + \beta_c \log i_{o,c}
\end{aligned}
$$

Group $\log i_{corr}$ terms on the left:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\beta_a + \beta_c)\log i_{corr} &= (E_{eq,c} - E_{eq,a}) + \beta_c \log i_{o,c} - \beta_a \log i_{o,a}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $i_{corr}$:

$$\boxed{\;i_{corr} = i_{o,c}^{\beta_a/(\beta_a+\beta_c)} \cdot i_{o,a}^{\beta_c/(\beta_a+\beta_c)} \cdot 10^{\,(E_{eq,c}-E_{eq,a})/(\beta_a+\beta_c)}\;} \qquad \text{(Eq. A6)}$$

For $E_{corr}$, substitute $i_{corr}$ back into (1):

$$\boxed{\;E_{corr} = E_{eq,a} + \beta_a \log\!\left(\dfrac{i_{corr}}{i_{o,a}}\right)\;} \qquad \text{(Eq. A7)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The corrosion current grows exponentially with the thermodynamic driving force $(E_{eq,c} - E_{eq,a})$ and is strongly influenced by the larger Tafel slope (the more polarized reaction is rate-limiting).

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Diffusion (Mass-Transport) Controlled Corrosion

#### 3.2.1 Start: Fick's First Law of Diffusion

Steady-state diffusion flux $J$ of a species through a stagnant boundary layer (Nernst diffusion layer of thickness $\delta$):

$$J = -D\,\dfrac{dC}{dx}$$

For a linear concentration profile (a common assumption that yields the analytical result):

$$\dfrac{dC}{dx} \approx \dfrac{C_s - C_b}{\delta}$$

Therefore the flux directed *toward* the surface is:

$$J = \dfrac{D(C_b - C_s)}{\delta} \qquad \text{(Eq. D1)}$$

> where $C_b$ is the bulk concentration and $C_s$ is the surface concentration of the reacting species.

#### 3.2.2 Convert Flux to Current Density

Each mole of reactant consumes $n$ moles of electrons; hence the current density $i$ carried by this flux is:

$$i = nFJ \qquad \text{(Faraday's Law)}$$

Substituting (Eq. D1):

$$i = nF\,\dfrac{D(C_b - C_s)}{\delta} \qquad \text{(Eq. D2)}$$

#### 3.2.3 Limiting Current Density

As the cathodic overpotential $\eta_c$ becomes more negative, the surface reaction accelerates, depleting the reactant at the surface. In the limit $C_s \rightarrow 0$ (the surface cannot supply reactant faster than diffusion can replenish):

$$\boxed{\;i_L = \dfrac{nFDC_b}{\delta}\;} \qquad \text{(Eq. D3 — Limiting Current Density)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Beyond this point, **no further increase in cathodic overpotential can increase the current**. The Evans diagram shows a horizontal plateau.

#### 3.2.4 Surface Concentration from the Nernst Equation

For the cathodic half-reaction $Ox + ne^- \rightarrow Red$ at the surface, Nernst gives:

$$E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln C_s$$

Relative to the bulk equilibrium potential (where $C = C_b$):

$$E = E_{eq} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_s}{C_b}\right)$$

For a cathodic overpotential $\eta_c = E - E_{eq}$ (with $E < E_{eq}$, so $\eta_c < 0$):

$$\eta_c = \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_s}{C_b}\right)$$

Exponentiating:

$$\dfrac{C_s}{C_b} = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{nF\eta_c}{RT}\right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_s = C_b \exp\!\left(\dfrac{nF\eta_c}{RT}\right) \qquad \text{(Eq. D4)}$$

#### 3.2.5 Combine Activation + Diffusion — Mass-Transport Corrected Polarization

Assume the surface kinetics remain Butler–Volmer with $C_s$ as the local concentration (the exchange current density scales linearly with surface concentration: $i_{o,local} = i_o \cdot C_s/C_b$):

$$i = i_o \dfrac{C_s}{C_b}\exp\!\left(\dfrac{-\beta F\eta_c}{RT}\right)$$

Substitute (Eq. D4) for $C_s$:

$$i = i_o \exp\!\left(\dfrac{nF\eta_c}{RT}\right) \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-\beta F\eta_c}{RT}\right) = i_o \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-(n-\beta) F\eta_c}{RT}\right)$$

But $i_L$ from (Eq. D3) sets the absolute ceiling. Using (Eq. D2) and (Eq. D3) we can write:

$$i = i_L\!\left(1 - \dfrac{C_s}{C_b}\right)$$

Substituting (Eq. D4):

$$i = i_L\!\left[1 - \exp\!\left(\dfrac{nF\eta_c}{RT}\right)\right]$$

Solving for $\eta_c$:

$$\exp\!\left(\dfrac{nF\eta_c}{RT}\right) = 1 - \dfrac{i}{i_L}$$

$$\dfrac{nF\eta_c}{RT} = \ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{i}{i_L}\right)$$

Converting to base-10 logarithm ($\ln x = 2.303 \log x$):

$$\eta_c = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log\!\left(1 - \dfrac{i}{i_L}\right)$$

For activation + diffusion combined, with cathodic Tafel slope $\beta_c = 2.303 RT/(\beta n F)$, the **complete cathodic polarization equation** becomes:

$$\boxed{\;\eta_c = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\beta F}\log\!\left(\dfrac{i_L}{i_L - i}\right)\;} \qquad \text{(Eq. D5)}$$

#### 3.2.6 Two Limiting Cases of (Eq. D5)

**Case 1: Activation-Controlled** — When $i \ll i_L$, then $i_L - i \approx i_L$, and the log term vanishes:

$$\eta_c \approx \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\beta F}\log(i_L/i_L) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Tafel behavior recovered}$$

**Case 2: Diffusion-Controlled** — When $i \rightarrow i_L$, the term $i_L/(i_L - i) \rightarrow \infty$ and $\eta_c \rightarrow -\infty$:

$$\eta_c \rightarrow -\infty \quad \text{(the system requires infinite driving force, i.e., it has hit the ceiling)}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

| Parameter | Activation (Charge-Transfer) Controlled | Diffusion (Mass-Transport) Controlled |
|---|---|---|
| **Rate-Limiting Step** | Electron transfer at interface | Transport across boundary layer |
| **Polarization Form** | $E \propto \log i$ (Tafel slope) | $E \propto \log[i_L/(i_L - i)]$ (asymptotic) |
| **As $i \rightarrow i_L$** | $E$ continues linearly (in log i) | Plateau — $E$ goes to $-\infty$ |
| **Effect of Stirring** | None | $i_L$ increases (smaller $\delta$) |
| **Effect of Temperature** | Strong (exponential in $T$) | Moderate (affects $D$, viscosity) |
| **Effect of Concentration** | Negligible | Linear in $C_b$ |
| **Diagnostic Signature** | Straight Tafel line over decades | Plateau at $i_L$ |
