## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short-Answer Type)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between uniform corrosion and pitting corrosion. Give one engineering example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Uniform Corrosion | Pitting Corrosion |
|--------|-------------------|--------------------|
| **Attack pattern** | Even, over entire exposed surface | Localized, small deep pits |
| **Predictability** | Easily predicted by weight-loss tests | Highly unpredictable |
| **Inspection** | Easy to detect visually | Hard to detect until leak |
| **Rate** | Linear, constant mpy | Autocatalytic, accelerates |
| **Example** | Zinc roofing sheet atmospheric corrosion | Stainless steel (304) in chloride-rich seawater |

*Marking key:* (1 mark) definition, (1 mark) contrast, (1 mark) example.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC). State the three conditions necessary for SCC to occur (the "SCC triplet").**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** SCC is the cracking of a metal due to the **combined action of tensile stress and a specific corrosive environment**, occurring at stress levels well below the ultimate tensile strength.

**SCC Triplet (2 marks):**
1. **Susceptible material** (e.g., austenitic SS, brasses, carbon steel)
2. **Specific corrosive environment** (e.g., Cl⁻ for SS, NH₃ for brass, OH⁻ for steel)
3. **Tensile stress** (residual or applied, typically ≥ yield)

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[CHOICE-A] OR [CHOICE-B]** — Answer either A or B in full.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
> Classify the **various forms of corrosion** with brief description and a typical engineering example of each. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**
> A cylindrical steel specimen (Fe, M = 55.85 g/mol, n = 2, ρ = 7.87 g/cm³) of exposed area 100 cm² loses 1.0 g over a 240-hour exposure to seawater. Calculate:
> (i) The corrosion current density $i_{corr}$.
> (ii) The corrosion rate in mpy.
> (iii) The corrosion rate in mm/yr.
> (iv) Comment on the severity of this corrosion. **(7 marks)**

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

> **(i) Uniform/General Corrosion (1 mark)** — Even attack across surface; example: atmospheric rusting of structural steel.

> **(ii) Galvanic Corrosion (1 mark)** — Dissimilar metals in electrical contact in electrolyte; example: steel rivet in copper plate.

> **(iii) Pitting Corrosion (1 mark)** — Localized, deep, narrow pits on passive surface; example: 304 SS in seawater.

> **(iv) Crevice Corrosion (1 mark)** — Under gaskets/bolts; example: flange face of SS piping.

> **(v) Intergranular Corrosion (1 mark)** — Grain-boundary attack due to Cr-depletion; example: welded 304 SS in HNO₃ service.

> **(vi) Stress Corrosion Cracking (1 mark)** — Brittle cracking under tensile stress in specific environment; example: brass in NH₃ ("season cracking").

> **(vii) Erosion–Corrosion (1 mark)** — Mechanical removal of film + corrosion; example: pipe bends in slurry service.

*Marking key:* Each form = (0.5 mark description + 0.5 mark example).

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $M = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho = 7.87$ g/cm³
- $A = 100$ cm², $t = 240$ hr, $\Delta W = 1.0$ g = 1000 mg

**Step 1 — Corrosion current density:**

$$i_{corr} = \dfrac{\Delta W \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot A \cdot t}$$

Convert time to seconds: $t = 240 \times 3600 = 8.64 \times 10^5$ s

$$i_{corr} = \dfrac{(1.0)(2)(96485)}{(55.85)(100)(8.64 \times 10^5)}$$

$$i_{corr} = \dfrac{192970}{4.82544 \times 10^9} = 4.00 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A/cm}^2$$

$$i_{corr} = 40.0 \text{ μA/cm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Corrosion rate in mpy:**

Convert area: $A = 100 / (2.54)^2 = 15.5$ in²

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{534 \cdot \Delta W}{A \cdot \rho \cdot t} = \dfrac{534 \times 1000}{15.5 \times 7.87 \times 240}$$

$$= \dfrac{534000}{29272.4} = 18.24 \text{ mpy}$$

**Step 3 — Corrosion rate in mm/yr:**

$$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot \Delta W}{A \cdot \rho \cdot t} = \dfrac{87.6 \times 1000}{100 \times 7.87 \times 240}$$

$$= \dfrac{87600}{188880} = 0.464 \text{ mm/yr}$$

**Step 4 — Severity comment:** A corrosion rate of ~18 mpy (~0.46 mm/yr) is **moderately high** — typical of unprotected mild steel in seawater. Recommended action: **cathodic protection** (sacrificial zinc/aluminum anodes) or **protective epoxy coating** for long-term service.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
> What are the **major factors influencing corrosion** of metals? Explain any **four (4) factors** in detail with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**
> Discuss the various **methods of corrosion control** with special reference to **cathodic protection** (SACP and ICCP). **(7 marks)**

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

Corrosion is influenced by **three categories** of factors: metal, environment, and design.

**(1) Effect of pH (1.75 marks):** 
- In acidic media (pH < 4), hydrogen evolution is the dominant cathodic reaction; corrosion accelerates.
- In neutral/alkaline media, oxygen reduction dominates.
- Example: Iron in 1 M H₂SO₄ → ~high corrosion rate; iron in 1 M NaOH → low rate (passive Fe(OH)₃ film).

**(2) Effect of Dissolved Oxygen (1.75 marks):**
- DO is the **primary cathodic depolarizer** in neutral waters.
- Example: Hot water systems with high DO → severe pitting of carbon steel; deaeration reduces corrosion markedly.

**(3) Effect of Temperature (1.75 marks):**
- Roughly follows Arrhenius; corrosion rate approximately **doubles per 10 °C** rise in open systems.
- Above ~80 °C, O₂ solubility drops → corrosion may decrease.
- Example: Boiler tubes above 60 °C with O₂ → severe corrosion; deaerators used.

**(4) Effect of Aggressive Ions (1.75 marks):**
- **Cl⁻ ions** disrupt passive films on SS, causing pitting.
- **SO₄²⁻, NO₃⁻** accelerate corrosion of steel.
- Example: 304 SS in 3% NaCl solution → pitting; same alloy in freshwater → passive.

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

**Six Control Methods (overview, 3 marks):**
1. **Material Selection** — Use corrosion-resistant alloys (SS, Ti, Ni-alloys, FRP) for the service environment.
2. **Protective Coatings** — Organic (epoxy, polyurethane) or metallic (galvanizing, electroplating, anodizing).
3. **Cathodic Protection** — Drive metal to be protected as cathode.
4. **Anodic Protection** — Maintain in passive region (suitable for Fe in H₂SO₄, Ni in H₃PO₄).
5. **Corrosion Inhibitors** — Anodic (Na₂CrO₄), cathodic (ZnSO₄, polyphosphates), or mixed (amines).
6. **Design Improvements** — Eliminate crevices, dissimilar metal junctions, ensure drainage.

**Cathodic Protection (CP) — Detailed (4 marks):**

**Principle:** Force the metal to become the **cathode** of the electrochemical cell, suppressing the anodic dissolution.

**Method 1 — Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP) (2 marks):**
- A more electrochemically **active** metal (Zn, Mg, Al) is electrically connected to the structure to be protected.
- The anode **sacrifices** itself, releasing electrons that flow into the structure.
- Used for short-term or localized protection (e.g., small pipelines, ship hulls).
- **Application:** Steel ship hulls in seawater with Zn anodes; underground storage tanks with Mg anodes.

**Method 2 — Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) (2 marks):**
- An external **DC power source** (rectifier) drives current from an **inert anode** (graphite, Ti-MMO, scrap steel, Fe-Si) to the protected structure.
- Current density is controlled; suitable for **large** structures (long pipelines, offshore platforms).
- **Reference electrode** (Cu/CuSO₄) monitors the protection potential; criterion: $E \le -0.85$ V vs Cu/CuSO₄ for steel.

**Comparison table (for full marks):**

| Feature | SACP | ICCP |
|---------|------|------|
| Power source | None (galvanic) | External DC rectifier |
| Anode material | Zn, Mg, Al | Graphite, Ti, scrap metal |
| Current capacity | Low | High |
| Best for | Localized/small | Large pipelines/structures |
| Maintenance | Anode replacement | Rectifier check |

**Anodic Protection (3 marks additional):**
- For metals that passivate (Fe in H₂SO₄, Ni in H₃PO₄, SS in HNO₃).
- A **potentiostat** maintains the metal within the **passive region** of its anodic polarization curve.
- Protects acid storage tanks and chemical reactors.
- **Limitation:** Only works where passivation is feasible.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Pitting and Crevice corrosion** — Pitting is *randomly nucleated* on a passive surface; crevice corrosion is *geometry-induced*. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Wrong unit in Faraday's law** — Using $t$ in hours instead of seconds when applying $i_{corr} = \Delta W n F / (MAt)$ gives wrong numerical answers. Always check units!
> 3. **PREN formula missing N₂ coefficient** — Forgetting the 16·%N term leads to an incorrect answer for nitrogen-bearing super-austenitic steels.
> 4. **SACP vs ICCP mix-up** — SACP needs NO external power; ICCP does. Examiners penalize misclassification.
> 5. **Forgetting the SCC triplet** — All three (susceptible alloy + specific environment + tensile stress) MUST coexist.
> 6. **Inhibitor categorization** — Anodic inhibitors (e.g., chromate) can cause **pitting** if under-dosed; mention this nuance to score full marks.

---

### 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2**

**Definitions (must be memorized):**
- ☐ Corrosion = spontaneous deterioration of a metal via electrochemical reaction with environment.
- ☐ Form = **morphology/shape** of the corrosion attack on the surface.

**Eight Form Names (in order of KTU importance):**
- ☐ Uniform → Galvanic → Pitting → Crevice → Intergranular → SCC → Erosion → Dealloying.
- ☐ Two extra forms for KTU 2024: Fretting + Hydrogen Embrittlement.

**Key Examples (one-liners):**
- ☐ IGC: Sensitization of 304 SS at 400–850 °C → Cr₂₃C₆ → Cr-depleted zones.
- ☐ Pitting: 304/316 SS in Cl⁻ → Cl⁻ attacks passive film → autocatalytic acid pit.
- ☐ SCC Triplet: Sensitive alloy + specific environment + tensile stress.
- ☐ Season Cracking: Brass + NH₃ atmosphere + cold-work residual stress.
- ☐ Dezincification: Cu-Zn brass loses Zn preferentially.

**Formulas to memorize cold:**
- ☐ $\Delta E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$
- ☐ $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$
- ☐ $PREN = \%Cr + 3.3\%Mo + 16\%N$
- ☐ CR (mpy) = $534 \cdot W / (D A t)$
- ☐ CR (mm/yr) = $87.6 \cdot W / (D A t)$
- ☐ $i_{corr} = W n F / (M A t)$
- ☐ Nernst: $E = E^0 + (0.0591/n) \log[Ox]/[Red]$

**Six Control Methods (always write in this order):**
1. ☐ Material selection
2. ☐ Protective coatings (organic + metallic)
3. ☐ Cathodic protection (SACP / ICCP)
4. ☐ Anodic protection (potentiostatic)
5. ☐ Corrosion inhibitors (anodic / cathodic / mixed / VPI)
6. ☐ Design improvement + maintenance

**Constants to remember:**
- ☐ **F = 96,485 C/mol ≈ 96,500 C/mol**
- ☐ **2.303 RT/F = 0.0591 V at 25 °C**
- ☐ **534** (mpy) and **87.6** (mm/yr) conversion constants.

**Severity Reference (memorize this table):**

| Corrosion Rate (mpy) | Severity |
|----------------------|----------|
| < 1 | Excellent |
| 1 – 5 | Good |
| 5 – 20 | Fair (acceptable for many services) |
| 20 – 50 | Poor |
| > 50 | Severe — redesign required |

**Most-tested KTU 2024 Areas:**
- ☐ Differences between Pitting and Crevice
- ☐ Mechanism of Intergranular corrosion + prevention
- ☐ SCC Triplet with examples
- ☐ Cathodic protection (SACP vs ICCP)
- ☐ Numerical on Faraday's law / mpy

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrodeposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT417 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Electroposition # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electrodeposition** is an **electrochemical surface-modification process** in which **positively charged metal cations (M^n+)** dissolved in an aqueous (or non-aqueous) electrolyte are **reduced at the cathode surface** and deposited as a coherent, adherent metallic layer onto a conductive substrate. The driving force is supplied by an external DC power source (rectifier), and the process obeys the quantitative framework of **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**. In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the half-reaction governing deposition at the cathode is written as: $$ \text{M}^{n+}_{(aq)} + n e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{M}_{(s)} $$ A concurrent **anodic reaction** (dissolution of a soluble anode, or oxygen evolution at an insoluble anode) maintains electroneutrality and sustains ionic current through the electrolyte. > [!NOTE] > **Key Governing Constant:** **Faraday's Constant, F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹** (≈ 96,500 C mol⁻¹ for engineering calculations). This is the charge carried by **one mole of electrons**. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are a **mason building a brick wall** where the bricks (metal ions) are scattered in a pond (electrolyte bath). A **magnetic crane** (electric current) selectively lifts only the bricks of a specific color (a chosen metal) from the pond and places them, layer by layer, onto a **wooden platform** (the cathode/substrate) in the exact geometry of your choice. - The **crane's pull strength** = **Current Density (A/dm²)**. Too weak → patchy wall; too strong → uneven, burnt deposit. - The **water flow** that refills the pond with fresh bricks = **electrolyte agitation / mass transport**. - The **bricks already on the wall** that fall off because the glue was weak = **poor adhesion** caused by inadequate **surface pretreatment** (cleaning, pickling, activation). - The **factory manager** that determines how many bricks actually reach the wall vs. how many are lost = **Cathodic Current Efficiency (η %)**. This mental model makes the abstract current–potential–concentration relationships immediately tangible for first-time learners. --- ## 1.3 Essential Terminology Snapshot | Term | Engineering Meaning | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | **Cathode** | Workpiece to be plated; site of reduction | — | | **Anode** | Source of metal ions (soluble) or site of O₂/Cl₂ evolution (insoluble) | — | | **Electrolyte** | Aqueous solution of metal salts, conducting salts, additives | — | | **Current Density (i)** | Current per unit cathodic area | **A/dm²** | | **Cathodic Current Efficiency (η)** | Fraction of total charge used for metal deposition | **%** (often 60–98 %) | | **Throwing Power (TP)** | Bath's ability to give uniform thickness on complex shapes | **%** | | **Plating Time (t)** | Duration of deposition | **minutes** | | **Coating Thickness (δ)** | Average vertical thickness of deposit | **μm** | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** Always specify units for current density in **A/dm²** (not A/m²) — this is the universal convention in plating industry and KTU textbooks. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework ### Step A — The Electrochemical Cell Assembly A plating line consists of: 1. **DC Rectifier** (constant current or constant voltage mode) 2. **Anode** (positive terminal) — soluble (Cu, Zn, Ni, Ag) or insoluble (Pb, Pt, graphite, Ti-coated with Pt/IrO₂) 3. **Cathode** (negative terminal) — the substrate to be coated (steel, brass, plastics with electroless Cu seed layer) 4. **Electrolyte Bath** — contains metal ions, supporting electrolyte (H₂SO₄, NaCl, etc.), and organic additives (brighteners, levelers, wetting agents) 5. **Auxiliary systems** — heating coil, filtration pump, agitation (air or mechanical), pH/temperature controllers ### Step B — Transport of Ions to the Cathode Three mechanisms deliver M^n+ to the cathode surface: - **Migration** — motion under the electric field (dominant for ions of the depositing metal) - **Diffusion** — motion down a concentration gradient (dominant at limiting current) - **Convection** — forced or natural bulk motion (driven by agitation) The **Nernst diffusion layer (δ_N)** of thickness ~0.1–0.5 mm is where concentration drops from bulk value C_bulk to surface value C_surface. ### Step C — Charge Transfer (Electrocrystallization) Once M^n+ reaches the cathode, electrons tunnel across the double layer and reduce it: - **Ad-atom formation** → surface diffusion → incorporation into a crystal lattice at an **active site** (kink, edge, vacancy) - If reduction is **slow (charge-transfer controlled)** → activation polarization - If ion supply is **slow (mass-transport controlled)** → concentration polarization ### Step D — Faraday's Two Laws (Quantitative Backbone) **First Law (1833):** *The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed.* **Second Law (1834):** *The masses of different substances liberated by the same quantity of electricity are proportional to their chemical equivalent weights (E = M/n).* ### Step E — Performance Descriptors - **Current Efficiency (η)** — accounts for side reactions (H₂ evolution is the chief competitor at the cathode) - **Throwing Power (TP)** — ability to coat recessed/low-current-density regions uniformly - **Limiting Current Density (i_L)** — maximum usable CD before burning / dendrite formation - **Deposit Quality Descriptors** — brightness, hardness, internal stress, ductility, porosity, adhesion --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **canonical KTU cheat sheet** for Module 3 numerical problems. Memorize units precisely. | # | Quantity | Formula | Definition of Symbols | Typical Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Faraday's 1st Law (mass deposited) | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | I = current, t = time, M = molar mass, n = valency, F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹ | **grams** | | 2 | Theoretical mass (alternative) | $m_{th} = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{Q}{96{,}485} \cdot E$ | Q = I·t, E = M/n (equivalent weight) | grams | | 3 | Cathodic Current Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | η expressed as percentage | **%** | | 4 | Coating Thickness (uniform current) | $\delta = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M \cdot \eta}{n \cdot F \cdot A \cdot \rho}$ | A = cathode area, ρ = density of deposit | **cm or μm** | | 5 | Coating Thickness (using current density) | $\delta = \dfrac{i \cdot t \cdot M \cdot \eta}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | i = I/A in A cm⁻² (note: convert from A/dm²) | cm | | 6 | Faraday's 2nd Law (two-substance) | $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$ | E = equivalent weight = M/n | ratio | | 7 | Current Density Conversion | $i \,[\text{A/cm}^2] = \dfrac{i \,[\text{A/dm}^2]}{100}$ | 1 dm² = 100 cm² | A/cm² | | 8 | Charge passed | $Q = I \cdot t = n \cdot F \cdot N$ | N = moles of electrons | **Coulombs (C)** | | 9 | Equivalent Weight | $E = \dfrac{M}{n}$ | M = atomic/molar mass, n = electrons transferred | g/eq | | 10 | Throwing Power (Field's formula) | $TP = \dfrac{K - M}{K + M - 2} \times 100$ | K = ratio of primary to secondary CD, M = ratio of actual metal distribution | **%** | | 11 | Wagner's Number | $W = \dfrac{\kappa}{\left(\dfrac{d\eta_c}{di}\right)} \cdot \dfrac{\partial \ln i}{\partial \ln C}$ | κ = electrolyte conductivity, dη_c/di = slope of polarization curve | dimensionless | | 12 | Limiting Current Density | $i_L = \dfrac{n \cdot F \cdot D \cdot C_b}{\delta_N}$ | D = diffusion coeff., C_b = bulk conc., δ_N = Nernst layer thickness | A/cm² | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive industry** — decorative + corrosion-resistant Cu/Ni/Cr multilayers on bumpers, grills (≈ 20–35 μm Cr over 15–25 μm Ni) - **Electronics / PCBs** — Cu plating of through-holes (acid sulfate bath, η ≈ 95–100 %); Au/Ni on connectors for solderability and wear resistance - **Aerospace** — Cd plating of high-strength steel fasteners (against galvanic corrosion), replaced by Sn–Zn or Zn–Ni for environmental reasons - **Renewable Energy** — Cu, Ni, Ag, Sn plating for silicon solar cell metallization; Zn plating for galvanic protection of wind-turbine towers - **Precision Engineering** — electroforming (a near-100 % η variant) to manufacture Ni foils, moulds for CDs/DVDs, and large astronomical mirrors # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Faraday's First Law from First Principles **Goal:** Derive the linear relationship $m \propto Q$ for an electrochemical deposition reaction. **Starting point — definition of electric current:** Current is the rate of flow of charge: $$ I = \frac{dQ}{dt} $$ **For a constant current I applied for time t, total charge passed is:** $$ Q = I \cdot t \quad \text{(Coulombs)} $$ **Charge required to reduce ONE mole of a metal of valency n:** Each ion M^n+ requires n electrons. One mole of electrons carries a charge equal to Faraday's constant **F = N_A · e**, where N_A = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹ and e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C. Thus: $$ \text{Charge per mole of M} = n \cdot F \quad \text{(Coulombs)} $$ **Number of moles of metal reduced when charge Q is passed:** $$ N_{moles} = \frac{Q}{n \cdot F} $$ **Mass of metal deposited = moles × molar mass:** $$ m = N_{moles} \times M = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} $$ **Substituting Q = I · t:** $$ \boxed{\,m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{96{,}485} \times \frac{M}{n}\,} $$ This is **Faraday's First Law** in its complete KTU-ready form. The proportionality constant $\dfrac{M}{nF}$ is the **electrochemical equivalent (z)** of the metal, with units of **g C⁻¹**. **Numerical example of z for copper (Cu²+):** $$ z_{Cu} = \frac{63.55 \, \text{g/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \, \text{C/mol}} = 3.294 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{g/C} = 0.3294 \, \text{mg/C} $$ --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Coating Thickness Formula **Starting point:** We know the mass deposited is $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M \cdot \eta}{n \cdot F}$ (introducing η since not all charge produces metal). **Volume of the deposit** (assumed uniform and fully dense, no porosity): $$ V = \frac{m}{\rho} \quad \text{(cm}^3 \text{, with } \rho \text{ in g/cm}^3\text{)} $$ **Average thickness δ** of a uniform layer covering area A is: $$ \delta = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot A} $$ **Substituting the expression for m:** $$ \delta = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M \cdot \eta}{n \cdot F \cdot A \cdot \rho} $$ **Introducing current density $i = I/A$:** $$ \boxed{\,\delta = \frac{i \cdot t \cdot M \cdot \eta}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}\,} $$ This is the **workhorse formula** for predicting deposition time required to achieve a target thickness, or vice versa. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Typical Metal Plating Parameters | Metal | Bath Type | Valency n | M (g/mol) | ρ (g/cm³) | η (typical, %) | z (mg/C) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Copper (Cu)** | Acid sulfate / Cyanide / Pyrophosphate | 2 | 63.55 | 8.96 | 95–100 | 0.3294 | | **Nickel (Ni)** | Watts / Sulphamate | 2 | 58.69 | 8.90 | 92–98 | 0.3041 | | **Chromium (Cr)** | Chromic acid (CrO₃ + H₂SO₄) | 6 | 52.00 | 7.20 | 12–18 (low!) | 0.0898 | | **Zinc (Zn)** | Acid chloride / Alkaline cyanide-free | 2 | 65.38 | 7.14 | 90–95 | 0.3388 | | **Silver (Ag)** | Cyanide / Thiosulfate | 1 | 107.87 | 10.49 | 98–100 | 1.1180 | | **Gold (Au)** | Cyanide (acid/neutral) | 1 / 3 | 196.97 | 19.32 | 60–90 | varies | | **Tin (Sn)** | Acid sulphate / Alkaline stannate | 2 / 4 | 118.71 | 7.30 | 90–95 | 0.6150 | > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** Chromium has exceptionally low current efficiency (~12–18 %) because most charge is wasted in **hydrogen evolution** and **Cr(VI) → Cr(III) reduction**. This is why decorative Cr plating requires thick underlayers of Ni (~20 μm) — to provide corrosion protection independently. --- ## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** A steel component of total surface area **5 dm²** is to be copper-plated from an acid sulfate bath. The desired average thickness is **25 μm**. If the current density used is **2.5 A/dm²** and the cathodic current efficiency is **96 %**, calculate the **plating time required**. **Given data:** - A = 5 dm² = 500 cm² - δ = 25 μm = 25 × 10⁻⁴ cm = 2.5 × 10⁻³ cm - i = 2.5 A/dm² = 0.025 A/cm² - η = 96 % = 0.96 - For Cu²+: n = 2, M = 63.55 g/mol, ρ = 8.96 g/cm³ - F = 96,485 C/mol **Starting formula:** $$ \delta = \frac{i \cdot t \cdot M \cdot \eta}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} $$ **Solving for t:** $$ t = \frac{\delta \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho}{i \cdot M \cdot \eta} $$ **Substituting values:** $$ t = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 2 \times 96{,}485 \times 8.96}{0.025 \times 63.55 \times 0.96} $$ **Numerator evaluation step-by-step:** $$ 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 = 5.0 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 96{,}485 = 482.425 $$ $$ 482.425 \times 8.96 = 4322.93 $$ **Denominator evaluation step-by-step:** $$ 0.025 \times 63.55 = 1.58875 $$ $$ 1.58875 \times 0.96 = 1.52520 $$ **Final division:** $$ t = \frac{4322.93}{1.52520} \approx 2834.3 \, \text{seconds} $$ **Converting to minutes:** $$ \boxed{\,t \approx 47.24 \, \text{minutes}\,} $$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Electrodeposition Cell — Mermaid Block Architecture The diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of an industrial electroplating line, including the interlocked subsystems a KTU examiner expects in descriptive answers. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[DC RECTIFIER
3 Phase Variable Output 0 to 12 V] --> B[AMMETER
Monitors Total Current] B --> C[VOLTMETER
Across Cell Terminals] C --> D[BUSBAR ASSEMBLY
Copper Conductor] D --> E[ANODE
Soluble or Insoluble] D --> F[CATHODE
Workpiece on Rack] E --> G[ELECTROLYTE BATH
Acid or Alkaline Solution] F --> G G --> H[HEAT EXCHANGER
Maintains 25 to 60 C] G --> I[FILTRATION UNIT
0.5 to 5 micron cartridge] G --> J[AGITATION SYSTEM
Air or Cathode Rod] G --> K[CONTROL PANEL
pH, T, i, level sensors] K --> A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The rectifier drives current through the busbar → anode → electrolyte → cathode → back to rectifier. The auxiliary systems (heat, filtration, agitation, control) maintain the bath in the precise operating window required for defect-free deposits. --- ## 4.2 Process Flow of a Complete Plating Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[INCOMING
SUBSTRATE] --> P2[ALKALINE
DEGREASING] P2 --> P3[WATER
RINSE] P3 --> P4[ACID
PICKLING] P4 --> P5[SECOND WATER
RINSE] P5 --> P6[ACTIVATION
STRIKE DIP] P6 --> P7[PLATING TANK
Main Electrodeposition] P7 --> P8[RECOVERY
RINSE] P8 --> P9[DRYING
and INSPECTION] P9 --> P10[FINISHED
COMPONENT] ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Approximately **60–70 % of all plating defects** (peeling, blistering, poor adhesion, porosity) originate from **inadequate pre-treatment**, NOT from the plating tank itself. Examiners frequently award marks for clearly identifying this in process-flow answers. --- ## 4.3 Polarization Curve — Conceptual Map (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR O[START] --> R1[LOW CD REGION
Activation Polarization
Tafel Slope dominant] R1 --> R2[TRANSITION
Mixed Control] R2 --> R3[LIMITING CD iL
Concentration Polarization
Mass Transport Bottleneck] R3 --> R4[BEYOND iL
Burning Dendrites pH Rise] R4 --> END[DEFECTIVE DEPOSIT] ``` | Region on Curve | Controlling Step | Deposit Appearance | |---|---|---| | Activation (low CD) | Charge transfer kinetics | Smooth, fine-grained | | Mixed | Both charge + mass transport | Bright, leveled | | Limiting plateau (i_L) | Diffusion of M^n+ | Powdery, rough, burnt edges | | Beyond i_L | Side reactions dominate | Dendrites, H₂ gas pitting | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Convention:** Part A questions test direct recall of definitions, formulae, or one-line explanations. Two-mark derivations, one-mark final answer. ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model] **State Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis. Define its proportionality constant.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Statement (1 M):** *The mass of a substance liberated or dissolved at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte.* - **Mathematical form (1 M):** $m \propto Q$, i.e., $m = z \cdot Q$, where $z$ is the **electrochemical equivalent** of the substance. - **Definition of z (1 M):** $z = \dfrac{M}{n \cdot F}$ (g/C) — it is the mass of substance liberated by 1 Coulomb of charge. For Cu²+, $z = 0.3294$ mg/C. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **What is cathodic current efficiency in electrodeposition? Why is it always less than 100 %?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1 M):** Cathodic current efficiency η is the ratio (in %) of actual mass deposited to the theoretical mass predicted by Faraday's law: $\eta = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$. - **Reason for η < 100 % (2 M):** A fraction of the total charge is consumed in **side reactions**, chiefly **hydrogen evolution** at the cathode ($2H^+ + 2e^- \to H_2 \uparrow$). For metals with very negative reduction potentials (Cr, Zn in some baths, Fe), or in low-pH baths, this parasitic reaction is substantial. Cr plating has η ≈ 12–18 %, whereas Cu, Ag, and Ni plating typically have η ≈ 95–100 %. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU End Semester Examination Part B Module-Internal Choice: Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Provide full derivations and units. --- ### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model]** **(a)** State Faraday's two laws of electrolysis. Derive the expression for the mass of metal deposited at the cathode in terms of current, time, atomic mass, valency and Faraday's constant. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A brass article of surface area **300 cm²** is to be silver-plated from a cyanide bath. Calculate the **time required** to deposit a uniform silver layer of **20 μm** thickness. Given: density of Ag = 10.49 g/cm³, atomic mass = 107.87 g/mol, valency = 1, and current efficiency = 90 %. Use a total current of **2.0 A**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### SOLUTION TO Q-A(a): **Faraday's First Law (3 Marks):** *The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of charge passed.* **Faraday's Second Law (2 Marks):** *When the same quantity of charge is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their equivalent weights (E = M/n).* **Derivation (2 Marks):** For charge Q passed through a solution of M^n+: - 1 mole of M requires charge = $n \cdot F$ - Moles deposited = $\dfrac{Q}{n \cdot F}$ - Mass = moles × molar mass = $\dfrac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ --- #### SOLUTION TO Q-A(b): **Given:** A = 300 cm², δ = 20 μm = 20 × 10⁻⁴ cm = 2 × 10⁻³ cm, ρ = 10.49 g/cm³, M = 107.87 g/mol, n = 1, η = 0.90, I = 2.0 A, F = 96,485 C/mol. **Formula:** $$ \delta = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M \cdot \eta}{n \cdot F \cdot A \cdot \rho} $$ **Solving for t:** $$ t = \frac{\delta \cdot n \cdot F \cdot A \cdot \rho}{I \cdot M \cdot \eta} $$ **Substituting:** $$ t = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1 \times 96{,}485 \times 300 \times 10.49}{2.0 \times 107.87 \times 0.90} $$ **Numerator step-by-step:** $$ 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 96{,}485 = 192.97 $$ $$ 192.97 \times 300 = 57{,}891 $$ $$ 57{,}891 \times 10.49 \approx 6{,}072{,}770 $$ **Denominator step-by-step:** $$ 2.0 \times 107.87 = 215.74 $$ $$ 215.74 \times 0.90 = 194.166 $$ **Final division:** $$ t = \frac{6{,}072{,}770}{194.166} \approx 31{,}272 \, \text{seconds} $$ **Converting to minutes:** $$ \boxed{\,t \approx 521.2 \, \text{minutes} \approx 8.69 \, \text{hours}\,} $$ --- ### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** **(a)** Define and explain the term **Throwing Power** in electrodeposition. Discuss its significance and how **Field's empirical formula** is used to evaluate it numerically. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the **Watts nickel plating bath** in detail. Explain the role of each component and the effect of current density on deposit quality. **(7 Marks)** --- #### SOLUTION TO Q-B(a): **Definition (2 Marks):** *Throwing Power is the ability of an electroplating bath to deposit metal of relatively uniform thickness on a cathode having an irregular shape, i.e., producing uniform coverage even on recessed (low-current-density) areas.* **Significance (2 Marks):** - For decorative articles with sharp corners and recesses, good TP ensures uniform appearance - For functional coatings (corrosion resistance), uniform thickness = predictable service life - High TP baths reduce over-plating on edges (saving metal) and under-plating in recesses (avoiding rust) **Field's Formula (3 Marks):** For a Hull Cell or Haring-Blum cell, with primary CD ratio K = i_near/i_far and actual metal ratio M = mass_near/mass_far: $$ \boxed{\,TP = \frac{K - M}{K + M - 2} \times 100 \, \%\,} $$ A bath with TP = 100 % is ideal (perfect uniformity); TP = 0 % means thickness is inversely proportional to local CD (very poor). For acid copper, TP ≈ −10 to +5 %; for alkaline cyanide copper, TP ≈ +40 to +65 %; for chloride zinc, TP ≈ +20 to +45 %. --- #### SOLUTION TO Q-B(b): **Watts Bath Composition (per litre) (3 Marks):** | Component | Function | Quantity | |---|---|---| | Nickel Sulphate (NiSO₄·6H₂O) | Source of Ni²+ ions | 240–300 g | | Nickel Chloride (NiCl₂·6H₂O) | Improves anode dissolution, increases conductivity | 30–60 g | | Boric Acid (H₃BO₃) | pH buffer (maintains pH 3.5–4.5) | 30–40 g | | Water (distilled) | Solvent | Balance | | Wetting agent (e.g., SDS) | Reduces pitting by H₂ bubbles | 0.1–0.5 g | | Brightener (organic, e.g., saccharin) | Grain refinement, stress relief | 0.5–5 g | **Role of each component (2 Marks):** - **NiSO₄** is the primary Ni²+ source; concentration controls maximum allowable CD - **NiCl₂** activates the soluble Ni anode by Cl⁻ attack; without it anode passivates - **H₃BO₃** buffers pH near cathode surface, preventing Ni(OH)₂ precipitation and pitting - **Additives** refine grain size, improve brightness, and reduce internal stress **Effect of Current Density (2 Marks):** - **Low CD (<2 A/dm²)** → matte, dull, low-stress deposits - **Moderate CD (3–5 A/dm²)** → bright, smooth, level deposits (optimum range) - **High CD (>8 A/dm²)** → burnt, rough, dendritic, with H₂ pitting at edges (i > i_L) --- ## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Failing to convert μm to cm, or A/dm² to A/cm². These cost 1–2 marks silently in every numerical. > 2. **Forgetting to apply current efficiency (η):** Many students compute theoretical mass and forget to multiply by η/100, or compute time without η. Always check whether η is given. > 3. **Confusing valency with oxidation state:** Ag is +1 (n=1); Cu²+ has n=2; Cr from CrO₃ has n=6. > 4. **In coating thickness derivations:** Students often forget to divide by area A or density ρ. Unit analysis catches this. > 5. **Throwing Power misuse:** Field's formula requires specific K and M ratios measured in a Haring-Blum cell, not just CD on the workpiece. Do not apply Field's formula blindly. > 6. **Faraday's constant:** Use **96,485 C/mol** (precise) or **96,500 C/mol** (approximate) — both are accepted, but be consistent. --- ## 5.5 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!TIP] > **Print this checklist for last-minute revision before any KTU Module 3 test on Electrodeposition.** - **Faraday's First Law:** $m = \dfrac{I t M}{n F}$ — memorize the four variables (I, t, M, n) and F = 96,485 C/mol. - **Faraday's Second Law:** $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$ — used in series-cell problems. - **Current Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$. Less than 100 % due to H₂ evolution. - **Coating Thickness:** $\delta = \dfrac{i t M \eta}{n F \rho}$ — always keep i in A/cm² when using M in g/mol and ρ in g/cm³ to get δ in cm. - **Throwing Power:** Field's formula; higher TP = more uniform deposits; cyanide baths > acid baths in TP. - **Limiting Current Density:** Beyond i_L, deposits burn/dendrites form; operating CD = 30–60 % of i_L is industry practice. - **Anode types:** Soluble (Cu, Ni, Zn, Ag) — supplies metal ions; Insoluble (Pb, Pt, graphite) — for Cr, Au, complex baths. - **Watts Nickel Bath:** NiSO₄ (main salt) + NiCl₂ (anode activator) + H₃BO₃ (pH buffer) + organic additives (brighteners). - **Chromium's low η:** ~12–18 % — most charge goes to H₂ and Cr(VI)→Cr(III) reduction; hence thick Ni undercoat is mandatory. - **Polarization types:** Activation (low CD), Concentration (high CD), Ohmic (electrolyte resistance). - **Pre-treatment sequence:** Degrease → Rinse → Pickle → Rinse → Activate → Plate — 70 % of defects originate here. - **Applications:** Decorative (Au, Ag, Cr); Protective (Zn, Cd, Sn); Functional (Cu for PCBs, Ni for wear); Electroforming (Ni, Cu for moulds).

topics.title: Electrorefining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT417 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrorefining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Electrorefining** is an **electrometallurgical process** in which an impure metal is purified to a high degree of purity (typically **> 99.99%**) through the controlled dissolution and re-deposition of the metal using **electrolytic principles**. The impure metal serves as the **soluble anode**, a pure strip of the same metal serves as the **cathode**, and both are immersed in an aqueous electrolyte containing ions of the metal to be refined. On application of a **direct current (DC)** from an external source, the metal atoms at the anode oxidize (dissolve) into the solution, while the same metal ions reduce (plate) onto the cathode. The **noble impurities** (Au, Ag, Pt) do not dissolve and fall to the bottom as **anode slime**, while the **base impurities** (Fe, Zn, Ni) dissolve but do not deposit at the applied potential.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Electrorefining is the **purification route** — distinct from **Electrowinning**, which is the **extraction route** from ores. The student must be able to differentiate the two clearly in Part A and Part B questions.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a crowded, dirty swimming pool where you want only the cleanest swimmers to reach the opposite end.

- The **pool water** is the **electrolyte** (a solution of metal ions).
- The **starting end** of the pool is the **impure anode** (mixed metal ingot).
- The **finishing end** is the **pure cathode** (a thin starter sheet of pure metal).
- An external "wind" (the **DC current**) forces the swimmers across.
- Only swimmers that can "swim" at the exact speed set by the current (the correct **deposition potential**) make it across. Too slow? They fall out as **anode slime**. Too fast? They dissolve into the water and stay there (impurities in solution).
- Result: A clean, uniform metal plate is built up at the cathode.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** In industrial electrorefining, the purity of cathode copper routinely reaches **99.99% (Four-Nines copper)** or even **99.999% (Five-Nines)** for specialized electronics-grade applications.

## Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|
| Faraday's Constant (**F**) | **96,485 C mol⁻¹** |
| Current Efficiency (**η**) | **92 – 98 %** |
| Cell Voltage (**V_cell**) | **0.2 – 0.4 V** (per cell) |
| Energy Consumption | **200 – 400 kWh per tonne** of refined Cu |
| Operating Temperature | **55 – 65 °C** |
| Cathode Current Density | **150 – 300 A m⁻²** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electrochemical Cell Architecture

A typical electrorefining cell is constructed as follows:

1. **Anode:** Cast slab of **impure metal** (e.g., blister copper at **~ 99% purity**). The anode is suspended in the electrolytic bath by conducting hooks connected to the positive bus-bar.
2. **Cathode:** A thin **starter sheet** of pure metal (often the same metal deposited previously on a stainless-steel or titanium blank). Suspended on the negative bus-bar.
3. **Electrolyte:** An aqueous solution of a soluble salt of the metal — for copper, this is **CuSO₄ + H₂SO₄** (typically **40 – 50 g/L Cu²⁺** and **150 – 200 g/L H₂SO₄**).
4. **Power Source:** Low-voltage, high-current DC rectifier (**2 – 8 V** at thousands of amperes).
5. **Anode Slime Collection:** A collection trough or bag at the bottom of the cell to recover noble metals (Au, Ag, Se, Te, Pt).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Electrode Reactions (Using Copper as the Reference System)

### At the Anode (Oxidation)
The **major reaction** is the dissolution of copper:
$$Cu_{(s)} \rightarrow Cu^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-}$$

Side reactions involve dissolution of base metals:
$$Fe_{(s)} \rightarrow Fe^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-}$$
$$Zn_{(s)} \rightarrow Zn^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-}$$

Noble metals (Ag, Au, Pt) **do not oxidize** and drop to the cell bottom as **anode slime** (also called **anode mud**).

### At the Cathode (Reduction)
$$Cu^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow Cu_{(s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of Selectivity:** The applied cell voltage is carefully tuned to be **above the deposition potential of Cu²⁺/Cu (≈ +0.34 V vs SHE)** but **below that of Fe²⁺/Fe (≈ −0.44 V)** and **Zn²⁺/Zn (≈ −0.76 V)**. Hence only copper — and not the dissolved base impurities — plates onto the cathode.

## 2.3 Concept of Anode Slime and Purification by-Products

- **Anode slime** is a treasure trove in non-ferrous metallurgy. From the slime of copper electrorefining, **gold, silver, selenium, tellurium, and platinum-group metals** are extracted.
- The **weight of slime** is typically **0.5 – 1.0 %** of the anode mass processed.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's First Law (Theoretical mass deposited) | kg |
| 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Cathode (or Anode) Current Efficiency | % |
| 3 | $E_{spec} = \dfrac{V_{cell} \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot 3600} \times \dfrac{1000}{\eta}$ | Specific Energy Consumption (per tonne) | kWh/tonne |
| 4 | $V_{cell} = E_{anode}^{eq} - E_{cathode}^{eq} + \eta_{anode} + \eta_{cathode} + IR_{cell}$ | Decomposition Voltage + Overpotentials + Ohmic drop | V |
| 5 | $W = \dfrac{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{1000}$ | Total Electrical Energy Consumed | kWh |
| 6 | $Q = I \cdot t = n \cdot F \cdot N$ | Total Charge passed (Q = It, also nFN) | Coulombs |
| 7 | $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$ | Faraday's Second Law (chemical equivalents) | — |
| 8 | $E_w = \dfrac{M}{n}$ | Equivalent Weight of metal | g/equiv |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Reminder:** In KTU numerical problems, $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value must be written as $\lvert x \rvert$. Use of bare pipe symbols inside table rows will break the table layout.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Copper Electrorefining** produces **99.99% pure copper** for electrical wiring, transformers, and printed circuit boards.
- **Aluminum electrorefining** (Hoopes process) is used for further purification of aluminum from **99.5% to 99.99%**.
- **Lead, Zinc, Nickel, Silver, and Gold** are all industrially electrorefined.
- **Recovery of precious metals** from anode slime offsets the operating cost of copper electrorefining plants.

---

## SECTION 2.5: Worked Example — Cell Voltage and Energy Consumption for Copper Electrorefining

**Given:** A copper electrorefining cell operates at **10,000 A**. Cell voltage = **0.30 V**. Current efficiency = **95%**. Compute the mass of copper deposited per 24 hours and the specific energy consumption in **kWh/tonne**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Theoretical Mass (Faraday's Law):**
$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{10000 \times (24 \times 3600) \times 63.55}{2 \times 96485}$$

$$m = \frac{10000 \times 86400 \times 63.55}{192970} = 284{,}435 \text{ g} = 284.44 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Actual Mass:**
$$m_{actual} = \eta \times m_{theoretical} = 0.95 \times 284.44 = 270.22 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Specific Energy Consumption:**
$$E_{spec} = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{1000 \times m_{actual}} = \frac{0.30 \times 10000 \times 86400}{1000 \times 270.22}$$

$$E_{spec} = 95.92 \text{ kWh for } 270.22 \text{ kg}$$

Per tonne: $E_{spec, tonne} = \dfrac{95.92}{270.22} \times 1000 = 355.0 \text{ kWh/tonne}$

**Result:** **270.22 kg of Cu/day** deposited, with energy consumption of **355 kWh/tonne** — well within the industrial range stated earlier.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Faraday's Laws Applied to Electrorefining

### Step 1 — Starting from Faraday's First Law

The mass of a substance liberated at an electrode is **proportional to the total electric charge** passed through the electrolyte.

$$m \propto Q$$

where $Q = I \cdot t$. To convert the proportionality to equality, we introduce the **electrochemical equivalent** $Z$:

$$m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$$

### Step 2 — Expressing Z in terms of Fundamental Constants

For one mole of a metal of atomic mass $M$ deposited by $n$ electrons per ion, the charge required is $nF$. Therefore the mass deposited per unit charge is:

$$Z = \frac{M}{nF}$$

### Step 3 — Combined Equation (Faraday's First Law, Final Form)

$$\boxed{m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}}$$

### Step 4 — Faraday's Second Law (Combination Law)

When the **same quantity of electricity** is passed through several electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their **equivalent weights** ($E_w = M/n$):

$$\frac{m_1}{m_2} = \frac{E_{w,1}}{E_{w,2}} = \frac{M_1/n_1}{M_2/n_2}$$

This law is critical when KTU examiners pose a **bi-electrode / multi-metal numerical**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Energy Consumption (kWh per tonne)

### Step 1 — Total Energy Input
The total electrical energy supplied is:

$$W_{total} = V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t \quad \text{(in Joules, since V is volts)}$$

### Step 2 — Convert to kWh
$$W_{kWh} = \frac{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{1000 \times 3600} = \frac{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{3.6 \times 10^6}$$

### Step 3 — Mass of Metal Actually Deposited
$$m_{actual} = \eta \cdot \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

### Step 4 — Specific Energy per Tonne
Divide total kWh by actual mass in tonnes ($m_{actual, tonnes} = m_{actual, kg}/1000$):

$$E_{spec} = \frac{W_{kWh}}{m_{actual,tonnes}} = \frac{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t \cdot 1000}{1000 \times \eta \cdot M \cdot I \cdot t / (nF)}$$

$$\boxed{E_{spec} = \frac{V_{cell} \cdot n \cdot F}{3.6 \times \eta \cdot M} \quad \text{kWh/tonne}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Remarkable insight:** Notice that $E_{spec}$ **does NOT depend on current $I$ or time $t$** — it depends only on **cell voltage, current efficiency, and equivalent weight** of the metal. This is a frequently asked viva question.

## 3.3 Derivation: Current Efficiency with Side Reactions

In practice, side reactions (e.g., hydrogen evolution at the cathode) consume some current.

Let:
- $I_{main}$ = current used for intended metal deposition
- $I_{side}$ = current wasted in side reactions
- $I_{total} = I_{main} + I_{side}$

Cathode current efficiency:

$$\eta_c = \frac{I_{main}}{I_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{m_{actual} \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot I \cdot t} \times 100$$

Anode current efficiency is similarly defined, but in copper refining the **anode dissolution is nearly 100%** because all soluble impurities dissolve; only the slime-forming noble metals remain undissolved.

## 3.4 Derivation: Mass of Anode Slime Recovered

If the anode contains **x%** noble impurities (Au + Ag + Pt) and the anode mass is $M_A$:

$$m_{slime} = \frac{x}{100} \times M_A$$

In industrial practice, the slime is **further processed** by pyrometallurgical or hydrometallurgical routes to recover pure noble metals.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Between Electrorefining and Electrowinning

| Feature | **Electrorefining** | **Electrowinning** |
|---|---|---|
| **Anode** | **Impure** metal to be purified | **Inert** (Pb, Pb-Ag alloy, or dimensionally stable anode) |
| **Feedstock** | Impure metal ingot (≥ 95%) | Pregnant leach solution from ore processing |
| **Cathode Product** | Pure metal | Pure metal |
| **Electrolyte** | Salt of the metal to be refined | Salt solution from leaching |
| **Purpose** | **Purification** | **Extraction** |
| **Typical Metal Example** | Cu (99 → 99.99%) | Zn (from ZnSO₄ leach liquor) |
| **Anode Slime** | **Yes** (rich in Au, Ag) | **No** (anode is inert) |
| **Energy Consumption** | **200 – 400 kWh/tonne** | **3000 – 5000 kWh/tonne** |
| **Cell Voltage** | **0.2 – 0.4 V** | **2.5 – 3.5 V** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: Electrorefining Process Flow

```mermaid
    slimeTray --> recoveryNode[Precious Metal Recovery Plant]

    pureOut --> endNode[Quality Check Purity greater than 99.99 percent]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define electrorefining. Mention the role of anode slime.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Electrorefining is an electrolytic purification process in which an impure metal is used as the **anode** and a pure sample of the same metal as the **cathode**, both immersed in an electrolyte containing ions of that metal. On passing direct current, the metal dissolves at the anode and pure metal deposits at the cathode.
- **Role of anode slime (1 mark):** The insoluble noble impurities (Au, Ag, Pt) do not oxidize at the applied potential and collect at the cell bottom as **anode slime**, which is a valuable by-product for **precious metal recovery**.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between electrorefining and electrowinning in two points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | **Electrorefining** | **Electrowinning** |
|---|---|---|
| **Anode Type** | Soluble (impure metal) | **Inert** (Pb or Pb-Ag alloy) |
| **Purpose** | **Purification** of an impure metal | **Extraction** of metal from leach liquor |

*(2 marks for the table, 1 mark for an example such as Cu refining vs Zn extraction.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Full-Length ESE Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with the structure **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** is permitted.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 MARKS `[KTU University Exam - 2024 ESE Pattern]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat cell diagram, explain the **electrorefining of copper**. Discuss the role of **anode slime** and write the **electrode reactions**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A copper electrorefining cell operates at **15,000 A** with a cell voltage of **0.32 V** and cathode current efficiency of **96%**. Calculate:  
  (i) The mass of copper deposited in **24 hours**.  
  (ii) The **specific energy consumption** in kWh/tonne of Cu.  
**Given:** Atomic mass of Cu = 63.55 g/mol, n = 2, F = 96,485 C/mol. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 marks

1. **Cell Construction (2 marks):**
   - **Anode:** Cast impure copper slab (≈ 99% pure, blister copper).
   - **Cathode:** Pure copper starter sheet (or stainless-steel blank with deposited Cu seed).
   - **Electrolyte:** Aqueous solution of **CuSO₄ (40 – 50 g/L Cu²⁺)** and **H₂SO₄ (150 – 200 g/L)**.
   - **DC source:** Low voltage (0.2 – 0.4 V) at high current (thousands of amperes).

2. **Electrode Reactions (3 marks):**

   **Anode (oxidation):**
   $$Cu_{(s)} \rightarrow Cu^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-} \quad E^\circ = +0.34\text{ V}$$
   $$\text{Side: } Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^{-} \quad Zn \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$

   **Cathode (reduction):**
   $$Cu^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow Cu_{(s)}$$

3. **Anode Slime (2 marks):**
   - Noble metals (Ag, Au, Pt, Se, Te) remain insoluble and accumulate as **anode slime (mud)** at the bottom.
   - This slime is the **economic backbone** of the process — it is processed to recover **Au, Ag, Se, Te**.

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Mass of Copper Deposited in 24 hours (3 marks):**

$$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \times \eta$$

$$m = \frac{63.55 \times 15000 \times (24 \times 3600)}{2 \times 96485} \times 0.96$$

$$m = \frac{63.55 \times 15000 \times 86400}{192970} \times 0.96$$

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{63.55 \times 15000 \times 86400}{192970} = 426{,}651 \text{ g} = 426.65 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{actual} = 0.96 \times 426.65 = 409.59 \text{ kg}$$

**∴ Mass deposited = 409.59 kg per 24 hours** ✓

**(ii) Specific Energy Consumption (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total Energy in kWh:**
$$W = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{1000} = \frac{0.32 \times 15000 \times 86400}{1000} = 414.72 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Mass in tonnes:**
$$m = \frac{409.59}{1000} = 0.4096 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 3 — Specific Energy:**
$$E_{spec} = \frac{414.72}{0.4096} = 1012.5 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

**∴ Specific Energy Consumption = 1012.5 kWh/tonne** ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Many students forget to **multiply by current efficiency (η)** when computing actual mass, leading to an **over-estimation of mass and under-estimation of specific energy**. Always write $m_{actual} = \eta \times m_{theoretical}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** In the energy formula, ensure unit consistency. If you use $t$ in **seconds**, you must divide by **1000 × 3600** to get kWh. Forgetting the 3600 conversion gives an answer off by 3600×.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE) `[KTU University Exam - 2024 ESE Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the **principles of electrorefining** with reference to **selective dissolution and deposition**. Discuss **why anodes contain 0.5 – 1.0 % noble impurities** and how they are recovered. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An electrorefining cell deposits **silver** from an AgNO₃ electrolyte. Given: I = 5000 A, t = 10 hours, η = 98%, atomic mass of Ag = 107.87 g/mol, n = 1. Calculate:  
  (i) The **theoretical** and **actual mass** of silver deposited.  
  (ii) If the cell voltage is **0.5 V**, compute the **energy consumption in kWh**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 marks

1. **Principle of Selective Dissolution (2 marks):**  
   In electrorefining, the **anode potential** is set above the dissolution potential of the metal to be refined but below that of the noble impurities. This ensures that only the base metal (e.g., Cu, Ag, Ni) oxidizes, while the noble metals remain untouched.

2. **Principle of Selective Deposition (2 marks):**  
   The **cathode potential** is set so that only the **most noble** of the dissolved ions (e.g., Cu²⁺) reduces, while less noble ions (Fe²⁺, Zn²⁺) stay in solution. This is achieved by carefully controlling **current density** and **electrolyte composition**.

3. **Anode Composition and Slime Recovery (3 marks):**
   - Anode is **deliberately cast with 0.5 – 1.0% noble impurities** because:
     - Complete removal of Au/Ag in the pyrometallurgical stage is **uneconomical**.
     - The electrorefining step doubles as a **pre-concentration stage** for precious metals.
   - The slime is filtered, washed, and sent to a **dedicated recovery plant**:
     - **Au** is recovered by **electrolytic parting** (Wohlwill process) or **aqua regia leaching**.
     - **Ag** is recovered by **electrolytic refining** in **Moebius or Thum cells** using AgNO₃ electrolyte.
     - **Se and Te** are recovered by alkaline leaching of slime followed by reduction.

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Theoretical and Actual Mass (3.5 marks):**

**Theoretical Mass:**
$$m_{th} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} = \frac{107.87 \times 5000 \times (10 \times 3600)}{1 \times 96485}$$

$$m_{th} = \frac{107.87 \times 5000 \times 36000}{96485} = \frac{1.9417 \times 10^{10}}{96485} = 201{,}243 \text{ g} = 201.24 \text{ kg}$$

**Actual Mass:**
$$m_{actual} = 0.98 \times 201.24 = 197.22 \text{ kg}$$

**∴ Theoretical = 201.24 kg; Actual = 197.22 kg** ✓

**(ii) Energy Consumption (3.5 marks):**

$$W = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{1000} = \frac{0.5 \times 5000 \times 36000}{1000} = 90.0 \text{ kWh}$$

**∴ Energy consumption = 90.0 kWh** ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #3:** For **silver (n = 1)**, students often mistakenly use **n = 2** (confusing with Cu). The number of electrons transferred equals the **valency of the depositing ion**, not the valency of the metal in some other context. For Ag⁺ + e⁻ → Ag, n = 1.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #4:** In Question B part (a), examiners **deduct 1 mark** if the student does not state the **range of operating cell voltage (0.2 – 0.4 V)** for copper refining. Always include the quantitative operating window in your answer.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⚡ **Definition:** Electrorefining = **purification** of an impure metal using a **soluble anode** and a **pure cathode** of the same metal, in an electrolyte of its own salt solution.
- ⚡ **Cell Voltage:** Industrially kept low at **0.2 – 0.4 V** for Cu (single cell); multiple cells are connected in **series** for high amperage operation.
- ⚡ **Anode:** Impure metal (e.g., blister Cu at 99%); dissolves as $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$.
- ⚡ **Cathode:** Pure metal starter sheet; deposition as $M^{n+} + ne^- \rightarrow M$.
- ⚡ **Anode Slime:** Collects **noble impurities** (Au, Ag, Pt, Se, Te) — the **economic profit centre** of a copper refinery.
- ⚡ **Current Efficiency (η):** Typically **92 – 98 %**; losses due to H₂ evolution at cathode, short-circuits, and stray current.
- ⚡ **Energy Consumption:** **200 – 400 kWh/tonne** for Cu refining; **3000 – 5000 kWh/tonne** for electrowinning.
- ⚡ **Faraday's First Law:** $m = \dfrac{M I t}{n F}$ — must be multiplied by η to get **actual mass**.
- ⚡ **Specific Energy Formula:** $E_{spec} = \dfrac{V_{cell} \cdot n \cdot F}{3.6 \cdot \eta \cdot M}$ kWh/tonne — note the **absence of I and t**.
- ⚡ **Distinguishing Feature vs Electrowinning:** **Soluble anode** = electrorefining; **inert anode** = electrowinning.
- ⚡ **Industrial Examples:** Cu, Ni, Ag, Pb, Zn, Al (Hoopes process) — all use this principle.
- ⚡ **Slime Composition:** Au (typically 0.5 – 5% of slime), Ag (5 – 30%), Se, Te, Pt-group metals.
- ⚡ **Operating Conditions:** Temperature **55 – 65 °C**; current density **150 – 300 A/m²**; circulation of electrolyte for concentration uniformity.
- ⚡ **KTU Exam Trivia:** Examiner's most loved question is "**Why is the cell voltage very low in electrorefining?**" — answer: because the anode and cathode reactions are **reversible** ($E^\circ_{anode} \approx E^\circ_{cathode}$), the theoretical decomposition voltage is **~ 0**, and only **IR drop + small overpotentials** need to be overcome.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electroforming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT417 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electroforming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electroforming** is a specialized electrochemical manufacturing process in which a metallic layer is electrodeposited onto a pre-shaped mandrel (master pattern) until a self-supporting, free-standing metal structure of the desired thickness is obtained. After deposition, the mandrel is mechanically or chemically separated from the deposit, leaving behind a precise metallic replica.

The process is governed by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis (1834)** and is essentially an extension of **electroplating**, but with a critical distinction: in electroplating the deposit remains *bonded* to the substrate as a surface coating, whereas in electroforming the deposit *becomes the product itself*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT417 / Module 3):**
> "Electroforming is the process of producing or reproducing articles by electrodeposition upon a mandrel or matrix, which is subsequently separated from the deposit."

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Picture)

Imagine blowing a **bubble of metal** around a wax ball:

1. Take a wax ball of the desired shape.
2. Dip it into a metal salt solution and pass electric current.
3. Metal atoms slowly "grow" over the wax like a metallic shell.
4. After the shell is thick enough, melt the wax out.
5. You are left with a hollow, precise metal egg.

That is **electroforming in a nutshell**. The mandrel is the "wax ball," the electrolyte is the "metal soup," and the current is the "force" that pulls metal ions onto the surface atom-by-atom.

## 1.3 Key Process Metrics (Standard Industrial Values)

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Operating current density | **2 – 30 A/dm²** |
| Cell voltage | **3 – 12 V** |
| Bath temperature | **40 – 70 °C** |
| Deposition rate (Ni) | **12 – 60 μm/hr** (theoretical) |
| pH of electrolyte | **3.5 – 4.5** (Ni sulfamate bath) |
| Faraday constant | **F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Electroforming matters industrially:**
> It is the only commercial process capable of producing **complex, seamless, hollow metal parts** with micron-level dimensional accuracy — used in aerospace waveguides, rocket thrust chambers, optical reflectors, micro-electro-mechanical systems (MEMS), and even coin/medal manufacturing.

## 1.4 Distinguishing Electroforming from Related Processes

| Feature | Electroplating | Electroforming | Electrowinning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Surface coating | Stand-alone part | Metal extraction |
| **Bond to substrate** | Permanent | **Separated later** | N/A (powder/deposit) |
| **Deposit thickness** | 1 – 50 μm | **100 μm – 10 mm** | Bulk |
| **Mandrel required** | No | **Yes (essential)** | No |
| **End product** | Coated article | **Free metal part** | Crude metal |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Fundamentals — The "Why" and "How"

Electroforming relies on the **controlled cathodic reduction** of metal ions from an aqueous (or occasionally non-aqueous) electrolyte:

$$M^{n+}_{(aq)} + n\,e^{-} \longrightarrow M_{(s)}$$

The metallic atoms nucleate and grow as a coherent, dense layer on the **mandrel surface**, which acts as the cathode. The anode (often the same metal being deposited) dissolves to replenish the electrolyte.

### Step-by-Step Process Logic

1. **Mandrel Preparation** — Surface is polished, degreased, and (for non-conductors) made electrically conductive by silver sputtering, graphite coating, or chemical deposition.
2. **Rinsing & Activation** — Acid dip removes oxides and activates the surface for uniform nucleation.
3. **Immersion in Electrolyte** — Mandrel submerged in an aerated/stirred plating bath with controlled pH and temperature.
4. **Current Application** — D.C. current is passed; deposition rate follows Faraday's law.
5. **Thickness Build-up** — Process continues for hours-to-days until target thickness is reached.
6. **Separation** — Mandrel is withdrawn (permanent mandrels) or dissolved (sacrificial mandrels).
7. **Post-Processing** — Trimming, machining, polishing as required.

## 2.2 Classification of Mandrels

### A) Permanent (Reusable) Mandrels
- Made of **stainless steel, brass, nickel, or titanium**.
- The deposit is mechanically pulled off after slight differential thermal expansion (heating/cooling).
- Used for high-volume production (e.g., CDs, DVDs stamper).

### B) Sacrificial (Fusible / Soluble) Mandrels
- Made of **aluminum, zinc, low-melting alloys, wax, or plastics**.
- Removed by **melting, chemical dissolution (e.g., NaOH for Al), or burning out**.
- Used for intricate internal geometries (e.g., hollow waveguides).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Criterion (Examiner Hot-Topic):**
> Choose **permanent mandrels** for high repeatability and tight tolerances (≥ ±2.5 μm).
> Choose **sacrificial mandrels** for complex internal contours, undercuts, or one-piece hollow parts.

## 2.3 Critical Process Parameters

- **Current Density (J)** — Higher J → faster deposition but coarser grain, internal stress, and risk of "burning" (H₂ evolution, pitting).
- **Temperature** — Higher T → better ion mobility, finer grain, lower stress; but accelerates additive decomposition.
- **Agitation / Flow** — Removes H₂ bubbles, prevents concentration polarization, ensures throwing power.
- **pH** — Influences H₂ evolution, hydroxide inclusion, and stress.
- **Additives** — Stress-reducers (saccharin), levelers, brighteners, wetting agents.
- **Throwing Power** — Ability to deposit uniformly on recessed/3D surfaces — critical in electroforming.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's First Law (mass deposited) | grams |
| 2 | $m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$ | Mass via electrochemical equivalent $Z = M/(nF)$ | grams |
| 3 | $\eta_{c} = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Cathodic current efficiency | % |
| 4 | $h = \dfrac{J \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} \times 10^{4}$ | Deposit thickness | μm |
| 5 | $v = \dfrac{J \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} \times 36$ | Deposition rate | μm/hr |
| 6 | $R_{s} = \dfrac{\sigma_{internal}}{\Delta T \cdot E}$ | Risk of stress cracking (qualitative) | — |
| 7 | $TP(\%) = \dfrac{K - M_{log}}{K + M_{log} - 2} \times 100$ | Throwing power (Haring–Blum cell) | % |
| 8 | $P = \dfrac{\rho_{metal}}{\rho_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Deposit porosity / density ratio | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:** $J$ = current density in A/cm², $t$ = time in seconds, $M$ = atomic mass (g/mol), $n$ = electrons transferred, $F$ = 96,485 C/mol, $\rho$ = density of metal (g/cm³).
> For $J$ in **A/dm²** and $t$ in **hours**, use the corrected coefficient (see Section 3 derivation).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Manufacturing of seamless nickel thrust chambers (Rama rocket nozzle) — only electroforming can deposit a single-piece, stress-free chamber.
- **Optics:** X-ray telescope mirrors (e.g., NASA's Chandra X-ray Observatory) — gold/nickel shells electroformed on polished mandrels to 0.5 nm surface finish.
- **Electronics:** Microfluidic channels, MEMS heat sinks, foil-wound inductors.
- **Medical:** Stents, hearing-aid shells, orthodontic appliances.
- **Currency & Medals:** Master dies for stamps, coins (Mint process).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Electroforming Thickness Equation (Faraday's Law → Thickness)

We derive the **master equation** used universally for predicting deposition rate in electroforming cells.

**Given:**
- Current $I$ (A) flowing for time $t$ (s)
- Cathodic reaction: $M^{n+} + ne^{-} \rightarrow M_{(s)}$
- Atomic mass of metal $M$ (g/mol), density $\rho$ (g/cm³)

### Step 1 — Apply Faraday's First Law

The mass of metal deposited is proportional to the total charge passed:

$$m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

where $Q = I \cdot t$ (Coulombs).

### Step 2 — Convert Mass to Volume

Volume of deposit is mass divided by density:

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

Units check: $\dfrac{(A)(s)(g/mol)}{(C/mol)(g/cm^{3})} = \dfrac{(C)(g/mol)(cm^{3})}{(C/mol)(g)} = cm^{3}$ ✓

### Step 3 — Relate Volume to Thickness and Area

For a deposit of uniform thickness $h$ over an area $A$:

$$V = A \cdot h \quad \Longrightarrow \quad h = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$$

### Step 4 — Introduce Current Density

Define $J = I/A$ (A/cm²). Substituting:

$$h = \frac{J \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} \quad \text{(in cm)}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Micrometers

Since $1 \text{ cm} = 10^{4} \text{ μm}$:

$$\boxed{\,h \, (\mu m) = \frac{J \cdot t \cdot M \times 10^{4}}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}\,}$$

with $J$ in A/cm² and $t$ in seconds.

### Step 6 — Convenient Form (J in A/dm², t in hours)

Multiply numerator and denominator: $J_{A/cm^{2}} = J_{A/dm^{2}}/100$ and $t_{s} = t_{hr} \times 3600$:

$$h \, (\mu m) = \frac{(J/100)(t \times 3600)\, M \times 10^{4}}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

Simplifying:

$$\boxed{\,h \, (\mu m) = \frac{360 \, \cdot\, J_{A/dm^{2}} \cdot\, t_{hr} \cdot\, M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}\,}$$

For **nickel** ($M = 58.69$, $n = 2$, $\rho = 8.90$):

$$h_{Ni} \, (\mu m/hr) = 0.0123 \times J_{A/dm^{2}} \quad \text{(theoretical, 100\% efficiency)}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1 — Nickel Electroforming (Simple)

**Problem:** A nickel mandrel of area **5 dm²** is electroformed at **4 A/dm²** for **6 hours**. Calculate (a) mass of Ni deposited (assume 95% efficiency), (b) average thickness.

**Solution:**

*Part (a):* Total current $I = J \times A = 4 \times 5 = 20$ A. Total charge $Q = I \cdot t = 20 \times 6 \times 3600 = 4.32 \times 10^{5}$ C.

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{4.32 \times 10^{5} \times 58.69}{2 \times 96485} = \frac{2.535 \times 10^{7}}{1.930 \times 10^{5}} = 131.4 \text{ g}$$

Applying efficiency:

$$m_{actual} = \eta \cdot m_{theoretical} = 0.95 \times 131.4 = 124.8 \text{ g}$$

*Part (b):* Using the simplified formula:

$$h = \frac{360 \cdot J \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} = \frac{360 \times 4 \times 6 \times 58.69}{2 \times 96485 \times 8.90} \times \eta$$

$$h_{theoretical} = \frac{360 \times 4 \times 6 \times 58.69}{1{,}717{,}633} = \frac{507{,}053}{1{,}717{,}633} = 295.2 \, \mu m$$

$$h_{actual} = 0.95 \times 295.2 = 280.4 \, \mu m \approx 0.28 \text{ mm}$$

### Example 2 — Copper Electroforming Time Calculation

**Problem:** A copper electroforming cell operates at **3 A/dm²**. How long will it take to deposit a **200 μm thick** copper foil on a **2 dm²** mandrel? Assume 100% current efficiency. ($M_{Cu} = 63.55$, $\rho_{Cu} = 8.96$)

**Solution:**

Rearranging the thickness equation:

$$t = \frac{h \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho}{360 \cdot J \cdot M}$$

$$t = \frac{200 \times 2 \times 96485 \times 8.96}{360 \times 3 \times 63.55} = \frac{3.458 \times 10^{8}}{6.864 \times 10^{4}} = 5038 \text{ s} \approx 1.40 \text{ hr}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Parameters on Deposit Quality

| Parameter ↑ | Deposition Rate | Grain Size | Internal Stress | Hardness | Purity |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Current density | ↑↑ | ↑ (coarser) | ↑ (tensile) | ↑ | ↓ |
| Temperature | ↑ | ↓ (finer) | ↓ (relieved) | ↓ | ↑ |
| Agitation | ↑ (mass transport) | ↓ | — | ↓ | ↑ |
| pH (acidic side) | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | varies | ↑ |
| Additive (saccharin) | — | ↓↓↓ (finer) | ↓↓ (less stress) | ↓ | — |
| Ag⁺ brightener | — | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert (Examiner's Trap):** Students often equate "high current density = better" — but beyond ~30 A/dm² in Ni baths, you get **burnt deposits** (black, powdery) due to simultaneous H₂ evolution. The optimal Ni electroforming window is **2–10 A/dm²**.

## 3.4 Derivation of Throwing Power (Haring–Blum Concept)

The **Haring–Blum cell** quantitatively measures throwing power using two cathodes at unequal distances $L_1$ and $L_2$ from a common anode.

$$\text{TP (\%)} = \frac{K - M_{log}}{K + M_{log} - 2} \times 100$$

where $K = L_2/L_1$ (distance ratio) and $M_{log} = \log R$ with $R = $ ratio of metal distribution.

For **perfect throwing**: $R \rightarrow 1$ → TP = 100 %.
For **no throwing**: $R \rightarrow K$ → TP = 0 %.

---

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Electroforming Process Flow (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Design Mandrel] --> B[Mandrel Material Selection]
    B --> B1[Permanent: Stainless Steel / Ti / Brass]
    B --> B2[Sacrificial: Al / Wax / Low-melt Alloy]
    B1 --> C[Surface Preparation]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[Cleaning and Degreasing]
    D --> E[Acid Activation Pickling]
    E --> F[Conductive Coating if Non-Metallic]
    F --> G[Immerse Mandrel as Cathode in Electrolyte]
    G --> H[Apply DC Current at Controlled J and T]
    H --> I[Monitor Thickness via Faraday Law]
    I --> J{Target Thickness Reached?}
    J -->|No| H
    J -->|Yes| K[Remove Mandrel from Bath]
    K --> L[Separate Deposit from Mandrel]
    L --> M1[Mechanical Pull Off for Permanent Mandrels]
    L --> M2[Chemical Dissolution for Sacrificial Mandrels]
    M1 --> N[Post Processing Trim Polish]
    M2 --> N
    N --> O[Final Free Standing Metal Part]
```

## 4.2 Electroforming Cell Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PSU[DC Power Supply] -->|Positive Lead| ANO[Anode: Soluble Metal M]
    PSU -->|Negative Lead| CAT[Cathode: Mandrel]
    ANO -.->|M to M plus n electrons| ELY[Electrolyte: M_n plus Salt Solution]
    ELY -.->|Metal ions migrate to mandrel| CAT
    CAT --> DEP[Metal Deposit Growing on Mandrel]
    DEP -->|Thickness increases with Q| REM[Removal Stage]
    TEMP[Temperature Controller 40 to 70 C] -.-> ELY
    AGIT[Agitation Pump] -.-> ELY
    pH[pH Controller 3.5 to 4.5] -.-> ELY
    FILTER[Filtration Unit] -.-> ELY
```

## 4.3 Mandrel Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Internal Undercuts or Hollow Geometry?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| S1[Use Sacrificial Mandrel]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Production Volume?}
    Q2 -->|High Volume above 1000 parts| P1[Use Permanent Stainless Mandrel]
    Q2 -->|Low Volume below 100| S1
    S1 --> S1A[Materials: Al, Zn, Wax, Acrylic]
    P1 --> P1A[Materials: SS 304, Brass, Ni, Ti]
    S1 --> OUT1[Final Part: Hollow One Piece]
    P1 --> OUT2[Final Part: Open or Externally Shaped]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Block — Electroforming vs Electroplating vs Electrowinning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROC[Electrochemical Manufacturing Family]
        EP[Electroplating]:::plate
        EF[Electroforming]:::form
        EW[Electrowinning]:::win
    end
    EP -->|Coating on substrate| P1[Finished Coated Part]
    EF -->|Self supporting deposit separated| P2[Standalone Metal Component]
    EW -->|Bulk recovery from ore leachate| P3[Pure Metal Cathode]
    classDef plate fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef form fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
```
