# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Define solar absorptance (α) and thermal emittance (ε). Why is the ratio α/ε used as the figure of merit for a selective solar coating?**

**Model Answer (Concise KTU Style — 3 marks):**

> **Solar absorptance (α):** The fraction of incident solar radiation (weighted by the AM 1.5 spectrum, 0.3–2.5 µm) that is absorbed by the surface.
>
> **Thermal emittance (ε):** The fraction of blackbody thermal radiation emitted by the surface at its operating temperature, integrated over the IR spectrum (2.5–50 µm).
>
> **Why α/ε is the figure of merit:** In a solar collector, useful heat gained is **α·I_s** and heat lost by radiation is **ε·σT⁴**. To *maximise* the first and *minimise* the second simultaneously, the ratio α/ε is the natural performance index. A higher α/ε means more solar energy is captured for each unit of thermal re-radiation loss. Typical targets: α/ε ≥ 10 (good), ≥ 25 (excellent).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**List any three electrochemically deposited selective coatings and give their typical α/ε values.**

**Model Answer (Concise KTU Style — 3 marks):**

> 1. **Black Chrome (Cr-oxide cermet on Cu):** α ≈ 0.95 – 0.97, ε ≈ 0.08 – 0.12 → α/ε ≈ 10 – 12
> 2. **Black Nickel (NiS–ZnS composite on Cu):** α ≈ 0.90 – 0.95, ε ≈ 0.10 – 0.15 → α/ε ≈ 6 – 9
> 3. **Black Cobalt (Co₃O₄ on Cu):** α ≈ 0.92 – 0.96, ε ≈ 0.10 – 0.18 → α/ε ≈ 6 – 9
>
> All three are produced by **electrodeposition** from acidic sulphate/chromate baths, making them economically viable for mass production of flat-plate solar collectors.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

#### (a) **Explain the principle of selectivity in solar absorber coatings. Derive the relation between semiconductor band-gap energy and the cutoff wavelength, and compute the cutoff wavelength for silicon (E_g = 1.12 eV) and germanium (E_g = 0.67 eV). Comment on which is more suitable for a low-temperature solar collector operating at 100 °C. [7 marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Principle of Selectivity (2 marks):** A selective solar coating exploits the *spectral mismatch* between incoming solar radiation (0.3–2.5 µm) and outgoing thermal radiation (2.5–50 µm at operating T). The coating is engineered to have:
   - **High absorptance (α ≥ 0.90)** in the 0.3–2.5 µm range (catches the solar flux).
   - **Low thermal emittance (ε ≤ 0.10)** in the 2.5–50 µm range (suppresses re-radiation losses).
   - The transition ("cutoff") is engineered to occur at the **1.5–2.0 µm knee** of the AM 1.5 spectrum.

2. **Derivation (3 marks):** As shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes, equating photon energy to band-gap gives:
   $$E_{g} = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda_{c}} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \lambda_{c} = \dfrac{1240}{E_{g}(\text{eV})}\ \text{nm}$$

3. **Numerical Computation (1 mark):**
   - For **Si (E_g = 1.12 eV):** λ_c = 1240 / 1.12 ≈ **1107 nm ≈ 1.11 µm**
   - For **Ge (E_g = 0.67 eV):** λ_c = 1240 / 0.67 ≈ **1851 nm ≈ 1.85 µm**

4. **Comment (1 mark):** Germanium, with its longer cutoff of 1.85 µm, absorbs a **larger portion of the near-IR solar flux** (extending closer to the 2.5 µm edge) and is therefore *more suitable* for a low-temperature collector at 100 °C, where the thermal emission still lies predominantly at longer wavelengths (≈ 7.8 µm). Silicon, with λ_c = 1.11 µm, absorbs less of the near-IR solar energy and is therefore a less ideal intrinsic selective material.

#### (b) **Describe the electrochemical preparation of black chrome selective coating. Discuss the role of current density, bath composition, and deposit thickness on α and ε. [7 marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Bath Composition (2 marks):**
   - **Chromic acid (CrO₃):** 200 – 300 g/L (source of Cr⁶⁺ / Cr³⁺)
   - **Sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄):** 2.0 – 3.0 g/L (catalyst for chromic acid reduction)
   - **Acetate buffer (NaCH₃COO):** to maintain pH = 2.5 – 3.0
   - **Additives:** fluosilicate or fluoride ions (e.g., Na₂SiF₆) to refine grain structure

2. **Electrodeposition Parameters (2 marks):**
   - **Anode:** Lead or lead-antimony alloy (inert to chromic acid)
   - **Cathode (substrate):** Polished Cu or Ni-plated mild steel
   - **Current density:** **15 – 40 A/dm²** (DC); below 15 A/dm² gives a brown, loosely adherent film; above 40 A/dm² gives powdery, non-adherent deposits.
   - **Temperature:** 20 – 30 °C
   - **Deposition time:** 2 – 8 minutes
   - **Thickness:** **0.5 – 2.0 µm** optimum. Thicker films increase α (good) but also increase ε (bad), so an optimum exists near 1.0 µm.

3. **Reaction Mechanism (2 marks):**
   - **Cathode:** Cr⁶⁺ + 6 e⁻ → Cr⁰ (metallic Cr) *and* Cr⁶⁺ → Cr³⁺ → Cr₂O₃ (oxide) — co-deposited as a *cermet* (Cr metal + Cr₂O₃).
   - The cermet's metal content gives it **low IR emissivity**, while the oxide content gives it **high visible absorptance** — the perfect selective combination.
   - **Anode:** 2 H₂O → O₂ + 4 H⁺ + 4 e⁻ (oxygen evolution; Pb anode is passivated by PbO₂ layer).

4. **Effect of Process Variables on Performance (1 mark):**
   - Higher current density → more Cr metal, more metallic-grey (lower α, lower ε).
   - Lower current density → more Cr₂O₃, darker (higher α, higher ε).
   - Optimum: **20 – 30 A/dm², 1.0 µm thickness** → α ≈ 0.95, ε ≈ 0.10, α/ε ≈ 10.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]`

#### (a) **With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the construction and working of a DMD (dielectric-metal-dielectric) selective solar coating. Compare its performance with black chrome. [7 marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Construction (3 marks):** As shown in **Section 4.4 (Figure 4)** of these notes, a DMD stack consists of five layers:
   - **Layer 1 — Bottom dielectric:** Al₂O₃ (~80 nm) — antireflection for the metal undercoat.
   - **Layer 2 — Metal undercoat (IR reflector):** Mo or Cu (~100 nm) — provides the *low-ε backbone*; reflects thermal IR.
   - **Layer 3 — Absorbing cermet:** Mo metal + Al₂O₃ dielectric composite (~80 nm) — provides *high α* in the solar band.
   - **Layer 4 — Top antireflection coating:** Al₂O₃ (~80 nm) — reduces Fresnel reflection of solar wavelengths.
   - **Substrate:** Polished Cu or Mo foil (0.3 – 0.5 mm).
   - A more advanced variant — *graded-index (GRIN) DMD* — uses a continuously varying metal volume fraction in the cermet layer for impedance matching across the solar spectrum.

2. **Working Principle (2 marks):**
   - The **metal undercoat** reflects IR strongly (Drude reflection below plasma wavelength).
   - The **cermet layer** absorbs solar photons via localised surface plasmons and interband transitions in Mo nanoparticles.
   - The **top dielectric** minimises reflection loss at the air–coating interface, increasing α.
   - The combination yields **α ≈ 0.92 – 0.95** and **ε ≈ 0.04 – 0.08** — α/ε up to 20.

3. **Comparison with Black Chrome (2 marks):**

| Property | Black Chrome | DMD (Mo-Al₂O₃) |
|---|---|---|
| α | 0.95 – 0.97 | 0.92 – 0.95 |
| ε (100 °C) | 0.08 – 0.12 | **0.04 – 0.08** |
| α/ε | 10 – 12 | **15 – 20** |
| Max operating T | 400 °C | **500 °C** |
| Deposition | **Electrochemical** (cheap) | Sputtering/e-beam (expensive) |
| Substrate flexibility | Complex shapes possible | Flat panels preferred |
| Mass production | **Yes** (cheap) | Limited |

> **Conclusion:** DMD has *superior* selectivity and thermal stability, but **black chrome wins on cost and mass-manufacturability** — the reason it dominates the domestic solar-thermal market.

#### (b) **Derive the energy balance equation for a flat-plate solar collector and show how the α/ε ratio governs the maximum stagnation temperature. [7 marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Energy Balance Statement (1 mark):** In steady state, the useful heat delivered equals the absorbed solar flux minus all thermal losses (radiation + convection + conduction):

   $$Q_{u} = A_{c}\left[\alpha \tau I_{s} - \varepsilon \sigma (T_{p}^{4} - T_{sky}^{4}) - U_{L}(T_{p} - T_{a})\right]$$

2. **Simplification for stagnation (2 marks):** *Stagnation* = no fluid flow → Q_u = 0. With T_sky ≈ T_a for opaque surroundings, the balance becomes:

   $$0 = \alpha \tau I_{s} - \varepsilon \sigma T_{stag}^{4} - U_{L}(T_{stag} - T_{a})$$

   For an *ideal* collector with zero conductive/convective loss (U_L = 0):

   $$\alpha \tau I_{s} = \varepsilon \sigma T_{stag}^{4} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; T_{stag} = \left[\dfrac{\alpha I_{s}}{\varepsilon \sigma}\right]^{1/4}$$

3. **Dependence on α/ε (2 marks):** The stagnation temperature scales as the **fourth root of the selectivity ratio**:

   $$\boxed{\;T_{stag} = \left[\dfrac{I_{s}}{\sigma}\cdot\dfrac{\alpha}{\varepsilon}\right]^{1/4}\;}$$

   - Doubling α/ε increases T_stag by a factor of **2^(1/4) ≈ 1.19** (~19 %).
   - Going from black paint (α/ε ≈ 1) to black chrome (α/ε ≈ 10) raises T_stag from ≈ **365 K (92 °C)** to ≈ **650 K (377 °C)** — a *huge* jump.

4. **Numerical Illustration (2 marks):**
   With I_s = 1000 W/m², σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴:
   - For α/ε = 1: T_stag = [1000 / 5.67×10⁻⁸]^(1/4) ≈ **365 K (92 °C)**
   - For α/ε = 10: T_stag = [10 × 1000 / 5.67×10⁻⁸]^(1/4) ≈ **650 K (377 °C)**
   - For α/ε = 25: T_stag = [25 × 1000 / 5.67×10⁻⁸]^(1/4) ≈ **816 K (543 °C)**

> [!NOTE]
> **The α/ε ratio literally determines the upper temperature limit of a solar collector** — a direct, exam-worthy conclusion.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on Selective Solar Coatings:**
>
> 1. **Confusing α (absorptance) with absorptivity (per unit material property).** Use "absorptance" for a surface, "absorptivity" for a material in a textbook sense. KTU answer scripts that mix the two lose ½ mark each time.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the spectral limits.** "Absorptance" must specify the spectrum (e.g., "solar absorptance in 0.3–2.5 µm"). Writing simply "α = 0.95" without a spectrum is incomplete — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Quoting black chrome α/ε values without the operating temperature.** ε is temperature-dependent; always state "ε at 100 °C" or "ε at 80 °C".
>
> 4. **Writing "selective coating = black paint".** Black paint is *not* selective — its ε is high. Use the term "selective" only when α/ε > 5.
>
> 5. **In derivations, skipping the unit conversion of h·c.** The h·c = 1240 eV·nm step is worth 1 full mark. Do *not* skip it.
>
> 6. **Mixing up anode/cathode in electrodeposition.** Cr is deposited at the **cathode** (substrate); the **anode** is inert lead. Many students write it backwards.
>
> 7. **Omitting the W/m²·K⁴ units of σ.** Always state σ with units — KTU mandates it.
>
> 8. **In Drude model derivation, writing `e` (electron charge) without specifying its sign or magnitude in Coulombs.**
>
> 9. **Forgetting to mention the AM 1.5 spectrum when computing α.** α depends on the *incident spectrum* — a blackbody at 5778 K and AM 1.5 give different α values.
>
> 10. **Concluding a DMD question without comparing to black chrome.** KTU 14-mark questions almost always want a *comparative* answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Module-3 Cheat List — Selective Solar Coatings**

- [x] **Selective coating = high α (≥ 0.90) in 0.3–2.5 µm AND low ε (≤ 0.10) in 2.5–50 µm.**
- [x] **Figure of merit = α/ε.** Higher is better. Targets: ≥ 10 (good), ≥ 25 (excellent).
- [x] **α is the *solar* absorptance (AM 1.5 weighted, 0.3–2.5 µm).** **ε is the *thermal* emittance (BB-weighted at operating T, 2.5–50 µm).**
- [x] **Cutoff wavelength formula: λ_c = 1240 / E_g(eV) nm** — derived from hν = E_g.
- [x] **Ideal intrinsic E_g ≈ 0.5 eV** for an absorber operating near 100 °C (λ_c ≈ 2.5 µm).
- [x] **Stagnation temperature: T_stag = [I_s/σ · α/ε]^(1/4)** — fourth-root dependence on selectivity.
- [x] **Five coating classes: Intrinsic, Composite, Multilayer (DMD), Textured, Electrochemically deposited.**
- [x] **Black Chrome:** Cr-oxide cermet, electrodeposited from CrO₃/H₂SO₄ bath at 20–40 A/dm²; α ≈ 0.95, ε ≈ 0.10; thickness ≈ 1.0 µm optimum.
- [x] **Black Nickel:** NiS-ZnS from Watts bath with ZnS particles; α ≈ 0.92, ε ≈ 0.12; cheaper but less stable.
- [x] **Black Cobalt:** Co₃O₄ from CoSO₄ bath; α ≈ 0.94, ε ≈ 0.14; useful up to 300 °C.
- [x] **Anodized Aluminium:** Al₂O₃ grown electrochemically in H₂SO₄; cheap but α/ε only ~5.
- [x] **DMD multilayer:** Al₂O₃ / Mo / Al₂O₃; α ≈ 0.93, ε ≈ 0.05; α/ε up to 20; **highest** thermal stability (500 °C).
- [x] **Drude model gives λ_p of Cu ≈ 150 nm (deep UV)** → Cu is an excellent IR reflector → ideal low-ε substrate.
- [x] **Wien's law at 5778 K (sun) → 0.50 µm; at 373 K (100 °C collector) → 7.8 µm** — explains why the solar/thermal spectra do not overlap.
- [x] **Key constants for KTU numericals:** σ = 5.67×10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴; I_s (AM 1.5) = 1000 W/m²; h = 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s; c = 3×10⁸ m/s; hc = 1240 eV·nm.
- [x] **Hottel's optimum criterion:** dα/dε < 1 — small gain in α is worth a larger loss in ε.
- [x] **Planck's law (BB emissive power):** E_bλ(T) = C₁ / [λ⁵ (exp(C₂/λT) − 1)].
- [x] **Butler–Volmer kinetics apply to electrodeposition rate** of black chrome and black nickel — relevant for 7-mark sub-parts.
- [x] **KTU favourite comparisons:** Black Chrome vs DMD; Selective vs Non-selective; Intrinsic vs Composite absorbers.
- [x] **Real-world application hierarchy:** Domestic water heater → Black Chrome ; Parabolic trough CSP → DMD/GRIN cermet ; Low-cost rural → Anodized Al.
- [x] **Always mention operating temperature when quoting ε** — KTU answer-key penalty otherwise.
- [x] **Mass-production winner: Black Chrome (electrodeposition, low cost, complex shapes).**
- [x] **High-performance winner: DMD / GRIN cermet (α/ε up to 20, stable to 500 °C).**

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Mantra for Module 3 — Selective Solar Coatings:** *"Absorb the sun like a black body, emit the heat like a polished mirror — the smaller the cutoff wavelength, the higher the selectivity, the hotter the absorber."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Primary and Secondary Batteries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT417 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Primary and Secondary Batteries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electrochemical Power Sources — The Big Picture

An **electrochemical cell** is a device that converts chemical free energy into electrical energy (galvanic/voltaic mode) or uses electrical energy to drive a non-spontaneous reaction (electrolytic mode). When one or more such cells are packaged with appropriate containers, separators, and terminals, the assembly is called a **battery** (in engineering parlance, a "battery" may even refer to a single cell).

From the standpoint of the **KTU 2024 syllabus** for Electrochemical Engineering, electrochemical power sources are classified by **reversibility**:

- **Primary Batteries** — Galvanic cells whose electrochemical reaction is **practically irreversible**. Once discharged, they cannot be efficiently recharged by applying external current and must be discarded.
- **Secondary Batteries** — Galvanic cells whose electrochemical reactions are **reversible** (or quasi-reversible). They can be recharged by passing a current opposite to the discharge direction, restoring the original active materials.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — Definition to memorize verbatim**
> *"A primary battery is a non-rechargeable electrochemical cell in which the cell reaction proceeds in only one direction under normal operating conditions, while a secondary (or storage) battery is a rechargeable cell in which the chemical reactions are electrochemically reversible, allowing electrical energy to be stored as chemical energy during charge and released during discharge."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The "Water Tank and Tap" model**
> - A **primary battery** is like a **disposable water bottle with a one-way tap**: the water (chemical energy) can flow out (discharge) but you cannot easily push it back in. Once empty, you throw it away.
> - A **secondary battery** is like a **rechargeable water tank with a pump**: water can flow out under gravity (discharge) or be pumped back in using electricity (charge). The same tank is used thousands of times.

**Another analogy** — financial:
- Primary battery → A **fixed-deposit account** you can withdraw from but cannot refill.
- Secondary battery → A **savings/current account** that can be debited (discharged) and credited (charged) repeatedly.

## 1.3 The Role of Faradays, Thermodynamics, and Kinetics

Every battery, primary or secondary, obeys the same fundamental laws:

1. **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis** govern the **quantity of charge** that can be delivered per unit mass of active material.
2. **Nernst Equation** governs the **thermodynamic equilibrium potential (EMF)** as a function of reactant/product activities.
3. **Gibbs Free Energy** relates the EMF to the **maximum reversible electrical work** obtainable:
   $$\Delta G^{\circ} = -n F E^{\circ}$$
   where **n** is the number of electrons transferred, **F = 96 485 C mol⁻¹** (Faraday constant), and **E°** is the standard cell potential.
4. **Activation, Ohmic, and Concentration Polarization** (overpotentials) reduce the practical terminal voltage below the thermodynamic EMF during operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metrics for batteries (KTU frequently asked)**
> - **EMF / Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV):** Thermodynamic voltage at zero current (Volts).
> - **Capacity (C):** Total charge deliverable, measured in **Ampere-hours (Ah)**.
> - **Specific Capacity:** Capacity per unit mass (Ah kg⁻¹).
> - **Energy Density (Wh kg⁻¹ or Wh L⁻¹):** Energy per unit mass/volume.
> - **Power Density (W kg⁻¹):** Rate of energy delivery.
> - **Cycle Life (N):** Number of charge–discharge cycles before capacity drops to 80% of rated value.
> - **Coulombic Efficiency (η_c):** Ratio of discharge capacity to charge capacity for one cycle.
> - **Self-Discharge Rate (%/month):** Capacity lost when the battery is idle.

## 1.4 Operational Distinction — A Quick Comparison Table

| Feature | Primary Battery | Secondary Battery |
|---|---|---|
| **Rechargeable** | No (single use) | Yes (multiple cycles) |
| **Reaction** | Irreversible | Reversible |
| **Initial cost** | Low (per cell) | High |
| **Operating cost** | High (replacement) | Low (electricity) |
| **Self-discharge** | Very low (years shelf life) | Higher (few %/month) |
| **Internal resistance** | Higher | Lower (better for high current) |
| **Typical use** | Watches, remotes, torches | EVs, laptops, UPS, grid storage |
| **Examples** | Leclanché, Alkaline, Zn–HgO, Li primary | Pb–acid, Ni–Cd, Ni–MH, Li-ion |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electrochemical Basis of a Battery

A battery is a closed thermodynamic system that exchanges **electrons via an external circuit** and **ions via an internal electrolyte**. The three functional regions are:

1. **Anode (negative electrode)** — Site of **oxidation** during discharge. Supplies electrons to the external circuit.
2. **Cathode (positive electrode)** — Site of **reduction** during discharge. Accepts electrons from the external circuit.
3. **Electrolyte** — Ionic conductor (aqueous KOH, H₂SO₄, or non-aqueous Li-salt solutions) that completes the internal circuit.

The two electrodes must be **electronically isolated** (via a separator) but **ionically connected** (via the electrolyte).

## 2.2 The Energy Confinement Principle

During **discharge** of a galvanic cell:
- Free energy is released: $\Delta G < 0$
- A fraction of $\Delta G$ is delivered as electrical work: $W_{elec} = -\Delta G = nFE$
- The remainder appears as heat: $Q = T \Delta S$

During **charge** of a secondary cell, electrical work is stored as chemical free energy:
$$W_{charge} = -\Delta G_{charge} = nF E_{charge}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does a primary battery fail to recharge?**
> The reaction products may be **gaseous (H₂),** physically migrate away from the electrode, form **insoluble/passivating films,** or undergo **side reactions** (e.g., water decomposition). On reversing current, the original reactants cannot be regenerated in their original location — the cell "dies."

## 2.3 Detailed Analysis — Primary Batteries

### 2.3.1 Leclanché Cell (Zinc–Manganese Dioxide Dry Cell)

- **Anode:** Zinc container (also serves as the negative terminal).
- **Cathode:** Carbon (graphite) rod surrounded by a compressed mixture of **MnO₂** + **carbon black** (depolarizer).
- **Electrolyte:** Paste of **NH₄Cl + ZnCl₂** in starch (the "dry" cell — actually moist, not liquid).
- **OCV:** **1.5 V** (drops during discharge due to polarization).
- **Anode reaction (oxidation):**
  $$\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$$
- **Cathode reaction (reduction, simplified):**
  $$2\text{MnO}_2 + 2\text{NH}_4^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Mn}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- **Overall cell reaction:**
  $$\text{Zn} + 2\text{MnO}_2 + 2\text{NH}_4\text{Cl} \rightarrow \text{Zn}(\text{NH}_3)_2\text{Cl}_2 + \text{Mn}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- **Depolarizer role:** MnO₂ reduces to Mn₂O₃, suppressing H₂ evolution that would otherwise polarize the cathode.
- **Drawbacks:** Voltage droop under load; acidic NH₄Cl corrodes the Zn can; **only ~25% of theoretical capacity** is realized.
- **Applications:** Torches, transistor radios, low-drain remote controls.

### 2.3.2 Alkaline Zinc–MnO₂ Cell

- **Anode:** Zinc powder (high surface area) in a gelled KOH electrolyte.
- **Cathode:** Compressed MnO₂ + graphite.
- **Electrolyte:** **KOH (35–52 wt%)** — alkaline, non-acidic (no Zn can corrosion).
- **OCV:** **1.5 V.**
- **Anode:**
  $$\text{Zn} + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{ZnO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^-$$
- **Cathode:**
  $$2\text{MnO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Mn}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{OH}^-$$
- **Overall:**
  $$\text{Zn} + 2\text{MnO}_2 \rightarrow \text{ZnO} + \text{Mn}_2\text{O}_3$$
- **Advantages over Leclanché:** 3–8× higher capacity, longer shelf life, lower internal resistance, can deliver high current, no acidic corrosion.
- **Applications:** Toys, digital cameras, portable electronics.

### 2.3.3 Lithium Primary Batteries

Lithium is the most electropositive practical metal (E° = –3.04 V vs SHE), giving the highest theoretical specific energy. Different cathode chemistries give different voltages:

| Cell | Cathode | Electrolyte | OCV (V) | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Li–MnO₂ | MnO₂ | LiClO₄ in PC/DME | **3.0** | 270 |
| Li–SO₂ | SO₂ (dissolved) | LiBr in AN | **2.8** | 280 |
| Li–SOCl₂ | Thionyl chloride | LiAlCl₄ in SOCl₂ | **3.6** | 590 |
| Li–I₂ | Polyvinylpyridine-I₂ | Solid (LiI) | **2.8** | 220 |

**Generic anode reaction:**
$$\text{Li} \rightarrow \text{Li}^+ + e^-$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Li–I₂ cells** are the power source for **pacemakers** — solid-state design gives 10+ years of reliable operation with very low self-discharge.

## 2.4 Detailed Analysis — Secondary Batteries

### 2.4.1 Lead–Acid Battery (Invented by Gaston Planté, 1859)

- **Anode:** Sponge lead (Pb).
- **Cathode:** Lead dioxide (PbO₂).
- **Electrolyte:** **Aqueous H₂SO₄ (~37% w/w, density 1.28 g cm⁻³).**
- **OCV per cell:** **2.05 V** (six cells in series = **12 V** automotive battery).
- **Anode (discharge, oxidation):**
  $$\text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^-$$
- **Cathode (discharge, reduction):**
  $$\text{PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- **Overall (discharge):**
  $$\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- **Charge:** Reverse of the above. End-of-charge signaled by **gassing** (2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂) at ~2.4 V/cell.
- **Specific Energy:** 30–40 Wh kg⁻¹.
- **Cycle Life:** 200–300 (SLI), 1000+ (VRLA deep cycle).
- **Applications:** Automotive SLI, UPS, traction (forklifts), emergency lighting.
- **Variants:** Flooded (wet), **VRLA** (Valve-Regulated Lead-Acid) — AGM (Absorbed Glass Mat) and gel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sulfation:** If a lead-acid cell is left discharged, large PbSO₄ crystals form and cannot be reconverted → permanent capacity loss. Always store fully charged!

### 2.4.2 Nickel–Cadmium (Ni–Cd) Battery

- **Anode:** Cadmium metal (Cd).
- **Cathode:** Nickel oxyhydroxide (NiOOH).
- **Electrolyte:** **Aqueous KOH (~30%).**
- **OCV per cell:** **1.2 V.**
- **Anode (discharge):**
  $$\text{Cd} + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{Cd(OH)}_2 + 2e^-$$
- **Cathode (discharge):**
  $$2\text{NiOOH} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow 2\text{Ni(OH)}_2 + 2\text{OH}^-$$
- **Overall:**
  $$\text{Cd} + 2\text{NiOOH} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Cd(OH)}_2 + 2\text{Ni(OH)}_2$$
- **Specific Energy:** 40–60 Wh kg⁻¹.
- **Cycle Life:** 1000–2000.
- **Drawback:** **Memory effect** (apparent capacity loss if cycled to same shallow DOD repeatedly), toxic Cd → EU RoHS restricted.

### 2.4.3 Nickel–Metal Hydride (Ni–MH) Battery

- Same cathode (NiOOH) and electrolyte (KOH) as Ni-Cd.
- **Anode:** Hydrogen-absorbing alloy (e.g., LaNi₅, MmNi₅ where Mm = mischmetal).
- **Anode (discharge):**
  $$\text{MH} + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^-$$
- **Cathode (discharge):**
  $$\text{NiOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \rightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^-$$
- **OCV:** **1.2 V.**
- **Specific Energy:** 60–120 Wh kg⁻¹.
- **Cycle Life:** 500–1000.
- **Advantages:** Higher energy density, no toxic Cd, less memory effect.
- **Applications:** Early hybrid vehicles (Toyota Prius Gen 1–3), AA rechargeables.

### 2.4.4 Lithium-Ion (Li-ion) Battery

- **Anode:** Graphite intercalated with Li (LiC₆).
- **Cathode:** LiCoO₂ (LCO), LiFePO₄ (LFP), LiMn₂O₄ (LMO), Li(NiₓMnᵧCo_z)O₂ (NMC).
- **Electrolyte:** **LiPF₆ in EC:DMC (1:1)** — non-aqueous, organic carbonate.
- **Separator:** Polyethylene/polypropylene microporous film.
- **OCV per cell:** **3.6–3.7 V** (depending on cathode).
- **Anode (discharge):**
  $$\text{LiC}_6 \rightarrow \text{C}_6 + \text{Li}^+ + e^-$$
- **Cathode (discharge, LCO example):**
  $$\text{Li}_{1-x}\text{CoO}_2 + x\text{Li}^+ + xe^- \rightarrow \text{LiCoO}_2$$
- **Overall (discharge):**
  $$\text{LiC}_6 + \text{Li}_{1-x}\text{CoO}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_6 + \text{LiCoO}_2 \;\;(\text{with }x\text{ Li}^+ \text{ shuttled})$$
- **Specific Energy:** 150–250 Wh kg⁻¹.
- **Cycle Life:** 500–2000.
- **Applications:** Smartphones, laptops, EVs (Tesla, BYD, Tata), grid storage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU — Common trap:** Li-ion does **not** use Li metal as the anode (that's lithium primary). The Li⁺ ions **shuttle (intercalate/de-intercalate)** between two host structures — hence "rocking-chair" cell.

## 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$ | Gibbs free energy ↔ EMF | J mol⁻¹, V |
| 2 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | Nernst equation | V |
| 3 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0592}{n}\log_{10}Q$ | Nernst at 25 °C | V |
| 4 | $Q = It = nFN$ | Faraday's 1st law (charge) | C, mol |
| 5 | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{nF} = \dfrac{MQ}{nF}$ | Mass deposited/consumed | kg |
| 6 | $C_{th} = \dfrac{nF}{3600 \cdot M}$ | Theoretical specific capacity | Ah kg⁻¹ |
| 7 | $E_{th} = \dfrac{E^{\circ} \cdot nF}{3600 \cdot \sum M}$ | Theoretical specific energy | Wh kg⁻¹ |
| 8 | $E_{th} = 26.8 \cdot \dfrac{nE^{\circ}}{\sum M}$ | Simplified specific energy | Wh kg⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\rho_V = \dfrac{nF \cdot C_m}{3600}$ | Energy density (volumetric) | Wh L⁻¹ |
| 10 | $P_d = V \cdot I$ | Power density | W kg⁻¹ |
| 11 | $\eta_c = \dfrac{C_{discharge}}{C_{charge}} \times 100$ | Coulombic efficiency | % |
| 12 | $V_{term} = E_{oc} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$ | Terminal voltage (load) | V |
| 13 | $SOC = \dfrac{C_{remaining}}{C_{rated}}$ | State of charge | – |
| 14 | $DOD = 1 - SOC$ | Depth of discharge | – |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical for KTU calculations:** In the simplified specific-energy formula, $\sum M$ is the **sum of molecular weights of all active species** that appear in the overall cell reaction. Use atomic weights: Li = 6.94, Mn = 54.94, O = 16.00, Pb = 207.2, S = 32.07, C = 12.01, H = 1.008, etc.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** Selection depends on specific energy (range) vs power density (acceleration) vs cycle life (warranty).
- **Grid Storage:** Cost per kWh-cycle dominates → LFP (LiFePO₄) and Sodium-sulfur preferred over NMC.
- **Aerospace:** High specific energy and reliability → Li–SO₂, Li–SOCl₂, or Li-ion.
- **Medical Implants:** Long shelf life, low self-discharge, safety → Li–I₂.
- **Automotive SLI:** Cost, surge current, cold-cranking amps → Lead-acid remains king after 165 years!
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Specific Capacity of an Electrode Material

**Statement:** The theoretical specific capacity of an active material is the maximum charge deliverable per unit mass, governed solely by Faraday's laws and the molar mass.

**Step 1 — Start with Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis:**

$$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

This says: the mass *m* (kg) of substance reacted equals the molar mass *M* (kg mol⁻¹) times the total charge *Q = I·t* (C), divided by *n* electrons per formula unit times Faraday's constant *F* (C mol⁻¹).

**Step 2 — Recognize that charge is current × time:**

For a given mass *m* = 1 kg, the charge deliverable is:

$$Q = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot m}{M}$$

**Step 3 — Convert charge to capacity in Ampere-hours:**

By definition, $1 \text{ Ah} = 3600 \text{ C}$. Hence:

$$C \;(\text{Ah}) = \frac{Q}{3600} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot m}{3600 \cdot M}$$

**Step 4 — Specific capacity = capacity per unit mass (set m = 1 kg):**

$$\boxed{\,C_{th} = \frac{n \cdot F}{3600 \cdot M} \;\; \text{Ah kg}^{-1}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting F = 96 485 C mol⁻¹:**

$$C_{th} = \frac{n \times 96485}{3600 \times M} = \frac{26.8 \, n}{M} \;\; \text{Ah kg}^{-1}$$

**Engineering insight:** Materials with small molar mass and large *n* maximize specific capacity. **Lithium** (M = 6.94, n = 1) gives 3.86 Ah g⁻¹ — the highest of any practical electrode material.

## 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Specific Energy of a Cell

**Step 1 — Specific energy is the product of voltage and specific capacity:**

$$E_{th} = E_{cell} \times C_{th} = E_{cell} \times \frac{nF}{3600 \, M}$$

**Step 2 — For a *complete* cell, we must use the combined mass of all active species on both sides:**

$$\boxed{\,E_{th} = \frac{nF \cdot E_{cell}}{3600 \cdot \sum M} = \frac{26.8 \cdot n \cdot E_{cell}}{\sum M} \;\; \text{Wh kg}^{-1}\,}$$

**Step 3 — Worked example: Lead–acid cell**

Overall reaction:

$$\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Molar masses (g mol⁻¹): Pb = 207.2, PbO₂ = 239.2, H₂SO₄ = 98.08, PbSO₄ = 303.3, H₂O = 18.02.

Following the reaction, **2 moles of electrons (n = 2)** are transferred per formula event. Sum of reactant molar masses:

$$\sum M = 207.2 + 239.2 + 2(98.08) = 642.56 \;\; \text{g mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute theoretical specific energy:**

$$E_{th} = \frac{26.8 \times 2 \times 2.05}{642.56} = \frac{109.88}{642.56} \approx 0.171 \;\; \text{Wh g}^{-1}$$

$$E_{th} = 171 \;\; \text{Wh kg}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with practical value:** Commercial lead-acid delivers **30–40 Wh kg⁻¹**, i.e., only **~20% of theoretical**, due to:
- Inactive mass (current collectors, casing, electrolyte water).
- Voltage drop under load.
- Sulfation, gassing, internal resistance losses.

## 3.3 Derivation: Nernst Equation (from Thermodynamics)

**Step 1 — Gibbs free energy change for a general cell reaction:**

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$:**

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the cell potential E:**

$$\boxed{\,E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q\,}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to base-10 logarithm at 25 °C (T = 298 K):**

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} Q \;\; \text{at } 25 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Worked example — Daniell cell at non-standard conditions:**

$$\text{Zn} \vert \text{Zn}^{2+}(0.01 M) \Vert \text{Cu}^{2+}(1.0 M) \vert \text{Cu}$$

$$E^{\circ} = E^{\circ}_{Cu} - E^{\circ}_{Zn} = 0.34 - (-0.76) = 1.10 \;\; \text{V}$$

$$Q = \frac{[\text{Zn}^{2+}]}{[\text{Cu}^{2+}]} = \frac{0.01}{1.0} = 0.01$$

$$E = 1.10 - \frac{0.0592}{2} \log(0.01) = 1.10 - 0.0296 \times (-2) = 1.10 + 0.0592 = 1.1592 \;\; \text{V}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Theoretical Capacity of a Lithium-Metal Half-Cell

**Step 1 — Half-reaction:**

$$\text{Li} \rightarrow \text{Li}^+ + e^-$$

**Step 2 — For n = 1, M = 6.94 g mol⁻¹:**

$$C_{th} = \frac{1 \times 96485}{3600 \times 6.94} = \frac{96485}{24984} = 3.862 \;\; \text{Ah g}^{-1} = 3862 \;\; \text{Ah kg}^{-1}$$

This is the **theoretical ceiling** of any electrode using elemental Li. No other practical material comes close — explaining the dominance of Li chemistries.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Battery Comparison (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Leclanché Dry Cell | Alkaline MnO₂ | Lead-Acid (Pb) | Ni-Cd | Ni-MH | Li-ion (LCO) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **OCV (V)** | 1.5 | 1.5 | 2.05 | 1.2 | 1.2 | 3.7 |
| **Specific Energy (Wh kg⁻¹)** | 65 | 90 | 35 | 50 | 80 | 180 |
| **Cycle Life** | Single use | Single use | 300 | 1500 | 800 | 1000 |
| **Self-Discharge (%/mo)** | ~4 | ~2 | 4–6 | 15 | 25 | 2–5 |
| **Memory Effect** | None | None | None | Yes | Slight | None |
| **Toxicity** | Low (Mn) | Low | High (Pb) | High (Cd) | Low | Low |
| **Cost (per Wh)** | Lowest | Low | Lowest secondary | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Operating Temp (°C)** | –5 to 55 | –20 to 55 | –40 to 60 | –40 to 60 | –20 to 50 | –20 to 60 |
| **Key Use** | Torches | Cameras | Cars/UPS | Tools, aviation | Toys, early EVs | Phones, EVs |

## 3.6 Coulombic & Energy Efficiency Derivations

**Coulombic Efficiency (η_c):**
$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_{discharge}}{Q_{charge}} \times 100 = \frac{\int I_{d} \, dt}{\int I_{c} \, dt} \times 100$$

**Energy Efficiency (η_E):**
$$\eta_E = \eta_c \times \frac{\bar{V}_{discharge}}{\bar{V}_{charge}} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why η_E < η_c always:** The average charge voltage is always higher than the average discharge voltage (due to overpotentials during charge), so the energy returned is always less than the energy input. Typical lead-acid: η_c ≈ 90%, η_E ≈ 75%.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 (Model)]**
*Distinguish between primary and secondary batteries. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer:**

A **primary battery** is one in which the cell reaction is **not reversible** by the application of external current. The active materials are consumed irreversibly during discharge, and the battery must be discarded after a single use. Examples: **Leclanché dry cell, alkaline Zn–MnO₂ cell, lithium primary cell (Li–MnO₂).**

A **secondary (storage) battery** is one in which the cell reaction is **electrochemically reversible**, allowing the battery to be recharged by passing a current opposite to the discharge current, thereby regenerating the original active materials. Examples: **Lead–acid battery, nickel–cadmium (Ni–Cd) battery, lithium-ion battery.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key differences (1 mark):** rechargeability, reversibility of reaction, total cost over lifetime, internal resistance, self-discharge rate.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*Write the cell reaction of a lead–acid battery during discharge. What is the OCV per cell?*

**Model Answer:**

**OCV per cell = 2.05 V** (a 12 V automotive battery has 6 cells in series).

**Anode (oxidation):**
$$\text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^-$$

**Cathode (reduction):**
$$\text{PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Overall (discharge):**
$$\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

---

## 5.2 KTU Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of the **Leclanché dry cell**. Write the cell reaction. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the construction and working of the **alkaline Zn–MnO₂ cell**. Compare its performance with the Leclanché cell. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q A(a):

**Construction (3 marks):**
The Leclanché dry cell is a cylindrical cell comprising:
- A **zinc can** that serves as both the **anode (–)** and the container.
- A **central carbon (graphite) rod** acting as the **cathode (+)** current collector.
- A **depolarizer mix** of MnO₂ and carbon black packed around the carbon rod.
- An **electrolyte paste** of NH₄Cl + ZnCl₂ in starch/flour (the "dry" aspect — actually a moist paste, not liquid).
- A **porous separator** (paper or fabric) preventing direct contact between the mix and the zinc can.
- A **sealing bitumen** at the top with a vent to release H₂.

**Working (2 marks):**
On connecting to an external load, Zn is oxidized at the anode, releasing electrons that flow through the load to the carbon rod. At the cathode, MnO₂ is reduced in the presence of NH₄⁺, suppressing H₂ evolution (the role of the *depolarizer*).

**Cell Reaction (1 mark):**
$$\text{Zn} + 2\text{MnO}_2 + 2\text{NH}_4\text{Cl} \rightarrow \text{Zn}(\text{NH}_3)_2\text{Cl}_2 + \text{Mn}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Advantages (0.5):** Cheap, simple construction, readily available, adequate for low-drain intermittent use.

**Limitations (0.5):**
- Voltage drops sharply under continuous load due to NH₃ accumulation and polarization.
- Zinc can corrodes from acidic NH₄Cl — shelf life is limited (~1–2 years).
- Only ~25% of theoretical capacity utilized.
- Cannot be recharged — primary cell.

---

#### Model Solution to Q A(b):

**Construction of Alkaline Zn–MnO₂ (2 marks):**
- **Anode:** Finely divided **zinc powder** (high surface area) dispersed in a **gelled KOH electrolyte**.
- **Cathode:** **MnO₂ powder** mixed with graphite, pressed into a cylindrical pellet.
- **Electrolyte:** **Aqueous KOH (35–52 wt%)** — strongly alkaline, non-corrosive to Zn.
- **Separator:** Non-woven fabric or cellophane.
- **Current collector at anode:** Brass pin (Zn powder has poor electronic conductivity).
- **Sealed steel can** — Zn is no longer the container, eliminating corrosion issues.

**Working (2 marks):**
During discharge, Zn is oxidized to ZnO in the alkaline medium; MnO₂ is reduced to Mn₂O₃ / MnOOH. KOH provides OH⁻ ionic conduction. The alkaline medium does not attack zinc and the cell maintains a flat voltage profile.

**Cell Reactions (1 mark):**
- Anode: $\text{Zn} + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{ZnO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^-$
- Cathode: $2\text{MnO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Mn}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{OH}^-$
- Overall: $\text{Zn} + 2\text{MnO}_2 \rightarrow \text{ZnO} + \text{Mn}_2\text{O}_3$

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Leclanché | Alkaline |
|---|---|---|
| Electrolyte | NH₄Cl (acidic) | KOH (alkaline) |
| Capacity | Low (40 mAh typical AA) | High (~3–8×) |
| Voltage droop | Severe | Flatter |
| Shelf life | 1–2 years | 5–7 years |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| High-drain use | Poor | Good |

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** Describe the construction, working, and cell reactions of the **lead–acid battery** during discharge and charge. Mention its specific energy and applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of the **nickel–cadmium (Ni–Cd) battery** with cell reactions. What is "memory effect"? Mention any two advantages of Ni–MH over Ni–Cd. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q B(a):

**Construction (2 marks):**
- **Anode:** Sponge lead (Pb) grid.
- **Cathode:** Lead dioxide (PbO₂) coated on a lead–antimony alloy grid.
- **Electrolyte:** ~37% w/w aqueous H₂SO₄ (density 1.28 g cm⁻³ at full charge; drops during discharge as water is formed).
- **Separator:** Microporous polyethylene / rubber / glass mat (AGM in VRLA).
- **Container:** Polypropylene (in modern cells).
- **Six cells** in series give a nominal **12 V** automotive battery.
- In **VRLA**, electrolyte is either absorbed in AGM or gelled with SiO₂ — cell is sealed with a pressure-relief valve.

**Working — Discharge (2 marks):**
On connecting to a load, Pb is oxidized at the anode and PbO₂ is reduced at the cathode. Both electrodes are converted to **PbSO₄** (a poor conductor that passivates the electrodes). H₂SO₄ is consumed and water is produced, so the **electrolyte density drops** — used as a state-of-charge indicator.

**Discharge Reactions (1 mark):**
- Anode: $\text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^-$
- Cathode: $\text{PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- Overall: $\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$

**Charge Process (1 mark):**
An external DC source (~2.4 V/cell) drives the reactions in reverse. PbSO₄ at the anode is reduced back to Pb; at the cathode, PbSO₄ is oxidized to PbO₂. End of charge is signaled by gassing: $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2 \uparrow + \text{O}_2 \uparrow$.

**Specific Energy, Applications (1 mark):**
- Specific Energy: 30–40 Wh kg⁻¹.
- Applications: Automotive SLI (starting, lighting, ignition), UPS, traction (forklifts, golf carts), emergency lighting, telecom backup.

---

#### Model Solution to Q B(b):

**Ni–Cd Construction (1.5 marks):**
- **Anode:** Cadmium metal powder mixed with iron (conductivity enhancer) and binder, pasted on a nickel-plated steel substrate.
- **Cathode:** Nickel hydroxide Ni(OH)₂ mixed with graphite, pasted on a nickel-plated substrate.
- **Electrolyte:** ~30% aqueous KOH (low solubility of Ni(OH)₂).
- **Separator:** Non-woven nylon or polyolefin.
- **Cell design:** Cylindrical (AA, C, D) or prismatic; vented or sealed.

**Working & Cell Reactions (1.5 marks):**
- Anode (discharge): $\text{Cd} + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{Cd(OH)}_2 + 2e^-$
- Cathode (discharge): $2\text{NiOOH} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow 2\text{Ni(OH)}_2 + 2\text{OH}^-$
- Overall: $\text{Cd} + 2\text{NiOOH} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Cd(OH)}_2 + 2\text{Ni(OH)}_2$

On charge, the reactions are reversed. KOH is **not consumed** — only acting as the ionic conductor — so its concentration and the cell OCV (1.2 V) remain constant throughout the cycle.

**Memory Effect (2 marks):**
The "memory effect" is the phenomenon in which a Ni–Cd battery that is repeatedly discharged to the same shallow depth of discharge and then recharged **appears to "remember" this shallow capacity**. On later deep discharge, the voltage suddenly drops prematurely at the memorized shallow DOD level, and the unused capacity is unavailable until the cell is fully cycled (deep discharge → full recharge). It is attributed to the formation of stable Ni(OH)₂ crystallographic phases that are hard to reduce.

**Mitigation:** Periodically perform a full deep discharge before recharge; use pulse-charging schemes; avoid repeated shallow cycling.

**Advantages of Ni–MH over Ni–Cd (2 marks):**
1. **No toxic cadmium** — Ni–MH is environmentally friendlier, easier to recycle, and not restricted under EU RoHS directives.
2. **Higher specific energy** — 60–120 Wh kg⁻¹ vs 40–60 Wh kg⁻¹ for Ni–Cd, due to the lightweight H-absorbing alloy anode (M_H) replacing dense Cd.
3. **Reduced memory effect** — Ni–MH exhibits only a very mild memory effect.
4. **Higher volumetric energy density** — important for compact electronics and hybrid vehicles.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> **Where students lose marks on this module:**
> 1. **Confusing cell reaction direction** — Always check whether the cell is in **discharge (galvanic)** or **charge (electrolytic)** mode. The half-reactions reverse between modes.
> 2. **Forgetting electrolyte mass** in specific energy calculations — Use the **sum of all active species' molar masses**, not just one electrode.
> 3. **Writing "Li" metal in Li-ion batteries** — This is a common error. The Li-ion cell uses **Li⁺ shuttling between two intercalation hosts**; no metallic lithium is present.
> 4. **Mixing up anode/cathode** — Remember: **anode = oxidation, cathode = reduction during discharge.** In an electrolytic cell the convention swaps for the external supply.
> 5. **Omitting depolarizer role** in Leclanché — MnO₂ is the **depolarizer** (consumes H₂ that would otherwise polarize the cell), and the NH₄Cl is the **electrolyte**.
> 6. **Not specifying n in the Nernst equation** — Always divide RT by **nF**; for the Daniell cell n = 2, for Li primary n = 1.
> 7. **Writing "Pb" in PbO₂** — In the lead-acid cell, both electrodes contain Pb but in different oxidation states (+2 in PbSO₄, 0 in Pb sponge, +4 in PbO₂). The state of oxidation is what matters.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Primary and Secondary Batteries**

**A. Foundational Concepts**
- ☐ EMF, OCV, terminal voltage, polarization (activation, ohmic, concentration).
- ☐ Faraday's laws: $m = \frac{MQ}{nF}$, $C_{th} = \frac{26.8 n}{M}$ Ah kg⁻¹.
- ☐ Nernst equation: $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n}\log Q$ (at 25 °C).
- ☐ Specific energy: $E_{th} = \frac{26.8 \, n E^{\circ}}{\sum M}$ Wh kg⁻¹.
- ☐ Capacity (Ah), energy (Wh), power (W), cycle life, coulombic efficiency.

**B. Primary Batteries (Non-Rechargeable)**
- ☐ **Leclanché Dry Cell** — Zn/NH₄Cl+ZnCl₂/MnO₂, OCV 1.5 V, single use.
- ☐ **Alkaline Zn–MnO₂** — KOH electrolyte, 3–8× higher capacity, longer shelf.
- ☐ **Lithium Primary** — Li–MnO₂ (3.0 V), Li–SO₂ (2.8 V), Li–SOCl₂ (3.6 V, 590 Wh kg⁻¹).
- ☐ **Li–I₂** — Solid-state, used in cardiac pacemakers.
- ☐ **Zinc–Air** — Uses atmospheric O₂, hearing aids.

**C. Secondary Batteries (Rechargeable)**
- ☐ **Lead–Acid** — Pb/PbO₂/H₂SO₄, OCV 2.05 V, 30–40 Wh kg⁻¹, SLI cars.
- ☐ **Ni–Cd** — Cd/NiOOH/KOH, OCV 1.2 V, memory effect, toxic Cd.
- ☐ **Ni–MH** — MH/NiOOH/KOH, OCV 1.2 V, higher energy, less memory.
- ☐ **Li-ion** — Graphite/LiMO₂/LiPF₆ in organic carbonate, OCV 3.7 V, 150–250 Wh kg⁻¹.
- ☐ **Na–S** — Molten Na/S at 300 °C, used in grid storage.

**D. Critical Numerical Skills**
- ☐ Compute theoretical specific energy given a cell reaction.
- ☐ Apply Nernst equation to compute EMF at non-standard activities.
- ☐ Convert Faraday-based mass to capacity in Ah.
- ☐ Compare specific energy of two chemistries to rank them.

**E. Common Pitfalls to Avoid**
- ☐ Do not use **Li metal** in Li-ion reactions.
- ☐ Do not forget the **electrolyte** in cell reactions (KOH, H₂SO₄, etc.).
- ☐ Do not confuse **anode/cathode** between galvanic and electrolytic modes.
- ☐ Always state **n** (electrons transferred) in Nernst calculations.

**F. KTU-Weight Topics (Repeated in Past Papers)**
- ☐ Lead-acid battery: cell reaction, specific energy, applications.
- ☐ Ni–Cd battery: cell reaction, memory effect, Ni–MH comparison.
- ☐ Li-ion: rocking-chair mechanism, anode/cathode materials, advantages.
- ☐ Alkaline vs Leclanché: comparison and cell reactions.
- ☐ Derivation of specific capacity / specific energy from Faraday's law.

---

**End of Module 3 — Primary and Secondary Batteries Notes**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT417 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Batteries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Electrochemical Battery?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **battery** is one or more electrochemical cells electrically connected to produce a controlled direct current (DC) through the spontaneous conversion of stored chemical energy into electrical energy. Each cell consists of two dissimilar electrodes (anode = oxidation site, cathode = reduction site) immersed in a suitable electrolyte, separated physically by an ion-conducting but electronically insulating separator.

The cell voltage $E_{cell}$ is governed by the **Nernst Equation**:

$$E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

where $E^{\circ}_{cell}$ is the standard cell potential, $R$ is the universal gas constant (**8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**), $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin, $n$ is the number of electrons transferred, $F$ is Faraday's constant (**96,485 C mol⁻¹**), and $Q$ is the reaction quotient.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Tower of Chemistry"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine two water tanks connected by a pipe. One tank sits higher than the other. When a tap is opened, water flows from the higher tank to the lower tank — the gravitational potential energy converts to kinetic energy (and useful work at a turbine).
> In an **electrochemical battery**, the two tanks are the **anode** (higher chemical potential — "negative terminal") and the **cathode** (lower chemical potential — "positive terminal"). The pipe is the external circuit carrying electrons. The "water" is the ionic current flowing through the electrolyte.
> When the levels equalize, the battery is **discharged**. A *rechargeable* battery uses an external pump (DC power supply) to push water back uphill.

---

### 1.3 Broad Classification of Batteries

Electrochemical batteries are classified based on several criteria:

**A. By Rechargeability**
1. **Primary Batteries** — single-use, non-rechargeable; once discharged, cannot be reversed electrically (e.g., Leclanché dry cell, alkaline MnO₂ cell, zinc–air button cell, lithium primary cells).
2. **Secondary Batteries** — rechargeable; electrochemical reactions are reversible by applying external DC current (e.g., Lead–acid, Ni–Cd, Ni–MH, Li-ion, Li-polymer, Sodium–sulfur).

**B. By Electrolyte State**
1. **Wet cells** — liquid electrolyte (flooded lead–acid, lithium-ion with liquid organic electrolyte).
2. **Dry cells** — immobilized electrolyte (gel, paste, polymer matrix).

**C. By Operating Temperature**
1. **Ambient-temperature cells** — Lead–acid, Li-ion, Ni–Cd.
2. **High-temperature cells** — Na–S (≈ 300–350 °C), Li–FeS₂ (thermal battery, ≈ 400–500 °C).

**D. By Construction / Application**
1. **Reserve batteries** — electrolyte stored separately until activation (e.g., Mg–AgCl sea-water activated battery for torpedoes, thermal batteries).
2. **Flow batteries** — electrolyte is circulated from external tanks (e.g., Vanadium Redox Flow Battery — VRFB, Zn–Br).
3. **Fuel cells** — reactants supplied externally, not "stored" inside (H₂–O₂ PEMFC, SOFC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 3):**
> The student must be able to **differentiate primary, secondary, reserve, and fuel-cell-type batteries**, identify their half-cell reactions, calculate theoretical specific energy using Faraday's law, and discuss engineering applications (automotive, portable electronics, space, military, grid storage).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Primary Batteries (Non-Rechargeable)

#### 2.1.1 Leclanché Dry Cell (Zn–MnO₂, Acidic NH₄Cl)
- **Anode:** Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻
- **Cathode:** 2MnO₂ + 2NH₄⁺ + 2e⁻ → Mn₂O₃ + 2NH₃ + H₂O
- **Cell voltage:** ≈ **1.5 V** nominal; falls with discharge.
- **Limitations:** Polarization due to ammonia gas accumulation, internal short life, low energy density (≈ **55 Wh/kg**).

#### 2.1.2 Alkaline Manganese Dioxide Cell (Zn–MnO₂, KOH)
- **Anode:** Zn + 2OH⁻ → ZnO + H₂O + 2e⁻
- **Cathode:** 2MnO₂ + H₂O + 2e⁻ → Mn₂O₃ + 2OH⁻
- **Voltage:** **1.55 V**; higher capacity, longer shelf life (≈ **110 Wh/kg**), superior low-temperature performance over Leclanché.

#### 2.1.3 Zinc–Mercuric Oxide Cell (Zn–HgO "Button Cell")
- **Anode:** Zn + 2OH⁻ → ZnO + H₂O + 2e⁻
- **Cathode:** HgO + H₂O + 2e⁻ → Hg + 2OH⁻
- **Voltage:** stable **1.35 V** flat discharge curve; excellent for hearing aids, watches, military.
- *Disadvantage:* Mercury toxicity.

#### 2.1.4 Zinc–Silver Oxide Cell (Zn–Ag₂O)
- **Anode:** Zn + 2OH⁻ → Zn(OH)₂ + 2e⁻
- **Cathode:** Ag₂O + H₂O + 2e⁻ → 2Ag + 2OH⁻
- **Voltage:** **1.55–1.6 V**; high specific energy ≈ **130 Wh/kg**; used in watches, photo-flash, space.

#### 2.1.5 Lithium Primary Cells (Lithium–MnO₂, Lithium–Thionyl Chloride Li–SOCl₂)
- Lithium's strongly negative standard potential (Li⁺/Li = **−3.04 V vs SHE**) gives very high cell voltages.
- **Li–MnO₂:** Voltage **3.0 V**, energy density **270 Wh/kg**, used in cameras, watches.
- **Li–SOCl₂:** Voltage **3.6 V**, energy density **500–700 Wh/kg**, used in military, utility metering, memory backup (Bobbin construction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Lithium primary cells dominate applications demanding long shelf life (10–20 years), wide temperature range (−55 °C to +85 °C), and high energy density — meter readers, pacemakers, emergency locator beacons.

---

### 2.2 Secondary Batteries (Rechargeable)

#### 2.2.1 Lead–Acid Battery (SLA / VRLA)
- **Anode (discharge):** Pb + SO₄²⁻ → PbSO₄ + 2e⁻
- **Cathode (discharge):** PbO₂ + SO₄²⁻ + 4H⁺ + 2e⁻ → PbSO₄ + 2H₂O
- **Overall:** Pb + PbO₂ + 2H₂SO₄ → 2PbSO₄ + 2H₂O
- **Nominal cell voltage:** **2.0 V** (battery = 6 cells = 12 V).
- **Specific energy:** **30–40 Wh/kg** (low), but **cheap, robust, recyclable**.
- **Construction variants:** Flooded (wet), AGM (Absorbent Glass Mat) — VRLA, Gel-cell — VRLA.
- **Applications:** Automotive SLI (Starting, Lighting, Ignition), UPS, traction (forklifts), standby power for telecom, solar storage.

#### 2.2.2 Nickel–Cadmium (Ni–Cd)
- **Anode:** Cd + 2OH⁻ → Cd(OH)₂ + 2e⁻
- **Cathode:** 2NiO(OH) + 2H₂O + 2e⁻ → 2Ni(OH)₂ + 2OH⁻
- **Cell voltage:** **1.2 V**; energy density **40–60 Wh/kg**; **cycle life > 1000 cycles**.
- **Engineering caveats:** Suffer from **memory effect** (voltage depression if repeatedly shallow-discharged), toxicity of Cd.

#### 2.2.3 Nickel–Metal Hydride (Ni–MH)
- **Anode:** MH + OH⁻ → M + H₂O + e⁻ (M = intermetallic alloy — e.g., LaNi₅, MmNi₅ where Mm = Misch metal)
- **Cathode:** NiO(OH) + H₂O + e⁻ → Ni(OH)₂ + OH⁻
- **Cell voltage:** **1.2 V**; energy density **60–120 Wh/kg**; less memory effect than Ni–Cd; used in hybrid vehicles (Toyota Prius first-generation), AA rechargeables.

#### 2.2.4 Lithium-Ion (Li-ion)
- **Anode (intercalation):** LiₓC₆ ↔ 6C + xLi⁺ + xe⁻
- **Cathode (intercalation):** Li₁₋ₓMO₂ + xLi⁺ + xe⁻ ↔ LiMO₂ (M = Co, Ni, Mn, Fe, etc.)
- **Electrolyte:** LiPF₆ in EC/DMC organic carbonates; separator = polyethylene / polypropylene.
- **Cell voltage:** **3.6–3.7 V** (≈ 3× Ni–Cd); energy density **150–250 Wh/kg**; cycle life 500–2000.
- **Cathode chemistries:** LCO (LiCoO₂), NMC (LiNi₁/₃Mn₁/₃Co₁/₃O₂), NCA, LFP (LiFePO₄ — **1.5× safer, longer life**, 3.3 V), LMO (LiMn₂O₄).
- **Anode chemistries:** Graphite (standard), Si-graphite composites, Li-metal (next-gen).

#### 2.2.5 Sodium–Sulfur (Na–S)
- **Cell:** Na (l) | β-alumina solid electrolyte | S (l) + carbon felt
- **Anode:** 2Na → 2Na⁺ + 2e⁻
- **Cathode:** S + 2e⁻ → S²⁻ (further polysulfides Na₂Sₓ)
- **OCV:** **2.0 V** at 350 °C; energy density **200 Wh/kg**; cycle life ≈ **2500**; used for **grid storage** (NGK Insulators, Japan — 50 MW-class installations).

#### 2.2.6 Lithium–Iron Disulfide (Li–FeS₂, Thermal Battery)
- Activated by pyrotechnic heat source; molten salt electrolyte (LiCl–KCl eutectic, m.p. **352 °C**).
- Voltage **1.6 V**, very high power density; activated life 5–60 min; used in missiles, ejection seats.

---

### 2.3 Reserve Batteries
Stored in dry, inert state until *activated* by:
- (i) addition of electrolyte (e.g., sea-water activated Mg–AgCl for torpedoes, sonobuoys);
- (ii) application of heat (thermal batteries — Li–FeS₂, Li–CoS₂);
- (iii) gas generation (ammonia activated).
**Advantage:** Shelf life of **10–25 years** with zero self-discharge.

---

### 2.4 Flow Batteries
Active material dissolved in liquid electrolyte stored in external tanks.

**Vanadium Redox Flow Battery (VRFB):**
- Positive side: V⁴⁺ ⇌ V⁵⁺ + e⁻ (VO²⁺ ⇌ VO₂⁺ in H₂SO₄)
- Negative side: V³⁺ + e⁻ ⇌ V²⁺
- **OCV:** **1.4 V**; battery stack voltage built by series cells (typically 30–80 cells).
- **Energy rating** decoupled from **power rating** → ideal for **large-scale grid storage**.
- **Efficiency:** 70–85% round-trip.
- **Applications:** 200 MWh installations in China, California (e.g., Sumitomo Dalian, Rongke Power).

**Zinc–Bromine Flow Battery:**
- Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ ⇌ Zn(s) (deposit/strip on carbon electrode)
- 2Br⁻ ⇌ Br₂ + 2e⁻
- Cell voltage **1.8 V**; energy density 60–70 Wh/kg electrolyte.

---

### 2.5 Fuel Cells (Conceptually Distinct, but Studied under Batteries)
A **fuel cell** is a *non-classical* electrochemical converter where reactants (fuel and oxidant) are supplied **continuously from outside**; products are removed continuously.
- **PEMFC:** H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O, OCV **1.23 V**, 50–60% efficiency.
- **SOFC:** OCV 0.8–1.0 V at 800 °C, 50–60% electrical + 25–35% CHP → **85% total**.
- **AFC:** Used in space (Apollo, Space Shuttle); concentrated KOH electrolyte.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The distinction: a battery has **finite** stored energy (capacity in Ah). A fuel cell has **theoretically infinite** stored energy as long as fuel is supplied — defined by **power density (W/cm²)** rather than Wh.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Battery Parameters

| Quantity | Symbol | Equation | Units |
|----------|--------|----------|-------|
| Theoretical Specific Capacity | $Q_{sp}$ | $Q_{sp} = \dfrac{nF}{M_w}$ | Ah/kg |
| Theoretical Specific Energy | $W_{sp}$ | $W_{sp} = \dfrac{nF\,E^{\circ}}{M_w}$ | Wh/kg |
| Practical Specific Energy | $W_{p}$ | $W_{p} = \eta_{\text{voltage}} \cdot \eta_{\text{coulombic}} \cdot W_{sp}$ | Wh/kg |
| Energy Density (Volumetric) | $E_v$ | $E_v = Q_{sp} \cdot E^{\circ} \cdot \rho$ | Wh/L |
| Cell Capacity | $C$ | $C = I \cdot t$ | Ah |
| Faradaic (Coulombic) Efficiency | $\eta_F$ | $\eta_F = \dfrac{C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | % |
| Voltage Efficiency | $\eta_V$ | $\eta_V = \dfrac{E_{\text{avg,discharge}}}{E_{\text{avg,charge}}}$ | % |
| Energy Efficiency | $\eta_E$ | $\eta_E = \eta_F \times \eta_V$ | % |
| Nernst Equation | $E$ | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | V |
| Self-Discharge Rate | $r_{sd}$ | $r_{sd} = \dfrac{C_{\text{lost}}}{\Delta t \cdot C_{\text{rated}}} \times 100$ | %/month |
| Power Density | $P_{sp}$ | $P_{sp} = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{m}$ | W/kg |
| C-rate | $C$ | $I = C_{\text{rated}} \cdot C\text{-rate}$ | A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always use **theoretical specific energy** in derivations because the practical value depends on engineering design (porosity, packaging, connectors). For numericals, if not given, take Faraday's constant $F = 96485$ C/mol and assume 25 °C so $RT/F = 0.0257$ V.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Preferred Battery | Why |
|-------------|-------------------|-----|
| Smartphones / Laptops | Li-ion (NMC / LFP) | High energy density, no memory effect |
| EV (Tesla, Tata, Hyundai) | Li-ion (NCA, NMC) | Energy + power |
| Two-wheeler (e.g., Ather, Ola) | Li-ion + LFP | Cycle life, cost |
| Hybrid (Toyota) | Ni–MH (older), Li-ion (newer) | Power density |
| UPS / Inverter / Car SLI | Lead–acid (VRLA / AGM) | Cost, surge current, recyclability |
| Grid storage (MW–MWh) | VRFB / Na–S / Li-ion | Decoupled energy/power, longevity |
| Satellites / Space | Ni–Cd, Ni–H₂, Li-ion | Wide temp tolerance, no outgassing |
| Torpedoes / Military | Reserve (Mg–AgCl, Li–thermal) | Long shelf life, instant activation |
| Watches / Hearing Aids | Zn–Ag₂O, Zn–air, Li–MnO₂ | Coin format, low drain |
| Implantable medical | Li–I₂ | 10+ year primary |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Specific Capacity from Faraday's Law

**Statement:** The capacity of a cell is determined by the number of moles of active material and the stoichiometric number of electrons transferred per mole.

**Starting point — Faraday's First Law:**

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_w}{n \cdot F}$$

where $m$ = mass of active material consumed (g), $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M_w$ = molecular weight of active material (g/mol), $n$ = electrons transferred per molecule, $F$ = Faraday constant.

**Step 1.** Specific capacity is defined as charge per unit mass:

$$Q_{sp} = \frac{I \cdot t}{m}$$

**Step 2.** Substitute $m$ from Faraday's law:

$$Q_{sp} = \frac{I \cdot t}{\dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M_w}{n \cdot F}} = \frac{n \cdot F}{M_w}$$

**Step 3.** Convert from C/kg to Ah/kg by dividing by 3600 s/h:

$$Q_{sp} = \frac{n \cdot F}{3600 \cdot M_w} \;\; \text{(Ah/kg)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> $F/3600 = 26.802$ Ah/mol — this is the **Faraday equivalent of charge** in Ah. So $Q_{sp} = 26.802 \cdot n/M_w$ Ah/kg.

**Numerical verification (Pb in lead–acid, n = 2):**

$$Q_{sp} = \frac{2 \times 26.802}{207.2} = \frac{53.604}{207.2} = 0.2587 \text{ Ah/g} = 258.7 \text{ Ah/kg}$$

This is **theoretical**. Practical capacity of lead–acid anode is ≈ 120 Ah/kg (≈ 46% utilization).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Specific Energy

The specific energy is energy per unit mass:

$$W_{sp} = Q_{sp} \cdot E^{\circ} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}}{3600 \cdot M_w} \;\; (\text{Wh/kg})$$

**Numerical — Li/MnO₂ primary cell:**

Active masses:
- Li anode: $M_{Li} = 6.94$ g/mol, n = 1 per Li
- MnO₂ cathode: $M_{MnO_2} = 86.94$ g/mol, n = 1 per Mn

Assume **1 mol of each** reacts in cell. Total active mass = $6.94 + 86.94 = 93.88$ g.
Number of electrons transferred per **total cell reaction** = $n = 1$.
Standard cell EMF: $E^{\circ} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode} = (+0.95) - (-3.04) = 3.99$ V, but real OCV is **3.0 V**.

$$W_{sp} = \frac{1 \times 96485 \times 3.0}{3600 \times 93.88}$$

**Step-by-step arithmetic:**

$$W_{sp} = \frac{96485 \times 3.0}{3600 \times 93.88}$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{96485 \times 3.0}{3600} = \frac{289455}{3600} = 80.404 \text{ Wh}$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{80.404}{93.88} = 0.8565 \text{ Wh/g} = 856.5 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

**Observed (practical):** 270 Wh/kg → **utilization factor** = 270/856.5 ≈ **31.5 %**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lead-Acid Theoretical Specific Energy (Full Cell)

**Reaction:**
$$\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\,\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}, \quad n = 2$$

Active masses (per **2 mol** of electrons):
- Pb: 207.2 g
- PbO₂: 239.2 g
- 2 H₂SO₄: 2 × 98 = 196 g (sometimes excluded as electrolyte)
- Total: $207.2 + 239.2 + 196 = 642.4$ g (or 446.4 g without electrolyte).

OCV: $E^{\circ} = 2.05$ V (nominally 2.0 V).

**With all masses:**

$$W_{sp} = \frac{2 \times 96485 \times 2.05}{3600 \times 642.4} = \frac{395{,}588}{2{,}312{,}640} = 0.1711 \text{ Wh/g} = 171.1 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

**Excluding electrolyte (engineering convention):**

$$W_{sp} = \frac{395{,}588}{3600 \times 446.4} = \frac{395{,}588}{1{,}607{,}040} = 0.2462 \text{ Wh/g} = 246.2 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

**Practical lead–acid:** ≈ 35 Wh/kg → utilization ≈ **14–20 %**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Round-Trip Energy Efficiency

Consider a Ni–MH cell cycled at the **1C rate**, with measured average charge voltage $E_c = 1.40$ V and average discharge voltage $E_d = 1.20$ V. Coulombic efficiency $\eta_F = 0.98$ (98%).

$$\eta_E = \eta_F \times \eta_V = 0.98 \times \frac{1.20}{1.40} = 0.98 \times 0.857 = 0.840$$

$$\boxed{\eta_E = 84.0\%}$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Self-Discharge Half-Life

A Ni–Cd cell rated 1000 mAh loses 60 mAh per month at room temperature.

Self-discharge rate (per day) = $r = 60/30 = 2$ mAh/day.

In fraction: $r_f = 2/1000 = 0.002$ per day.

Half-life (exponential decay, $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$):

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.6931}{0.002} = 346.6 \text{ days} \approx 11.5 \text{ months}$$

---
