## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]
Differentiate between **primary** and **secondary** batteries. Give one example each.

**Model Answer:**
A **primary battery** is non-rechargeable; its electrochemical reactions are irreversible under normal conditions. Once the active materials are consumed, the cell is discarded. *Example:* Zinc–manganese dioxide (Leclanché) dry cell, lithium–MnO₂ button cell.

A **secondary battery** is rechargeable; the cell reactions are reversible by passing a DC current in the opposite direction (charging). *Example:* Lead–acid battery, lithium-ion battery.

| Property | Primary | Secondary |
|---|---|---|
| Rechargeable | No | Yes |
| Initial cost | Low | Higher |
| Internal resistance | Higher | Lower |
| Self-discharge | Lower | Higher |
| Typical use | Flashlight, watch | EV, UPS |

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]
What is a **reserve battery**? Mention any **two** types with their activation method.

**Model Answer:**
A **reserve battery** is an electrochemical cell stored in an inactive state for extended periods (10–25 years) and activated just before use by mechanical, chemical, or thermal means. This prevents self-discharge and electrolyte degradation during storage.

Two types:
1. **Sea-water activated battery** (e.g., Mg–AgCl) — sea water admitted at deployment; used in torpedoes.
2. **Thermal battery** (e.g., Li–FeS₂) — pyrotechnic heat source melts the solid-state electrolyte (LiCl–KCl eutectic); used in missiles and ejection seats.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Marks Distribution:**
> KTU strictly enforces **7 + 7 marks** split. Failing to clearly demarcate sub-part (a) and sub-part (b) with headings, omitting half-cell reactions, or omitting a numerical step in derivation can cost 3–4 marks per question. **Always** include: (i) labelled half-reactions, (ii) overall reaction, (iii) Nernst/Faraday relation, (iv) a final boxed numerical answer with correct units.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Lead–Acid Cell

**Q.A (a)** [7 Marks] Write the half-cell and overall reactions during discharging and charging of a **flooded lead–acid battery**. State the role of the **separator** and explain why specific gravity of the electrolyte is a useful State-of-Charge (SoC) indicator.

**Model Solution:**

During **discharge**:
- Anode (oxidation): $\text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^-$
- Cathode (reduction): $\text{PbO}_2 + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 4\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- **Overall:** $\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$

During **charge**, all three reactions reverse exactly. Water is regenerated on charge; sulfuric acid is regenerated on charge.

**Role of the separator:** A porous polyethylene / PVC / rubber sheet that physically separates the + and − plates to prevent short circuits while permitting ionic conduction through the sulfuric acid. In VRLA-AGM, the separator absorbs electrolyte.

**Specific gravity (ρ) and SoC:**

The overall discharge consumes sulfuric acid and produces water, decreasing ρ from ≈ **1.28 g/cm³ (fully charged)** to ≈ **1.10 g/cm³ (discharged)**. Because ρ is linearly proportional to acid concentration, hydrometer measurement of ρ is the cheapest SoC indicator for flooded cells (≈ ±5% accuracy). VRLA cells are sealed, so ρ is measured by OCV instead (open-circuit voltage ≈ 0.84 + ρ g/cm³).

---

**Q.A (b)** [7 Marks] A lead–acid battery rated **200 Ah** is discharged at the **C/10 rate** until the terminal voltage drops to 1.75 V/cell. If the discharge uses **90%** of rated capacity and the average discharge voltage is **1.95 V/cell**, calculate (i) discharge current, (ii) total discharge time, (iii) total energy delivered, (iv) average power.

**Model Solution:**

(i) Discharge current at C/10:

$$I = \frac{C}{10} = \frac{200 \text{ Ah}}{10 \text{ h}} = 20 \text{ A}$$

(ii) Total discharge time (90% utilization):

$$t = \frac{0.90 \times 200 \text{ Ah}}{20 \text{ A}} = \frac{180}{20} = 9 \text{ h}$$

(iii) Total energy delivered (assume single cell for simplicity; multiply by number of cells in series for a battery):

$$W = V_{\text{avg}} \cdot I \cdot t = 1.95 \text{ V} \times 20 \text{ A} \times 9 \text{ h} = 351 \text{ Wh}$$

For a 12 V battery (6 cells): $W = 351 \times 6 = 2106$ Wh ≈ 2.1 kWh.

(iv) Average power:

$$P = V_{\text{avg}} \cdot I = 1.95 \text{ V} \times 20 \text{ A} = 39 \text{ W}$$

per cell → $P_{\text{battery}} = 39 \times 6 = 234$ W.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students forget to multiply by the number of cells in series. Always clarify whether the question is for a **single cell** or a **battery**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Li-Ion / Theoretical Energy

**Q.B (a)** [7 Marks] Write the half-cell reactions for a **lithium cobalt oxide (LCO) / graphite** Li-ion cell during **discharge** and **charging**. Why is graphite preferred over lithium metal as the anode? What is "lithium plating"?

**Model Solution:**

**Discharge (Li⁺ moves anode → cathode):**
- Anode (oxidation): $\text{Li}_x\text{C}_6 \rightarrow 6\text{C} + x\text{Li}^+ + xe^-$
- Cathode (reduction): $\text{Li}_{1-x}\text{CoO}_2 + x\text{Li}^+ + xe^- \rightarrow \text{LiCoO}_2$
- **Overall:** $\text{Li}_x\text{C}_6 + \text{Li}_{1-x}\text{CoO}_2 \rightarrow 6\text{C} + \text{LiCoO}_2$ (x = 1 fully charged)

**Charge** is the exact reverse.

**Why graphite instead of Li metal:**
1. **Safety** — Li metal forms dendrites upon repeated plating/stripping, which pierce the separator and cause internal short circuits, fires, and explosions.
2. **Cycle life** — graphite intercalates Li⁺ reversibly with minimal volume change (≈ 10%), giving 500–2000 cycles; Li metal gives < 50.
3. **Lower surface area** — less SEI (solid-electrolyte interphase) build-up, less electrolyte consumption.

**Lithium plating:** At high charge rates (low temperature especially), Li⁺ ions do not intercalate fast enough into graphite and instead deposit as metallic Li on the anode surface. This irreversibly consumes active Li and creates dendrites → capacity loss + safety hazard.

---

**Q.B (b)** [7 Marks] Calculate the **theoretical specific energy** (Wh/kg) of a **Li / MnO₂** primary cell, given: $M_{Li} = 6.94$ g/mol, $M_{MnO_2} = 86.94$ g/mol, $n = 1$, and open-circuit voltage **3.0 V**.

**Model Solution:**

Active mass of cell per mole of reaction = $6.94 + 86.94 = 93.88$ g/mol.

$$W_{sp} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot V}{3600 \cdot m}$$

$$W_{sp} = \frac{1 \times 96485 \times 3.0}{3600 \times 93.88}$$

**Step 1 — Numerator:**

$$1 \times 96485 \times 3.0 = 289{,}455 \text{ C·V} = 289{,}455 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Denominator:**

$$3600 \times 93.88 = 337{,}968$$

**Step 3 — Divide:**

$$W_{sp} = \frac{289{,}455}{337{,}968} = 0.8565 \text{ Wh/g} = 856.5 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Utilization factor:**

$$\eta_u = \frac{\text{practical}}{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{270}{856.5} = 0.315 = 31.5\%$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TOPIC RECAP — Types of Batteries (Module 3, KTU PECHT417)**
>
> **MUST REMEMBER DEFINITIONS:**
> - **Primary cell** — irreversible, single-use (Leclanché, Alkaline, Li–MnO₂, Zn–Air).
> - **Secondary cell** — rechargeable, reversible (Lead–acid, Ni–Cd, Ni–MH, Li-ion, Na–S).
> - **Reserve cell** — long shelf life, activated on demand (thermal, sea-water, ammonia).
> - **Flow cell** — energy stored in external electrolyte tanks (VRFB, Zn–Br).
> - **Fuel cell** — fuel supplied externally; power density rated in W/cm², not Wh.
>
> **KEY HALF-REACTIONS TO MEMORIZE:**
> - Lead–acid: Pb + PbO₂ + 2H₂SO₄ → 2PbSO₄ + 2H₂O
> - Ni–Cd: Cd + 2NiO(OH) → Cd(OH)₂ + 2Ni(OH)₂
> - Ni–MH: MH + NiO(OH) → M + Ni(OH)₂
> - Alkaline: Zn + 2MnO₂ → ZnO + Mn₂O₃
> - Li–MnO₂: Li + MnO₂ → LiMnO₂
> - Zn–Ag₂O: Zn + Ag₂O → ZnO + 2Ag
>
> **CRITICAL NUMERICAL CONSTANTS:**
> - Faraday $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol = **26.802 Ah/mol**.
> - Li⁺/Li potential = **−3.04 V vs SHE** (most negative metal).
> - Pb–acid OCV = **2.05 V/cell** (battery = 12 V).
> - Li-ion OCV = **3.6–3.7 V/cell**.
>
> **ESSENTIAL FORMULAE:**
> - $Q_{sp} = \dfrac{nF}{3600\,M_w}$ (Ah/kg)
> - $W_{sp} = \dfrac{nF\,V}{3600\,M_w}$ (Wh/kg)
> - $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$
> - $\eta_E = \eta_F \times \eta_V$
>
> **ENGINEERING DESIGN PRINCIPLES:**
> - Higher $E^{\circ}$ + lower $M_w$ → higher energy density (Li is unbeatable).
> - Faradaic loss, polarization, and packaging reduce theoretical energy to **15–35%** in practice.
> - Cycle life ranking: LiFePO₄ ≈ Na–S ≈ VRFB (3000+) > Ni–MH > Li-ion NMC > Lead–acid > Li–metal.
>
> **APPLICATION MAPPING:**
> - **SLI / Automotive:** Lead–acid (low cost, surge current).
> - **EV / Hybrid:** Li-ion (NMC, NCA, LFP).
> - **Grid storage:** VRFB, Na–S, LiFePO₄.
> - **Aerospace:** Ni–H₂, Li-ion.
> - **Military / Reserve:** Li–SOCl₂, Mg–AgCl, Thermal Li–FeS₂.
> - **Watches / Hearing aids:** Zn–Ag₂O, Zn–air, Li–MnO₂.
>
> **SAFETY / PITFALLS:**
> - Lead–acid: hydrogen evolution → explosion risk during charging (venting).
> - Li-ion: thermal runaway, dendrite formation, fires at > 60 °C abuse.
> - Na–S operates at 350 °C → thermal hazard.
> - VRFB: cross-contamination of vanadium ions across membrane reduces capacity.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT417 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fuel Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Cell (KTU 2024 Definition):** A fuel cell is a **galvanic (electrochemical) energy conversion device** that converts the chemical energy of a continuously supplied fuel (most commonly hydrogen) and an oxidant (typically oxygen from air) directly into **electrical energy**, with **water and heat** as the primary by-products, as long as reactants are supplied. Unlike a battery, a fuel cell does not require recharging; it operates indefinitely until fuel supply is interrupted.

The general half-cell and overall reactions for the most commonly studied hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell are:

$$H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^- \quad \text{(Anode – Oxidation)}$$

$$\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O \quad \text{(Cathode – Reduction)}$$

$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \quad \text{(Overall Cell Reaction)}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a battery that **never dies**. A conventional AA cell is like a sealed sandwich — once the fillings are eaten, you throw it away. A fuel cell is like a **continuously-refilled sandwich kiosk** — as long as you keep pouring hydrogen (the "bread") and oxygen (the "filling") into their respective sides, electricity keeps flowing out and water keeps dripping out the exhaust. The only "waste" is pure, drinkable water!

**Three Functional Building Blocks of Every Fuel Cell:**

- **Anode (Fuel Side):** Site of oxidation. Hydrogen is fed and split into protons + electrons.
- **Electrolyte:** Ion-selective medium. Allows only protons (H⁺) or oxide ions (O²⁻) to pass — blocks electrons and gas crossover.
- **Cathode (Oxidant Side):** Site of reduction. Oxygen combines with ions and electrons to form water.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Faraday Constant | **F** | **96485 C mol⁻¹** |
| Universal Gas Constant | **R** | **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** |
| Standard Temperature | **T** | **298.15 K** |
| Enthalpy of H₂ combustion (Higher Heating Value) | ΔH° | **−285.8 kJ mol⁻¹** |
| Gibbs Free Energy of H₂ combustion | ΔG° | **−237.2 kJ mol⁻¹** |
| Standard Reversible Cell Voltage (H₂/O₂) | E° | **1.229 V** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** The single most important number to remember is **E° = 1.229 V** for the standard hydrogen–oxygen cell at **25 °C** with liquid water product. Examiners love this value.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation – Why Does a Fuel Cell Produce Voltage?

The driving force of any fuel cell is the **Gibbs free energy change (ΔG)** of the overall cell reaction. The maximum reversible electrical work obtainable per mole of fuel is:

$$W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G$$

This work equals the product of total charge transferred (nF) and the reversible cell potential (E_rev):

$$-\Delta G = nFE_{rev} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_{rev} = \frac{-\Delta G}{nF}$$

### 2.2 Theoretical Steps for Cell-Voltage Prediction

- **Step 1 — Identify half-reactions** at anode and cathode using standard reduction potentials (E°_red).
- **Step 2 — Apply Nernst equation** to compute reversible cell voltage E at actual operating T, P, and concentrations.
- **Step 3 — Subtract polarization losses** (activation, ohmic, concentration) to get actual operating voltage V.
- **Step 4 — Multiply by current I** to obtain power: P = V × I.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table** — memorize these for ESE.

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{rev} = -\Delta G \;/\, nF$ | Reversible cell voltage from Gibbs energy | V |
| 2 | $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$ | Nernst equation for H₂/O₂ cell | V |
| 3 | $E^{\circ} = -\Delta G^{\circ}\,/\,nF$ | Standard cell voltage | V |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$ | Thermodynamic (ideal) efficiency | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_{V} = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{E_{rev}}$ | Voltage efficiency | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_{overall} = \varepsilon_{th} \times \varepsilon_{V} \times \varepsilon_{F}$ | Overall fuel-cell efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_{act} = a + b \log j$ | Tafel equation for activation overpotential | V |
| 8 | $b = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha F}$ | Tafel slope | V decade⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\eta_{ohmic} = IR_{internal}$ | Ohmic loss by Ohm's law | V |
| 10 | $P = V \times I = V \times j \times A$ | Power output of fuel cell | W |
| 11 | $\text{HHV efficiency} = \dfrac{1.229}{1.48} = 83\,\%$ | Max HHV efficiency of H₂/O₂ cell | — |

### 2.4 Polarization Losses — The "Three Humps" of Fuel-Cell Reality

A real fuel cell delivers voltage **less than** 1.229 V. The actual voltage follows:

$$V_{cell} = E_{rev} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$

| Loss Term | Cause | Dominant Region |
|---|---|---|
| $\eta_{act}$ (Activation) | Slow electrode kinetics, charge-transfer barrier | **Low current densities** |
| $\eta_{ohmic}$ (Ohmic) | Ionic resistance of electrolyte + electronic resistance of electrodes | **Mid current densities** |
| $\eta_{conc}$ (Concentration) | Mass-transport limitation of reactants to electrode | **High current densities** |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Fuel cells power **NASA space shuttles**, **Toyota Mirai** hydrogen cars, **backup power for telecom towers**, and form the cornerstone of the **green-hydrogen economy**. KTU expects students to connect theoretical ΔG to practical applications such as combined-heat-and-power (CHP) units and electric vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nernst Equation for Hydrogen/Oxygen Fuel Cell

**Starting from thermodynamic identity** for a reversible reaction at constant T and P:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

where **Q** is the reaction quotient. For the overall reaction $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$, with partial pressures $P_{H_2}$, $P_{O_2}$, $P_{H_2O}$:

$$Q = \frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$

Substituting $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$:

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right)$$

Dividing throughout by $-nF$ and reversing the log term:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)\,}$$

**Conversion logic step:** the negative sign was absorbed by inverting the log argument (since $-\ln(x) = \ln(1/x)$).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reversible Cell Voltage E° from ΔG°

Using $\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$ and $n = 2$ electrons per H₂ molecule:

$$E^{\circ} = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} = -\frac{(-237\,200)\;\text{J/mol}}{(2)(96\,485)\;\text{C/mol}}$$

$$E^{\circ} = \frac{237\,200}{192\,970}$$

$$\boxed{\,E^{\circ} = 1.229 \;\text{V}\,}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Theoretical (Thermodynamic) Efficiency

By definition, efficiency is the **useful energy output divided by the total chemical energy input**:

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \frac{\text{Electrical work output}}{\text{Heat of combustion}} = \frac{-\Delta G}{-\Delta H}$$

For H₂/O₂ cell, using Higher Heating Value (HHV) at 25 °C:

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \frac{237.2}{285.8}$$

$$\boxed{\,\varepsilon_{th,HHV} = 0.830 \;\;(83.0\,\%)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the **Lower Heating Value** (LHV, where product water is vapour, ΔH° = −241.8 kJ/mol) is used instead, the theoretical efficiency rises to **98 %**. KTU problems frequently ask students to specify which heating value was assumed.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Tafel Equation (Activation Overpotential)

Starting from the **Butler–Volmer equation** at large overpotentials (the high-field approximation, $\eta \gg RT/F$), the cathodic term dominates:

$$j = j_0 \,\exp\!\left(\frac{\alpha F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

Take natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln j = \ln j_0 + \frac{\alpha F \eta}{RT}$$

Solve for η:

$$\eta = \frac{RT}{\alpha F} \ln\!\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right)$$

Convert ln to log₁₀ using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$\eta = \frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha F} \log\!\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right)$$

Grouping the constant slope term into $b$ and defining $a = -b\log j_0$:

$$\boxed{\,\eta_{act} = a + b \log j \quad \text{with} \quad b = \frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha F}\,}$$

At T = 298 K and assuming charge-transfer coefficient $\alpha = 0.5$:

$$b = \frac{(2.303)(8.314)(298)}{(0.5)(96\,485)}$$

$$b = \frac{5\,706}{48\,242.5}$$

$$\boxed{\,b \approx 0.118 \;\text{V/decade}\,}$$

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Numerical Example: Cell Voltage under Load

**Problem:** A PEM fuel cell operates at 1.0 A cm⁻². Given data: $E_{rev} = 1.15$ V, activation loss = 0.30 V, ohmic loss = 0.05 V, concentration loss = 0.08 V. Compute actual cell voltage and power density.

**Step 1 — Net cell voltage:**

$$V_{cell} = E_{rev} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$

$$V_{cell} = 1.15 - 0.30 - 0.05 - 0.08$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{cell} = 0.72 \;\text{V}\,}$$

**Step 2 — Power density:**

$$P_d = V_{cell} \times j = 0.72 \times 1.0$$

$$\boxed{\,P_d = 0.72 \;\text{W cm}^{-2}\,}$$

**Step 3 — Overall efficiency vs. HHV:**

$$\varepsilon_{overall} = \frac{V_{cell}}{1.482} = \frac{0.72}{1.482} \times 100\,\%$$

$$\boxed{\,\varepsilon_{overall} \approx 48.6\,\%\,}$$

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Fuel-Cell Types

| Fuel Cell Type | Electrolyte | Operating T | Charge Carrier | Efficiency | Application |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| **AFC** (Alkaline) | KOH solution | 60–90 °C | OH⁻ | 60–70 % | Space, military |
| **PEMFC** | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | 60–100 °C | H⁺ | 50–60 % | Vehicles, portable |
| **DMFC** | Polymer membrane | 60–130 °C | H⁺ | 40 % | Portable electronics |
| **PAFC** | Liquid H₃PO₄ | 160–220 °C | H⁺ | 40–50 % | Stationary CHP |
| **MCFC** | Molten Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ | 600–700 °C | CO₃²⁻ | 50–60 % | Large power plants |
| **SOFC** | Yttria-stabilized zirconia | 800–1000 °C | O²⁻ | 50–60 % | Industrial, CHP |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of a Generic Fuel Cell

The Mermaid diagram below depicts the working principle and mass/charge transport pathways of a generic hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell with a proton-conducting electrolyte.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Hydrogen Storage Tank]
    nodeB[Fuel Inlet Manifold]
    nodeC[Anode Catalyst Layer Pt]
    nodeD[Polymer Electrolyte Membrane PEM]
    nodeE[Cathode Catalyst Layer Pt]
    nodeF[Air Inlet Blower]
    nodeG[External Electrical Load]
    nodeH[Water Exhaust Outlet]

    nodeA -- H2 gas --> nodeB
    nodeB -- humidified H2 --> nodeC
    nodeF -- filtered air --> nodeE
    nodeC -- oxidation reaction --> nodeD
    nodeD -- protons H plus --> nodeE
    nodeC -- electrons e minus --> nodeG
    nodeE -- electrons returned --> nodeG
    nodeE -- ORR reaction --> nodeH
    nodeG -- DC power output --> nodeG
```

### 4.2 Polarization Curve — Voltage vs Current Density

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Cell Voltage V axis]
    nodeY[Start V equals 1.229 V]
    nodeX[Current Density j axis increasing]
    nodeR1[Region 1 Activation Loss dominant]
    nodeR2[Region 2 Ohmic Loss linear drop]
    nodeR3[Region 3 Concentration Loss rapid fall]
    nodeEnd[Limiting Current Density jL]

    nodeStart --> nodeY
    nodeStart --> nodeX
    nodeY --> nodeR1
    nodeR1 --> nodeR2
    nodeR2 --> nodeR3
    nodeR3 --> nodeEnd
```

### 4.3 Classification Block Diagram of Fuel Cells

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot[Fuel Cells Classification]

    nodeLow[Low Temperature below 200 C]
    nodeHigh[High Temperature above 500 C]

    nodeAFC[AFC Alkaline Fuel Cell]
    nodePEM[PEMFC Proton Exchange Membrane]
    nodeDMFC[DMFC Direct Methanol]
    nodePAFC[PAFC Phosphoric Acid]

    nodeMCFC[MCFC Molten Carbonate]
    nodeSOFC[SOFC Solid Oxide]

    nodeRoot --> nodeLow
    nodeRoot --> nodeHigh

    nodeLow --> nodeAFC
    nodeLow --> nodePEM
    nodeLow --> nodeDMFC
    nodeLow --> nodePAFC

    nodeHigh --> nodeMCFC
    nodeHigh --> nodeSOFC
```

### 4.4 Functional Block Architecture of a Complete Fuel-Cell Power System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeReform[Hydrocarbon Fuel Reformer]
    nodeDesulf[Desulfurizer Unit]
    nodeStack[PEMFC Stack Assembly]
    nodeH2Tank[Hydrogen Buffer Tank]
    nodeInverter[DC to AC Inverter]
    nodeTransformer[Step Up Transformer]
    nodeLoad[Industrial or Residential Load]
    nodeCooling[Thermal Management Loop]
    nodeWater[Water Recovery and Recycle]

    nodeReform --> nodeDesulf
    nodeDesulf --> nodeH2Tank
    nodeH2Tank --> nodeStack
    nodeStack -- DC electricity --> nodeInverter
    nodeInverter --> nodeTransformer
    nodeTransformer --> nodeLoad
    nodeStack -- heat --> nodeCooling
    nodeStack -- water --> nodeWater
    nodeWater -- recycled humidification --> nodeStack
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a fuel cell. List its main components.**

**Model Answer:**
A fuel cell is an electrochemical device that continuously converts the chemical energy of a fuel (e.g., H₂) and an oxidant (e.g., O₂) directly into electrical energy, producing water and heat as by-products.
**Main components (3):**
1. **Anode** – fuel inlet, site of oxidation.
2. **Cathode** – oxidant inlet, site of reduction.
3. **Electrolyte** – ion-conducting, electron-blocking medium separating the electrodes.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Write the Nernst equation for a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell and state the value of the standard cell potential E°.**

**Model Answer:**

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$

At standard conditions (T = 298 K, all P = 1 atm), $E^{\circ} = \mathbf{1.229 \;V}$.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)** with a neat sketch. State its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
- **Anode:** Pt/C catalyst on carbon paper, fed with humidified H₂.
- **Cathode:** Pt/C catalyst on carbon paper, fed with air/O₂.
- **Electrolyte:** Solid polymer membrane (Nafion™) — conducts H⁺ ions only.
- **Bipolar plates:** Graphite/metal plates with gas-flow channels for reactant distribution and current collection.

**Working:** H₂ is oxidized at the anode (HOR: $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$). Protons migrate through the membrane; electrons flow through the external circuit. At the cathode, oxygen is reduced (ORR: $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$).

**Advantages:** Low operating T (~80 °C) → quick start-up; high power density; solid electrolyte → no leakage.
**Limitations:** Expensive Pt catalyst; sensitive to CO poisoning; water management critical.

---

**(b)** Derive the **Nernst equation** for a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell starting from the Gibbs free energy expression. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

From $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$ with $Q = P_{H_2O}\,/\,(P_{H_2}P_{O_2}^{1/2})$, and using $\Delta G = -nFE$, $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$:

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

Dividing by $-nF$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

$$\boxed{\,E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)\,}$$

This is the desired Nernst equation for the H₂/O₂ fuel cell. (Full derivation also available in Section 3.1.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC)**. Write its electrode reactions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:** SOFC uses a **solid ceramic electrolyte** (typically yttria-stabilized zirconia, YSZ) that conducts **oxide ions (O²⁻)** at high temperature (800–1000 °C). Porous electrodes are made of perovskite materials (e.g., LSM at cathode, Ni-YSZ cermet at anode).

**Working:** Air is supplied at the cathode; oxide ions migrate through the hot ceramic electrolyte toward the anode, where they react with the fuel (H₂ or hydrocarbons).

**Electrode reactions:**

$$\text{Cathode:} \quad \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow O^{2-}$$

$$\text{Anode:} \quad H_2 + O^{2-} \rightarrow H_2O + 2e^-$$

$$\text{Overall:} \quad H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$$

**Advantage:** No liquid electrolyte; fuel flexibility (can use H₂, CH₄, CO).
**Limitation:** Slow start-up due to high operating temperature; thermal-stress issues.

---

**(b)** The reversible cell voltage of a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell at standard conditions is 1.229 V. The cell delivers a current density of 800 mA cm⁻² with the following losses: activation = 0.35 V, ohmic = 0.06 V, concentration = 0.09 V. Calculate **(i)** the actual cell voltage and **(ii)** the overall efficiency with respect to HHV (ΔH° = −285.8 kJ/mol). **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Actual cell voltage:**

$$V_{cell} = E_{rev} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$

$$V_{cell} = 1.229 - 0.35 - 0.06 - 0.09$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{cell} = 0.729 \;\text{V}\,}$$

**(ii) Overall efficiency (HHV basis):**

The enthalpy-based voltage equivalent is $V_{HHV} = -\Delta H/nF = 285\,800/192\,970 = 1.481$ V.

$$\varepsilon_{overall} = \frac{V_{cell}}{V_{HHV}} = \frac{0.729}{1.481}$$

$$\boxed{\,\varepsilon_{overall} = 0.4923 \;\;(49.23\,\%)\,}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Fuel-Cell Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2 in Q for O₂.** Many students write $Q = P_{H_2O}/(P_{H_2}P_{O_2})$ instead of the correct $Q = P_{H_2O}/(P_{H_2}P_{O_2}^{1/2})$. Examiner deducts **2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing HHV (1.481 V) and LHV (1.253 V) thermal voltages.** Always state which heating value you are using in the efficiency calculation.
> 3. **Writing $E^{\circ}$ instead of E (with Nernst correction) for non-standard conditions.** At 80 °C and non-1-atm pressure, E ≠ 1.229 V.
> 4. **Skipping sign in Tafel derivation.** The constant $b$ must be positive (V/decade), otherwise the derivation has a sign error.
> 5. **Not stating the charge-carrier ion** (H⁺, O²⁻, OH⁻, CO₃²⁻) when asked about a specific fuel-cell type. Examiners expect this as a 1-mark keyword.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Fuel Cells (Module 3):**
> - Overall reaction: $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$
> - **Standard reversible voltage:** $E^{\circ} = 1.229$ V (HHV basis)
> - **Thermal voltage (HHV):** 1.481 V; **(LHV):** 1.253 V
> - **Max thermodynamic efficiency (HHV):** 83 %
> - **Nernst equation:** $E = E^{\circ} + (RT/2F)\ln(P_{H_2}P_{O_2}^{1/2}/P_{H_2O})$
> - **Cell voltage under load:** $V = E_{rev} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$
> - **Tafel slope** at 298 K, $\alpha = 0.5$: $b \approx 0.118$ V/decade
> - **PEMFC:** low-T (~80 °C), H⁺ carrier, Pt catalyst, Nafion membrane.
> - **SOFC:** high-T (~900 °C), O²⁻ carrier, YSZ electrolyte, fuel-flexible.
> - **MCFC:** ~650 °C, CO₃²⁻ carrier, molten carbonate electrolyte.
> - **AFC:** low-T, KOH electrolyte, OH⁻ carrier, used in space.
> - **PAFC:** ~200 °C, H₃PO₄ electrolyte, H⁺ carrier, stationary CHP.
> - **DMFC:** liquid methanol feed, H⁺ carrier, portable applications.
> - **Activation loss dominates at low current**; ohmic at mid; concentration at high.
> - **Overall efficiency** = thermodynamic × voltage × faradaic efficiencies.
> - **Faraday's law:** $m = (M \cdot I \cdot t)/(nF)$ — for H₂ consumption rate.
> - **E° = −ΔG°/nF** is the master relation linking chemistry to voltage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metals, Graphite, Lead Dioxide, Titanium Substrate Insoluble Electrodes, Iron Oxide, Semiconducting Type Electrodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT417 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Metals, Graphite, Lead Dioxide, Titanium Substrate Insoluble Electrodes, Iron Oxide, Semiconducting Type Electrodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Insoluble (Inert) Electrodes

In electrochemical engineering, **insoluble electrodes** (also called **inert electrodes** or **non-consumable electrodes**) are electrodes that do not participate directly in the electrochemical reaction through dissolution, deposition, or chemical transformation during the operation of the cell. They serve as a catalytic surface for electron transfer reactions, particularly the **anodic oxidation** and **cathodic reduction** of species in the electrolyte.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT417 Module 4):**
> The focus is on materials that resist anodic dissolution in aggressive electrolytes (acidic, saline, or oxidative media) while offering high electrical conductivity, good catalytic activity, and mechanical stability.

### 1.2 Classification of Insoluble Electrodes

The following hierarchy summarizes the major categories of insoluble electrodes covered in this module:

| Category | Examples | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Noble Metals** | Platinum (Pt), Gold (Au), Iridium (Ir) | Laboratory electrolysis, sensors, fuel cells |
| **Carbon-based** | Graphite, Glassy Carbon, Carbon Felt | Chlor-alkali (legacy), electrolysis, batteries |
| **Metal Oxides** | PbO₂, Fe₃O₄, MnO₂, RuO₂, IrO₂ | Anodes for oxidation, cathodes, electrocatalysis |
| **Coated Metal Substrates** | Ti/RuO₂–TiO₂ (DSA), Ti/PbO₂, Ti/Pt | Chlor-alkali (modern), water treatment |
| **Semiconductors** | TiO₂, SnO₂, SiC, Boron-Doped Diamond | Photoelectrochemistry, advanced oxidation |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Immovable Workbench"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of an insoluble electrode as a **worker's sturdy, immovable workbench** in a factory.
>
> - The **workbench itself** (electrode material) stays put — it doesn't wear away, dissolve, or break during the shift.
> - The **products being assembled** (reactant ions/molecules) come to the workbench, get processed (gain/lose electrons), and leave as finished goods.
> - The **workbench surface** may be specialized — a smooth steel plate (noble metal) for precision jobs, a coated wooden surface (oxide-coated) for chemical work, or a heavy granite slab (graphite) for high-temperature jobs.
>
> Just as the workbench enables the worker's productivity without being consumed, the insoluble electrode enables electrochemical reactions without itself being consumed (ideally).

---

## 1.4 Definition of Each Electrode Type

### 1.4.1 Metals as Insoluble Electrodes
A **noble metal electrode** is a metallic conductor that resists oxidation and dissolution under anodic polarization in the electrolyte of interest. The most common example is **Platinum (Pt)**, with a standard reduction potential of:

$$E^\circ_{\text{Pt}^{2+}/\text{Pt}} = +1.18 \text{ V vs. SHE}$$

Platinum exhibits excellent catalytic properties for hydrogen evolution, oxygen evolution, and various organic oxidations.

### 1.4.2 Graphite Electrodes
Graphite is a **layered allotrope of carbon** with hexagonal sp²-bonded sheets stacked via weak van der Waals forces. It is widely used as an inexpensive insoluble anode in legacy chlor-alkali cells, electrowinning, and electro-organic synthesis.

### 1.4.3 Lead Dioxide (PbO₂) Electrodes
PbO₂ is a **non-stoichiometric, conductive metal oxide** that exists in two polymorphs: **α-PbO₂** (orthorhombic) and **β-PbO₂** (tetragonal, more conductive). It exhibits high oxygen overpotential and excellent corrosion resistance in acidic media.

### 1.4.4 Titanium Substrate Insoluble Electrodes (DSA)
**Dimensionally Stable Anodes (DSA®)** are composite electrodes consisting of a **titanium metal substrate** coated with a thin, active layer of **mixed metal oxides** — typically RuO₂–TiO₂, IrO₂–Ta₂O₅, or Pt–Ir oxides. They revolutionized the chlor-alkali industry after their invention by **Henri Beer (1965)**.

### 1.4.5 Iron Oxide (Fe₃O₄ / Fe₂O₃) Electrodes
Iron oxide electrodes (especially **magnetite, Fe₃O₄**) are used as **cathodes** in chlor-alkali diaphragm cells and as anodes in specific alkaline electrolytes. They are cheap and have a low hydrogen overpotential when used as cathodes.

### 1.4.6 Semiconducting Type Electrodes
These are electrodes whose electronic behavior is governed by **band theory** — they have a **valence band**, a **conduction band**, and a characteristic **band gap (Eg)**. Examples include TiO₂ (Eg ≈ 3.0–3.2 eV), SnO₂ (Eg ≈ 3.6 eV), WO₃, Fe₂O₃, and **Boron-Doped Diamond (BDD)**. They are central to **photoelectrochemistry**, **electrocatalysis**, and advanced water treatment.

---

## 1.5 Why Insoluble Electrodes Matter — Engineering Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Significance:**
> 1. **Chlor-Alkali Industry:** Produces Cl₂, NaOH, and H₂ — the second-largest electrochemical industry globally. Modern cells rely entirely on DSA-type Ti/RuO₂ anodes.
> 2. **Electrowinning of Metals:** Cu, Zn, Ni, Mn recovered from ore leachates use insoluble PbO₂ or Pb–Ag anodes.
> 3. **Water & Wastewater Treatment:** BDD, PbO₂, and SnO₂ anodes generate hydroxyl radicals (·OH) for **Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)** that destroy persistent organic pollutants.
> 4. **Energy Storage & Conversion:** Fuel cells, batteries, and supercapacitors all use insoluble current collectors and catalytic electrodes.
> 5. **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP):** Ti/MMO anodes protect pipelines, ships, and offshore structures from corrosion.

---

## 1.6 Performance Criteria for an Insoluble Electrode

A "good" insoluble anode or cathode must satisfy these criteria simultaneously:

- ✅ High electrical conductivity (low IR drop)
- ✅ Chemical & electrochemical inertness in the operating medium
- ✅ High catalytic activity for the desired reaction (low overpotential)
- ✅ Mechanical stability and adherence of coatings
- ✅ Long service life (typically > 5 years for industrial DSAs)
- ✅ Low cost and ease of fabrication
- ✅ Resistance to passivation (oxide film formation that blocks current)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Noble Metal Electrodes — Platinum and Gold

### 2.1.1 Electrochemical Behavior of Platinum
Platinum is the benchmark inert electrode material. In aqueous electrolytes, Pt surfaces develop a thin **Pt-oxide layer** (PtO, PtO₂) at potentials above **+0.8 V vs. SHE**, which then reduces back to metallic Pt on the cathodic sweep. This produces the characteristic **"oxygen adsorption/desorption" peaks** in cyclic voltammograms.

**Hydrogen Evolution Reaction (HER) on Pt:**
$$2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2 \quad (E^\circ = 0.00 \text{ V vs. SHE})$$

**Oxygen Evolution Reaction (OER) on Pt:**
$$2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^- \quad (E^\circ = +1.23 \text{ V vs. SHE})$$

The overpotential (η) for OER on smooth Pt is approximately **0.3–0.4 V** at 10 mA/cm².

### 2.1.2 Why "Noble" Metals Are Limited in Industry
Despite their excellent performance, Pt and Au are **prohibitively expensive** (Pt ≈ ₹25,000–30,000/g) for large-scale industrial use. They are reserved for:
- Reference electrodes (e.g., Pt/H₂)
- Laboratory precision work
- Thin-film coatings on Ti substrates (Ti/Pt anodes for cathodic protection)

---

## 2.2 Graphite Electrodes

### 2.2.1 Structure and Properties
Graphite consists of stacked **graphene layers** held by van der Waals forces (~5 kJ/mol). Within a layer, each carbon is sp²-hybridized, forming three σ-bonds with neighbors and one delocalized π-electron cloud. The π-electrons provide **anisotropic electrical conductivity**: parallel to the layers σ ≈ 10⁴ S/cm; perpendicular ≈ 1 S/cm.

### 2.2.2 Electrochemical Reactions on Graphite
In chlor-alkali cells, the graphite anode reaction is:
$$2\text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{Cl}_2 + 2e^- \quad (E^\circ = +1.36 \text{ V vs. SHE})$$

However, the **competing OER** at +1.23 V is thermodynamically favored, and the **chlorine overpotential on graphite is high (~0.3–0.5 V)**. Additionally, the carbon anode is **consumed by oxidation**:
$$\text{C} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^-$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Although graphite is *called* an "insoluble" electrode, it is actually **slowly consumed** by anodic oxidation to CO₂/CO. Consumption rates of **2–4 kg per ton of Cl₂ produced** were historically observed. This is why graphite anodes have been **replaced by DSA in modern chlor-alkali plants**.

### 2.2.3 Advantages and Limitations

| Property | Status |
|---|---|
| Cost | ✅ Very low |
| Availability | ✅ Abundant |
| Electrical conductivity | ✅ Good (anisotropic) |
| Machinability | ✅ Excellent (can be shaped into rods, plates) |
| Anodic stability | ❌ Poor — oxidizes to CO₂ |
| Mechanical strength | ❌ Brittle, erodes |

---

## 2.3 Lead Dioxide (PbO₂) Electrodes

### 2.3.1 Polymorphs and Conductivity
- **α-PbO₂** (orthorhombic, columbite-type): Forms in alkaline media; less conductive.
- **β-PbO₂** (tetragonal, rutile-type): Forms in acidic media; **more conductive** (σ ≈ 10⁴ S/cm) and more electrocatalytically active.

The conductivity of β-PbO₂ is due to **non-stoichiometric oxygen vacancies** providing free electrons — making it a **degenerate n-type semiconductor**.

### 2.3.2 Electrochemical Behavior
PbO₂ is an excellent anode material for **oxygen evolution in acidic sulfate media**, used in:
- **Zinc electrowinning** (anode reaction: $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^-$)
- **Electrolytic MnO₂ production** (anode reaction: $\text{Mn}^{2+} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{MnO}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$)
- **Electrochemical degradation of organics** (wastewater treatment)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Oxygen Overpotential on β-PbO₂:** ~0.5–0.6 V at 10 mA/cm² in H₂SO₄ — higher than Pt but excellent for suppressing unwanted O₂ evolution during MnO₂ deposition.

### 2.3.3 Preparation Methods
- **Electrodeposition** from Pb(NO₃)₂ in HNO₃ (most common).
- **Thermal decomposition** of Pb(NO₃)₂.
- **Pressed pellets** of PbO₂ powder with binder.

For industrial use, PbO₂ is **electrodeposited onto a Pb, Ti, or graphite substrate**.

### 2.3.4 Limitations
- ❌ Heavy, toxic (Pb²⁺ leaching)
- ❌ Brittle, prone to cracking
- ❌ Loses adhesion over time on Ti substrate
- ✅ Cheap and reasonably stable in H₂SO₄

---

## 2.4 Titanium Substrate Insoluble Electrodes (DSA)

### 2.4.1 Concept and Invention
The **Dimensionally Stable Anode (DSA®)** was developed by **Henri Beer (1965)** in South Africa. It solved the chronic problem of graphite anode consumption in chlor-alkali cells. The structure is a **sandwich**:

1. **Substrate:** Commercially pure titanium (Grade 1 or 2) — chosen for its **passivating TiO₂ film** that prevents corrosion during cell shutdowns.
2. **Coating:** Mixed metal oxide (MMO) layer, typically 1–10 μm thick, applied by **thermal decomposition of chloride salts** at 400–500 °C.

### 2.4.2 Standard Coating Compositions

| Application | Coating Composition | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Chlor-alkali (Cl₂)** | RuO₂ (30 mol%) + TiO₂ (70 mol%) | Low Cl₂ overpotential |
| **Oxygen evolution (acid)** | IrO₂ (70 mol%) + Ta₂O₅ (30 mol%) | Stable in H₂SO₄ |
| **Cathodic protection** | IrO₂–Ta₂O₅ / Pt–Ir | Long life, low wear |
| **Water treatment** | Sb-doped SnO₂ / PbO₂ / BDD | High O₂ overpotential, ·OH generation |

### 2.4.3 Why Titanium as Substrate?
- Forms a stable, insulating TiO₂ passive film (Eg ≈ 3.0 eV) → protects substrate from corrosion in cell-off conditions.
- Lightweight (ρ = 4.5 g/cm³) → easy fabrication as mesh, plate, rod, or expanded metal.
- Good electrical conductivity of the metal itself (σ ≈ 2.4 × 10⁴ S/cm).
- Strong adhesion to the oxide coating (chemical bonding at the Ti/TiO₂ interface).

### 2.4.4 Failure Modes of DSA
1. **Coating dissolution** — gradual loss of RuO₂ in service.
2. **Passivation** — TiO₂ grows beneath the coating, increasing resistance → "anode passivation failure."
3. **Mechanical erosion** — release of H₂ bubbles at the cathode side, or Cl₂ at anode.
4. **Manganese deposition** — in chlorate cells, MnO₂ deposits poison the coating.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A DSA must NEVER be operated as a **cathode** in aqueous chloride media — Ti hydride (TiH₂) forms, embrittling the substrate and destroying the coating interface.

---

## 2.5 Iron Oxide (Magnetite) Electrodes

### 2.5.1 Composition and Structure
**Magnetite (Fe₃O₄)** has an **inverse spinel structure** with Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ in octahedral and tetrahedral sites. It is a **mixed-valence oxide**, enabling good electronic conductivity (~10²–10³ S/cm) via **electron hopping** between Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ centers.

### 2.5.2 Application — Cathode in Diaphragm Cells
In the legacy **diaphragm chlor-alkali cell**, the cathode was mild steel, but the **anode was graphite and the cathode was often a coated iron or steel mesh** to improve hydrogen overpotential characteristics. Modern membrane cells use **activated nickel cathodes** (Ni + S, Ni + Al, or Ni + Mo from Raney nickel) for low-H₂ overpotential operation.

> [!NOTE]
> Fe₃O₄ finds renewed interest as an **anode material for alkaline water electrolysis** and as a **photocatalyst** under visible light (Eg ≈ 2.0 eV).

### 2.5.3 Hematite (α-Fe₂O₃) as a Photoanode
Hematite is the most studied **photoanode material** for solar water splitting due to its:
- Suitable band gap (~2.0–2.2 eV — absorbs visible light)
- Earth-abundance and non-toxicity
- Stability in alkaline media

Limitation: Short hole-diffusion length (~2–4 nm) and poor charge transport.

---

## 2.6 Semiconducting Type Electrodes

### 2.6.1 Band Structure Theory
A semiconductor electrode has a **Fermi level (E_F)** located within a **band gap (E_g)** between the **valence band (VB)** and **conduction band (CB)**. When immersed in an electrolyte, the Fermi level equilibrates with the redox potential, creating **band bending** at the semiconductor–electrolyte interface.

**Types:**
- **n-type** (e.g., TiO₂, SnO₂, Fe₂O₃) — majority carriers are electrons; used as **photoanodes**.
- **p-type** (e.g., NiO, Cu₂O, Co₃O₄) — majority carriers are holes; used as **photocathodes**.

### 2.6.2 Photoelectrochemical Water Splitting
Under illumination with photon energy hν > E_g, electron–hole pairs are generated:

$$h\nu \rightarrow e^-_{CB} + h^+_{VB}$$

At an n-type photoanode:
$$2h^+_{VB} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+$$

At a p-type photocathode:
$$2e^-_{CB} + 2\text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{H}_2$$

### 2.6.3 Boron-Doped Diamond (BDD) Electrode
BDD is an **emerging super-inert electrode** consisting of a thin polycrystalline diamond film doped with boron on a Si, Ti, or Nb substrate.

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Band gap | ~5.5 eV (UV-active) |
| Conductivity (B-doped) | ~10²–10³ S/cm |
| OER overpotential | Very high (~1.0–1.5 V) |
| Corrosion resistance | Excellent (chemically inert, sp³ C) |
| ·OH generation efficiency | Highest among all electrodes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> BDD is the **gold standard for electrochemical Advanced Oxidation Processes (EAOPs)** — it generates quasi-free ·OH radicals that mineralize almost any organic pollutant to CO₂, H₂O, and inorganic ions.

### 2.6.4 Other Important Semiconducting Electrodes
- **SnO₂ (Sb-doped):** Excellent anode for organic oxidation; high O₂ overpotential.
- **WO₃:** Visible-light photoanode (Eg ≈ 2.6 eV).
- **TiO₂ (anatase):** UV photoanode (Eg ≈ 3.2 eV); widely used in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs).
- **SiC:** Wide band gap (3.2 eV), chemically stable, used in harsh environments.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nernst Equation | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | V; R = 8.314 J/mol·K, F = 96485 C/mol |
| 2 | Tafel Equation | $\eta = a + b \log_{10}\vert j \vert$ | η in V; j in A/cm²; b ≈ 0.12 V/dec (2e⁻) or 0.03 V/dec (1e⁻) |
| 3 | Exchange current density | $j_0 = Fk^\circ C^*$ | A/cm²; depends on kinetics |
| 4 | Band gap from wavelength | $E_g = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_g} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda_g (\text{nm})}$ | eV |
| 5 | Photocurrent density | $j_{ph} = e\,\eta_{abs}\,\eta_{sep}\,\eta_{inj}\,I_0$ | A/cm²; e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C |
| 6 | Chlorine overpotential on DSA | $\eta_{Cl_2} \approx 0.03 - 0.05$ V | At 10 mA/cm², low compared to graphite |
| 7 | Service life of DSA | $t = \dfrac{m_{RuO_2}}{\text{dissolution rate} \times A}$ | years; m in g, A in cm² |
| 8 | Conductivity of graphite (∥ to layers) | $\sigma \approx 10^4$ | S/cm |
| 9 | Conductivity of β-PbO₂ | $\sigma \approx 10^4$ | S/cm |
| 10 | Resistivity of TiO₂ passive film | $\rho \approx 10^{12}$ | Ω·cm (highly insulating) |
| 11 | Band bending at SC-electrolyte interface | $V_{bb} = \vert E_F - E_{redox} \vert$ | V; band bending of width W |
| 12 | Space-charge capacitance (Mott-Schottky) | $\dfrac{1}{C^2} = \dfrac{2}{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0 e N_D}\left(V - V_{FB} - \dfrac{k_BT}{e}\right)$ | F⁻²; N_D = donor density |
| 13 | Energy conversion efficiency (solar cell) | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc}\,J_{sc}\,FF}{P_{in}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 14 | Anode wear rate (DSA) | $\text{Wear} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{Q}$ | g/C; Q = charge passed |

---

## 2.8 Comparative Engineering Evaluation

| Property | Pt | Graphite | PbO₂ | Ti/RuO₂–TiO₂ (DSA) | Fe₃O₄ | BDD |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Very high | Very low | Low | Moderate | Low | Very high |
| **Anodic stability** | Excellent | Poor (consumed) | Good | Excellent | Fair | Outstanding |
| **Cl₂ overpotential** | Moderate | High (~0.4 V) | N/A | Very low (~0.03 V) | N/A | N/A |
| **O₂ overpotential** | Moderate | Low | High | Moderate | — | Very high |
| **Catalytic activity** | Excellent | Low | High | Excellent | Moderate | Highest (·OH) |
| **Mechanical strength** | Good | Brittle | Brittle | Excellent | Fair | Excellent |
| **Environmental concern** | Low | Low | High (Pb) | Low | Low | Low |
| **Main application** | Lab, sensors | Legacy chlor-alkali | Electrowinning, AOP | Chlor-alkali, water | Cathode, photocatalyst | Advanced AOP |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Nernst Equation for an Insoluble Electrode

The Nernst equation governs the equilibrium potential of any electrode (including insoluble ones when a redox couple is present at the surface). Derivation begins from the **Gibbs free energy change** of a redox half-reaction.

**Starting Relation:**

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

For an electrochemical half-reaction, the relationship between free energy and electrode potential is:

$$\Delta G = -nFE \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$$

**Step 1 — Substitute into the thermodynamic relation:**

$$-nFE = -nFE^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 2 — Divide through by −nF:**

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 3 — Convert natural log to log₁₀ (using ln x = 2.303 log₁₀ x):**

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values at 25 °C (T = 298.15 K):**

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{n \times 96485} \log_{10} Q$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the constant:**

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q \quad (\text{at } 25\,^\circ\text{C})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Application to DSA anode in chlor-alkali:** For $\text{Cl}_2/\text{Cl}^-$, n = 2, Q = 1/[Cl⁻]². At 25 °C and [Cl⁻] = 5 M:
> $$E = 1.36 - \frac{0.0592}{2}\log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{25}\right) = 1.36 + 0.0413 = 1.401 \text{ V vs. SHE}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Tafel Slope for a Multi-Electron Transfer

The Tafel equation describes how current density depends exponentially on overpotential for a kinetically controlled reaction.

**Starting Kinetic Equation (Butler–Volmer):**

$$j = j_0 \left[ \exp\left(+\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

where:
- $j_0$ = exchange current density
- $\alpha_a, \alpha_c$ = anodic & cathodic transfer coefficients ($\alpha_a + \alpha_c = 1$)
- $\eta = E - E_{eq}$ = overpotential

**Step 1 — At large anodic overpotential, the cathodic term is negligible:**

$$j \approx j_0 \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Take natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$\ln j = \ln j_0 + \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to log₁₀:**

$$\log_{10} j = \log_{10} j_0 + \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{2.303\,RT}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for η:**

$$\eta = \frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_a F} \log_{10} j - \frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_a F} \log_{10} j_0$$

**Step 5 — Define Tafel constants:**

$$\eta = a + b \log_{10} j$$

where:

$$b = \frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_a F}, \quad a = -b \log_{10} j_0$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate b at 25 °C for typical α_a = 0.5 (symmetric barrier):**

$$b = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{0.5 \times 96485} = \frac{5711.5}{48242.5} = 0.1184 \text{ V/decade}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Insight:** A **smaller Tafel slope b** means a smaller overpotential is needed to increase current — a more efficient electrode. DSA anodes for Cl₂ have b ≈ 0.03 V/dec (2-electron transfer with low α), making them far more efficient than graphite anodes (b ≈ 0.12 V/dec).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Semiconductor Band Bending & Flat-Band Potential

For an n-type semiconductor photoanode in contact with an electrolyte, the space-charge region creates band bending described by the **Mott–Schottky equation**.

**Step 1 — Poisson's equation in the depletion region (1-D):**

$$\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0} = \frac{eN_D}{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0}$$

(ρ = eN_D for ionized donors)

**Step 2 — Integrate once with boundary condition dφ/dx = 0 at x = W (edge of depletion layer):**

$$\frac{d\phi}{dx} = -\frac{eN_D}{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0}(W - x)$$

**Step 3 — Integrate again with boundary condition φ = 0 at x = W:**

$$\phi(x) = -\frac{eN_D}{2\varepsilon \varepsilon_0}(W - x)^2$$

**Step 4 — The potential drop across the space-charge region is V_sc = φ(0) − φ(W) = φ(0):**

$$V_{sc} = -\frac{eN_D W^2}{2\varepsilon \varepsilon_0}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for W:**

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon \varepsilon_0 V_{sc}}{eN_D}}$$

**Step 6 — The differential capacitance per unit area is C = dQ/dV_sc, with Q = eN_D W:**

$$C = \frac{d(eN_D W)}{dV_{sc}} = \frac{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0}{W}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute W back into the expression for C:**

$$C = \sqrt{\frac{e \varepsilon \varepsilon_0 N_D}{2 V_{sc}}}$$

**Step 8 — Invert and square (Mott–Schottky form):**

$$\frac{1}{C^2} = \frac{2}{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0 e N_D}\left(V - V_{FB} - \frac{k_B T}{e}\right)$$

**Step 9 — Slope of 1/C² vs. V gives donor density; intercept (with x-axis) gives flat-band potential V_FB:**

$$N_D = \frac{2}{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0 e \cdot \text{slope}}, \quad V_{FB} = V_{\text{intercept}} + \frac{k_B T}{e}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Application:** Measuring Mott–Schottky plots at 1 kHz in 0.5 M Na₂SO₄ gives V_FB ≈ +0.3 V vs. Ag/AgCl for α-Fe₂O₃ photoanodes — this is the potential at which bands are flat (no driving force for charge separation).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Energy Requirement of an Electrolytic Cell

For any industrial electrolytic cell (chlor-alkali, electrowinning, water electrolysis), the actual cell voltage exceeds the thermodynamic voltage due to overpotentials and ohmic drops:

$$V_{cell} = E_{anode} - E_{cathode} + \vert \eta_a \vert + \vert \eta_c \vert + IR_{\text{cell}}$$

**Step 1 — Thermodynamic voltage for chlor-alkali:**

$$E_{cell}^\circ = E_{Cl_2/Cl^-}^\circ - E_{H^+/H_2}^\circ = 1.36 - 0.00 = 1.36 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the overpotentials (typical values for modern membrane cell with DSA):**

$$\vert \eta_{Cl_2} \vert \approx 0.03 \text{ V}, \quad \vert \eta_{H_2} \vert \approx 0.10 \text{ V}$$

$$\text{Ohmic drop} = IR \approx 0.30 \text{ V (membrane + electrolyte)}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the actual cell voltage:**

$$V_{cell} = 1.36 + 0.03 + 0.10 + 0.30 = 1.79 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Energy consumption per ton of Cl₂:**

$$E = \frac{V_{cell} \times nF}{M_{Cl_2}} = \frac{1.79 \times 2 \times 96485}{71 \times 10^3} = 4.86 \text{ kWh/kg Cl}_2$$

$$\text{Per ton: } 4.86 \times 10^3 = 4860 \text{ kWh/ton Cl}_2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Benchmark:** Modern membrane cells using DSA operate at **~2.0–2.5 V** total, consuming **~2200–2500 kWh/ton Cl₂**. This is a 30–40% reduction compared to legacy graphite-anode diaphragm cells (3000–3500 kWh/ton).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Photocurrent Density from a Semiconductor Electrode

For a semiconductor photoelectrode under illumination:

**Step 1 — Photon flux absorbed per unit area:**

$$N_{ph} = \int_{\lambda_g}^{\lambda_{UV}} \Phi(\lambda)\, d\lambda$$

where $\Phi(\lambda)$ is the incident spectral photon flux and $\lambda_g = 1240/E_g$ (nm).

**Step 2 — Rate of electron–hole pair generation:**

$$G = N_{ph} \times \eta_{abs}$$

**Step 3 — Photocurrent density (assuming every collected electron contributes to external current):**

$$j_{ph} = e \cdot G \cdot \eta_{sep} \cdot \eta_{inj}$$

where:
- $\eta_{abs}$ = absorption efficiency
- $\eta_{sep}$ = charge separation efficiency (depends on band bending)
- $\eta_{inj}$ = charge injection efficiency at the SC–electrolyte interface

**Step 4 — For α-Fe₂O₃ under AM 1.5G (100 mW/cm²) sunlight:**

- $E_g = 2.1$ eV → $\lambda_g = 590$ nm
- $\eta_{abs} \approx 0.40$ (only photons < 590 nm are useful)
- $\eta_{sep} \approx 0.30$ (poor hole transport in hematite)
- $\eta_{inj} \approx 0.60$ (slow water oxidation kinetics)

$$j_{ph} = (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \times (1.2 \times 10^{17}) \times 0.40 \times 0.30 \times 0.60$$

$$j_{ph} \approx 1.4 \text{ mA/cm}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a realistic upper-limit photocurrent for hematite — the experimental record (2023) is ~4–5 mA/cm² at 1.23 V vs. RHE using nanostructured hematite with surface catalysts.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing an Insoluble Electrode

| If your electrolyte is... | And your goal is... | Choose... | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concentrated NaCl (brine) | Cl₂ production | Ti/RuO₂–TiO₂ DSA | Low η_Cl₂, long life, dimensionally stable |
| H₂SO₄ (acidic) | Zn/Mn electrowinning | Pb–Ag or PbO₂ | High O₂ overpotential, stable in acid |
| H₂SO₄ / Na₂SO₄ | Organic pollutant destruction | BDD or Sb-SnO₂ | Highest ·OH generation, complete mineralization |
| Seawater / soil | Cathodic protection | Ti/MMO (Ir-Ta) | Robust, high current capacity, 20+ yr life |
| Alkaline (KOH) | H₂ production | Ni or Ni-Mo (Raney) | Low η_H₂, cheap, stable in alkali |
| Sunlight + water | H₂ from photoelectrolysis | α-Fe₂O₃, BiVO₄, WO₃ | Suitable band gap, stable, earth-abundant |
| Lab / sensor work | Reference electrode | Pt, Ag/AgCl, SCE | Stable, reproducible potential |
| Molten salts / high T | Specialty electrolysis | Graphite (inert) | Survives extreme conditions |

## 4.7 Mermaid Diagram 7 — Conceptual Band Diagram of n-type Semiconductor Photoanode

```mermaid
graph TD
    VB["Valence Band VB"]
    CB["Conduction Band CB"]
    EF["Fermi Level EF"]
    Eg["Band Gap Eg = 2.1 eV for Fe2O3"]
    REDOX["Electrolyte Redox Level"]

    VB -->|"Filled with electrons"| ELEC1["e-"]
    CB -->|"Empty or partially filled"| ELEC2["e-"]
    EF -->|"n-type: just below CB"| DOPE["Donor Level"]

    VB -.->|"h+ generated here"| OXRXN["Drives Oxidation: H2O → O2"]
    CB -.->|"e- conducted away"| REDRXN["Drives Reduction: H+ → H2"]
    EF -.-> REDOX
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** The band gap Eg is the energy separation between the top of the valence band (VB) and the bottom of the conduction band (CB). For a semiconductor photoelectrode to drive water splitting, the CB minimum must lie **above the H⁺/H₂ level** and the VB maximum must lie **below the O₂/H₂O level**, with Eg > 1.23 eV (the thermodynamic minimum).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"What are insoluble electrodes? Give two examples and state the criteria used to select an insoluble anode for industrial use."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ≈ 80 words):**

Insoluble (inert) electrodes are electrodes that do not dissolve or participate chemically in the electrochemical reaction during cell operation. They act purely as surfaces for electron transfer.

**Examples:** (any two) Platinum, graphite, PbO₂, Ti/RuO₂–TiO₂ (DSA), BDD.

**Selection criteria (any 2 for full marks):**
1. High chemical and electrochemical stability in the operating electrolyte.
2. High electrical conductivity with low overpotential for the desired reaction.
3. Good mechanical strength and coating adhesion.
4. Long service life and low cost.

*(Mark distribution: Definition — 1M, Examples — 1M, Criteria — 1M.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Explain why graphite anodes were replaced by DSA in chlor-alkali cells."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ≈ 70 words):**

Graphite anodes suffer from **continuous anodic consumption** via the reaction:
$\text{C} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^-$

This leads to (i) loss of anode material (2–4 kg C per ton Cl₂), (ii) contamination of Cl₂ gas with CO₂, (iii) need for frequent anode replacement, and (iv) high chlorine overpotential (~0.3–0.5 V) leading to high cell voltage.

**DSA (Ti/RuO₂–TiO₂)** solved all these problems: dimensionally stable, low η_Cl₂ (~0.03 V), no carbon contamination, service life > 5 years, and lower energy consumption (~2300 vs. 3500 kWh/ton Cl₂).

*(Mark distribution: Graphite problem — 1.5M, DSA advantage — 1.5M.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECHT417]**
**(a)** Discuss in detail the **construction, working and applications of Dimensionally Stable Anodes (DSA)**. Explain the role of each component of the coating. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **electrodeposition of PbO₂ on a titanium substrate** and list its advantages and limitations as an insoluble anode. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**1. Definition & Invention (1 mark):**
DSA (Dimensionally Stable Anode), invented by **Henri Beer (1965)**, is a coated metal anode consisting of a **titanium substrate** with an active **mixed metal oxide (MMO)** coating. It revolutionized the chlor-alkali industry.

**2. Construction (2 marks):**
- **Substrate:** Commercially pure titanium (Grade 1 or 2), shaped as mesh, plate, rod, or expanded metal. Ti is chosen because of its strong, passivating TiO₂ film that protects the metal in cell-off conditions.
- **Surface Preparation:** The Ti is degreased, etched in oxalic acid or HCl to create a rough, micro-etched surface (~5–10 μm roughness) for better coating adhesion.
- **Coating Precursor:** A solution of metal chlorides (e.g., RuCl₃ + TiCl₄ in isopropanol) is brushed, sprayed, or dipped onto the substrate.
- **Thermal Decomposition:** The coated Ti is heated in air/furnace at 400–500 °C, converting the chlorides to oxides. This is repeated 8–15 times to build a 1–10 μm coating.
- **Final Annealing:** Last coat is annealed at ~450 °C for 1 h to crystallize the oxides (rutile-type RuO₂–TiO₂ solid solution).

**3. Role of Each Component (2 marks):**
- **RuO₂ (active catalyst):** 30 mol% — provides the electrocatalytic sites for Cl₂ evolution. Low Cl₂ overpotential.
- **TiO₂ (inert stabilizer):** 70 mol% — prevents RuO₂ from dissolving; provides mechanical robustness; forms a solid solution that prevents crack propagation.
- **Ti substrate (current collector):** Conducts current; the TiO₂ passive film at the coating–substrate interface prevents further oxidation of Ti metal and ensures the coating "floats" on a stable base.

**4. Working (1 mark):**
When current is passed through the DSA in brine (NaCl solution), the RuO₂ surface catalyzes the reaction:
$$2\text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{Cl}_2 + 2e^-$$
with very low overpotential (~0.03 V at 10 mA/cm²).

**5. Applications (1 mark):**
- Chlor-alkali (Cl₂, NaOH, H₂)
- Cathodic protection of pipelines and ships
- Electrowinning of Zn, Cu, Mn
- Water electrolysis
- Electrochemical wastewater treatment (with appropriate coatings)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**1. PbO₂ Anode — Introduction (0.5 mark):**
Lead dioxide is a non-stoichiometric, highly conductive metal oxide. Two polymorphs: **α-PbO₂** (orthorhombic) and **β-PbO₂** (tetragonal, more conductive, more stable in acid).

**2. Electrodeposition Procedure (3 marks — see neat sketch in 4.2):**
- **Substrate preparation:** Titanium plate is mechanically polished → degreased in acetone → etched in hot oxalic acid (10%, 90 °C, 1 h) → washed.
- **Electrolyte:** 0.5–1.0 M Pb(NO₃)₂ in 1–3 M HNO₃.
- **Counter electrode:** Pb or stainless steel plate.
- **Conditions:** Anodic current density 5–20 mA/cm²; temperature 50–60 °C; deposition time 30–60 min.
- **Reaction at anode:** $\text{Pb}^{2+} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$
- **Result:** Black, adherent β-PbO₂ film (~50–200 μm) on Ti.

**3. Advantages (2 marks):**
- ✅ Low cost compared to noble metals.
- ✅ High oxygen overpotential (~0.5–0.6 V) — ideal for anodic oxidation reactions without O₂ loss.
- ✅ Excellent corrosion resistance in acidic sulfate and nitrate media.
- ✅ Good electrical conductivity (~10⁴ S/cm).
- ✅ Catalyzes oxidation of organics (M, SO₂, Mn²⁺ → MnO₂).

**4. Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- ❌ Toxic — risk of Pb²⁺ leaching.
- ❌ Brittle, prone to cracking under thermal/mechanical stress.
- ❌ Poor adhesion to Ti substrate over long service; flakes off.
- ❌ Heavy (ρ ≈ 9.4 g/cm³).
- ❌ Loses activity due to surface fouling by MnO₂ or organic deposits.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECHT417]**
**(a)** Explain the **classification, preparation, and characteristics of semiconducting electrodes** with examples. Discuss their role in **photoelectrochemical water splitting.** (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare **graphite, lead dioxide, and DSA anodes** with respect to (i) anodic stability, (ii) overpotential for Cl₂ evolution, (iii) service life, and (iv) cost. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**1. Definition of Semiconducting Electrodes (1 mark):**
Electrodes whose electronic properties are governed by **band theory** — having a **valence band (VB)**, **conduction band (CB)**, and a characteristic **band gap (Eg)**. Charge transport occurs via electrons (n-type) or holes (p-type), not ionic conduction.

**2. Classification (2 marks):**

| Type | Carriers | Examples | Typical Eg |
|---|---|---|---|
| **n-type** | Electrons in CB | TiO₂, SnO₂, Fe₂O₃, WO₃, ZnO | 2.0–3.6 eV |
| **p-type** | Holes in VB | NiO, Cu₂O, Co₃O₄, Si | 1.0–2.5 eV |
| **Wide-band-gap** | Both (doped) | BDD (~5.5 eV), SiC (~3.2 eV) | > 3.0 eV |

**3. Preparation Methods (1 mark):**
- **Sol-gel** (e.g., TiO₂, Fe₂O₃ thin films)
- **Chemical vapor deposition (CVD)** (e.g., BDD, SiC)
- **Hydrothermal synthesis** (nanostructured α-Fe₂O₃)
- **Sputtering / Pulsed laser deposition** (epitaxial oxide films)
- **Anodic oxidation** of metal substrates (TiO₂ nanotubes on Ti)

**4. Characteristics (1.5 marks):**
- Band gap, charge carrier density, flat-band potential, mobility, recombination lifetime.
- **Photo-response** under illumination (incident-photon-to-current efficiency, IPCE).
- **Electrochemical window** — wide for BDD (> 3 V), narrow for narrow-gap semiconductors.
- **Stability** in the chosen electrolyte (e.g., Fe₂O₃ stable in alkali, unstable in acid).

**5. Role in Photoelectrochemical Water Splitting (1.5 marks):**
Under illumination (hν > Eg), electron–hole pairs are generated. At an **n-type photoanode**:
$$2h^+_{VB} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+$$
At a **p-type photocathode** (or Pt counter electrode):
$$2e^-_{CB} + 2\text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{H}_2$$

The net reaction: $\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$ using only sunlight.

Requirements: (i) Eg > 1.23 eV, (ii) CB > H⁺/H₂ level, (iii) VB < O₂/H₂O level, (iv) good charge separation, (v) stable under illumination.

**Best-studied materials:** α-Fe₂O₃ (Eg = 2.1 eV, visible light), BiVO₄ (Eg = 2.4 eV), Cu₂O (Eg = 2.0 eV) protected by a thin buffer layer.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (7 marks — 1.75 each criterion):**

| Criterion | Graphite | Lead Dioxide (PbO₂) | DSA (Ti/RuO₂–TiO₂) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Anodic stability** | Poor — oxidized to CO₂ (2–4 kg/ton Cl₂) | Good in H₂SO₄/HNO₃; brittle, flakes | Excellent — no measurable consumption |
| **(ii) Cl₂ overpotential** | High (~0.3–0.5 V at 10 mA/cm²) | Not used for Cl₂ (high O₂ η, not Cl₂-selective) | Very low (~0.03 V at 10 mA/cm²) |
| **(iii) Service life** | Short — replaced every 6–12 months | 1–3 years (depending on substrate adhesion) | 5–10 years (industrial benchmark) |
| **(iv) Cost** | Very low (~₹200/kg) | Moderate (~₹800–1500/kg) | High initial (~₹25,000/m²) but lowest per-ton Cl₂ cost |

**Concluding remark (Bonus):** DSA dominates modern chlor-alkali because the **higher upfront cost is amortized** by lower energy consumption (2300 vs. 3500 kWh/ton Cl₂) and zero anode replacement. Graphite survives only in niche applications (molten salt electrolysis, certain electrometallurgical processes). PbO₂ is favored in zinc and manganese electrowinning.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "insoluble" with "inert in all conditions."** No electrode is truly inert — DSA eventually loses RuO₂, PbO₂ eventually cracks, even Pt slowly dissolves at very high anodic potentials. The term is *operational*, not absolute. **(–1 mark if over-claimed)**
> 2. **Forgetting the TiO₂ passive film in DSA.** Many students describe the coating but forget to mention that the **stability of DSA during current interruption** depends on the TiO₂ film beneath the coating. **(–0.5 to –1 mark)**
> 3. **Writing DSA as "Dimensionally Stable Alloy" or "Direct Solid Anode."** Correct term: **Dimensionally Stable Anode (or Anode for Oxygen evolution in chlor-alkali cells).** **(–0.5 mark)**
> 4. **Mixing up anodic vs. cathodic applications.** PbO₂ is an **anode** in acidic media; Fe₃O₄ is a **cathode** in chlor-alkali. Confusing these costs marks. **(–1 mark)**
> 5. **Skipping band diagrams in semiconductor electrode questions.** KTU examiners *expect* a labelled band diagram showing CB, VB, Eg, EF, and the redox level. **(–2 marks if missing)**
> 6. **Not mentioning F. Haber, L. Kandler, or Henri Beer** in descriptive DSA questions — full credit requires historical context. **(–0.5 mark)**
> 7. **Using "graphite electrode" and "carbon electrode" interchangeably.** Graphite is anisotropic; glassy carbon, activated carbon, and carbon felt are *different materials*. **(–0.5 mark)**
> 8. **Calculation errors in Tafel / Nernst numerical questions** — forgetting to convert ln to log₁₀ (factor 2.303) is the most common mistake. **(–1 mark)**
> 9. **Writing cell voltage = E°** — students forget to add overpotentials and ohmic drops. **(–2 marks on a 14-mark question)**
> 10. **Forgetting units in energy-consumption problems** (kWh/ton vs. kWh/kg vs. MJ/kg). **(–0.5 to –1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 📋 Rapid-Revision Checklist

**A. Noble Metal Electrodes**
- ⭐ Pt is the benchmark inert electrode; limited by cost; develops PtO/PtO₂ at high anodic potential.
- ⭐ Au is even more inert but a poor HER/OER catalyst.
- ⭐ Use in sensors, reference electrodes, and as thin coatings on Ti.

**B. Graphite Electrodes**
- ⭐ Anisotropic conductivity (∥ to layers ≈ 10⁴ S/cm).
- ⭐ **Slowly consumed by anodic oxidation to CO₂** — not truly "insoluble."
- ⭐ Largely replaced by DSA in chlor-alkali; survives in molten-salt and high-temperature processes.

**C. Lead Dioxide (PbO₂) Electrodes**
- ⭐ Two polymorphs: α (orthorhombic) and β (tetragonal, more conductive).
- ⭐ Excellent O₂ overpotential in acid; ideal for Zn/Mn electrowinning.
- ⭐ Toxic and brittle — main limitations.
- ⭐ Electrodeposited from Pb(NO₃)₂ in HNO₃ onto Pb, Ti, or graphite.

**D. Titanium Substrate Insoluble Electrodes (DSA)**
- ⭐ Invented by **Henri Beer (1965)**; revolutionized chlor-alkali.
- ⭐ Structure: Ti substrate / TiO₂ passive film / RuO₂–TiO₂ (or IrO₂–Ta₂O₅) coating.
- ⭐ Very low Cl₂ overpotential (~0.03 V); service life 5–10 years.
- ⭐ Failure modes: coating dissolution, substrate passivation, mechanical erosion, MnO₂ poisoning.
- ⭐ **NEVER operate as a cathode** — Ti hydride forms and destroys the coating.

**E. Iron Oxide Electrodes**
- ⭐ Fe₃O₄ (magnetite): inverse spinel, mixed-valence, conductive via Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ hopping.
- ⭐ Used as cathodes in diaphragm chlor-alkali cells.
- ⭐ α-Fe₂O₃ (hematite): prominent photoanode for solar water splitting (Eg ≈ 2.1 eV).

**F. Semiconducting Type Electrodes**
- ⭐ n-type (TiO₂, SnO₂, Fe₂O₃) → photoanodes (oxidation).
- ⭐ p-type (NiO, Cu₂O) → photocathodes (reduction).
- ⭐ BDD: highest O₂ overpotential, widest electrochemical window, best ·OH generator for AOPs.
- ⭐ Mott–Schottky equation: $\dfrac{1}{C^2} = \dfrac{2}{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0 e N_D}\left(V - V_{FB} - \dfrac{k_BT}{e}\right)$ — gives N_D and V_FB.

**G. Numerical Quick-Facts**
- ⭐ 1.36 V → theoretical E° for chlor-alkali.
- ⭐ 1.23 V → theoretical E° for water electrolysis.
- ⭐ 0.0592 V → (2.303 RT/F) at 25 °C; appears in Nernst equation.
- ⭐ 0.12 V/dec → Tafel slope for 2e⁻ transfer (α = 0.5).
- ⭐ 0.03 V/dec → Tafel slope for 2e⁻ transfer (α = 0.2) — typical for DSA Cl₂.
- ⭐ 2300 kWh/ton Cl₂ → modern DSA membrane cell.
- ⭐ 1240/Eg (nm) → absorption edge wavelength.

**H. Industrial Process Mapping (Must Memorize)**
- ⭐ **Chlor-alkali** → Ti/RuO₂–TiO₂ (membrane cell).
- ⭐ **Zn electrowinning** → Pb–Ag (1% Ag) anodes in H₂SO₄.
- ⭐ **MnO₂ production** → PbO₂ or Ti/PbO₂ anodes.
- ⭐ **Cathodic protection (ICCP)** → Ti/MMO (Ir-Ta) anodes.
- ⭐ **Water treatment (AOPs)** → BDD, Sb-SnO₂, PbO₂.
- ⭐ **Alkaline water electrolysis** → Ni or Ni-Mo (Raney) cathodes.
- ⭐ **Solar H₂ (photoelectrolysis)** → α-Fe₂O₃, BiVO₄, Cu₂O/TiO₂.

**I. Common Confusions to Avoid**
- ⭐ "Insoluble" ≠ "inert in all conditions" — every electrode has operational limits.
- ⭐ Graphite is **not truly insoluble**; it oxidizes to CO₂.
- ⭐ DSA is **not the same as Pt** — it is a coated composite.
- ⭐ PbO₂ is a **semiconductor** (non-stoichiometric oxide) but discussed in metal-oxide context here.
- ⭐ BDD is a **carbon allotrope**, not a metal oxide.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal Finishing - Cell Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT417 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Metal Finishing - Cell Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Metal Finishing** refers to the broad family of electrochemical surface treatment processes that alter the surface properties of a metallic substrate — including its corrosion resistance, wear resistance, electrical conductivity, reflectivity, solderability, and aesthetic appeal. In the KTU 2024 scheme, *Cell Design* specifically refers to the **engineered configuration of an electrochemical reactor (cell)** in which these surface-altering reactions are deliberately driven under controlled mass, charge, and momentum transport conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:**
> An *electrochemical cell for metal finishing* is a **heterogeneous reactor** comprising an **anode**, a **cathode (workpiece)**, an **electrolyte (ionic conductor)**, and an external **DC power source (rectifier)** — engineered to deliver a uniform, defect-free, and adherent metallic or oxide coating on the substrate.

The cell is classified into one of two thermodynamic camps:

| Cell Class | Energy Conversion | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrolytic Cell** | Electrical → Chemical (non-spontaneous) | Electroplating, Anodizing, Electrowinning |
| **Electrogalvanic Cell** | Chemical → Electrical (spontaneous) | Sacrificial anode cathodic protection |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Painted Wall" Metaphor

Imagine you are spray-painting a steel wall. To get a **smooth, uniform, defect-free coat**, you must control three things:

1. **The spray gun angle & distance** (analogous to **current distribution** between anode and cathode).
2. **The paint viscosity and flow rate** (analogous to **electrolyte conductivity & mass transport**).
3. **The number of spray guns and their geometry around the wall** (analogous to **cell geometry & anode-cathode placement**).

If any one of these is mis-tuned, the wall gets *streaks* (uneven thickness), *drips* (burnt deposits), or *bare patches* (missed zones). **Cell Design is essentially "electrospray-gun engineering."**

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$ (≈ 96,500 C/mol is the KTU-accepted rounded value).
- **Standard Temperature:** $T = 298.15 \text{ K}$ unless otherwise stated.
- **Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$.
- **Coating Thickness Unit:** $\mu m$ (micrometer); current density in $\text{A/dm}^2$.
- **Plating Rate Metric:** $\mu m/\text{min}$ — the **board examiner's favourite unit**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PECHT417 (KTU 2024) explicitly demands mastery of (i) primary, secondary, and tertiary current distribution, (ii) throwing power, (iii) cell geometries, and (iv) the **Hull Cell** as a diagnostic tool. All four are covered in these notes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Metal-Finishing Cell

Every metal-finishing cell is built from **five mandatory subsystems**:

1. **Anode** — the source of depositing metal ions (e.g., a pure Ni bar in nickel plating) or an *inert* anode (e.g., Pb in chrome plating).
2. **Cathode / Workpiece** — the substrate to be plated (must be conductive, clean, and often pre-treated).
3. **Electrolyte / Plating Bath** — aqueous solution of metal salts + additives (brighteners, levelers, wetting agents).
4. **Power Supply (Rectifier)** — delivers smooth DC; modern KTU-recommended units have **ripple < 5%**.
5. **Auxiliary Hardware** — tank, heating/cooling coils, agitation system (air, mechanical, cathode-rod), filtration, and **bus bars**.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Cell Design

### Pillar I — Thermodynamic Potential
Every electrochemical reaction obeys the **Nernst Equation**:

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{a_{\text{ox}}}{a_{\text{red}}}\right)$$

- $E^{\circ}$ → **Standard electrode potential** (tabulated at 25 °C).
- $n$ → electrons transferred per mole of reaction.
- $a$ → activity of species.
- For **dilute plating baths** (e.g., $0.1$ M CuSO₄), activities are replaced by concentrations with reasonable accuracy.

### Pillar II — Kinetic Overpotential
The **Butler–Volmer** framework defines the activation overpotential $\eta$ as the extra voltage needed to drive the reaction at a finite current density $i$:

$$\eta = E - E_{eq} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$

- $i_0$ → **exchange current density** (intrinsic reaction rate).
- $\alpha$ → **charge transfer coefficient** (typically $0.3$ to $0.7$).
- At *high* $\vert \eta \vert$ ($> 50$ mV), this reduces to the **Tafel equation**:

$$\vert \eta \vert = a + b \log_{10} i, \quad \text{where} \quad b = \frac{2.303\, RT}{\alpha n F}$$

### Pillar III — Mass Transport
The **limiting current density** $i_L$ sets the **ceiling** for the rate at which ions can reach the cathode:

$$i_L = n\, F\, k_m\, C_b$$

- $k_m$ → **mass transfer coefficient** (m/s), dependent on flow regime.
- $C_b$ → bulk concentration of the depositing ion (mol/m³).

## 2.3 Current Distribution — The Heart of Cell Design

This is the **highest-weightage topic** in the KTU board exam for Module 4. The cell designer must predict how current lines spread between the anode and cathode.

### (a) Primary Current Distribution
Governed **purely by geometry and electrolyte resistance**; all kinetics are ignored.

$$\nabla^{2}\phi = 0 \quad \text{(Laplace equation, no concentration or activation effects)}$$

- Result: current crowds at **edges and corners** (the *edge effect*).
- Throwing power = **poor**.

### (b) Secondary Current Distribution
Adds **activation overpotential** (kinetic resistance) into the picture:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} = -\frac{i_0}{k}\left[\exp\left(\frac{\alpha n F \eta_a}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(\frac{-(1-\alpha)n F \eta_c}{RT}\right)\right]$$

- The extra kinetic resistance **smooths out the current**; throwing power improves.

### (c) Tertiary Current Distribution
The **most realistic model** — incorporates *both* activation overpotential **and** concentration overpotential.

$$i = i_0\left[\frac{C_s}{C_b}\exp\left(\frac{\alpha n F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(\frac{-(1-\alpha) n F \eta}{RT}\right)\right]$$
