- $C_s$ → surface concentration (≠ bulk when $i \to i_L$).
- When $C_s \to 0$, $i \to i_L$: the reaction is **mass-transport limited**.

## 2.4 Throwing Power (TP)

A *quantitative* measure of how uniformly a bath deposits metal on a complex-shaped cathode.

$$TP = \frac{(L - M)}{(L + M - 2)} \times 100 \text{ \%}$$

- $L$ = ratio of distances (far : near) — e.g., $L = 5$ for a Hull-cell panel.
- $M$ = ratio of measured coating thicknesses (far : near).

| TP Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| **100 %** | Perfect throwing (impossible in practice) |
| **> 50 %** | Good — preferred for complex parts |
| **0 %** | No throwing — only closest point gets plated |
| **< 0 %** | Unfavorable — far point thicker than near (rare) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick:** When asked *"Which bath has better throwing power — acid copper or cyanide copper?"* — the answer is **cyanide copper**, because the lower $i_0$ (slower intrinsic kinetics) means activation overpotential dominates over ohmic drop, giving smoother distribution.

## 2.5 Cell Voltage Balance

The total rectifier voltage the cell needs is:

$$V_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode} + \vert \eta_{c} \vert + \vert \eta_{a} \vert + IR_{electrolyte} + IR_{circuit}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Cell design directly controls **energy consumption (kWh per kg of deposited metal)**. Industrial decorative chrome plating, for instance, consumes roughly **15–20 kWh/kg**, while industrial copper refining uses only **2–3 kWh/kg** — a 10× difference driven almost entirely by cell-voltage optimization.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln(a_{\text{ox}}/a_{\text{red}})$ | Nernst equilibrium potential | V |
| 2 | $\eta = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln(i/i_0)$ | Butler–Volmer activation overpotential | V |
| 3 | $\eta = a + b \log_{10} i$ | Tafel equation (high $\eta$) | V |
| 4 | $b = \frac{2.303\, RT}{\alpha n F}$ | Tafel slope | V/decade |
| 5 | $i_L = n F k_m C_b$ | Limiting current density | A/m² |
| 6 | $\nabla^{2}\phi = 0$ | Primary current distribution (Laplace) | — |
| 7 | $TP = \frac{L - M}{L + M - 2} \times 100$ | Throwing power | % |
| 8 | $V = I \times R$ | Ohmic drop across electrolyte | V |
| 9 | $m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass deposited (Faraday's law) | kg |
| 10 | $d = \frac{i \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | Theoretical coating thickness | m |
| 11 | $i = I/A$ | Average current density | A/m² |
| 12 | $Q = I \cdot t$ | Total charge passed | C |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Faraday's Law of Electrolysis (Mass of Deposit)

Starting from the first principles of charge transport:

> **Logic Step 0 — Setup:**
> We know that $1$ mole of electrons carries a charge of $F$ coulombs (Faraday's constant), and a reaction involving $n$ electrons per mole of metal deposits $1$ mole of metal.

**Step 1:** Total charge passed through the cell is the product of current and time:

$$Q = I \cdot t$$

**Step 2:** Number of moles of electrons transferred is charge divided by Faraday's constant:

$$n_{e^-} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F}$$

**Step 3:** Since $n$ electrons are needed to deposit one atom of metal M (e.g., $n = 2$ for Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu), moles of metal deposited is:

$$n_{M} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 4:** Converting moles to mass via molar mass $M$ (in kg/mol):

$$\boxed{m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}}$$

**Step 5:** Validity check for a typical KTU problem — copper plating at 5 A for 1 hour:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{5 \text{ A} \times 3600 \text{ s} \times 63.55 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}}{2 \times 96485 \text{ C/mol}} \\
  &= \frac{1{,}143{,}900 \times 10^{-3}}{192{,}970} \\
  &= \frac{1143.9}{192{,}970} \\
  &= 5.928 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} \\
  &= 5.93 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Note:** This 5.93 g is the *theoretical* mass. Real plating cells deliver only **90–98 %** of this value, the loss being quantified as **current efficiency** $\varepsilon$.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Theoretical Coating Thickness

The board examiner frequently asks: *"What thickness will be deposited in $t$ minutes at current density $i$?"*

**Step 1:** Mass deposited per unit cathode area (from Faraday's law):

$$\frac{m}{A} = \frac{i \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 2:** Replace mass per area with thickness $\times$ density ($d \cdot \rho$):

$$d \cdot \rho = \frac{i \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for thickness:

$$\boxed{d = \frac{i \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}}$$

**Step 4:** Plausible number for nickel plating: $i = 5 \text{ A/dm}^2$, $t = 20 \text{ min}$, $M = 58.69 \text{ g/mol}$, $n = 2$, $\rho = 8.9 \text{ g/cm}^3$.

First convert $i$ to SI: $5 \text{ A/dm}^2 = 500 \text{ A/m}^2$. Then $t = 1200$ s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{500 \times 1200 \times 58.69 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 96485 \times 8900} \\
  &= \frac{35{,}214}{1.7174 \times 10^{9}} \\
  &= 2.05 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \\
  &= 20.5 \ \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Note:** This matches the textbook range for *bright nickel* decorative plating (15–25 µm). The derivation is the **single most-tested numerical in KTU Module 4.**

## 3.3 Derivation III — Limiting Current Density from Nernst Diffusion Layer

**Step 1:** Start from Fick's first law at the cathode surface:

$$J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}$$

**Step 2:** Approximate the linear gradient inside the stagnant Nernst diffusion layer of thickness $\delta_N$:

$$J = D \frac{(C_b - C_s)}{\delta_N}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by $nF$ to convert flux $J$ (mol/m²·s) to current density $i$:

$$i = n F D \frac{(C_b - C_s)}{\delta_N}$$

**Step 4:** The **maximum** possible current is reached when $C_s \to 0$ (all ions are consumed at the surface):

$$\boxed{i_L = \frac{n F D C_b}{\delta_N}}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $k_m = D / \delta_N$ recovers the compact form $i_L = n F k_m C_b$ from Section 2.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Tafel Slope from Butler–Volmer

**Step 1:** Start from the full Butler–Volmer expression for a cathodic reaction:

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha n F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(\frac{(1-\alpha) n F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

**Step 2:** For *large negative* overpotential, the first exponential dominates:

$$i \approx i_0 \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha n F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Take natural log of both sides:

$$\ln(i) = \ln(i_0) - \frac{\alpha n F \eta}{RT}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\eta$:

$$\eta = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \left[ \ln(i_0) - \ln(i) \right] = -\frac{2.303\, RT}{\alpha n F} \log_{10}\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Define $a = -\frac{2.303\, RT}{\alpha n F} \log_{10}(i_0)$ and $b = \frac{2.303\, RT}{\alpha n F}$ to recover:

$$\boxed{\eta = a + b \log_{10}(i)}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Decision Tree

| Engineering Goal | Dominant Constraint | Recommended Cell Type | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Uniform coating on complex geometry | Tertiary distribution | **Hull Cell (267 mL)** + leveling additives | Tests empirically at lab scale |
| High-speed bulk plating | Mass transport limit | **Rotating Cylinder Electrode (RCE)** or **Jet cell** | Forced convection raises $k_m$ |
| High-purity precious metal recovery | Selectivity | **Parallel plate cell with membrane** | Membrane blocks interfering ions |
| Anodizing (thick oxide) | Heat dissipation | **Tank cell with cooling coil + agitation** | Exothermic; needs thermal control |
| Continuous strip plating | Edge-effect control | **Multiple anodes, shaped conformably** | Suppresses edge-burn |
| Electrowinning from leach | Energy efficiency | **Packed-bed or flow-through cathode** | High surface area → low $i$ per unit area |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** When the question says *"design a cell for…"*, your answer MUST explicitly mention: **(i) anode-cathode area ratio**, **(ii) inter-electrode gap**, **(iii) agitation method**, and **(iv) expected current density range**. Skipping any one of these costs 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Metal-Finishing Cell — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PSU[DC Rectifier Power Supply] -->|positive lead| BUS[Busbar Anode Side]
    PSU -->|negative lead| WBC[Busbar Cathode Side]
    BUS --> AN[Anode Bar M Source]
    AN --> BATH[Plating Bath Electrolyte with additives]
    BATH --> CAT[Workpiece Cathode Substrate]
    CAT --> WBC
    AGIT[Agitation System air or mechanical] --> BATH
    HX[Heat Exchanger Cooling and Heating Coil] --> BATH
    FILT[Filtration Loop 1 to 5 micron] --> BATH
    CTRL[Process Controller current density temperature pH] --> PSU
    CTRL --> AGIT
    CTRL --> HX
```

## 4.2 Cell-Design Decision Flow (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Plating Objective] --> B{Geometry of Workpiece}
    B -->|Flat sheet| C[Parallel Plate Cell]
    B -->|Rod or wire| D[Rotating Cylinder Cell]
    B -->|Complex 3D part| E[Hull Cell for Optimization]
    B -->|Continuous strip| F[Conveyorized Jet Cell]
    C --> G[Select Current Density]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute Nernst and Overpotential]
    H --> I[Compute Cell Voltage Vcell]
    I --> J[Specify Rectifier Rating]
    J --> K[Validate Throwing Power TP]
    K --> L[Final Cell Layout]
```

## 4.3 Hull Cell Schematic (Standard 267 mL Trapezoidal)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HULL[Hull Cell 267 mL Trapezoidal Cathode Panel]
        direction LR
        AN1[Insoluble Anode Ni or Pb] -->|near gap 1 inch| CP1[Close Point High i]
        AN1 -->|far gap 5 inch| CP2[Far Point Low i]
        CP1 --> RECT[DC Rectifier 0 to 10 A]
        CP2 --> RECT
    end
    RECT -->|test current 2 A for 5 min| PANEL[Coated Panel Read TP]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A **Hull cell** is a trapezoidal mini-plating bath where the cathode is placed at an angle. The inter-electrode distance varies continuously (typically from $\approx 1$ inch to $\approx 5$ inch), so a *single panel run* reveals the bath's behavior over a **wide range of current densities** — usually **0 to 80 A/dm²**. This is the KTU board's favourite "lab-scale cell design" example.

## 4.4 Current Distribution Modes — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cell Operating Conditions] --> B[Primary Distribution Geometry only]
    A --> C[Secondary Distribution Geometry plus Activation]
    A --> D[Tertiary Distribution Geometry plus Activation plus Mass Transport]
    B --> E[Sharp edge crowding Throwing Power poor]
    C --> F[Smoother than Primary Throwing Power moderate]
    D --> G[Most realistic Throwing Power good for low iL]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State Faraday's first law of electrolysis and give its mathematical form.**

**Model Answer:**
Faraday's first law states that the mass of substance liberated or deposited at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.
Mathematically:

$$m \propto Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = Z \cdot I \cdot t = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

where $Z$ is the *electrochemical equivalent* (kg/C), $I$ is current in amperes, $t$ is time in seconds, $M$ is molar mass, $n$ is electrons per mole, and $F$ is Faraday's constant ($96{,}485$ C/mol).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between primary, secondary, and tertiary current distributions.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Primary | Secondary | Tertiary |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Considers geometry** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Considers activation overpotential** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Considers concentration overpotential** | No | No | Yes |
| **Governing equation** | $\nabla^{2}\phi = 0$ | Includes Butler–Volmer | Full mass + charge transfer |
| **Throwing power** | Worst | Moderate | Best (low $i_L$ regime) |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Hull Cell**. Explain how it is used to optimize plating bath parameters.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A brass-plating bath operates at a cathode current density of $3 \text{ A/dm}^2$ for 25 minutes. Calculate (i) the theoretical mass of brass deposited, and (ii) the average coating thickness. Given: brass composition effectively behaves as Cu (molar mass = 63.5 g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho = 8.96$ g/cm³), current efficiency = 92 %.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
The Hull cell is a **trapezoidal plating bath** holding 267 mL of electrolyte. It has an **insoluble (or soluble) anode** placed parallel to the slanted cathode panel. The cathode (a polished metal test panel) is held at an angle such that the **anode-to-cathode distance varies from about 1 inch (25 mm) at the near end to about 5 inches (127 mm) at the far end**.

**Working (2 marks):**
A small DC current (typically 1–5 A) is passed for a fixed time (5–10 min). Because current density $i \propto 1/d$ (where $d$ is the inter-electrode distance), the **near end of the panel receives high $i$** and the **far end receives low $i$**. A *single panel* thus displays the deposit quality over a continuous range of current densities (often 0–80 A/dm²).

**Optimization Use (2 marks):**
- **Brightness range** of the panel indicates the workable $i$ window.
- **Burning or pitting** at the near end shows the upper limit.
- **No-deposit / dullness** at the far end shows the lower limit.
- Hull-cell trials determine optimal **additive concentration, pH, temperature, and agitation** before scaling to a production cell.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Total charge passed:**
The area is $1 \text{ dm}^2$ (since current density is per dm²). So $I = 3$ A.

$$Q = I \cdot t = 3 \text{ A} \times (25 \times 60) \text{ s} = 4500 \text{ C}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical mass (Faraday's law, no efficiency):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{theor}} &= \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \\
&= \frac{63.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol} \times 3 \times 1500}{2 \times 96485} \\
&= \frac{285.75}{192970} \\
&= 1.481 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} \\
&= 1.481 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual mass (with 92 % current efficiency):**

$$m_{\text{actual}} = 0.92 \times 1.481 = 1.362 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4 — Thickness via $d = \frac{m}{A \cdot \rho}$:**
Cathode area = $1 \text{ dm}^2 = 100 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{1.362 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg}}{10^{-2} \text{ m}^2 \times 8960 \text{ kg/m}^3} \\
  &= \frac{1.362 \times 10^{-3}}{89.6} \\
  &= 1.52 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \\
  &= 15.2 \ \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** (i) **1.362 g of brass**, (ii) **15.2 µm coating thickness**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **concept of throwing power** in electroplating. Derive the Field's empirical formula and state the conditions under which a bath exhibits good throwing power.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A nickel-plating cell operates at 8 A on a panel of area $50 \text{ cm}^2$. The cell voltage is 4.5 V. The bath has resistance $0.3 \ \Omega$. Anode and cathode overpotentials are 0.4 V and 0.6 V respectively. If the reversible cell EMF is 1.2 V, calculate the energy consumed per kg of nickel deposited. Molar mass of Ni = 58.7 g/mol, $n = 2$.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Concept (2 marks):**
Throwing power is the ability of an electroplating bath to deposit metal **uniformly on a cathode of irregular shape** — i.e., to give comparable thickness on recessed (far) and exposed (near) regions.

**Field's Formula Derivation (4 marks):**
Field studied a cell with two cathodes at distances $a$ and $b$ from the anode, with $b > a$. He defined:

$$M = \frac{T_{\text{near}}}{T_{\text{far}}}, \quad L = \frac{b}{a}$$

Empirically (and from secondary current-distribution analysis), the relationship is:

$$M = 1 + \frac{L - 1}{1 + e^{\,K (L-1)}}$$

In the KTU board exam, the **linear form** is what is required:

$$TP = \frac{L - M}{L + M - 2} \times 100 \text{ \%}$$

**Conditions for Good TP (1 mark):**
- **Low $i_0$** (kinetically slow bath) — favours secondary distribution.
- **High electrolyte conductivity** — reduces ohmic drop penalty at far points.
- **Modest current density** — avoids approaching $i_L$.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Mass of Ni deposited per unit time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} \\
\text{For } t = 1 \text{ hour} = 3600 \text{ s:} \quad m &= \frac{8 \times 3600 \times 58.7 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 96485} \\
  &= \frac{1690.56}{192970} \\
  &= 8.762 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy per hour:**
Power $P = V \times I = 4.5 \times 8 = 36$ W.
Energy per hour $= 36 \text{ W} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 129{,}600 \text{ J} = 0.036 \text{ kWh}$.

**Step 3 — Energy per kg of Ni:**

$$\text{Energy} = \frac{129{,}600 \text{ J}}{8.762 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg}} = 1.479 \times 10^{7} \text{ J/kg} \approx 14.79 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

In kWh: $14.79 / 3.6 = 4.11 \text{ kWh/kg}$.

> **Voltage balance sanity check (1 mark):**
> $V_{cell} = E_{rev} + \eta_a + \eta_c + IR = 1.2 + 0.4 + 0.6 + (8 \times 0.3) = 1.2 + 0.4 + 0.6 + 2.4 = 4.6$ V
> The 0.1 V discrepancy with the given 4.5 V is within reasonable engineering tolerance — examiner-acceptable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting current efficiency** ($\varepsilon$): Always multiply theoretical mass by $\varepsilon$ to get the *actual* mass. Missing this costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors**: Mixing $\text{dm}^2$ and $\text{m}^2$ for current density is the single most common arithmetic mistake. Remember $1 \text{ A/dm}^2 = 100 \text{ A/m}^2$.
> 3. **Confusing $n$ (charge number) with the number of electrons in a half-reaction step** — for Ni²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Ni, $n = 2$ (not 1, not 4).
> 4. **Forgetting to convert time to seconds** when using SI for Faraday's law.
> 5. **Neglecting concentration overpotential** when a question says "at high current density" — this is the cue to mention the *tertiary* distribution and the *limit* $i_L$.
> 6. **In the Hull cell question, students often forget to mention agitation, temperature control, and the standard 267 mL volume** — examiners deduct 1 mark each.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Faraday's law** is the *quantitative backbone* of every metal-finishing calculation. Memorize $m = \frac{I t M}{n F}$ and $d = \frac{i t M}{n F \rho}$.
- **Current efficiency** $\varepsilon$ is *never* 100 % in real cells — typical range **80–98 %**; gold plating can be as low as **30–50 %** because of competing hydrogen evolution.
- **Throwing power** is the **single most important diagnostic** for cell-design quality. Cyanide and stannate baths have *excellent* TP; acidic sulphate and fluoborate baths have *poor* TP.
- **Hull cell** is the *indispensable* empirical tool. Remember: **267 mL**, **2 A for 5 min** is the KTU-recommended standard test.
- **Primary distribution** = geometry only, **Secondary** = + activation, **Tertiary** = + mass transport. Tertiary is the most realistic but hardest to solve.
- **Limiting current density** $i_L = n F k_m C_b$ sets the *upper bound* on the operating current; never design above $0.5\, i_L$.
- **Cell voltage** has *five* contributors: $E_{rev} + \eta_a + \eta_c + IR_{bath} + IR_{leads}$.
- **Anode : Cathode area ratio** typically 1 : 1 to 2 : 1 for soluble anodes; reversed ratios are used for insoluble anodes (e.g., chrome plating).
- **Agitation** dramatically raises $k_m$, raising $i_L$, allowing faster plating — but too much agitation causes *streaking* and *gas pitting*.
- **Standard KTU reference values** to memorize: $F = 96{,}500$ C/mol; $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K; standard Tafel slope for $n=1, \alpha=0.5$ at 25 °C = **0.118 V/decade**.
- **Industrial cell types** worth remembering: Parallel Plate, Rotating Cylinder Electrode (RCE), Jet Cell, Packed Bed, Hull Cell, Bipolar Cell, and Membrane-Divided Cell.
- **For decorative plating**, current density is typically **1–5 A/dm²**; for **hard chrome**, **20–50 A/dm²**; for **electrorefining of copper**, **2–3 A/dm²** with soluble anodes.
- **Energy consumption** benchmark: copper refining **2–3 kWh/kg**, decorative nickel **6–10 kWh/kg**, decorative chrome **15–20 kWh/kg** — KTU examiners love this comparison.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Electrochemical Reactors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT417 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Electrochemical Reactors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Electrochemical Reactor (ECR)** is an engineered device in which heterogeneous electron-transfer reactions (oxidation at the anode and reduction at the cathode) are deliberately coupled with ionic conduction through an electrolyte to drive a desired chemical transformation. It is the unit operation equivalent of a chemical reactor, but the driving potential is supplied electrically rather than thermally or via concentration gradients alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> An electrochemical reactor is a device in which electrical energy is directly converted to chemical change (electrosynthesis) or chemical energy is converted to electricity (galvanic cell mode). The classification of reactors is based on **mode of operation, electrode geometry, flow configuration, and current distribution**.

## 1.2 Primary Classification Axes (KTU Framework)

The KTU 2024 Scheme groups electrochemical reactors along **four orthogonal classification axes**:

| Axis | Categories | Engineering Significance |
|------|------------|--------------------------|
| **Mode of Operation** | Batch / Semi-batch / Continuous | Determines throughput and flexibility |
| **Electrode Geometry** | Two-Dimensional (2-D) / Three-Dimensional (3-D) | Determines specific surface area |
| **Flow Configuration** | Plug Flow (PFR) / Stirred Tank (CSTR) / Cascade | Determines conversion per pass |
| **Current Distribution** | Monopolar / Bipolar / Series-Parallel | Determines cell voltage and current efficiency |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Electrified Mixer Grinder"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Plain English:**
> Imagine a kitchen **mixer grinder** where the blades normally cut food. Now, instead of just mechanical blades, imagine two **electrically charged plates** inside the chamber. The food (electrolyte) is forced through this chamber, and as it passes, the **electric field forces chemical reactions** to occur — splitting, depositing, or transforming molecules. The **shape of the chamber** (tank, pipe, or packed column) and the **way the food flows** (batch vs. continuous) determine how much reaction happens — exactly like the different types of chemical reactors you studied in CRE. The electrical current is the "stirring force" that drives the reaction at the surface.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** In a chemical reactor, *temperature* drives the reaction. In an electrochemical reactor, *voltage* drives the reaction, while *current* tells you the **rate** of reaction (via Faraday's Law).

## 1.4 Essential Vocabulary for the Module

- **Anode** — Electrode where **oxidation** occurs; source of electrons to the external circuit.
- **Cathode** — Electrode where **reduction** occurs; sink of electrons from the external circuit.
- **Electrolyte** — Ionic conductor (aqueous, molten salt, or solid polymer) that closes the internal circuit.
- **Separator / Diaphragm** — Porous or ion-selective membrane that prevents bulk mixing of anolyte and catholyte.
- **Current Density (j)** — Current per unit electrode area; measured in **A·m⁻²**. Determines reaction rate.
- **Faradaic Efficiency (η_F)** — Fraction of passed charge that produces the desired product.
- **Space Velocity (v)** — Volumetric flow rate divided by reactor volume; the inverse of residence time.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Big Picture" Logic of ECR Design

The behaviour of any electrochemical reactor is governed by **three coupled transport phenomena** that must occur simultaneously:

1. **Electron transfer** across the electrode–electrolyte interface (governed by Butler–Volmer kinetics).
2. **Mass transfer** of reactant from the bulk electrolyte to the electrode surface.
3. **Charge (ionic) transport** through the bulk electrolyte and separator (governed by Ohm's law in the electrolyte phase).

The design equation of an electrochemical reactor is therefore a **triple coupling**:

$$
\text{Reactor Performance} = f\!\left(\text{Kinetics},\ \text{Mass Transport},\ \text{Ohmic Drop}\right)
$$

## 2.2 Classification Logic — Why the Categories Exist

### 2.2.1 By Mode of Operation
- **Batch ECR:** All reactant is charged at $t=0$; reaction proceeds for time $t_f$ until desired conversion $X_f$ is reached. Used for low-volume, high-value products (e.g., specialty organics, electroplating baths).
- **Continuous ECR:** Reactant feed and product withdrawal occur at steady rates. Used for high-volume commodity chemicals (e.g., chlor-alkali, water electrolysis).

### 2.2.2 By Electrode Geometry
- **2-D Electrodes (Plates, Meshes, Rotating Discs):** Area $A$ scales linearly with size. Limited by external surface.
- **3-D Electrodes (Packed Bed, Fluidized Bed, Reticulated Foam):** Area $A$ scales volumetrically ($A = a_s \cdot V$, where $a_s$ is specific area in **m²·m⁻³**). Gives 10–100× higher space-time yield.

### 2.2.3 By Flow Configuration
- **Plug Flow ECR (PFECR):** No axial mixing; conversion follows first-order analogue.
- **Continuous Stirred Tank ECR (CSTECR):** Perfect mixing; composition uniform everywhere.
- **Cascade of CSTECRs:** N stirred tanks in series; approaches PFECR as $N \to \infty$.

### 2.2.4 By Current Distribution / Electrical Connection
- **Monopolar:** Each electrode has its own current lead; cells in parallel share current.
- **Bipolar:** Only end electrodes have leads; intermediate electrodes are induced to be bipolar (one side anodic, the other cathodic). Cells in series share voltage.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and appear repeatedly in KTU End-Semester Examinations under Module 4.

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Use |
|---|---------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's Law — mass of product deposited/evolved |
| 2 | $r = \dfrac{j \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Areal reaction rate from current density |
| 3 | $X = 1 - \dfrac{C_A}{C_{A0}}$ | Conversion definition |
| 4 | $\eta_F = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} = \dfrac{I_{useful}}{I_{total}}$ | Faradaic (Current) Efficiency |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{V_R}{Q}$ | Space time (residence time) |
| 6 | $v = \dfrac{1}{\tau} = \dfrac{Q}{V_R}$ | Space velocity |
| 7 | $V_{cell} = E_{eq} + \vert \eta_a \vert + \vert \eta_c \vert + IR_{cell}$ | Cell voltage balance (KTU 2024) |
| 8 | $X_{PFR} = 1 - \exp(-k_a \tau)$ | Conversion in PFECR (1st-order) |
| 9 | $X_{CSTR} = \dfrac{k_a \tau}{1 + k_a \tau}$ | Conversion in CSTECR (1st-order) |
| 10 | $a_s = \dfrac{A_{electrode}}{V_{reactor}}$ | Specific electrode area (m²·m⁻³) |
| 11 | $P_{electrical} = V_{cell} \cdot I$ | Electrical power input |
| 12 | $SEC = \dfrac{V_{cell} \cdot F}{M \cdot \eta_F}$ | Specific Energy Consumption (kWh·kg⁻¹) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Equation (12) — Specific Energy Consumption — is the **figure of merit** used by industry to compare electrochemical reactor technologies. For chlor-alkali, modern membrane cells achieve SEC ≈ 2.0–2.3 MWh per ton of Cl₂; the target is to reduce this further.

## 2.4 Design Trade-offs — Why We Don't Use Just One Type

- **2-D Plate Cells** are mechanically simple but have low $a_s$ (~50–200 m²·m⁻³).
- **3-D Packed Beds** offer $a_s$ up to **5000 m²·m⁻³** but suffer from non-uniform current distribution (channeling, hot spots).
- **Monopolar** wiring gives low voltage, high current → safe, easy maintenance, but heavy bus-bars.
- **Bipolar** wiring gives high voltage, low current → reduced Joule losses in wiring, but a single cell failure kills the stack.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Material Balance for a Continuous PFECR

### 3.1.1 Set-up

Consider a differential slice of volume $dV$ in a plug-flow electrochemical reactor operating at steady state. Reactant $A$ enters with molar flow $F_A$ and leaves with $F_A + dF_A$. The reaction rate per unit volume is $r_A$ (mol·m⁻³·s⁻¹). The electrons supplied to this slice are governed by Faraday's Law.

**Step 1 — Generic mass balance on the slice:**

$$
\text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation} = 0
$$

$$
F_A - (F_A + dF_A) + r_A \, dV = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the differential equation:**

$$
-dF_A + r_A \, dV = 0
$$

$$
\frac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A
$$

**Step 3 — Express rate in terms of current density $j$:**

Using Faraday's Law on the local electrode area $a_s \, dV$ inside the slice:

$$
r_A = \frac{a_s \cdot j \cdot \eta_F}{n \cdot F}
$$

**Step 4 — Coupling:**

$$
\frac{dF_A}{dV} = \frac{a_s \cdot j \cdot \eta_F}{n \cdot F}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to conversion variable** $X = 1 - F_A/F_{A0}$:

Since $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X)$, we have $dF_A = -F_{A0} \, dX$. Substituting:

$$
F_{A0} \, dX = \frac{a_s \cdot j \cdot \eta_F}{n \cdot F} \, dV
$$

**Step 6 — Integration from $X=0$ to $X_f$ and $V=0$ to $V_R$** (assuming constant $j$ and $\eta_F$):

$$
\int_0^{X_f} dX = \frac{a_s \cdot j \cdot \eta_F}{n \cdot F \cdot F_{A0}} \int_0^{V_R} dV
$$

$$
X_f = \frac{a_s \cdot j \cdot \eta_F \cdot V_R}{n \cdot F \cdot F_{A0}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** The conversion achieved in a PFECR is **linear in reactor volume, current density, and Faradaic efficiency**, and **inversely linear in the inlet molar flow**. Doubling the current density doubles the conversion per pass.

## 3.2 Derivation: CSTR-Style Electrochemical Reactor

For a CSTECR at steady state, the balance is algebraic rather than differential.

**Step 1 — Mass balance over the entire tank:**

$$
Q \cdot C_{A0} - Q \cdot C_A + V_R \cdot r_A = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for residence time** $\tau = V_R/Q$:

$$
\tau = \frac{C_{A0} - C_A}{-r_A}
$$

**Step 3 — Express $-r_A$ via Faraday:**

$$
-r_A = \frac{a_s \cdot j \cdot \eta_F}{n \cdot F}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine with $C_{A0} - C_A = C_{A0} \cdot X$:**

$$
\tau = \frac{C_{A0} \cdot X}{\dfrac{a_s \cdot j \cdot \eta_F}{n \cdot F}}
$$

**Step 5 — Final expression for conversion:**

$$
X_{CSTR} = \frac{a_s \cdot j \cdot \eta_F \cdot \tau}{n \cdot F \cdot C_{A0}}
$$

## 3.3 Comparative Conversion — PFR vs. CSTR (Same Volume)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For any given first-order rate constant $k$ and residence time $\tau$, the PFR **always** gives higher conversion than the CSTR. This is mathematically verifiable as follows.

| Reactor Type | Conversion Formula | Numerical Check ($\tau=1$, $k_a=1$ s⁻¹) |
|--------------|--------------------|------------------------------------------|
| PFR (PFECR) | $X = 1 - \exp(-k_a \tau)$ | $X = 1 - e^{-1} = 0.632$ |
| CSTR (CSTECR) | $X = \dfrac{k_a \tau}{1 + k_a \tau}$ | $X = 1/2 = 0.500$ |
| 2 CSTRs in series | $X = 1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1 + k_a \tau/N}\right)^N$ | For $N=2$: $X = 1 - (2/3)^2 = 0.556$ |
| 3 CSTRs in series | Same with $N=3$ | $X = 1 - (3/4)^3 = 0.578$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Take-away:** As $N \to \infty$, the CSTR cascade approaches the PFR conversion. This is a classic question of comparison often asked in Part B.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-style):** A PFECR is used to reduce Cu²⁺ to Cu metal. Feed contains $C_{A0} = 500$ mol·m⁻³ Cu²⁺ at a flow rate $Q = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m³·s⁻¹. The electrode specific area is $a_s = 1000$ m²·m⁻³, current density $j = 200$ A·m⁻², Faradaic efficiency $\eta_F = 0.90$, $n = 2$. Find the reactor volume required for 80% conversion.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute inlet molar flow:**

$$
F_{A0} = Q \cdot C_{A0} = (2 \times 10^{-4})(500) = 0.1\ \text{mol·s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Use the PFR conversion formula derived in §3.1.6:**

$$
X_f = \frac{a_s \cdot j \cdot \eta_F \cdot V_R}{n \cdot F \cdot F_{A0}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_R$:**

$$
V_R = \frac{X_f \cdot n \cdot F \cdot F_{A0}}{a_s \cdot j \cdot \eta_F}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values** (using $F = 96485$ C·mol⁻¹):

$$
V_R = \frac{(0.80)(2)(96485)(0.1)}{(1000)(200)(0.90)}
$$

$$
V_R = \frac{15437.6}{180000} = 0.0858\ \text{m}^3 \approx 85.8\ \text{L}
$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check:** This is a reasonable laboratory-scale reactor volume for Cu recovery at the stated rate.

## 3.5 Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) Derivation

**Step 1 — Electrical energy input per unit time:**

$$
P = V_{cell} \cdot I
$$

**Step 2 — Moles of product per unit time (Faraday):**

$$
\dot{n} = \frac{I \cdot \eta_F}{n \cdot F}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of product per unit time** (using molecular mass $M$):

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{I \cdot \eta_F \cdot M}{n \cdot F}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy per unit mass:**

$$
SEC = \frac{P}{\dot{m}} = \frac{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot \eta_F \cdot M}
$$

$$
SEC = \frac{V_{cell} \cdot n \cdot F}{\eta_F \cdot M}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Use:** In chlor-alkali cells, $M_{Cl_2} = 71$ g·mol⁻¹, $n=2$, $\eta_F \approx 0.97$, $V_{cell} \approx 3.0$ V. Substituting:
> $$SEC = \frac{(3.0)(2)(96485)}{(0.97)(0.071)} \approx 2.42 \times 10^6\ \text{J per g} \approx 2.42\ \text{MWh per ton}$$
> which matches modern membrane-cell benchmarks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Master Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RootA[Electrochemical Reactors]
    A1[By Operation Mode]
    A2[By Electrode Geometry]
    A3[By Flow Configuration]
    A4[By Electrical Connection]

    B1[Batch ECR]
    B2[Semi-Batch ECR]
    B3[Continuous ECR]

    C1[2D Electrodes]
    C2[3D Electrodes]
    C1a[Plates]
    C1b[Rotating Cylinder]
    C1c[Rotating Disc]
    C2a[Packed Bed]
    C2b[Fluidized Bed]
    C2c[Reticulated Foam]

    D1[Plug Flow ECR]
    D2[CSTR ECR]
    D3[Cascade of CSTRs]

    E1[Monopolar]
    E2[Bipolar]
    E3[Series-Parallel Hybrid]

    RootA --> A1
    RootA --> A2
    RootA --> A3
    RootA --> A4
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    A2 --> C1
    A2 --> C2
    C1 --> C1a
    C1 --> C1b
    C1 --> C1c
    C2 --> C2a
    C2 --> C2b
    C2 --> C2c
    A3 --> D1
    A3 --> D2
    A3 --> D3
    A4 --> E1
    A4 --> E2
    A4 --> E3
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Filter-Press Cell (Most Common Industrial Type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Feed1[Anolyte In] --> InletA
    InletA --> FrameA[Anode Frame]
    FrameA --> Membrane[Separator Membrane]
    Membrane --> FrameC[Cathode Frame]
    FrameC --> OutletC
    OutletC --> Prod1[Catholyte Out]

    Feed2[Anolyte Recycle] --> InletC
    InletC --> FrameA

    PowerDC[DC Power Supply] --- B1[+ve Busbar]
    PowerDC --- B2[-ve Busbar]
    B1 --> FrameA
    B2 --> FrameC
```

## 4.3 Schematic: 3-D Packed-Bed Electrochemical Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Inlet3[Electrolyte In] --> DistA[Distributor Plate]
    DistA --> BedA[Packed Cathode Bed]
    BedA --> Mid[Feeder Electrode]
    Mid --> BedB[Packed Anode Bed]
    BedB --> DistB[Distributor Plate]
    DistB --> Outlet3[Electrolyte Out]

    BedA -.->|Electrons flow| PowerB[DC Source]
    PowerB -.->|Electrons flow| BedB
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow: Conversion vs. Reactor Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[PFECR] -->|Highest X for given V| OutX[Conversion Achieved]
    Q[Cascade 3 CSTR] --> OutX
    R[Cascade 2 CSTR] --> OutX
    S[Single CSTR] -->|Lowest X for given V| OutX
```

## 4.5 Comparative Reactor Matrix

| Reactor Type | $a_s$ Range (m²·m⁻³) | Typical Current Density (A·m⁻²) | Best-Suited Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plate-and-Frame (Filter-Press)** | 50 – 200 | 100 – 1000 | Chlor-alkali, electro-organic |
| **Rotating Cylinder Electrode (RCE)** | 200 – 500 | 1000 – 5000 | Metal recovery from dilute streams |
| **Rotating Disc Electrode (RDE)** | 50 – 200 | 100 – 2000 | Laboratory kinetic studies |
| **Packed-Bed (3-D)** | 1000 – 5000 | 50 – 500 | Trace metal removal, electrosynthesis |
| **Fluidized-Bed (3-D)** | 1000 – 3000 | 100 – 800 | Continuous heavy-metal remediation |
| **Reticulated Vitreous Carbon (RVC)** | 2000 – 10000 | 50 – 400 | High surface area redox flow |
| **Membrane Cell (Chlor-Alkali)** | 200 – 500 | 1000 – 4000 | NaOH, Cl₂, H₂ co-production |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Plug Flow Electrochemical Reactor (PFECR) and a Continuous Stirred Tank Electrochemical Reactor (CSTECR) with respect to (i) concentration profile, (ii) conversion for a given volume, and (iii) typical industrial example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per sub-point):**

(i) **Concentration Profile:** In a PFECR, concentration of reactant $A$ decreases continuously along the axial direction, from $C_{A0}$ at inlet to $C_{Af}$ at outlet. In a CSTECR, concentration is uniform throughout the tank and equal to the outlet concentration $C_A$ (well-mixed assumption). (1 mark)

(ii) **Conversion for a given volume:** For the same first-order rate constant and residence time, the PFECR always yields a higher conversion than the CSTECR. Mathematically, $X_{PFR} = 1 - \exp(-k_a \tau) > X_{CSTR} = k_a \tau / (1 + k_a \tau)$ for any $\tau > 0$ and $k_a > 0$. (1 mark)

(iii) **Industrial Example:** PFECR — chlor-alkali membrane cell (long narrow flow channels). CSTECR — electroplating barrel cells, electrorefining of copper. (1 mark)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Faradaic efficiency and specific energy consumption. Why are these two parameters critical for reactor selection?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Faradaic Efficiency (η_F):** The ratio of charge actually used for the desired electrochemical reaction to the total charge passed through the cell. Expressed as $\eta_F = m_{actual}/m_{theoretical} = I_{useful}/I_{total}$. (1.5 marks)

**Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** The electrical energy required to produce a unit mass (or unit mole) of the desired product, $SEC = (V_{cell} \cdot n \cdot F)/(\eta_F \cdot M)$ in kWh·kg⁻¹. (1 mark)

**Why critical:** Together they determine the **operating cost** of an electrochemical process. A reactor with high η_F but high cell voltage (e.g., due to large electrode gap) can still be uneconomical; conversely, low voltage with low η_F (due to side reactions) is wasteful. Both metrics must be optimized jointly for industrial viability. (0.5 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Q.A.(a) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**(7 marks)** Classify electrochemical reactors based on **electrode geometry** and **flow configuration**. Discuss the advantages and limitations of **3-D packed-bed electrodes** over **2-D plate electrodes** for metal-ion recovery from dilute solutions.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Classification (3 marks):**

- **By electrode geometry:**
  - *Two-dimensional (2-D) electrodes* — plates, meshes, expanded metal, rotating discs, rotating cylinders. Specific area $a_s$ is limited to ~50–500 m²·m⁻³ because the area scales with one physical dimension.
  - *Three-dimensional (3-D) electrodes* — packed beds, fluidized beds, reticulated vitreous carbon (RVC), felt electrodes. $a_s$ is enhanced by using porous/granular conductive media, reaching 1000–10000 m²·m⁻³.

- **By flow configuration:**
  - *Plug Flow Electrochemical Reactor (PFECR):* Reactant flows in one direction through the cell; no back-mixing; concentration falls monotonically.
  - *Continuous Stirred Tank Electrochemical Reactor (CSTECR):* Perfect mixing; concentration uniform and equal to exit concentration.
  - *Cascade of CSTRs:* Multiple stirred tanks in series, approaching PFECR behaviour.

**3-D Packed-Bed vs. 2-D Plate (4 marks):**

| Aspect | 2-D Plate | 3-D Packed-Bed |
|---|---|---|
| $a_s$ | 50 – 500 m²·m⁻³ | 1000 – 10000 m²·m⁻³ |
| Suitability for dilute streams | Poor (low area) | Excellent (high area) |
| Limiting-current behaviour | Easily reached at low $C$ | Suppressed due to high $a_s$ |
| Pressure drop | Very low | Moderate to high (needs pumping) |
| Current distribution | Uniform (well-defined) | Non-uniform (potential gradients in bed) |
| Risk of channeling/hot spots | None | Yes (esp. at high $j$) |
| Construction | Simple | Complex; needs current feeders |
| Cost per unit recovery | Higher for dilute | Lower for dilute streams |

**Conclusion:** For metal-ion recovery from dilute effluents (typical $C_{A0} = 10$–100 ppm), 3-D packed-bed electrodes achieve 90–99% removal at residence times of seconds to minutes, whereas 2-D cells would require impractically large electrode areas. The trade-off is the need for careful potential/current control to avoid non-uniform deposition and bed clogging.

---

#### Q.A.(b) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**(7 marks)** A continuous **PFECR** is used to electrodeposit nickel from a feed of $C_{A0} = 800$ mol·m⁻³ Ni²⁺ at a flow rate $Q = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³·s⁻¹. The specific electrode area is $a_s = 1500$ m²·m⁻³, applied current density $j = 250$ A·m⁻², Faradaic efficiency $\eta_F = 0.92$, and $n = 2$. Faraday constant $F = 96485$ C·mol⁻¹. Calculate the **reactor volume required for 90% conversion** and the **specific energy consumption** if the cell voltage is 3.5 V.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Inlet molar flow rate (1 mark):**

$$
F_{A0} = Q \cdot C_{A0} = (5 \times 10^{-4})(800) = 0.4\ \text{mol·s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply PFECR conversion formula (1.5 marks):**

$$
X_f = \frac{a_s \cdot j \cdot \eta_F \cdot V_R}{n \cdot F \cdot F_{A0}}
$$

Rearranging for $V_R$:

$$
V_R = \frac{X_f \cdot n \cdot F \cdot F_{A0}}{a_s \cdot j \cdot \eta_F}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve (1.5 marks):**

$$
V_R = \frac{(0.90)(2)(96485)(0.4)}{(1500)(250)(0.92)}
$$

$$
V_R = \frac{69469.2}{345000} = 0.2014\ \text{m}^3 \approx 201.4\ \text{L}
$$

**Step 4 — SEC formula (1 mark):**

$$
SEC = \frac{V_{cell} \cdot n \cdot F}{\eta_F \cdot M}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute with $M_{Ni} = 58.69 \times 10^{-3}$ kg·mol⁻¹ (1.5 marks):**

$$
SEC = \frac{(3.5)(2)(96485)}{(0.92)(58.69 \times 10^{-3})}
$$

$$
SEC = \frac{675395}{0.05399} = 1.251 \times 10^{7}\ \text{J·kg}^{-1}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kWh·kg⁻¹ (0.5 mark):**

$$
SEC = \frac{1.251 \times 10^{7}}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} = 3.48\ \text{kWh·kg}^{-1}\ \text{Ni}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students forget to use $M$ in **kg·mol⁻¹** (not g·mol⁻¹) when computing SEC, leading to answers off by a factor of 1000. Also, do not confuse the **inlet molar flow** $F_{A0}$ with the **inlet concentration** $C_{A0}$ — these are different quantities ($F_{A0} = Q \cdot C_{A0}$).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Q.B.(a) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(7 marks)** With neat sketches, explain the construction and working of a **Filter-Press (Plate-and-Frame) Electrochemical Reactor** and a **Rotating Cylinder Electrode (RCE) Reactor**. Compare their mass-transfer characteristics.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Filter-Press Reactor (3.5 marks):**

- **Construction:** Multiple flat frames are stacked and bolted together like a filter press. Each frame contains a flat electrode, and a separator membrane (cation-exchange or anion-exchange) is placed between anode and cathode frames. Gaskets seal each compartment. Electrolyte enters and exits through manifolds on the side.
- **Working:** Anolyte and catholyte flow in separate compartments in co-current or counter-current mode. Current is fed through end electrodes (monopolar) or through a series of bipolar electrodes.
- **Mass transfer:** Moderate; depends on flow velocity and channel geometry. Typical mass-transfer coefficient $k_m \approx 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ m·s⁻¹.

**RCE Reactor (2.5 marks):**

- **Construction:** A rotating cylindrical cathode (made of stainless steel, Ti, or graphite) concentric with a stationary anode. Electrolyte is contained in an annular tank.
- **Working:** The cathode rotates at 500–3000 rpm, creating intense turbulence. Reactant ions are transported to the cylinder surface by forced convection.
- **Mass transfer:** Very high; $k_m$ can reach $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ m·s⁻¹ due to the high shear. Useful for low-concentration feeds.

**Comparison (1 mark):** The RCE achieves mass-transfer coefficients one to two orders of magnitude higher than the filter-press, but it is mechanically complex (motor, bearings, seals) and has higher maintenance cost. For high-throughput commodity products, filter-press wins; for dilute or kinetically limited systems, RCE wins.

---

#### Q.B.(b) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(7 marks)** Derive the **design equation for a CSTR-type electrochemical reactor** in terms of conversion $X$, residence time $\tau$, current density $j$, and Faradaic efficiency $\eta_F$. A CSTECR operates with $C_{A0} = 200$ mol·m⁻³, $Q = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m³·s⁻¹, $a_s = 800$ m²·m⁻³, $j = 150$ A·m⁻², $\eta_F = 0.85$, $n = 1$. Determine the conversion achieved and the reactor volume if the residence time is fixed at 300 s.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Mass balance on the CSTR (1 mark):**

$$
Q \cdot C_{A0} - Q \cdot C_A + V_R \cdot r_A = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Express rate via Faraday (1.5 marks):**

$$
r_A = \frac{a_s \cdot j \cdot \eta_F}{n \cdot F}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to conversion and solve for $X$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
\tau = \frac{C_{A0} \cdot X}{r_A} \Rightarrow X = \frac{\tau \cdot r_A}{C_{A0}} = \frac{\tau \cdot a_s \cdot j \cdot \eta_F}{n \cdot F \cdot C_{A0}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values (1.5 marks):**

$$
X = \frac{(300)(800)(150)(0.85)}{(1)(96485)(200)}
$$

$$
X = \frac{30{,}600{,}000}{19{,}297{,}000} = 1.586 \rightarrow \text{Capped at } X = 1.0\ (100\%)
$$

**Step 5 — Since $X > 1$ is unphysical, we back-calculate $V_R$ for 80% conversion (1.5 marks):**

For $X = 0.80$:

$$
V_R = \frac{X \cdot n \cdot F \cdot C_{A0} \cdot Q}{a_s \cdot j \cdot \eta_F}
$$

Using the formula relating $\tau$ to $V_R$ via $Q$:

$$
V_R = \tau \cdot Q = 300 \times 10^{-3} = 0.3\ \text{m}^3 = 300\ \text{L}
$$

The original parameters would give complete conversion in less than 300 s. To find the actual $\tau$ needed for $X = 0.80$:

$$
\tau_{needed} = \frac{X \cdot n \cdot F \cdot C_{A0}}{a_s \cdot j \cdot \eta_F} = \frac{(0.80)(1)(96485)(200)}{(800)(150)(0.85)} = \frac{15{,}437{,}600}{102{,}000} = 151.4\ \text{s}
$$

So at $\tau = 151.4$ s, conversion is 80%, and corresponding $V_R = 151.4 \times 10^{-3} = 0.151$ m³.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do not report $X > 1$** — it is physically impossible. If your calculation gives $X > 1$, the reactor is **over-sized**; reduce $V_R$ or $j$ accordingly.
> 2. **Do not confuse $Q$ with $V_R$.** In CSTR design, $V_R = \tau \cdot Q$ is the volume, while $\tau$ is a derived parameter.
> 3. **Do not forget $\eta_F$** — its inclusion distinguishes a *real* electrochemical reactor from an *ideal* chemical reactor, and it is a frequent mark-losser.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Types of Electrochemical Reactors**

- **Four classification axes:** Operation mode, electrode geometry, flow configuration, electrical connection.
- **2-D electrodes** have low $a_s$ (50–500 m²·m⁻³); **3-D electrodes** have high $a_s$ (1000–10000 m²·m⁻³).
- **Filter-Press / Plate-and-Frame** is the **workhorse** of industrial electrochemistry (chlor-alkali, electro-organic).
- **RCE (Rotating Cylinder Electrode)** is preferred for **mass-transfer-limited** systems (dilute metal recovery).
- **Packed-Bed (3-D)** is best for **trace metal removal** from effluents; risk of channeling and non-uniform current.
- **Fluidized-Bed** allows continuous particle renewal; avoids clogging, but requires careful hydrodynamics.
- **Monopolar** = cells in parallel (low $V$, high $I$); **Bipolar** = cells in series (high $V$, low $I$).
- **PFR > CSTR** in conversion for equal volume and first-order kinetics; cascade of CSTRs bridges the gap.
- **Faraday's Law** is the **kinetic backbone**: $r = (j \cdot M)/(n \cdot F)$.
- **Faradaic efficiency $\eta_F$** must be incorporated for any real reactor; never assume 100%.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** is the **industrial figure of merit**; target is to minimize $V_{cell}/\eta_F$.
- **Cell voltage equation** has four terms: $V_{cell} = E_{eq} + \vert \eta_a \vert + \vert \eta_c \vert + IR$ — memorize in this order.
- **Space time** $\tau = V_R / Q$; **space velocity** $v = 1/\tau$.
- **Convert residence times and flow rates** to consistent SI units (m³, s, mol·m⁻³) before substituting.
- **For dilute streams, mass-transfer control** dominates; for concentrated streams, **ohmic drop** dominates.
- **Mention real examples** in 14-mark answers — chlor-alkali, electrorefining, electroplating, water electrolysis — examiners reward industrial context.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Batch Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT417 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Batch Cell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Batch Cell (Batch Electrochemical Reactor):** A batch electrochemical cell is a reactor configuration in which a **fixed quantity of electrolyte** is charged into the cell at the start of operation, allowed to undergo an electrochemical reaction for a specified duration under controlled conditions of current, voltage, or potential, and then discharged as a single batch once the desired conversion is achieved. There is **no inflow of reactant** and **no outflow of product** during the reaction period.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the batch cell is classified as a **non-steady-state (transient) electrochemical reactor** because the concentration of the electroactive species changes continuously with time during electrolysis. The system operates under the assumption of a **well-mixed (perfectly back-mixed)** electrolyte, so the bulk concentration at any instant is spatially uniform throughout the cell.

### Key Operating Characteristics of a Batch Cell

| Parameter | Behavior in Batch Operation |
|---|---|
| Electrolyte volume ($V$) | **Constant** (rigid tank assumption) |
| Reactant concentration $C_A(t)$ | **Decreases** with time |
| Product concentration $C_P(t)$ | **Increases** with time |
| Mass inflow/outflow | **Zero** (closed system boundary) |
| Operating state | **Unsteady / transient** |
| Conversion limit | Theoretically **100%** for an irreversible reaction |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cooking Pot"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine you are boiling a large pot of water to make Maggi noodles on a single stove. You pour exactly 2 litres of water and one packet of noodles into the pot. The noodles cook over time, absorbing water and softening. You do **not** add more dry noodles midway, and you do **not** drain water while cooking. After 4 minutes, you switch off the stove and serve the entire contents in one go. This is a **batch process**.

**Mapping the analogy to the batch electrochemical cell:**

- **The pot** → the electrochemical cell body (the reactor vessel).
- **The 2 litres of water** → the fixed volume of electrolyte $V$ charged initially.
- **The dry noodles** → the initial moles of electroactive reactant $n_{A,0} = C_{A,0} V$.
- **The cooking time** → the electrolysis time $t$.
- **The stove's heat knob** → the applied current $I$ or cell voltage $V_{cell}$.
- **The cooked noodles + remaining water served together** → the discharge of the entire reacted electrolyte once target conversion $X$ is reached.

Just as you cannot remove half-cooked noodles in a batch cook, a batch electrochemical cell does not produce any outflow until the cycle is complete. This makes the batch cell ideal for **high-value, low-volume specialty products** (e.g., pharmaceutical intermediates, precious metal recovery, electrosynthesis of fine chemicals).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Physical Constants used in Batch Cell Design:**
> - **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol (or C·eq⁻¹)
> - **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
> - **Standard temperature:** $T = 298.15$ K (for $25°$C reference)
> - **Charge of an electron:** $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C

The constant-volume batch assumption implies that the molar quantity of species is directly proportional to its concentration: $n_A(t) = C_A(t) \cdot V$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Batch Cell Operation

The batch cell is governed by a **transient material balance** on the electroactive species. The general differential form of the design equation for a constant-volume batch reactor is:

$$V \frac{dC_A}{dt} = r_A \cdot V$$

where $r_A$ is the **volumetric rate of disappearance** of species $A$ (mol·m⁻³·s⁻¹). Since the volume is constant, the equation reduces to:

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = r_A$$

In an electrochemical system, the rate is **linked to the current** through Faraday's law. The instantaneous faradaic current $I(t)$ is related to the rate of electrochemical reaction at the electrode surface by:

$$I(t) = n \cdot F \cdot \mathcal{R}_A(t)$$

where $\mathcal{R}_A(t)$ is the **extensive rate of reaction** (mol/s) and $n$ is the **number of electrons transferred** per mole of $A$.

For a batch cell with uniform concentration in the bulk (well-mixed), the rate expression depends on the **rate-controlling regime**:

### 2.1.1 Mass-Transfer Controlled Regime (Limiting Current Condition)

When the surface concentration $C_{A,s} \to 0$, the cell operates at the **limiting current** $I_L$, given by:

$$I_L(t) = n \cdot F \cdot A_e \cdot k_m \cdot C_A(t)$$

where $k_m$ is the mass-transfer coefficient (m/s) and $A_e$ is the effective electrode area (m²). Under this condition, the bulk concentration decays **exponentially** with time, as derived in Section 3.

### 2.1.2 Charge-Transfer (Kinetics) Controlled Regime

The current is governed by the Butler–Volmer equation:

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

### 2.1.3 Constant-Current (Galvanostatic) Operation

This is the **most common industrial batch operation mode**. The current $I$ is held fixed, and the cell voltage adjusts to maintain the current. The cumulative charge passed is:

$$Q(t) = I \cdot t = \int_0^t I(\tau) \, d\tau$$

The total charge required for **complete conversion** of $n_{A,0}$ moles is:

$$Q_{total} = n \cdot F \cdot n_{A,0} = n \cdot F \cdot C_{A,0} \cdot V$$

Therefore, the **conversion $X$ at any time $t$** under constant current is:

$$X(t) = \frac{C_{A,0} - C_A(t)}{C_{A,0}} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot C_{A,0} \cdot V}$$

The **time required to achieve a given conversion $X$** is:

$$t(X) = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot C_{A,0} \cdot V \cdot X}{I}$$

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Table for Batch Cell Calculations — KTU 2024 Scheme**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V \frac{dC_A}{dt} = r_A V$ | General batch design equation | mol/s |
| 2 | $I(t) = n F \mathcal{R}_A$ | Faraday's law linking current to rate | A |
| 3 | $X = \dfrac{C_{A,0} - C_A}{C_{A,0}}$ | Definition of fractional conversion | dimensionless |
| 4 | $Q = I \cdot t$ (galvanostatic) | Cumulative charge under constant current | C (A·s) |
| 5 | $X = \dfrac{I t}{n F C_{A,0} V}$ | Conversion vs. time (galvanostatic) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $t = \dfrac{n F C_{A,0} V X}{I}$ | Time to reach conversion $X$ | s |
| 7 | $C_A(t) = C_{A,0} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{k_m A_e t}{V}\right)$ | Concentration decay (mass-transfer limited) | mol/m³ |
| 8 | $X(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{k_m A_e t}{V}\right)$ | Conversion vs. time (mass-transfer limited) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $I_L = n F A_e k_m C_A$ | Limiting current expression | A |
| 10 | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{k_m A_e}$ | Characteristic reaction time (batch) | s |
| 11 | $V_{cell} = E_{eq} + \vert \eta_a \vert + \vert \eta_c \vert + IR_{cell}$ | Cell voltage balance | V |
| 12 | $P_{cell} = V_{cell} \cdot I$ | Electrical power consumed | W |
| 13 | $\epsilon_{current} = \dfrac{I_{used}}{I_{total}} = \dfrac{Q_{theoretical}}{Q_{actual}}$ | Current efficiency / coulombic efficiency | dimensionless |
| 14 | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{n F}{\mathcal{M}}$ | Specific charge of a species | C/kg |

**Boundary Conditions for the standard batch cell:**

- At $t = 0$: $C_A(0) = C_{A,0}$ and $X(0) = 0$.
- At $t \to \infty$ (mass-transfer limited): $C_A \to 0$ and $X \to 1$.
- Under galvanostatic operation: linear accumulation of charge, hence linear growth of $X$ with $t$.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The batch electrochemical cell is the workhorse of several industrial processes:

- **Electrowinning of precious metals** (gold, silver from cyanide leach solutions).
- **Electrorefining** of copper where impure anodes are dissolved batch-wise.
- **Electrochemical synthesis** of organic intermediates (e.g., adiponitrile from acrylonitrile — the Monsanto/Baizer process).
- **Cathodic protection** rectifier stations operating in batch mode.
- **Water treatment** for small-volume effluents containing heavy metals (Pb, Cd, Hg, Cr⁶⁺).
- **Battery testing laboratories** where individual cells are cycled as batch units.
- **Chlor-alkali diaphragm cells** historically operated in batch modes for capacity regulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The choice of batch over continuous flow is preferred when: (i) production volumes are small, (ii) reaction kinetics are slow, (iii) high per-batch purity is required, and (iv) the product mix changes frequently (multi-product facility).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion vs. Time under Constant Current (Galvanostatic)

### Statement
Derive the expression for fractional conversion $X(t)$ in a batch cell operating under a constant applied current $I$, assuming 100% current efficiency.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Start with Faraday's law linking cumulative charge to moles reacted.**

The moles of species $A$ consumed up to time $t$ is:

$$\Delta n_A(t) = \frac{Q(t)}{n F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n F}$$

**Step 2: Express the initial moles of $A$ present in the cell.**

For a constant cell volume $V$ with initial concentration $C_{A,0}$:

$$n_{A,0} = C_{A,0} \cdot V$$

**Step 3: Apply the definition of fractional conversion.**

The conversion $X$ is the ratio of moles reacted to the initial moles:

$$X(t) = \frac{\Delta n_A(t)}{n_{A,0}} = \frac{I \cdot t \, / \, (n F)}{C_{A,0} \cdot V}$$

**Step 4: Simplify the expression.**

$$\boxed{X(t) = \frac{I \, t}{n \, F \, C_{A,0} \, V}}$$

**Step 5: Verify the limit at $t = 0$.**

At $t = 0$: $X(0) = 0$. ✓ (No reaction has occurred yet.)

**Step 6: Determine the time to reach a target conversion $X^*$.**

Rearranging the boxed expression:

$$t^* = \frac{n \, F \, C_{A,0} \, V \, X^*}{I}$$

This is the **batch cycle time** required to reach the target conversion at the chosen current.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Concentration Decay under Mass-Transfer Limitation

### Statement
Derive the time-dependent concentration profile $C_A(t)$ in a batch cell operating at the limiting current $I_L(t)$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Write the material balance for species $A$ in the batch cell.**

For a closed system with no inflow or outflow:

$$\frac{dn_A}{dt} = -\mathcal{R}_A \quad \Rightarrow \quad V \frac{dC_A}{dt} = -\mathcal{R}_A$$

**Step 2: Apply Faraday's law to express $\mathcal{R}_A$ in terms of the current.**

$$\mathcal{R}_A = \frac{I_L}{n F}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the limiting current expression.**

The limiting current at any instant depends on the **instantaneous bulk concentration** $C_A(t)$:

$$I_L(t) = n \, F \, A_e \, k_m \, C_A(t)$$

**Step 4: Combine Steps 1–3 into a single first-order ODE.**

$$V \frac{dC_A}{dt} = -\frac{n F A_e k_m C_A(t)}{n F} = -A_e \, k_m \, C_A(t)$$

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -\frac{A_e \, k_m}{V} \, C_A(t)$$

**Step 5: Recognize this as a first-order linear ODE.**

Let $\kappa = \dfrac{k_m A_e}{V}$ (units: s⁻¹). Then:

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -\kappa \, C_A(t)$$

**Step 6: Solve the ODE with the initial condition $C_A(0) = C_{A,0}$.**

Separating variables and integrating:

$$\int_{C_{A,0}}^{C_A(t)} \frac{dC_A}{C_A} = -\kappa \int_0^t d\tau$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_A(t)}{C_{A,0}}\right) = -\kappa \, t$$

$$\boxed{C_A(t) = C_{A,0} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{k_m A_e}{V} \, t\right)}$$

**Step 7: Convert to conversion form.**

Using $X = 1 - C_A / C_{A,0}$:

$$X(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{k_m A_e}{V} \, t\right) = 1 - e^{-t / \tau}$$

where $\tau = V / (k_m A_e)$ is the **characteristic time constant** of the batch cell.

**Step 8: Verify the long-time limit.**

As $t \to \infty$, $C_A \to 0$ and $X \to 1$. ✓ (Complete conversion is theoretically achievable under sustained limiting-current operation.)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Time-Averaged Current Efficiency

### Statement
When the actual current $I$ exceeds the instantaneous limiting current $I_L(t)$, the excess current is wasted on side reactions (e.g., water electrolysis). Derive the **average current efficiency** over a batch run.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Express the instantaneous useful current.**

$$I_{useful}(t) = \min\!\left[I_{applied}, \, I_L(t)\right] = \min\!\left[I_{applied}, \, n F A_e k_m C_A(t)\right]$$

**Step 2: Write the charge consumed for the desired reaction.**

$$Q_{useful} = \int_0^{t_f} I_{useful}(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3: Write the total charge supplied by the source.**

$$Q_{total} = I_{applied} \cdot t_f$$

**Step 4: Define the average current efficiency.**

$$\bar{\epsilon}_I = \frac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{total}}$$

**Step 5: Apply this to the case where $I_{applied} \le I_{L,0}$ (current always below the initial limiting value).**

If $I_{applied} \le n F A_e k_m C_{A,0}$, then for all time $I_{useful} = I_{applied}$, so:

$$\bar{\epsilon}_I = 1.0 \quad (100\%)$$

**Step 6: Apply this to the case where $I_{applied} > I_{L,0}$ (current exceeds initial limiting value).**

In this case, the cell runs at the limiting current until $C_A$ drops enough that $I_{applied} \le I_L(t)$, after which it runs at the applied current. The average efficiency is **less than 1.0**.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A batch electrochemical cell contains 5 L of an electrolyte with an initial Cu²⁺ concentration of 0.2 mol/L. The electroactive reaction is:

$$\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \to \text{Cu}$$

The cell is operated at a constant current of $I = 9.65$ A. The mass-transfer coefficient is $k_m = 1 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s and the electrode area is $A_e = 0.05$ m². Calculate:
1. The conversion after 30 minutes.
2. The time required to reach 95% conversion.

### Solution

**Given data:**
- $V = 5 \text{ L} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m³
- $C_{A,0} = 0.2 \text{ mol/L} = 200 \text{ mol/m}^3$
- $n = 2$ electrons
- $I = 9.65$ A
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- $k_m = 1 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s
- $A_e = 0.05$ m²
- $t = 30$ min $= 1800$ s

**Step 1: Verify that the cell is operating below the initial limiting current.**

The initial limiting current:

$$I_{L,0} = n F A_e k_m C_{A,0} = (2)(96{,}485)(0.05)(1 \times 10^{-5})(200)$$

$$I_{L,0} = 19.297 \text{ A}$$

Since $I = 9.65 \text{ A} < I_{L,0} = 19.297$ A, the cell operates under galvanostatic (constant current) mode with **100% current efficiency** throughout. We can directly use the galvanostatic conversion formula.

**Step 2: Calculate the conversion at $t = 1800$ s.**

$$X = \frac{I \, t}{n \, F \, C_{A,0} \, V} = \frac{(9.65)(1800)}{(2)(96{,}485)(200)(5 \times 10^{-3})}$$

$$X = \frac{17{,}370}{192{,}970} = 0.0900$$

$$\boxed{X(1800 \text{ s}) = 0.0900 = 9.00\%}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the time to reach $X = 0.95$.**

$$t^* = \frac{n F C_{A,0} V X}{I} = \frac{(2)(96{,}485)(200)(5 \times 10^{-3})(0.95)}{9.65}$$

$$t^* = \frac{183{,}321.5}{9.65} = 18{,}996 \text{ s}$$

Converting to hours:

$$t^* = \frac{18{,}996}{3600} = 5.277 \text{ h}$$

$$\boxed{t^* \approx 5.28 \text{ hours}}$$

**Step 4: Validation check — final concentration.**

$$C_A(t^*) = C_{A,0}(1 - X) = 200 \times 0.05 = 10 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

The limiting current at the end:

$$I_L(t^*) = (2)(96{,}485)(0.05)(1 \times 10^{-5})(10) = 0.965 \text{ A}$$

Since $I = 9.65$ A $\gg I_L(t^*) = 0.965$ A, the assumption of galvanostatic 100% efficiency **breaks down** near the end. In practice, the current would need to be **ramped down** to maintain efficiency, OR a more accurate exponential model (Derivation 2) would be required for high conversions.

This example illustrates the **practical engineering trade-off** between throughput (high current) and current efficiency (low current) in batch cell design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Batch Cell Functional Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below represents the functional flow of a batch electrochemical cell. It captures the closed-system material flow, the electrical circuit, and the operational sequence (Charge → React → Discharge).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Batch Cycle] --> B[Charge Electrolyte to Cell]
    B --> C[Set Operating Parameters]
    C --> D[Apply Constant Current from DC Power Supply]
    D --> E[Electrochemical Reaction in Cell]
    E --> F[Concentration of Reactant Decreases with Time]
    F --> G[Monitor Current and Cell Voltage]
    G --> H{Target Conversion Reached?}
    H -->|No| I[Continue Electrolysis]
    I --> E
    H -->|Yes| J[Cut Off Power Supply]
    J --> K[Discharge Reacted Electrolyte and Product]
    K --> L[Clean and Prepare Cell for Next Batch]
    L --> A
    D -.-> M[DC Rectifier Power Supply]
    M -.-> D
    E -.-> N[Anode Oxidation Reaction]
    E -.-> O[Cathode Reduction Reaction]
    N -.-> P[Mass Transfer Boundary Layer]
    O -.-> P
    P -.-> Q[Bulk Electrolyte Well Mixed]
    Q -.-> F
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mass Balance and Charge Flow in a Batch Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph input[INPUT BOUNDARY]
        IA[Initial Charge Q0 equals 0]
        IC[Initial Concentration CA0]
    end
    subgraph cell[BATCH CELL INTERIOR]
        VM[Well Mixed Bulk Volume V]
        CE[Concentration of A at time t: CA of t]
        IE[Current I passes through electrodes]
    end
    subgraph output[OUTPUT BOUNDARY]
        OF[Outflow equals 0 Closed System]
        OC[Final Conversion X final]
    end
    IA --> cell
    IC --> cell
    cell --> OF
    cell --> OC
    CE --> VM
    VM --> CE
    IE --> CE
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic Description — Physical Layout of a Typical Batch Cell

A typical **laboratory-scale batch cell** consists of the following components arranged in a rectangular or cylindrical vessel:

1. **Cell body (vessel):** Constructed of glass, polypropylene, or stainless steel depending on electrolyte corrosivity. Contains the electrolyte volume $V$ (typically 0.1 – 10 L for laboratory scale, up to several m³ for industrial batch electrowinning).

2. **Anode:** Insoluble (e.g., Pb, Pb–Ag alloy, Ti coated with mixed metal oxides) for oxygen evolution, or soluble (e.g., impure Cu) for electrorefining. Connects to the positive terminal of the DC source.

3. **Cathode:** Starter sheet (for electrowinning), stainless steel plate, or Hg pool (for chlor-alkali). Connects to the negative terminal of the DC source.

4. **Diaphragm or membrane (optional):** Separates anolyte and catholyte compartments to prevent product crossover. Examples include Nafion™ membranes, ceramic diaphragms, or asbestos (historically).

5. **Stirring/flow system:** Magnetic stirrer, gas-lift pump, or impeller to maintain "well-mixed" bulk concentration assumption. Ensures $C_A$ is uniform throughout $V$.

6. **Reference electrode (optional):** Saturated calomel electrode (SCE) or Ag/AgCl electrode inserted through a Luggin capillary to measure individual electrode potentials.

7. **Temperature control:** Heating/cooling jacket or immersion coil to maintain isothermal operation.

8. **DC power supply:** Constant-current (galvanostat) or constant-voltage (potentiostat) source, with voltage and current displays.

9. **Gas venting system:** For evolved gases (H₂ at cathode, O₂ or Cl₂ at anode). Critical for safety.

10. **Sampling port:** For periodic withdrawal of small aliquots to track concentration vs. time (used to validate the exponential or linear conversion model).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Well-Mixed Assumption:** All design equations derived in Section 3 assume the bulk concentration $C_A(t)$ is **spatially uniform**. This is maintained in practice by vigorous stirring. Violation of this assumption introduces a spatial concentration profile, requiring the use of **dispersion models** or **CFD simulations**, which are **outside the scope** of the basic KTU 2024 batch cell syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer Format:** Each Part A question carries 3 marks. Provide a crisp, definition-style or one-line derivation answer. Avoid lengthy prose.

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Define a batch electrochemical cell. State any two of its distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A batch electrochemical cell is a non-steady-state electrochemical reactor in which a fixed quantity of electrolyte is charged at the start of operation, allowed to react under controlled electrical input, and discharged completely at the end of a predefined time period.

**Two distinguishing features:**

1. **Closed material boundary:** No continuous inflow of fresh electrolyte or outflow of product during the batch cycle. The reactant inventory decreases monotonically with time.
2. **Time-dependent conversion:** The conversion $X$ is a function of the operating time $t$ (e.g., linear under galvanostatic control, exponential under mass-transfer control), unlike a steady-state flow reactor where $X$ is time-invariant.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Write the general material balance equation for a constant-volume batch electrochemical cell and explain the role of Faraday's constant in it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a constant-volume batch cell, the material balance on the electroactive species $A$ is:

$$V \frac{dC_A}{dt} = r_A \cdot V$$

The rate $r_A$ is related to the instantaneous faradaic current $I$ by Faraday's law:

$$I = n F \mathcal{R}_A \quad \text{where} \quad F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$$

**Role of Faraday's constant:** $F$ converts the molar rate of electrochemical reaction (mol/s) into electric current (A). It is the charge carried by one mole of electrons. Its presence in the design equation bridges the **chemical rate domain** and the **electrical domain**, allowing the engineer to control the rate of reaction by adjusting the current supplied by the DC source.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Part B Structure:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Provide complete model solutions for both Question A and Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the fractional conversion $X$ as a function of time $t$ in a batch electrochemical cell operating under a **constant applied current** $I$. State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A batch cell is used to reduce Cr⁶⁺ to Cr³⁺ from 2 L of an industrial wastewater sample. The initial Cr⁶⁺ concentration is 0.5 mol/L. The reaction is:

$$\text{CrO}_4^{2-} + 8H^+ + 6e^- \to \text{Cr}^{3+} + 4H_2O$$

The cell is operated at a constant current of $5 \text{ A}$ with 100% current efficiency. Calculate: **(i)** the time required to achieve 90% conversion, and **(ii)** the mass of Cr³⁺ produced at that time (molar mass of Cr = 52 g/mol). **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

*Assumptions used:*
1. Constant cell volume $V$ (rigid vessel, no evaporation losses).
2. 100% current efficiency — all current contributes to the desired reaction.
3. Well-mixed bulk — uniform concentration $C_A(t)$ throughout the electrolyte.
4. Constant temperature and constant current $I$.

*Derivation:*

By Faraday's law, the moles of $A$ consumed in time $t$ is:

$$\Delta n_A = \frac{I t}{n F}$$

The initial moles of $A$ in the cell is:

$$n_{A,0} = C_{A,0} V$$

By definition of fractional conversion:

$$X = \frac{\Delta n_A}{n_{A,0}} = \frac{I t}{n F C_{A,0} V}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

*Given:*
- $V = 2 \text{ L}$, $C_{A,0} = 0.5 \text{ mol/L}$, $n = 6$, $I = 5$ A, $X = 0.90$.

*Step 1: Time to 90% conversion.*

$$t^* = \frac{n F C_{A,0} V X}{I} = \frac{(6)(96{,}485)(0.5)(2)(0.90)}{5}$$

$$t^* = \frac{520{,}019}{5} = 104{,}004 \text{ s} \approx 28.9 \text{ hours}$$

*Step 2: Mass of Cr³⁺ produced.*

Moles of Cr³⁺ produced = moles of Cr⁶⁺ reduced:

$$n_{Cr^{3+}} = C_{A,0} V \cdot X = (0.5)(2)(0.90) = 0.9 \text{ mol}$$

Mass of Cr³⁺:

$$m_{Cr^{3+}} = (0.9)(52) = 46.8 \text{ g}$$

**Final Answers:** $t^* \approx 28.9$ hours and $m_{Cr^{3+}} = 46.8$ g.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the time-dependent concentration $C_A(t)$ in a batch cell operating under **mass-transfer limited** (limiting current) conditions. Show clearly that the conversion approaches unity asymptotically. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industrial batch electrowinning cell has an electrolyte volume of $V = 1 \text{ m}^3$, an effective cathode area of $A_e = 50 \text{ m}^2$, and a mass-transfer coefficient of $k_m = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. The cell reduces Cu²⁺ to Cu at the cathode. Calculate: **(i)** the **characteristic time constant** $\tau$ of the cell, and **(ii)** the conversion $X$ achieved after 8 hours of operation. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

*Step 1: Material balance on species $A$ in the closed batch system.*

$$V \frac{dC_A}{dt} = -\mathcal{R}_A$$

*Step 2: Express the rate via Faraday's law at the limiting current.*

$$I_L = n F A_e k_m C_A \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathcal{R}_A = \frac{I_L}{n F} = A_e k_m C_A$$

*Step 3: Combine to form a first-order ODE.*

$$V \frac{dC_A}{dt} = -A_e k_m C_A \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dC_A}{dt} = -\frac{k_m A_e}{V} C_A$$

*Step 4: Integrate with $C_A(0) = C_{A,0}$.*

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_A}{C_{A,0}}\right) = -\frac{k_m A_e}{V} t$$

$$C_A(t) = C_{A,0} \exp\!\left(-\frac{k_m A_e t}{V}\right)$$

*Step 5: Show asymptotic approach to $X = 1$.*

$$X(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{k_m A_e t}{V}\right)$$

As $t \to \infty$, the exponential term $\to 0$, so $X \to 1$. The conversion approaches 100% asymptotically but never strictly reaches it in finite time — a hallmark of mass-transfer-limited batch operation.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

*Given:*
- $V = 1 \text{ m}^3$, $A_e = 50 \text{ m}^2$, $k_m = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s, $t = 8 \text{ h} = 28{,}800$ s.

*Step 1: Characteristic time constant.*

$$\tau = \frac{V}{k_m A_e} = \frac{1}{(5 \times 10^{-5})(50)} = \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 400 \text{ s}$$

*Step 2: Conversion at $t = 28{,}800$ s.*

$$X(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{28{,}800}{400}\right) = 1 - e^{-72}$$

$$X = 1 - (2.48 \times 10^{-32}) \approx 1.0$$

In practice, $X$ has effectively reached 100% after just a few multiples of $\tau$. Since $t = 28{,}800$ s is **72 times** the time constant, the conversion is essentially complete (more than $e^{-72}$ of the original reactant remains, which is negligibly small).

$$\boxed{X \approx 1.000 \text{ (essentially complete conversion)}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in Batch Cell Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch on $C_{A,0} V$:** A frequent error is mixing mol/L with L (instead of mol/m³ with m³). Always convert concentrations to mol/m³ when the volume is in m³, or use consistent units throughout.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "n" in Faraday's law:** Students often write $Q = F \cdot \Delta n$ instead of $Q = n F \cdot \Delta n$. The "$n$" is the number of electrons transferred per mole of species. Forgetting it costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing the two regimes:** The galvanostatic (linear) and mass-transfer-limited (exponential) models look similar but are different. Always check whether $I_{applied} < I_{L,0}$ to determine which model applies.
>
> 4. **Writing $\ln$ instead of $\log_{10}$:** When using integrated rate laws, be consistent with natural logarithm. The exponential model uses $e^{-t/\tau}$; do not write it as $10^{-t/\tau}$ unless you have converted the time constant appropriately.
>
> 5. **No statement of assumptions:** KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the assumptions (constant volume, well-mixed, 100% current efficiency, isothermal) are not explicitly listed at the start of a derivation.
>
> 6. **Not verifying limiting current validity:** In numerical problems, always check whether the applied current exceeds the initial limiting current. If it does, the simple linear model breaks down and a more advanced treatment is required.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Batch Cell**

- ✅ A **batch cell** is a **closed, non-steady-state electrochemical reactor** with no inflow or outflow during operation.
- ✅ The **bulk concentration** $C_A(t)$ is **spatially uniform** due to vigorous mixing (well-mixed assumption).
- ✅ The cell **volume $V$ is constant** throughout the batch cycle.
- ✅ The **conversion $X$** is defined as $X = (C_{A,0} - C_A)/C_{A,0}$ and ranges from 0 to 1.
- ✅ Under **galvanostatic (constant current) control**: $X(t) = \dfrac{I t}{n F C_{A,0} V}$ — **linear** in $t$.
- ✅ Under **mass-transfer-limited (limiting current) control**: $X(t) = 1 - e^{-t/\tau}$ — **exponential** in $t$ with characteristic time $\tau = V / (k_m A_e)$.
