- ✅ **Faraday's law** is the bridge between chemical rate and electrical current: $I = n F \mathcal{R}_A$.
- ✅ **Time to target conversion** under galvanostatic mode: $t^* = \dfrac{n F C_{A,0} V X}{I}$.
- ✅ **Current efficiency** $\epsilon_I$ falls below 100% when $I_{applied} > I_L(t)$ (excess current drives side reactions like water splitting).
- ✅ **Total charge** for complete conversion: $Q_{total} = n F C_{A,0} V$.
- ✅ **Specific charge** of a species: $E_{sp} = n F / \mathcal{M}$ (C/kg).
- ✅ **Key dimensionless number for batch cells**: ratio of operating time to characteristic time, $t/\tau$.
- ✅ Industrial applications: **electrowinning, electrorefining, electrosynthesis, water treatment**.
- ✅ **Comparison with flow cells**: Batch cells are preferred for small volumes, high purity, and flexible product mix; flow cells are preferred for large volumes and steady-state operation.
- ✅ The **assumptions** of constant volume, well-mixed bulk, isothermal operation, and 100% current efficiency are mandatory in any KTU derivation — always state them explicitly.
- ✅ The **$n$ in Faraday's law** refers to the **number of electrons transferred per mole of the electroactive species**, not the total cell stoichiometry coefficient.
- ✅ The **characteristic time** $\tau = V/(k_m A_e)$ is the design parameter that determines how long a batch takes — maximize $k_m$ (via flow) and $A_e$ to reduce cycle time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluidized Bed Electrochemical Reactor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT417 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fluidized Bed Electrochemical Reactor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Fluidized Bed Electrochemical Reactor (FBER)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> A **Fluidized Bed Electrochemical Reactor (FBER)** is a three-phase (or two-phase) electrochemical reactor in which a bed of small, conductive or non-conductive solid particles is suspended (fluidized) by the upward flow of an electrolyte, which acts simultaneously as the ionic conductor and the fluidizing medium. The suspended particles function as dispersed working electrodes, providing an extremely high specific surface area for electrochemical reactions such as metal recovery, deposition, or pollutant destruction.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Boiling Sand in a Glass of Water"

Imagine pouring water upward through a beaker containing fine sand at a controlled velocity:
- At **low flow** → the sand stays packed at the bottom (like a *packed bed*).
- At **higher flow** → the sand grains separate, swirl, and behave almost like a boiling liquid. This is the **"fluidized"** state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In an FBER, those swirling "sand grains" are actually **electrode particles** (often copper, nickel, or graphite). Because they are constantly moving, their surfaces are continuously renewed — just like a self-cleaning, ultra-high surface area working electrode. This solves one of the biggest headaches in conventional electrochemistry: **fouling and passivation** of the cathode.

**Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board Must-Know):**

| Metric | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Particle diameter, $d_p$ | **0.1 – 1.0 mm** | High surface/volume ratio |
| Bed voidage, $\varepsilon$ | **0.4 – 0.7** (fluidized) | Controls pressure drop |
| Minimum fluidization velocity, $u_{mf}$ | **0.5 – 10 mm/s** | Onset of fluidization |
| Current density, $i$ | **100 – 1000 A/m²** | High rate due to mass transfer |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world examples** you can quote in exams: Copper recovery from dilute mine drainage water, removal of heavy metals ($Cu^{2+}, Zn^{2+}, Cd^{2+}, Pb^{2+}$) from industrial wastewater, and the **Actinide Recovery** (e.g., the Elm Continuous Electrowinning process).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Structured Logic

The FBER works on four tightly coupled principles:

1. **Two-Phase Fluidization**
   - The electrolyte enters through a porous **distributor plate** at the bottom.
   - As velocity increases, drag force on particles = effective weight of particles → the bed fluidizes.
   - Beyond this point, the bed behaves like a liquid: pressure drop is essentially constant.

2. **Electrical Contact Strategy**
   - To make particles "electrodes," they must touch a **current feeder** (a current collector/feeder electrode, often placed centrally or as a grid).
   - When particles collide with the feeder, charge transfer occurs. The randomness of collisions is what makes FBER performance statistically driven.

3. **Mass Transfer Enhancement**
   - Particle motion + high turbulence → boundary layer is constantly disrupted.
   - This yields **mass transfer coefficients 5–20× higher** than in a planar or packed cell.

4. **Continuous Deposition & In-situ Renewal**
   - As metal deposits onto particles, they grow. They can be **withdrawn periodically**, and fresh particles added — enabling **continuous operation**.

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All equations below are **mandatory** for KTU ESE 2024 Scheme questions. Memorize units!

| # | Equation | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150(1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3}\dfrac{\mu u}{d_p^2} + \dfrac{1.75(1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon^3}\dfrac{\rho u^2}{d_p}$ | **Ergun Equation** – pressure drop across packed/fluidized bed | $\Delta P$ [Pa], $L$ [m], $\varepsilon$ [–], $\mu$ [Pa·s], $u$ [m/s], $d_p$ [m], $\rho$ [kg/m³] |
| 2 | $u_{mf} = \dfrac{d_p^2(\rho_p - \rho_f)g}{150\mu}\cdot\dfrac{\varepsilon_{mf}^3}{1-\varepsilon_{mf}} \quad (Re_{mf}<20)$ | **Min. fluidization velocity** (laminar/low Re) | $\rho_p,\rho_f$ [kg/m³], $g$ = **9.81 m/s²** |
| 3 | $u_{mf} = \sqrt{\dfrac{d_p(\rho_p-\rho_f)g\,\varepsilon_{mf}^3}{1.75\rho_f}} \quad (Re_{mf}>1000)$ | **Min. fluidization velocity** (turbulent/high Re) | — |
| 4 | $u_{mf} = \dfrac{Re_{mf}\,\mu}{d_p\,\rho_f}$ | Reynolds number definition at $u_{mf}$ | $Re_{mf}$ [–] |
| 5 | $Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408\,Ar} - 33.7$ | **Wen & Yu correlation** using Archimedes number | $Ar$ [–] |
| 6 | $Ar = \dfrac{d_p^3\rho_f(\rho_p-\rho_f)g}{\mu^2}$ | **Archimedes number** | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Sh = \dfrac{k_m d_p}{D} = 2 + 0.95\,Re_p^{0.5}Sc^{0.33}$ | **Mass transfer correlation** (FBER specific) | $k_m$ [m/s], $D$ [m²/s] |
| 8 | $Sc = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho_f D}$ | **Schmidt number** | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\eta_c = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{m\,n\,F}{I\,t} \times 100\%$ | **Current efficiency** | $m$ [kg], $I$ [A], $t$ [s], $F$ = **96,485 C/mol** |
| 10 | $\dfrac{\varepsilon^n}{u} = \text{const}$ (Richardson–Zaki) | **Bed expansion** correlation | $n$ ≈ 2.4–4.65 |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mining & Hydrometallurgy** → Recovery of Cu, Zn, Ni from leach liquors.
- **Environmental Engineering** → Heavy-metal removal from industrial effluents (regulatory compliance).
- **Electrosynthesis** → Continuous production of chemicals with self-cleaning electrodes.
- **Nuclear Industry** → Recovery of uranium/plutonium from spent fuel dissolver solutions.

> [!TIP]
> **Why FBER beats a regular cell?** A flat-plate cathode has area $A$. A kilogram of 0.5 mm copper spheres has surface area $\approx 5.7$ m² — meaning **5,000× more active area per unit volume**!
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Minimum Fluidization Velocity (from Ergun Equation)

The Ergun equation (Eq. 1) describes pressure drop across a bed. At the **onset of fluidization**, the pressure drop exactly balances the buoyant weight of the bed. We set up this equality and solve for $u_{mf}$.

### Step 1 — Force Balance at the Onset of Fluidization

The pressure drop multiplied by cross-sectional area $A$ must equal the effective weight (gravity minus buoyancy) of the particles:

$$\Delta P \cdot A = (\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g \cdot L \cdot A \cdot (1 - \varepsilon_{mf})$$

Dividing both sides by $L \cdot A$:

$$\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = (\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g\,(1 - \varepsilon_{mf})$$

### Step 2 — Equate with Ergun Equation

Substitute the Ergun pressure drop into the LHS of Step 1:

$$\dfrac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})^2}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3}\dfrac{\mu u_{mf}}{d_p^2} + \dfrac{1.75(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3}\dfrac{\rho_f u_{mf}^2}{d_p} = (\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g\,(1 - \varepsilon_{mf})$$

### Step 3 — Simplify Algebraically

Divide both sides by $(1-\varepsilon_{mf})$:

$$\dfrac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3}\dfrac{\mu u_{mf}}{d_p^2} + \dfrac{1.75}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3}\dfrac{\rho_f u_{mf}^2}{d_p} = (\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g$$

Multiply throughout by $\dfrac{\varepsilon_{mf}^3\,d_p^2}{1.75\,\rho_f\,d_p}$ to express in terms of the Reynolds number $Re_{mf} = \dfrac{d_p u_{mf}\rho_f}{\mu}$:

$$\dfrac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{1.75}\,Re_{mf} + Re_{mf}^2 = \dfrac{d_p^3\,\rho_f(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g\,\varepsilon_{mf}^3}{1.75\,\mu^2}$$

### Step 4 — Recognize the Archimedes Number

By definition $Ar = \dfrac{d_p^3\,\rho_f(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g}{\mu^2}$, so:

$$Re_{mf}^2 + \dfrac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{1.75}\,Re_{mf} - \dfrac{Ar\,\varepsilon_{mf}^3}{1.75} = 0$$

### Step 5 — Solve the Quadratic for $Re_{mf}$

Using the quadratic formula $x = \dfrac{-b + \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$:

$$Re_{mf} = \dfrac{-C_1 + \sqrt{C_1^2 + C_2\,Ar}}{2}$$

where $C_1 = \dfrac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{2(1.75)} = \dfrac{42.86(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{1}$ and $C_2 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{mf}^3}{1.75}$.

### Step 6 — Approximation by Wen & Yu (1973)

For typical $\varepsilon_{mf} \approx 0.4$, this reduces to the famous KTU-board result:

$$Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408\,Ar} - 33.7$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU ESE Style)

**Problem:** An FBER fluidizes copper particles (density $\rho_p = 8960$ kg/m³) of diameter $d_p = 500\ \mu\text{m}$ in an aqueous CuSO₄ solution (density $\rho_f = 1100$ kg/m³, viscosity $\mu = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s). Given $\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.45$, calculate the minimum fluidization velocity.

### Step-by-step solution:

**Step 1 — Compute the Archimedes number:**

$$Ar = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-4})^3 \times 1100 \times (8960-1100) \times 9.81}{(1.2 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

$$Ar = \dfrac{1.25 \times 10^{-10} \times 1100 \times 7860 \times 9.81}{1.44 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$Ar = \dfrac{1.0605}{1.44 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 7.365 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 2 — Plug into the low-$Re$ correlation (Eq. 2):**

$$u_{mf} = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-4})^2 \times (8960-1100) \times 9.81}{150 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3}} \times \dfrac{(0.45)^3}{1-0.45}$$

$$u_{mf} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-7} \times 7860 \times 9.81}{0.18} \times \dfrac{0.0911}{0.55}$$

$$u_{mf} = \dfrac{0.01927}{0.18} \times 0.1656 = 0.1071 \times 0.1656$$

$$\boxed{u_{mf} \approx 1.77 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m/s} = 17.7\ \text{mm/s}}$$

**Step 3 — Verify $Re_{mf}$:**

$$Re_{mf} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.0177 \times 1100}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 8.11$$

Since $Re_{mf} < 20$, our use of the laminar correlation (Eq. 2) was **valid**. ✔

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: FBER vs Other Reactors

| Feature | Packed Bed | FBER | Parallel Plate (Cell) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific electrode area | High | **Very High** | Low |
| Mass transfer coefficient | Moderate | **Highest** | Low |
| Continuous metal removal | Poor | **Excellent** | Excellent |
| Particle fouling | Severe | **Negligible** | Severe |
| Particle attrition/abrasion | None | **Moderate** | None |
| Current efficiency | 60–80% | 70–95% | 80–95% |
| Operational complexity | Low | **High** | Lowest |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Typical FBER

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrolyte Inlet] --> B[Pump Reservoir]
    B --> C[Flow Control Valve]
    C --> D[Flow Distributor Plate]
    D --> E[Conical Reactor Vessel Bottom]
    E --> F[Fluidized Particle Bed]
    G[Central Current Feeder Anode] --> F
    H[Cathode Current Collector Grid] --> F
    F --> I[Expanded Freeboard Zone]
    I --> J[Particle Entrainment Cyclone]
    J --> K[Clean Electrolyte Outlet]
    J --> L[Recycled Particles Back to Bed]
    F --> M[DC Power Supply 2 to 12 V]
    M --> G
    M --> H
    N[Control Loop Flow Rate and pH Sensor] --> B
    N --> O[Data Acquisition PLC]

    classDef inout fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef reactor fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef power fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000
    class A,K inout
    class F,G,H,I reactor
    class M power
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Walkthrough** (for examiners who prefer reading architecture over physical sketches):
> 1. **Pump + Valve** → Sets the linear velocity $u$.
> 2. **Distributor Plate** → Ensures uniform flow and supports the bed at shutdown.
> 3. **Central Anode (Feeder)** → Plugs the bed into the DC circuit.
> 4. **Cathode Grid** → Provides return path; particles behave as mobile cathodes.
> 5. **Freeboard + Cyclone** → Stops fine particles from escaping.

## 4.2 Process Flow Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Start: Pump On] --> P2{u more than umf}
    P2 -- No --> P3[Bed is Packed or Channelling]
    P2 -- Yes --> P4[Fluidized Bed: Particles Mobile]
    P4 --> P5[Metal Ions in Solution Diffuse to Particles]
    P5 --> P6[Electrochemical Reduction on Particle Surface]
    P6 --> P7{Metal Loading on Particle}
    P7 -- Below Critical --> P4
    P7 -- At Threshold --> P8[Withdraw Loaded Particles]
    P8 --> P9[Replenish Fresh Seed Particles]
    P9 --> P4
    P3 --> P10[Increase Flow Rate]
    P10 --> P2
```

## 4.3 Voltage–Current–Mass Transfer Relationship Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    U[Superficial Velocity u] -- "Ergun Equation" --> PD[Pressure Drop DP]
    U -- "Re" --> MT[Mass Transfer Coefficient km]
    PD -- "Equilibrium at umf" --> MFV[Minimum Fluidization]
    MT -- "Higher Sh number" --> CE[Current Efficiency]
    MFV --> CE
    I[Applied Current] --> CE
    CE --> R[Metal Recovery Rate]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a Fluidized Bed Electrochemical Reactor. State any two industrial applications.*

**Model Answer:**
A Fluidized Bed Electrochemical Reactor is a three-phase electrochemical system in which an upward flow of electrolyte suspends (fluidizes) a bed of fine conducting particles, which act as dispersed working electrodes. The particles are maintained in dynamic suspension, providing high specific surface area and excellent mass transfer.

**Applications (any two):**
1. Recovery of heavy metals (Cu, Zn, Cd, Pb) from industrial wastewater streams.
2. Electrowinning of metals from dilute leach liquors in hydrometallurgy (e.g., the **Elm/Chemelec cell** for Cu).
3. Electrolytic destruction of organic pollutants.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Write the Ergun equation. What is the significance of the term $(1-\varepsilon)$ in it?*

**Model Answer:**
The Ergun equation for pressure drop per unit length of bed is:

$$\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150(1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3}\dfrac{\mu u}{d_p^2} + \dfrac{1.75(1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon^3}\dfrac{\rho_f u^2}{d_p}$$

The term $(1-\varepsilon)$ is the **solid volume fraction** of the bed. It represents the fraction of bed volume occupied by particles. As $\varepsilon \rightarrow 1$ (all fluid), $(1-\varepsilon)\rightarrow 0$ and pressure drop vanishes — consistent with empty pipe flow.

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] *Derive an expression for the minimum fluidization velocity starting from the Ergun equation and the force balance on a fluidized bed.*

**Model Answer (Step-by-step):**

**Step 1 — Force balance:** At incipient fluidization, the pressure drop balances the net buoyant weight of particles.

$$\Delta P = (\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g\,L\,(1-\varepsilon_{mf})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Ergun equation for $\Delta P$:**

$$\dfrac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})^2}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3}\dfrac{\mu u_{mf}}{d_p^2} + \dfrac{1.75(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3}\dfrac{\rho_f u_{mf}^2}{d_p} = (\rho_p-\rho_f)\,g\,(1-\varepsilon_{mf})$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $(1-\varepsilon_{mf})$ and rearrange:**

$$\dfrac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})\mu u_{mf}}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3\,d_p^2} + \dfrac{1.75\,\rho_f u_{mf}^2}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3\,d_p} = (\rho_p-\rho_f)\,g$$

**Step 4 — Multiply throughout by $\dfrac{d_p^3\,\rho_f}{150\,\mu^2}$ and substitute $Re_{mf}=d_p u_{mf}\rho_f/\mu$:**

$$\dfrac{Re_{mf}}{1.75}\left[\dfrac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})\mu}{1.75} + Re_{mf}\right] = \dfrac{d_p^3\rho_f(\rho_p-\rho_f)g\,\varepsilon_{mf}^3}{1.75\,\mu^2}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the Archimedes number $Ar$:**

$$Re_{mf}^2 + \dfrac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{1.75}Re_{mf} = \dfrac{Ar\,\varepsilon_{mf}^3}{1.75}$$

**Step 6 — Solve the quadratic using the positive root:**

$$\boxed{Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408\,Ar} - 33.7}$$

This is the **Wen & Yu correlation** (also known as the KTU-recognised form).

**(b)** [7 Marks] *An FBER uses 0.6 mm copper particles in 0.5 M CuSO₄ solution. Given: $\rho_p = 8960$ kg/m³, $\rho_f = 1100$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.0\times10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\varepsilon_{mf}=0.45$. Calculate $u_{mf}$ using the laminar correlation. Also estimate the bed expansion if superficial velocity is doubled.*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Archimedes number:**

$$Ar = \dfrac{(6\times10^{-4})^3 \cdot 1100 \cdot (8960-1100) \cdot 9.81}{(10^{-3})^2} = \dfrac{2.545\times10^{-5} \cdot 7.86\times10^6}{10^{-6}} = 2.00\times10^{8}$$

Wait — recomputing carefully:

$$Ar = \dfrac{(2.16\times10^{-10})(1100)(7860)(9.81)}{1\times10^{-6}} = \dfrac{1.832\times10^{-2}}{10^{-6}} = 1.832\times10^{4}$$

**Step 2 — Laminar formula (Eq. 2):**

$$u_{mf} = \dfrac{(6\times10^{-4})^2 (7860)(9.81)}{150(10^{-3})}\cdot\dfrac{(0.45)^3}{0.55}$$

$$u_{mf} = \dfrac{(3.6\times10^{-7})(7860)(9.81)}{0.15}\cdot\dfrac{0.0911}{0.55}$$

$$u_{mf} = \dfrac{0.02775}{0.15}\cdot 0.1656 = 0.185 \times 0.1656 = 0.0306\ \text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{u_{mf} \approx 30.6\ \text{mm/s}}$$

**Step 3 — Verify Re:**

$$Re_{mf} = \dfrac{6\times10^{-4}\times 0.0306 \times 1100}{10^{-3}} = 20.2 \approx 20 \text{ (borderline OK)}$$

**Step 4 — Bed expansion at $u = 2 u_{mf}$:** Using Richardson–Zaki with $n\approx 3$:

$$u = u_i \cdot \varepsilon^n \;\Rightarrow\; \varepsilon = \left(\dfrac{u}{u_i}\right)^{1/n} \approx \left(\dfrac{2u_{mf}}{u_i}\right)^{1/3}$$

Assuming $u_i \approx u_{mf}$ and $n=3$: $\varepsilon \approx (2)^{1/3} \approx 1.26$ → but this exceeds 1, so physically the bed would expand into freeboard. Practically, voidage rises to **0.55–0.65**, giving bed height expansion of **~25–45%** over static.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] *With the help of a neat functional block diagram, describe the construction and working of a fluidized bed electrochemical reactor. Discuss its advantages over a conventional cell.*

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (refer to Section 4 diagram):** The FBER consists of:
- A **vertical cylindrical (or conical-bottom) vessel** to promote uniform flow.
- A **porous distributor plate** at the base for even electrolyte distribution.
- **Anode feeder electrodes** (often made of PbO₂, DSA, or graphite rods placed centrally).
- **Cathode particle bed** (fine conducting particles such as Cu beads, Ni, or graphite).
- A **freeboard region** to allow particle disengagement and prevent entrainment.
- A **cyclone separator** to capture fine particles from the outlet stream.
- A **recirculation pump** and a **DC power source (2–12 V, high current)**.

**Working Principle:**
1. Electrolyte containing metal ions enters from the bottom.
2. As flow increases to $u_{mf}$, particles get suspended.
3. On applying DC, the feeder (anode) polarizes the particles through particle–feeder collisions.
4. Metal ions reduce and deposit on particle surfaces.
5. Loaded particles can be withdrawn continuously for harvesting.

**Advantages over a conventional cell:**
- **Self-cleaning cathode surface** → no fouling.
- **High mass transfer** (5–20× that of a flat plate).
- **Operates on very dilute streams** (down to ~10 ppm of metal).
- **Continuous operation** possible with particle replenishment.

**(b)** [7 Marks] *A Cu-rod cathode in a planar cell operates at 200 A/m², with $k_m = 2\times 10^{-5}$ m/s. In an FBER, $k_m = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s. Compare the limiting current densities and explain why the FBER allows much higher recovery rates from dilute solutions.*

**Model Answer:**

The limiting current density is given by:

$$i_L = n F k_m C_{bulk}$$

For $Cu^{2+}$ ($n=2$), assume $C_{bulk}=100$ mol/m³ (= 0.1 M):

**Planar cell:**

$$i_L = 2 \times 96485 \times 2\times 10^{-5} \times 100 = 386\ \text{A/m}^2$$

**FBER:**

$$i_L = 2 \times 96485 \times 1.5\times 10^{-4} \times 100 = 2894\ \text{A/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{i_{L,FBER}}{i_{L,planar}} = 7.5}$$

The FBER achieves **7.5× the limiting current density** of the planar cell, so it is **7.5× faster** at metal removal for the same bulk concentration. For dilute solutions (e.g., $C_{bulk} = 1$ mol/m³), the planar cell is so slow it is uneconomical, but the FBER can still process 100 mol/m³/h with ease. The FBER's turbulence continually renews the boundary layer, preventing the diffusion-limited depletion zone from forming.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully**
> 1. **Do not** confuse **bed voidage ($\varepsilon$)** with **solid fraction ($1-\varepsilon$)** in Ergun's equation. KTU examiners will deduct 1 mark.
> 2. The **Ergun equation has TWO terms**: a viscous (laminar) term and an inertial (turbulent) term. Forgetting either one = **−2 marks** in derivations.
> 3. **Units of $u_{mf}$** must be in **m/s** in the final answer, not mm/s.
> 4. Many students write $Re_{mf}=u_{mf}d_p/\mu$ — the correct form is $Re_{mf} = u_{mf}d_p\rho_f/\mu$ (fluid density, not particle density).
> 5. The Wen & Yu correlation is the **Wen & Yu correlation**, not "Wen-Yu equation" — phrasing matters in 14-mark derivations.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] FBER = **three-phase (or pseudo-two-phase) reactor** with **dispersed cathode particles**.
- [x] **Distributor plate** is critical for uniform fluidization.
- [x] **Ergun equation** has viscous + inertial terms — both are mandatory.
- [x] $u_{mf}$ is determined by **force balance**: pressure drop = buoyant weight.
- [x] For $Re_{mf}<20$: use **laminar formula**; for $Re_{mf}>1000$: use **turbulent** formula.
- [x] **Wen & Yu correlation** = $Re_{mf}=\sqrt{33.7^2+0.0408\,Ar}-33.7$ is the KTU preferred form.
- [x] **Richardson–Zaki** governs bed expansion: $u = u_i \varepsilon^n$.
- [x] Mass transfer in FBER follows $Sh = 2 + 0.95 Re^{0.5} Sc^{0.33}$ — remember the **0.5 and 0.33 exponents**.
- [x] Current efficiency: $\eta_c = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$.
- [x] FBER **advantages**: high $k_m$, self-cleaning, dilute streams, continuous operation.
- [x] FBER **disadvantages**: particle attrition, complex current collection, fluidization energy cost.
- [x] **Key constants**: $g = 9.81$ m/s², $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).
- [x] **Typical applications**: heavy-metal recovery, electrowinning, wastewater treatment, nuclear fuel processing.
- [x] **Compare with**: packed bed (fouling-prone), parallel plate (low area), moving bed (mechanical complexity).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Mnemonic for Ergun Equation:** *"**VIM** to fluidize — **V**iscous, **I**nertial, **M**omentum balance"*. The viscous term dominates at low $Re$, the inertial at high $Re$, and the moment balance gives $u_{mf}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Filter Press Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT417 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Filter Press Cell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Filter Press Cell (Plate-and-Frame Electrochemical Cell):** An electrochemical reactor of the **parallel-plate (PP) flow type** in which a stack of alternating **anode** and **cathode** plates, separated by **gaskets** and (optionally) **diaphragms/membranes**, is clamped between two heavy end-plates by tie-rods — geometrically and mechanically analogous to a conventional **filter press** used for solid-liquid separation. The electrolyte is forced through narrow, rectangular inter-electrode channels under **flow-by** or **flow-through** hydrodynamic regimes.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the filter press cell is the canonical **plate-type electrochemical reactor** covered under **electrochemical reactor design (Module 4)**. It is the workhorse reactor of industrial electrolysis whenever compactness, modularity, and uniform current distribution are required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECHT417, Module 4):** Students are expected to know — (i) construction, (ii) classification into **monopolar** and **bipolar** configurations, (iii) the governing **mass-transfer correlations** (Lévêque equation), (iv) **current distribution** concepts, and (v) representative **industrial applications** such as chlor-alkali, water electrolysis, and electro-organic synthesis.

## 1.2 The "Why" — Conceptual Analogy

Think of a **sandwich press / panini maker** in your kitchen. You take two hot iron plates, place fillings between them, and clamp them down with a screw. Now scale this idea to 30, 50, or 100 plate pairs, where each "filling" is a thin, electrolyte-filled chamber, and each "iron plate" is either an anode or a cathode. That is essentially a **filter press cell**.

A still closer engineering analogy is the **plate heat exchanger** used in dairy and HVAC industries — same geometry, same concept of stacked flat plates with gaskets, but the "fluid" being transferred here is **charge (electrons/ions)** rather than heat.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook:** *Filter-Press = Plate-and-Frame = Sandwich with many fillings*. Each filling is an electrochemical cell; the bread-slices are the electrodes.

## 1.3 Why It Matters in Engineering

The filter press cell offers the **highest specific electrode area (a_e, m²/m³)** among conventional electrochemical reactor geometries — typically **100–500 m²/m³** versus 10–50 m²/m³ for tanks and 30–80 m²/m³ for fluidized-bed cells. This directly translates into:

- **Lower specific cell volume** for a given production rate.
- **Lower ohmic (IR) drop** because inter-electrode gaps can be as small as **1–5 mm**.
- **Modular scale-up** by simply adding more plate pairs.

**Standard metrics you must memorise for KTU exams:**

| Metric | Typical Range | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Inter-electrode gap | **1 – 10 mm** | $b$ |
| Specific electrode area | **100 – 500 m²/m³** | $a_e$ |
| Operating current density | **100 – 5000 A/m²** | $i$ |
| Typical linear flow velocity | **0.01 – 0.5 m/s** | $v$ |
| Cell voltage per compartment | **2 – 5 V** | $V_{cell}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy — Block-by-Block

A filter press cell is an assembly of the following sub-systems:

1. **End Plates (Head & Foot Plates):** Two thick metal plates (steel, sometimes rubber-lined) that bear the entire mechanical compression load.
2. **Tie Rods / Tension Rods:** High-tensile steel rods (typically 4 of them) that compress the stack. Torque-controlled nuts apply a uniform sealing pressure of roughly **5–15 bar** on the gaskets.
3. **Electrodes (Anodes & Cathodes):**
   - **Bipolar configuration:** Each plate acts as anode on one face and cathode on the other. Electrodes are *not* externally wired — current passes *through* them.
   - **Monopolar configuration:** All anodes are connected to the positive bus-bar, all cathodes to the negative bus-bar. Plates are externally wired.
4. **Gaskets / Frames:** Inert elastomeric (EPDM, Viton, PTFE) frames that define the flow channel geometry and prevent leaks.
5. **Diaphragms or Membranes** (optional): Asbestos, ion-exchange membrane (Nafion™ in chlor-alkali), or porous polymer separator placed between electrode pairs to compartmentalise anolyte and catholyte.
6. **Inlet & Outlet Manifolds:** Distribution channels that feed fresh electrolyte and collect spent electrolyte uniformly to every cell pair.
7. **Current Bus-bars / Electrical Connections:** Copper or aluminium plates carrying current to (or from) the end electrodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently-Tested Fact:** In a **bipolar** filter press, the **number of cell pairs = (number of plates − 1)**, and the **total stack voltage = (number of cell pairs) × V_cell**. The current is the *same* through every cell pair. In a **monopolar** filter press, the voltage is the *same* across every cell pair, but the total current = (number of cell pairs) × current per cell.

## 2.2 Operational Sequence

The electrolyte is pumped from a storage/feed tank → through the inlet manifold → into the narrow inter-electrode channel → across the electrode surface (where reaction occurs) → out through the outlet manifold → back to the tank (recirculation mode) or to a downstream product-separation step (once-through mode).

Step-wise logic of a single channel:

- **Step 1:** Electrolyte enters the channel with a uniform velocity profile.
- **Step 2:** Ions migrate under the influence of the **electric field** (perpendicular to flow) toward their respective electrodes.
- **Step 3:** Electrochemical reactions at anode (oxidation) and cathode (reduction) consume/produce species. Concentration gradients develop → mass transfer controls the limiting current.
- **Step 4:** Spent electrolyte leaves with depleted reactant concentration. The depleted stream is re-mixed in the external tank to maintain overall conversion.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important page** for KTU Module 4 numericals. Memorise it before attempting any exam question on filter press cells.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cell voltage (overall) | $V_{cell} = E_{eq} + \vert \eta_a \vert + \vert \eta_c \vert + \sum IR$ | $E_{eq}$ = thermodynamic reversible emf; $\eta$ = overpotentials; $IR$ = ohmic drops |
| 2 | Specific electrode area | $a_e = \dfrac{n \cdot A_{plate}}{V_{reactor}}$ | $A_{plate}$ = active area of one electrode face; $V_{reactor}$ = total reactor volume; units **m²/m³** |
| 3 | Hydraulic diameter (rectangular channel) | $d_h = \dfrac{4 \cdot b \cdot w}{2(b+w)} \approx 2b$ for $w \gg b$ | $b$ = inter-electrode gap; $w$ = channel width |
| 4 | Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, d_h}{\mu}$ | **Laminar: $Re < 2000$**; **Turbulent: $Re > 4000$** |
| 5 | Schmidt number | $Sc = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho \, D}$ | $D$ = diffusivity (m²/s); $Sc \gg 1$ for liquids |
| 6 | Sherwood number | $Sh = \dfrac{k_m \, d_h}{D}$ | $k_m$ = mass-transfer coefficient (m/s) |
| 7 | **Lévêque correlation (Laminar, $Re < 2000$, $Sc \gg 1$)** | $Sh = 1.85 \, (Re \cdot Sc \cdot d_h / L)^{1/3}$ | The **signature equation** for parallel-plate electrochemical reactors. KTU loves this. |
| 8 | Turbulent correlation | $Sh = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Sc^{1/3}$ | Dittus–Boelter analog for mass transfer |
| 9 | Limiting current density | $i_L = z \, F \, k_m \, C_{bulk}$ | The maximum current before concentration polarisation dominates |
| 10 | Energy consumption | $W = \dfrac{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{m_{product} \cdot 1000} \;\; \text{kWh/kg}$ | $V_{cell}$ in volts, $I$ in amps, $t$ in hours |
| 11 | Space velocity (inverse of space time) | $s_v = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{V_{reactor}} \;\; \text{s}^{-1}$ | $\dot{V}$ = volumetric flow rate |
| 12 | Fractional conversion per pass | $X = 1 - \exp(-k_m \, a_e \, \tau)$ | $\tau = V_{reactor}/\dot{V}$ = residence time |
| 13 | Pressure drop (laminar, rectangular channel) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \, \mu \, L \, v}{b^2}$ | Pure Hagen–Poiseuille analog for $w \gg b$ |
| 14 | Wagner number (current distribution) | $Wa = \dfrac{\kappa \cdot (dE/di)}{L}$ | $\kappa$ = electrolyte conductivity; $Wa \to \infty$ ⇒ uniform current |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-notation trap:** KTU sometimes writes the Lévêque equation as $k_m = 1.85 \cdot D \cdot \left( \dfrac{v}{d_h L} \right)^{1/3} \cdot \left( \dfrac{D}{d_h} \right)^{1/3}$. The two forms are **dimensionally and numerically equivalent** — derive them from the same Sh definition before the exam so you don't get confused.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The filter press cell is *the* reactor of choice in:

- **Chlor-alkali industry (NaCl → Cl₂, H₂, NaOH):** Modern membrane cells (e.g., Uhde, Asahi Kasey) are bipolar filter presses with up to **150+ cell pairs** in a single press, producing thousands of tonnes of chlorine per day.
- **Water electrolysis:** Alkaline and PEM electrolyser stacks (Nel, Siemens Silyzer) are essentially bipolar filter presses with diaphragms/membranes.
- **Electrowinning of Cu, Zn, Ni, Mn** (though tank-house cells dominate in primary metal refining, bench-scale filter-press prototypes are used for R&D).
- **Electro-organic synthesis:** m-, p-aminophenol, adiponitrile (Monsanto process), and many fine chemicals.
- **Electrochemical wastewater treatment:** For COD removal, heavy-metal recovery, and disinfection.
- **Redox flow batteries** (vanadium, iron-chromium) — stack architectures borrowed directly from filter-press design.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical note:** This section contains the **most important derivations** in KTU Module 4. You should be able to reproduce them on a blank sheet within 8–10 minutes during the examination. The step-by-step logic mirrors what is expected in the model answer sheet.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Mass-Transfer Coefficient via the Lévêque Equation

The Lévêque equation is obtained by solving the **convective-diffusion equation** in a thin boundary layer near a flat plate under **laminar, fully-developed flow**. The full derivation requires solving the parabolic velocity profile together with Fick's second law in the diffusion boundary layer. We present the engineering derivation that KTU expects.

### Step 1: Set up the convective diffusion equation in the channel

For a rectangular channel of width $w$ and inter-electrode gap $b$, with $w \gg b$ (so that the velocity is essentially one-dimensional in the $x$ (flow) direction and varies only in the $y$ (wall-normal) direction):

$$v_x(y) = v_{max} \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{y}{b/2} \right)^2 \right]$$

where $v_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2} \bar{v}$ is the centreline velocity and $\bar{v}$ is the mean velocity.

### Step 2: Apply Fick's second law in the mass-transfer boundary layer

The steady-state, fully-developed concentration boundary layer is governed by:

$$v_x(y) \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2}$$

### Step 3: Approximate the velocity near the wall (the Lévêque approximation)

Close to the wall, the parabolic profile is **linearised**:

$$v_x(y) \approx \left( \frac{d v_x}{d y} \right)_{y=0} \cdot y = \left( \frac{4 v_{max}}{b} \right) y$$

For a hydraulic-diameter-based mean velocity $\bar{v}$, this becomes:

$$v_x(y) \approx \left( \frac{2 \bar{v}}{b/2} \right) y = \frac{4 \bar{v}}{b} \, y$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why linearise?** The concentration boundary layer is **very thin** compared to the channel half-gap, so only the region very close to the wall — where $v_x$ is linear in $y$ — matters. This single linearisation is what gives the Lévêque equation its famous **one-third power law**.

### Step 4: Substitute and solve

Substituting into the diffusion equation:

$$\frac{4 \bar{v}}{b} \, y \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2}$$

This PDE admits a self-similar solution of the form $\eta = y / \left( 9 D b x / (4 \bar{v}) \right)^{1/3}$:

$$C(x,y) = C_{bulk} \cdot \frac{1}{\Gamma(4/3)} \int_0^{\eta} \exp(-\xi^3/9) \, d\xi$$

### Step 5: Extract the wall flux and obtain $k_m$

The local mass-transfer coefficient is:

$$k_m(x) = \frac{1}{\Gamma(4/3)} \left( \frac{4 \bar{v} D^2}{9 b x} \right)^{1/3}$$

Averaging over the channel length $L$:

$$k_m = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L k_m(x) \, dx = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\Gamma(4/3)} \left( \frac{4 \bar{v} D^2}{9 b L} \right)^{1/3}$$

### Step 6: Numerically evaluate the constant

Using $\Gamma(4/3) \approx 0.8930$ and simplifying:

$$k_m = 1.85 \, D \left( \frac{\bar{v}}{b \, L} \right)^{1/3} \left( \frac{D}{b} \right)^{1/3} \cdot \frac{1}{b^{1/3}}$$

Regrouping:

$$k_m = 1.85 \left( \frac{\bar{v} D^2}{b^2 L} \right)^{1/3}$$

### Step 7: Convert to dimensionless form (Sherwood number)

Dividing both sides by $D/b$ and recognising $d_h = 2b$ for a wide rectangular channel:

$$\frac{k_m \, d_h}{D} = 1.85 \left( Re \cdot Sc \cdot \frac{d_h}{L} \right)^{1/3}$$

$$\boxed{\;Sh = 1.85 \, (Re \cdot Sc \cdot d_h / L)^{1/3}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity window for KTU:** This equation is valid for $Re < 2000$ (laminar flow), $Sc > 100$ (typical for liquid electrolytes), and **fully-developed flow** (i.e., entrance length $L_e \approx 0.05 \, Re \cdot d_h \ll L$ is *not* required — the Lévêque analysis inherently accounts for developing flow).

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Limiting Current Density from Lévêque

The limiting current density is the current at which the surface concentration of the electroactive species drops to zero:

$$i_L = z \, F \, k_m \, C_{bulk}$$

Substituting the Lévêque expression for $k_m$:

$$i_L = z F C_{bulk} \cdot 1.85 \left( \frac{\bar{v} D^2}{b^2 L} \right)^{1/3}$$

This can also be expressed in dimensionless form. Multiplying and dividing by appropriate powers of $b$ and $D$:

$$\frac{i_L \, b}{z F D C_{bulk}} = 1.85 \left( \frac{\bar{v} b}{D} \right)^{1/3} \left( \frac{b}{L}\right)^{1/3}$$

Or, in terms of the Sherwood, Reynolds, and Schmidt numbers:

$$\frac{i_L \, b}{z F D C_{bulk}} = 1.85 \, (Re \cdot Sc \cdot b/L)^{1/3} \cdot \frac{1}{2^{1/3}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering implication:** The limiting current density scales with the **cube root** of the mean velocity. Doubling the flow rate increases $i_L$ by a factor of $2^{1/3} \approx 1.26$ only. This is why simply pumping harder has limited benefit — **mass transfer in filter-press cells is fundamentally diffusion-limited at the wall**, and the only way to dramatically raise $i_L$ is to either narrow the gap $b$ or increase $D$ via temperature, supporting electrolyte, or turbulence promoters.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Overall Cell Voltage for a Filter-Press Cell

For a single cell pair in the stack, the operating cell voltage is the sum of every voltage loss:

$$V_{cell} = E_{cathode}^{eq} - E_{anode}^{eq} + \eta_{a,act} + \eta_{c,act} + \eta_{a,conc} + \eta_{c,conc} + \eta_{ohm,electrolyte} + \eta_{ohm,electrode} + \eta_{ohm,contact} + \eta_{ohm,membrane}$$

### Step-by-step expansion:

- **(a) Thermodynamic term:** $\Delta E_{eq} = E_{cathode}^{eq} - E_{anode}^{eq}$ — obtained from the Nernst equation.
- **(b) Activation overpotentials:** $\eta_{a,act}$ and $\eta_{c,act}$ — given by the Butler–Volmer equation. For high overpotentials, Tafel approximation: $\eta_{act} = (RT/\alpha z F) \ln(i / i_0)$.
- **(c) Concentration overpotentials:** $\eta_{conc} = (RT/zF) \ln(C_{bulk}/C_{surface})$.
- **(d) Ohmic drop in electrolyte:** $\eta_{ohm,el} = i \cdot b / \kappa$ — for a planar gap of thickness $b$ and conductivity $\kappa$.
- **(e) Ohmic drop in membrane** (chlor-alkali case): $\eta_{ohm,m} = i \cdot R_m$ where $R_m$ is the area-specific membrane resistance (typically **0.5–2 Ω·cm²**).
- **(f) Ohmic drop in electrode + contacts:** usually small, lumped into $0.01$–$0.05$ V.

### Worked numerical template for a KTU-style problem:

> **Given:** $i = 3000 \,\text{A/m}^2$, $b = 3 \,\text{mm}$, $\kappa = 20 \,\text{S/m}$, $E_{eq} = 2.19 \,\text{V}$ (chlor-alkali), $\eta_{a,act} = 0.18 \,\text{V}$, $\eta_{c,act} = 0.22 \,\text{V}$, $R_m = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \,\Omega\cdot\text{m}^2$, contact/diaphragm drop $\approx 0.15 \,\text{V}$.
>
> **Calculate:** $V_{cell}$.
>
> **Solution:**

$$\eta_{ohm,el} = \frac{i \cdot b}{\kappa} = \frac{3000 \times 0.003}{20} = 0.45 \;\text{V}$$

$$\eta_{ohm,m} = i \cdot R_m = 3000 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.45 \;\text{V}$$

$$V_{cell} = 2.19 + 0.18 + 0.22 + 0.45 + 0.45 + 0.15 = 3.64 \;\text{V}$$

This value (~3.6 V) is characteristic of modern membrane chlor-alkali cells, validating the model.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Filter Press Cell?

| Criterion | Filter Press Cell | Tank / Pond Cell | 3-D / Fluidised Cell |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific electrode area | **High (100–500 m²/m³)** | Low (10–50) | Medium (30–80) |
| Mass-transfer coefficient | High (laminar Lévêque) | Low | Medium |
| Modular scale-up | **Excellent** | Poor | Moderate |
| Solid handling | Poor (clogs) | Good (settles) | **Excellent** |
| Capital cost per m² electrode | Moderate | Low | High |
| Best for gas-evolving reactions | **Yes (Cl₂, H₂, O₂)** | Yes | Limited |
| Best for soluble product + high purity | **Yes** | No | No |
| Heat removal | Good (thin gap) | Poor | Moderate |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU one-liner for viva:** *"Filter press cells are preferred when the reactant/product is in solution, when gas evolution is significant, and when modular scale-up is required. They are avoided when the feed contains suspended solids that would clog the narrow inter-electrode gap."*

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Bipolar Filter Press Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INLET["FRESH ELECTROLYTE FEED TANK"] --> PUMP1["CIRCULATION PUMP"]
    PUMP1 --> MANIFOLDIN["INLET MANIFOLD"]
    MANIFOLDIN --> CHAMBER1["CELL PAIR 1 - ANOLYTE CHAMBER"]
    CHAMBER1 --> MEMB1["MEMBRANE OR DIAPHRAGM"]
    MEMB1 --> CHAMBER2["CELL PAIR 1 - CATHOLYTE CHAMBER"]
    CHAMBER2 --> CHAMBER3["CELL PAIR 2 - ANOLYTE CHAMBER"]
    CHAMBER3 --> MEMB2["MEMBRANE OR DIAPHRAGM"]
    MEMB2 --> CHAMBER4["CELL PAIR 2 - CATHOLYTE CHAMBER"]
    CHAMBER4 --> MANIFOLDOUT["OUTLET MANIFOLD"]
    MANIFOLDOUT --> SEP["GAS LIQUID SEPARATOR"]
    SEP --> SPENT["SPENT ELECTROLYTE TANK"]
    SPENT --> PUMP1
    BUSPOS["DC POSITIVE BUSBAR"] --> ENDANODE["END ANODE PLATE"]
    ENDANODE -. CURRENT FLOWS THROUGH STACK .-> ENDCAThode["END CATHODE PLATE"]
    ENDCAThode --> BUSNEG["DC NEGATIVE BUSBAR"]
    BUSBAR_CNTRL["RECTIFIER AND CURRENT CONTROL"] --> BUSPOS
    BUSBAR_CNTRL --> BUSNEG
    COOLING["HEAT EXCHANGER FOR COOLING"] --> MANIFOLDIN
    MANIFOLDOUT --> COOLING
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENDP["A END PLATE"] --> G1["A GASKET"]
    G1 --> ANODE1["A ANODE PLATE"]

    G2 --> DIA1["A DIAPHRAGM OR MEMBRANE"]
    DIA1 --> G3["A GASKET FRAME"]
    G3 --> CATHODE1["A CATHODE PLATE"]
    CATHODE1 --> G4["A GASKET"]

    DIA2 --> G6["A GASKET"]

    CATHODE2 --> G7["A GASKET"]

```

## 4.3 Process Flow Inside a Single Cell Channel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START["ELECTROLYTE ENTERS CHANNEL AT X EQUALS ZERO"] --> DEV["HYDRODYNAMIC ENTRANCE REGION L_E"]
    DEV --> CL["CONVECTIVE DIFFUSION BOUNDARY LAYER DEVELOPS"]
    CL --> RXN["ELECTROCHEMICAL REACTION AT ELECTRODE SURFACE"]
    RXN --> MASS["MASS TRANSFER COEFFICIENT K_M FROM LEVEQUE"]
    MASS --> LIMIT["LIMITING CURRENT I_L REACHED WHEN C_SURF EQUALS ZERO"]
    LIMIT --> END["DEPLETED ELECTROLYTE EXITS CHANNEL AT X EQUALS L"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation guide for the diagrams above:**
> - **Diagram 1** (functional architecture): Shows the **flow loop** and the **current path** through the stack. Note the **bipolar** connection — current enters at the *positive* end-plate, flows through every alternating plate, and exits at the *negative* end-plate.
> - **Diagram 2** (component map): Shows the **layer-by-layer sandwich** structure. The "A" prefix on every node label is the alpha-prefix required by the Mermaid node-identifier rule.
> - **Diagram 3** (channel flow): Shows the **spatial evolution** of the boundary layer along the channel length. The Lévêque equation gives the *local* $k_m(x)$, which is then integrated over $L$.
