# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1.  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

> **Differentiate between a monopolar and a bipolar filter press cell with respect to electrical connections, total voltage, total current, and number of electrode plates.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Monopolar | Bipolar |
|---|---|---|
| External electrical connection | Every plate individually wired to the bus-bar | Only the two **end** plates are connected |
| Current path | **Parallel** — current is collected from each plate | **Series** — current passes *through* each plate |
| Total stack voltage | = $V_{cell}$ of one pair | = (n−1) × $V_{cell}$, where n = number of plates |
| Total stack current | = n × $I_{cell}$ | = $I_{cell}$ (same through every pair) |
| Number of cell pairs | (n/2) | (n−1) |
| Typical application | Small electrolyser stacks | Large industrial plants (chlor-alkali) |

*(Full marks require a tabular comparison with all four features plus the concluding line: "Bipolar cells give high voltage/low current → better for power transmission losses, while monopolar cells give low voltage/high current → better when currents are small and voltages are easy to insulate.")*

### Question A2.  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

> **State and explain the Lévêque equation for mass transfer in a parallel-plate electrochemical reactor. State its range of validity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Lévêque equation for the Sherwood number in a parallel-plate (filter press) cell operating under laminar, fully-developed, high-Schmidt-number conditions is:

$$Sh = 1.85 \, (Re \cdot Sc \cdot d_h / L)^{1/3}$$

**Range of validity:**
1. $Re < 2000$ (laminar flow).
2. $Sc \gg 1$ (true for liquid electrolytes, typically $Sc \approx 500$–$5000$).
3. Entrance length effects are absorbed in the derivation — it is valid for **developing** flow.
4. Steady state, no homogeneous reaction, constant physical properties.

*(Add the one-line physical interpretation: "Lévêque's $1/3$-power law reflects the cubic growth of the diffusion boundary layer in the flow direction — the wall flux decreases as $x^{-1/3}$.")*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions of 14 marks **always** carry internal choice. We provide **two completely independent alternatives**, and you are expected to answer **one**. Each is split into two 7-mark sub-parts to match the KTU 2024 answer-booklet layout.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a bipolar filter press cell** as used in the **chlor-alkali industry**. List the materials of construction for anode, cathode, membrane, and gaskets.
>
> **(b) [7 marks)]** A **bipolar filter press cell** operates with **50 cell pairs** at an average **current density of 3000 A/m²**. The active area of each electrode is **1.5 m²**. The thermodynamic emf is **2.19 V**, activation overpotential at each electrode is **0.20 V**, electrolyte conductivity is **20 S/m**, inter-electrode gap is **3 mm**, and the membrane area-specific resistance is **1.5 × 10⁻⁴ Ω·m²**. Contact and bus-bar drops total **0.10 V** per cell pair. Calculate the **(i) total stack voltage, (ii) total current, (iii) total power input, and (iv) specific energy consumption (kWh/kg of Cl₂ produced)**, given that the current efficiency for Cl₂ is **96 %** and the equivalent weight of Cl₂ is **35.45 g/equiv**.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Construction & Working

> *[Provide a 1-page labelled diagram description covering: end plates, tie rods, alternating anode (Ti/RuO₂-coated) and cathode (Ni or steel) plates, Nafion™ membrane, EPDM gaskets, inlet/outlet manifolds, gas–liquid separators. Explain that 2NaCl + 2H₂O → Cl₂ + H₂ + 2NaOH, and that brine flows through the anolyte chamber, water through the catholyte chamber, Na⁺ migrates selectively through the membrane from anolyte to catholyte.]*

#### Part (b) — Numerical

**Given:**
- $N = 50$ cell pairs
- $i = 3000 \,\text{A/m}^2$
- $A_{plate} = 1.5 \,\text{m}^2$
- $E_{eq} = 2.19 \,\text{V}$
- $\eta_{a,act} = \eta_{c,act} = 0.20 \,\text{V}$ each
- $\kappa = 20 \,\text{S/m}$
- $b = 3 \,\text{mm} = 0.003 \,\text{m}$
- $R_m = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \,\Omega\cdot\text{m}^2$
- $V_{contact} = 0.10 \,\text{V}$ per cell pair
- $\eta_c = 96\%$
- $EW_{Cl_2} = 35.45 \,\text{g/eq}$

**Step 1 — Ohmic drops:**

$$\eta_{ohm,electrolyte} = \frac{i \cdot b}{\kappa} = \frac{3000 \times 0.003}{20} = 0.45 \;\text{V}$$

$$\eta_{ohm,membrane} = i \cdot R_m = 3000 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.45 \;\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage per cell pair:**

$$V_{cell} = 2.19 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.45 + 0.45 + 0.10 = 3.59 \;\text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Total stack voltage:**

$$V_{stack} = N \times V_{cell} = 50 \times 3.59 = 179.5 \;\text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Total current (bipolar ⇒ same through every pair):**

$$I = i \times A_{plate} = 3000 \times 1.5 = 4500 \;\text{A}$$

**Step 5 — Total power input:**

$$P = V_{stack} \times I = 179.5 \times 4500 = 807{,}750 \;\text{W} \approx 807.75 \;\text{kW}$$

**Step 6 — Mass of Cl₂ produced per hour:**

$$m = \frac{I \times t \times EW \times \eta_c}{1000} = \frac{4500 \times 1 \times 35.45 \times 0.96}{1000} = 153.14 \;\text{kg/h}$$

**Step 7 — Specific energy consumption:**

$$W = \frac{P}{m} = \frac{807.75}{153.14} = 5.27 \;\text{kWh/kg Cl}_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers (A-b):** $V_{stack} = 179.5 \,\text{V}$; $I = 4500 \,\text{A}$; $P = 807.75 \,\text{kW}$; $W = 5.27 \,\text{kWh/kg Cl}_2$.
>
> **Sanity check:** Real-world membrane chlor-alkali cells consume **2.3–2.8 kWh/kg Cl₂** at current densities of 4–5 kA/m². The higher value (5.27) obtained here is consistent with the lower current density and the higher assumed contact drop.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **Lévêque equation** for mass transfer in a laminar flow filter press cell, starting from the **convective diffusion equation** in the mass-transfer boundary layer. Clearly state the **boundary conditions** and the **range of validity** of the final expression.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A filter press cell is used for **electrochemical destruction of phenol in wastewater**. The cell has **20 cell pairs**, each with an inter-electrode gap of **2 mm**, electrode width 0.5 m, and electrode length 1.0 m. The mean electrolyte velocity is **0.05 m/s**, the kinematic viscosity is **1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**, and the diffusivity of phenoxide ion is **5.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ m²/s**. Bulk concentration of phenol is **5 mol/m³**. Calculate the **(i) Reynolds number, (ii) Sherwood number, (iii) mass-transfer coefficient $k_m$, and (iv) limiting current density** if the reaction is a **2-electron transfer** ($z = 2$).

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation

> *[Reproduce the full derivation from Section 3.1: convective diffusion equation → linearisation of velocity near wall → self-similar variable $\eta = y / (9 D b x / (4 v))^{1/3}$ → wall-flux integration → final $Sh = 1.85(Re \cdot Sc \cdot d_h/L)^{1/3}$.]*

#### Part (b) — Numerical

**Given:**
- $N = 20$ (not needed for mass-transfer calcs but useful for context)
- $b = 2 \,\text{mm} = 0.002 \,\text{m}$
- $w = 0.5 \,\text{m}$, $L = 1.0 \,\text{m}$
- $\bar{v} = 0.05 \,\text{m/s}$
- $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}^2\text{/s}$
- $D = 5.0 \times 10^{-10} \,\text{m}^2\text{/s}$
- $C_{bulk} = 5 \,\text{mol/m}^3$
- $z = 2$, $F = 96485 \,\text{C/mol}$

**Step 1 — Hydraulic diameter:**

For a wide rectangular channel ($w \gg b$): $d_h = 2b = 2 \times 0.002 = 0.004 \,\text{m}$

**Step 2 — Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\bar{v} \cdot d_h}{\nu} = \frac{0.05 \times 0.004}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 200$$

> $Re = 200 < 2000$ ⇒ **Laminar** — Lévêque is valid. ✓

**Step 3 — Schmidt number:**

$$Sc = \frac{\nu}{D} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-6}}{5.0 \times 10^{-10}} = 2000$$

> $Sc = 2000 \gg 1$ ⇒ Lévêque's "high Schmidt" approximation is justified. ✓

**Step 4 — Sherwood number (Lévêque):**

$$Sh = 1.85 \, \left( Re \cdot Sc \cdot \frac{d_h}{L} \right)^{1/3} = 1.85 \times (200 \times 2000 \times 0.004/1.0)^{1/3}$$

$$Sh = 1.85 \times (1600)^{1/3} = 1.85 \times 11.696 = 21.64$$

**Step 5 — Mass-transfer coefficient:**

$$k_m = \frac{Sh \cdot D}{d_h} = \frac{21.64 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-10}}{0.004} = 2.705 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Limiting current density:**

$$i_L = z F k_m C_{bulk} = 2 \times 96485 \times 2.705 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 = 2.61 \;\text{A/m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers (B-b):** $Re = 200$; $Sh = 21.64$; $k_m = 2.71 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m/s}$; $i_L = 2.61 \,\text{A/m}^2$.
>
> **Sanity check:** A limiting current of 2.6 A/m² is small (typical electro-winning runs at 200–500 A/m²). To get a higher throughput one would need to either (i) increase the flow velocity — but with a $1/3$-power law, even a 10× velocity bump only triples $i_L$ — or (ii) increase $C_{bulk}$ (concentration), (iii) raise $D$ (temperature), or (iv) add turbulence promoters / static mixers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways KTU examiners dock marks on Filter Press Cell questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the bipolar contact drop / bus-bar drop.** It is NOT zero. A 0.05–0.15 V per cell pair must be added.
> 2. **Mixing $d_h$ definitions.** For a rectangular channel, $d_h = 2b$ only when $w \gg b$. If $w$ is comparable to $b$, use the full formula $d_h = 4 b w / (2(b+w))$.
> 3. **Using the wrong mass-transfer correlation.** Lévêque is for **laminar** flow only. If the problem gives $Re > 2000$, switch to $Sh = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Sc^{1/3}$.
> 4. **Sign convention for overpotentials.** $\eta_a$ and $\eta_c$ are added (as positive numbers) to $E_{eq}$ — they are losses, not gains. Some students subtract them by mistake.
> 5. **Specific energy consumption units.** The answer MUST be in **kWh per kg of product**. A common slip is leaving it in J/kg or kJ/kg and losing 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — print this page the night before the exam.**

- ✅ **Filter press cell = plate-and-frame, sandwich-type, modular electrochemical reactor.** Used wherever high $a_e$ and good mass transfer are needed.
- ✅ **Two configurations:** **Monopolar** (parallel, low-V/high-I) and **Bipolar** (series, high-V/low-I). Bipolar is the industrial default for large plants.
- ✅ **Components:** End plates, tie rods, electrodes, gaskets, diaphragms/membranes, manifolds, bus-bars.
- ✅ **Cell voltage equation** — memorise: $V_{cell} = E_{eq} + \eta_{a} + \eta_{c} + \eta_{ohm,el} + \eta_{ohm,m} + V_{contact}$.
- ✅ **$\eta_{ohm,el} = i \cdot b / \kappa$** — small $b$ is the *single most effective* way to cut ohmic loss.
- ✅ **Lévêque equation:** $Sh = 1.85 (Re \cdot Sc \cdot d_h / L)^{1/3}$ — valid for **laminar**, **high-Sc**, **fully-developed or developing** flow in a wide rectangular channel.
- ✅ **Limiting current density:** $i_L = z F k_m C_{bulk}$ — the *ceiling* on the operating current.
- ✅ **Pressure drop (laminar):** $\Delta P = 12 \mu L v / b^2$ — for $w \gg b$.
- ✅ **Specific electrode area** of a filter press cell: **100–500 m²/m³** — highest among conventional reactor geometries.
- ✅ **Hydraulic diameter for $w \gg b$:** $d_h \approx 2b$.
- ✅ **Industrial uses:** Chlor-alkali, water electrolysis, electro-organic synthesis, redox-flow batteries, electro-Fenton wastewater treatment.
- ✅ **Scale-up rule:** Add cell pairs to the stack. Capital cost scales linearly; specific energy consumption does not change.
- ✅ **Why $1/3$ power?** The cubic growth of the diffusion boundary layer in the flow direction is the deep mathematical reason — the wall flux decays as $x^{-1/3}$.
- ✅ **Common KTU numerical ask:** "Calculate stack voltage, total current, power input, and specific energy consumption for a chlor-alkali filter press cell of $N$ pairs." Practise at least three such numericals before the exam.
- ✅ **Common KTU theory ask:** "Compare monopolar and bipolar" / "Derive Lévêque equation" / "Discuss current distribution in filter press cells."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swiss Roll Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT417 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swiss Roll Cell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Swiss Roll Cell** is a compact, high-surface-area **electrochemical reactor** architecture in which two long, flat strips of porous electrode material (anode and cathode) are physically separated by an **ion-exchange membrane** (or a porous separator) and tightly wound in a **spiral (helical) configuration** — much like the rolled pastry of a Swiss roll cake. The geometry maximizes the active interfacial area per unit reactor volume, dramatically reducing cell footprint while preserving efficient mass transport, ionic conduction, and gas evolution kinetics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECHT417 – Module 4):**
> The Swiss Roll Cell is studied under the advanced electrochemical reactor design category, alongside plate-and-frame cells, filter-press cells, and zero-gap cells. It is valued in KTU assessments for its **engineering advantages** (compactness, high current density, low ohmic drop) over conventional H-cell or tank-cell designs.

The fundamental governing equation for any electrochemical cell, applicable here, is:

$$E_{cell} = E_{cell}^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q - \eta_{anode} - \vert\eta_{cathode}\vert - iR_{cell}$$

where the term $iR_{cell}$ (the **ohmic drop**) is the parameter most strongly impacted by the spiral geometry.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have **two long, thin sheets of aluminum foil** (representing anode and cathode) and a piece of **cling film** (representing the separator/membrane) of the same length. If you lay them flat on a table, they occupy a large area but contain very little electrolyte volume. Now, take all three sheets and **roll them tightly together like a cinnamon roll pastry**. You will get a compact, dense cylinder that is only a few centimeters long, yet when you unroll it, the total electrode area could be **several square meters**.

This is exactly what a Swiss Roll Cell does at the laboratory/process scale. The key engineering benefits emerge because:

- The **current path** is short and uniform across the spiral.
- The **ionic path** through the membrane is thin and consistent.
- The **gas bubbles** generated at the electrodes have a short distance to escape the spiral stack.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of a Swiss Roll Cell as a **"rolled-up fuel cell"** — the same physics as a proton-exchange-membrane (PEM) fuel cell, but compressed into a spiral, allowing a high **power-to-volume** (W/cm³) ratio. This makes it a favorite for portable hydrogen-generation systems, lab-scale electrolyzers, and flow-electrode reactors.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are routinely used in Swiss Roll Cell analysis:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value | Units |
|--------|----------|----------------|-------|
| $F$ | Faraday constant | **96,485** | C mol⁻¹ |
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | **8.314** | J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ |
| $T$ | Standard temperature | **298.15** | K |
| $j$ | Current density | **100 – 10,000** | A m⁻² |
| $A_{sp}$ | Specific area | **> 5,000** | m²/m³ |
| $V_{cell}$ | Typical operating voltage | **1.8 – 2.5** | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **specific area** ($A_{sp}$) is the defining metric of a Swiss Roll Cell. A well-designed spiral can achieve $A_{sp} > 5{,}000 \ \text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$, an order of magnitude higher than a conventional plate-and-frame cell ($\approx 100\text{–}500 \ \text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Construction Logic

The Swiss Roll Cell is built in a strict, layered sequence. Each step has a specific engineering rationale.

- **Step 1 – Selection of electrode substrate.**
  Long, thin strips of porous **carbon paper**, **nickel foam**, **titanium mesh**, or **stainless-steel felt** are chosen. *Why?* Porosity allows electrolyte permeation and gas venting; a long, thin form factor enables the spiral winding.

- **Step 2 – Application of catalyst layer.**
  A thin catalytic coating (e.g., **Pt/C** for HER, **IrO₂** for OER, or non-precious Ni/Fe catalysts) is deposited on the electrode strip. *Why?* Catalysts lower the activation overpotential ($\eta_{act}$), enabling operation at higher current densities.

- **Step 3 – Membrane/separator placement.**
  A **proton-exchange membrane (Nafion™)**, **anion-exchange membrane (AEM)**, or **porous polymer separator (Celgard™)** is sandwiched between the two electrode strips. *Why?* This enforces ionic conduction (cations/anions travel selectively) while physically separating the gaseous products of the anode and cathode half-reactions.

- **Step 4 – Spiral winding.**
  The three-layer sandwich is wound tightly around a central **mandrel** (a cylindrical rod) to form a compact spiral. The number of turns ($N$), the strip width ($w$), and the membrane thickness ($t_m$) collectively determine the active area.

- **Step 5 – End-plate assembly & current collection.**
  The exposed ends of the anode and cathode strips are connected to **current collectors** (copper or titanium busbars), and the cell is sealed inside a **housing** with inlet/outlet ports for electrolyte circulation and gas venting.

## 2.2 Why the Spiral Geometry Works — The Underlying Physics

Three fundamental electrochemical-transport phenomena benefit from the spiral layout:

- **Shortened ionic path → reduced ohmic drop.**
  The membrane thickness in a Swiss Roll Cell is typically **50 – 250 µm**, which means the ionic path length is fixed and small. Since the **ohmic drop** is $\Delta V_{ohm} = i \cdot R_{cell}$, and $R_{cell} \propto t_m / \kappa$ (where $\kappa$ is ionic conductivity), this architecture directly minimizes $iR$-losses.

- **Uniform current distribution.**
  Because the anode and cathode are parallel and continuous along the entire spiral length, the current density distribution is more **uniform** than in a rectangular plate cell, where edge effects dominate.

- **Enhanced mass transport.**
  The spiral channels act as thin, high-aspect-ratio flow paths. **Forced convective flow** (in pumped-electrolyte designs) or **natural convection** (in stagnant designs) continually refreshes the boundary layer at the electrode-membrane interface.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** that KTU examiners expect students to be fluent with for the Swiss Roll Cell topic.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $A_{active} = 2 \cdot w \cdot L \cdot N$ | Active electrode area (factor 2 for both sides) | m² |
| 2 | $V_{reactor} = \pi r_{out}^{2} \cdot h - \pi r_{in}^{2} \cdot h$ | Reactor volume (hollow cylinder) | m³ |
| 3 | $A_{sp} = A_{active} / V_{reactor}$ | Specific area | m² m⁻³ |
| 4 | $R_{cell} = \dfrac{t_m}{\kappa \cdot A_{active}}$ | Ohmic resistance of the membrane | Ω |
| 5 | $\Delta V_{ohm} = i \cdot R_{cell}$ | Ohmic voltage drop | V |
| 6 | $\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | Activation overpotential (Butler–Volmer low-$\eta$ form) | V |
| 7 | $\eta_{conc} = \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(1 - \dfrac{i}{i_L}\right)$ | Concentration overpotential (mass-transport limit) | V |
| 8 | $E_{cell} = E_{cell}^{\circ} + \eta_{a} + \vert\eta_{c}\vert + iR_{cell}$ | Full operating cell voltage (thermodynamic + kinetic + ohmic) | V |
| 9 | $I_{total} = j \cdot A_{active}$ | Total current from current density | A |
| 10 | $\dot{n} = I_{total} / (nF)$ | Molar production rate (Faraday's law) | mol s⁻¹ |
| 11 | $r_{in} = r_{core} + N \cdot (t_a + t_c + t_m)$ | Inner radius after N turns | m |
| 12 | $\epsilon_{porosity} = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{true}}$ | Electrode porosity | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\dot{V}_{H_2} = \dot{n} \cdot V_m$ | Volumetric H₂ production rate | m³ s⁻¹ |
| 14 | $\eta_{cell} = \dfrac{E_{thermo}}{E_{cell}}$ | Voltage (energy) efficiency | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols Glossary:**
> $w$ = strip width, $L$ = strip length, $N$ = number of turns, $r_{out}/r_{in}$ = outer/inner radius, $h$ = cell height, $t_a, t_c, t_m$ = anode/cathode/membrane thickness, $\alpha$ = charge-transfer coefficient, $i_0$ = exchange current density, $i_L$ = limiting current density, $V_m$ = molar volume of gas at STP.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Swiss Roll Cells are used across several high-impact engineering applications:

- **Lab-scale hydrogen production** – Compact electrolysis demonstrator units.
- **Redox-flow battery research** – High surface area for redox couples.
- **Electrochemical CO₂ reduction** – Maximizes catalyst exposure per unit reactor volume.
- **Wastewater treatment** – Electro-Fenton, electrocoagulation in compact reactors.
- **Portable power** – Micro-fuel cells and micro-electrolyzers.
- **Sensor platforms** – Spiral electrodes offer enhanced sensitivity per unit footprint.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Active Electrode Area of a Swiss Roll Cell

**Statement.** A Swiss Roll Cell is wound by tightly rolling two electrode strips (anode + cathode), each of width $w$ and length $L$, together with a separator of thickness $t_m$. Each full turn contributes electrode surface on **both faces** of each strip. Show that the active area is:

$$A_{active} = 2 \cdot w \cdot L \quad \text{(per full strip) and } A_{active,total} = 4 \cdot w \cdot L \text{ (cell)}$$

> **Note:** This derivation treats a single, uncut strip wound as a continuous spiral. The total exposed catalytic surface of *one* electrode strip is $2 w L$ (two faces, length $L$, width $w$). Both anode and cathode together give $4 w L$.

### Step-by-step:

**Step 1.** Consider a single electrode strip lying flat before winding. Its geometric area is:

$$A_{flat} = w \cdot L$$

**Step 2.** The strip is **double-sided** (catalyst is coated on both faces), so the active area *per strip* becomes:

$$A_{one\_strip} = 2 \cdot A_{flat} = 2 w L$$

**Step 3.** Since the cell contains **two** strips (anode and cathode), the total reactive surface is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{active,total} &= 2 \cdot A_{one\_strip} \\
&= 2 \cdot (2 w L) \\
&= 4 w L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.** If the strip is wound in **$N$ turns**, the *length per turn* is not the same; the effective area scales as $\sum_{k=1}^{N} 2 w \cdot 2\pi r_k$, where $r_k$ is the radius of the $k^{th}$ turn. For a thin-cell approximation (low $N$), the previous formula is used. The full integral form is:

$$
A_{active,total} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} 4 w \cdot 2\pi r_k = 8\pi w \sum_{k=1}^{N} r_k
$$

But for KTU exam purposes, the **single-strip equivalent** expression $A_{active} = 2 w L$ is the standard form.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Surface Area $A_{sp}$

**Statement.** Derive an expression for the specific area (active area per unit reactor volume) of a Swiss Roll Cell with inner radius $r_{in}$, outer radius $r_{out}$, and height $h$ (length of the mandrel).

### Step-by-step:

**Step 1.** The reactor volume (the volume occupied by the rolled spiral, treated as an annular cylinder) is:

$$V_{reactor} = \pi (r_{out}^{2} - r_{in}^{2}) \cdot h$$

**Step 2.** The active area is approximated as:

$$A_{active} = 2 w L$$

**Step 3.** The specific area is the ratio:

$$
A_{sp} = \dfrac{A_{active}}{V_{reactor}} = \dfrac{2 w L}{\pi (r_{out}^{2} - r_{in}^{2}) \cdot h}
$$

**Step 4.** Using the relation $L = \pi (r_{out}^{2} - r_{in}^{2}) / t_{total}$ (where $t_{total} = t_a + t_c + t_m$ is the thickness of one turn), we get:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{sp} &= \dfrac{2 w \cdot \pi (r_{out}^{2} - r_{in}^{2}) / t_{total}}{\pi (r_{out}^{2} - r_{in}^{2}) \cdot h} \\
&= \dfrac{2w}{h \cdot t_{total}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This elegant result shows that **$A_{sp}$ is independent of the spiral radius** and depends only on the strip width $w$, the cell height $h$, and the layer thickness $t_{total}$. Decreasing the layer thickness and the height (making a flatter, denser spiral) increases $A_{sp}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Cell Voltage (Polarization Curve)

**Statement.** Show that the operating cell voltage of a Swiss Roll Cell is the sum of the thermodynamic potential, activation overpotentials at both electrodes, concentration overpotentials, and the ohmic drop.

### Step-by-step:

**Step 1.** Begin with the **Nernst equation** for the open-circuit (thermodynamic) voltage:

$$E_{cell}^{\circ} = E_{cathode}^{\circ} - E_{anode}^{\circ}$$

**Step 2.** Add the **activation overpotential** at the anode (oxidation kinetics):

$$V = E_{cell}^{\circ} + \eta_{act,a}$$

**Step 3.** Add the **activation overpotential** at the cathode (reduction kinetics). The sign is positive for the magnitude:

$$
V = E_{cell}^{\circ} + \eta_{act,a} + \vert\eta_{act,c}\vert
$$

**Step 4.** Add the **concentration overpotentials** on both sides:

$$
V = E_{cell}^{\circ} + \eta_{act,a} + \vert\eta_{act,c}\vert + \eta_{conc,a} + \vert\eta_{conc,c}\vert
$$

**Step 5.** Add the **ohmic drop** using Ohm's law applied to the cell resistance $R_{cell}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= E_{cell}^{\circ} + \eta_{act,a} + \vert\eta_{act,c}\vert + \eta_{conc,a} + \vert\eta_{conc,c}\vert + i \cdot R_{cell} \\
&= E_{cell}^{\circ} + \sum \eta + i R_{cell}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6.** Substitute the explicit form of the activation overpotential from the **Butler–Volmer equation** (low-overpotential limit):

$$
\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)
$$

**Step 7.** Substitute the explicit form of the **ohmic resistance** in terms of the membrane:

$$
R_{cell} = \frac{t_m}{\kappa \cdot A_{active}}
$$

**Step 8.** Final compact form:

$$
V = E_{cell}^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{\alpha_a n F}\ln\left(\frac{i}{i_{0,a}}\right) + \left\vert\frac{RT}{\alpha_c n F}\ln\left(\frac{i}{i_{0,c}}\right)\right\vert + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(1 - \frac{i}{i_L}\right) + i \cdot \frac{t_m}{\kappa \cdot A_{active}}
$$

This is the **complete polarization equation** for a Swiss Roll Cell and is the single most important equation for KTU ESE problems.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem.** A Swiss Roll Cell uses two electrode strips of width $w = 5$ cm and length $L = 2$ m, separated by a Nafion-117 membrane of thickness $t_m = 183$ µm. The cell operates at $T = 298$ K with a current density $j = 1{,}000$ A/m². The ionic conductivity of the membrane is $\kappa = 10$ S/m. Compute (a) active area, (b) cell resistance, (c) ohmic drop.

**Solution:**

**(a) Active area** (per strip, both sides):

$$
A_{active} = 2 w L = 2 \times (0.05 \text{ m}) \times (2 \text{ m}) = 0.20 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**(b) Cell resistance:**

$$
R_{cell} = \frac{t_m}{\kappa \cdot A_{active}} = \frac{183 \times 10^{-6}}{10 \times 0.20} = \frac{1.83 \times 10^{-4}}{2.0} = 9.15 \times 10^{-5} \ \Omega
$$

**(c) Ohmic drop:**

$$
\Delta V_{ohm} = I \cdot R_{cell} = (j \cdot A_{active}) \cdot R_{cell} = (1{,}000 \times 0.20) \times 9.15 \times 10^{-5} = 0.0183 \ \text{V} = 18.3 \ \text{mV}
$$

This tiny ohmic drop is a **direct demonstration** of the Swiss Roll Cell's superiority in minimizing $iR$ losses.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow (Spiral Layered Assembly)

The following Mermaid block renders the **layered functional architecture** of a Swiss Roll Cell — from raw materials to wound spiral assembly. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed to satisfy the KTU compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Select porous electrode substrate - Carbon paper or Ni foam]
    A2[Step 2: Coat both faces with catalyst - Pt/C or IrO2 layer]
    A3[Step 3: Cut strip to width w and length L]
    A4[Step 4: Position ion exchange membrane between anode and cathode]
    A5[Step 5: Insert three layer sandwich around central mandrel]
    A6[Step 6: Wind spiral with N turns under constant tension]
    A7[Step 7: Connect current collectors at exposed strip ends]
    A8[Step 8: Seal cell housing with electrolyte inlet and gas outlet]
    A9[Step 9: Final Swiss Roll Cell ready for operation]
    B1[Input: DC power supply or load]
    B2[Output: Gaseous products and ionic current]
    C1[Anode strip - oxidation reaction site]
    C2[Cathode strip - reduction reaction site]
    C3[Membrane - selective ion conductor]
    C4[Mandrel - central mechanical support]
    B1 --> A9
    A9 --> B2
    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8 --> A9
    A4 --> C1
    A4 --> C2
    A4 --> C3
    A5 --> C4
```

## 4.2 Charge and Mass Transport Pathway Flow

This second diagram illustrates the **pathways of charge, ions, and gases** through the cell — a common visual expected in KTU Module-4 answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[External DC Source] -->|Electron e- in| Q1[Anode Current Collector]
    Q1 -->|Oxidation reaction| Q2[Anode Catalyst Surface]
    Q2 -->|Cation H+ moves through| Q3[Ion Exchange Membrane]
    Q3 -->|Cation arrives at| Q4[Cathode Catalyst Surface]
    Q4 -->|Reduction reaction| Q5[Cathode Current Collector]
    Q5 -->|Electron e- out| P1
    Q2 -->|Gas O2 vented| R1[Anode Gas Outlet]
    Q4 -->|Gas H2 vented| R2[Cathode Gas Outlet]
    Q3 -.Spiral path.-> Q3
    P1 --> S1[External Circuit]
    S1 --> P1
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic of the Spiral Stack (Block-Level View)

A physical cross-section of the wound spiral is best represented as a **block-level functional map** of the repeating layered unit. The following block diagram shows one "turn" of the spiral, indicating the order of layers encountered from the mandrel outward.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Central Mandrel - Al or SS rod] --> L2[Anode Substrate - Porous Carbon]
    L2 --> L3[Anode Catalyst Layer - Pt or IrO2]
    L3 --> L4[Membrane - Nafion 117]
    L4 --> L5[Cathode Catalyst Layer - Pt]
    L5 --> L6[Cathode Substrate - Porous Carbon]
    L6 --> L7[Separator or Gasket - Optional]
    L7 --> L8[Next Anode Layer - Spiral continues]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** From the center outward, each "turn" of the spiral contains **mandrel → anode → membrane → cathode**, and then the spiral winds back to begin the next anode layer. The two electrodes are never in direct contact — they are always separated by the membrane. This is the geometric essence of the Swiss Roll Cell.

## 4.4 Engineering Advantages vs. Disadvantages — Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ADV[Swiss Roll Cell Engineering Trade-offs]
    ADV --> ADV1[ADVANTAGE 1: Specific area exceeds 5000 m2 per m3]
    ADV --> ADV2[ADVANTAGE 2: Ohmic drop below 50 mV at 1 kA per m2]
    ADV --> ADV3[ADVANTAGE 3: Compact and portable geometry]
    ADV --> ADV4[ADVANTAGE 4: Uniform current distribution]
    ADV --> DIS1[DISADVANTAGE 1: Membrane stress from tight winding]
    ADV --> DIS2[DISADVANTAGE 2: Difficult scale-up beyond 50 cm2 active area]
    ADV --> DIS3[DISADVANTAGE 3: Gas bubble trapping in inner turns]
    ADV --> DIS4[DISADVANTAGE 4: Non-uniform electrolyte access to inner core]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]
**Q: Define a Swiss Roll Cell and list any three of its engineering advantages over a conventional plate-and-frame cell.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Swiss Roll Cell** is an electrochemical reactor in which two long, flat electrode strips (anode and cathode) separated by an ion-exchange membrane are wound together in a spiral configuration, providing a high active surface area per unit volume.

The three principal engineering advantages are:
1. **Very high specific area** ($A_{sp} > 5{,}000$ m²/m³), an order of magnitude greater than a plate-and-frame cell ($\approx 100$–$500$ m²/m³).
2. **Low ohmic drop** due to the thin, uniform membrane path (typically $iR < 50$ mV at $1 \text{ kA/m}^2$).
3. **Compact, portable geometry** allowing laboratory and field deployment where volume is a constraint.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Suggested]
**Q: Write the Butler–Volmer low-overpotential form of the activation overpotential and explain why activation losses are still a significant contribution in a Swiss Roll Cell even though it minimizes ohmic losses.**

**Model Answer:**
The Butler–Volmer equation in the low-overpotential (linearized) limit is:

$$\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$

**Why activation losses still matter:** The Swiss Roll Cell geometry minimizes the *ohmic* ($iR$) and *concentration* ($\eta_{conc}$) losses by virtue of its short ionic path and high surface area. However, the activation overpotential is governed by the **intrinsic kinetics** of the catalyst-electrolyte interface, not by the cell geometry. Therefore, $\eta_{act}$ depends only on the choice of catalyst (i.e., the exchange current density $i_0$), and it remains a dominant voltage loss term in the polarization curve, particularly at low current densities.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1] — 14 Marks

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the **constructional features** of a Swiss Roll Cell. List any **four engineering advantages** of this design over a conventional H-cell or plate-and-frame cell. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **active electrode area** and the **specific surface area** of a Swiss Roll Cell in terms of the strip width $w$, strip length $L$, cell height $h$, and total layer thickness $t_{total} = t_a + t_c + t_m$. Show that the specific area is independent of the spiral radius. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a):

A **Swiss Roll Cell** consists of the following constructional features (refer to the Mermaid schematic in Section 4.1):

- **Anode strip:** A long, thin strip of porous **carbon paper** or **nickel foam** coated on both faces with an oxidation catalyst (e.g., IrO₂ for OER).
- **Cathode strip:** A similar strip coated with a reduction catalyst (e.g., Pt/C for HER).
- **Ion-exchange membrane:** A **Nafion-117** (proton-exchange) or **AEM** (anion-exchange) sheet that physically separates the electrodes and conducts ions selectively.
- **Central mandrel:** A cylindrical rod (aluminum or stainless steel) that serves as the winding core.
- **Current collectors:** Copper or titanium busbars connected to the exposed ends of the anode and cathode strips.
- **Cell housing:** A sealed container with ports for **electrolyte inlet**, **electrolyte outlet**, and **gas venting**.

**Four Engineering Advantages:**

1. **High specific surface area** ($A_{sp} > 5{,}000$ m²/m³), far exceeding conventional cells.
2. **Reduced ohmic drop** because the membrane thickness is fixed and the active area is large.
3. **Compact, portable geometry** suitable for lab and field use.
4. **Uniform current distribution** due to the continuous parallel geometry of the spiral.

---

#### Model Solution (b):

**Active area per strip (both sides):**

$$A_{active} = 2 w L$$

**Reactor volume (annular cylinder):**

$$V_{reactor} = \pi (r_{out}^{2} - r_{in}^{2}) h$$

**Specific area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{sp} &= \frac{A_{active}}{V_{reactor}} = \frac{2 w L}{\pi (r_{out}^{2} - r_{in}^{2}) h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substitute** $L = \dfrac{\pi (r_{out}^{2} - r_{in}^{2})}{t_{total}}$ (the strip length equals the unfolded spiral length, which equals the annular area divided by layer thickness):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{sp} &= \frac{2 w \cdot \pi (r_{out}^{2} - r_{in}^{2}) / t_{total}}{\pi (r_{out}^{2} - r_{in}^{2}) h} \\
&= \frac{2 w}{h \cdot t_{total}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $A_{sp}$ depends only on the strip width $w$, the height $h$, and the layer thickness $t_{total}$ — **not** on the spiral radius $(r_{out}, r_{in})$. Hence the specific area is geometrically decoupled from the spiral dimensions, which is a key design insight of the Swiss Roll Cell. **(7 Marks — full derivation shown)**

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 3, Internal Choice] — 14 Marks

**(a)** A Swiss Roll Cell uses two electrode strips of width $w = 4$ cm and length $L = 1.5$ m, with a separator of total thickness $t_{total} = 600$ µm. Calculate the **active electrode area**, the **reactor volume** (take $r_{in} = 0.5$ cm, $r_{out} = 2.5$ cm, $h = 4$ cm), and the **specific surface area**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **four major voltage losses** in a Swiss Roll Cell polarization curve and discuss, with reasons, which loss is **most effectively reduced** by the spiral geometry. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a):

**Active area:**

$$A_{active} = 2 w L = 2 \times (0.04 \text{ m}) \times (1.5 \text{ m}) = 0.12 \ \text{m}^2$$

**Reactor volume:**

$$
V_{reactor} = \pi (r_{out}^{2} - r_{in}^{2}) h = \pi \times ((0.025)^{2} - (0.005)^{2}) \times 0.04
$$

$$
= \pi \times (6.25 \times 10^{-4} - 2.5 \times 10^{-5}) \times 0.04
$$

$$
= \pi \times 6.00 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.04 = 7.54 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^3
$$

**Specific area:**

$$
A_{sp} = \frac{A_{active}}{V_{reactor}} = \frac{0.12}{7.54 \times 10^{-5}} = 1{,}592 \ \text{m}^2/\text{m}^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The specific area of $\sim 1{,}600 \ \text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$ is much higher than a plate-and-frame cell ($\sim 200 \ \text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$) and is a clear numerical demonstration of the Swiss Roll Cell's compactness. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (b):

The **four major voltage losses** in the polarization curve of a Swiss Roll Cell are:

1. **Activation overpotential ($\eta_{act}$):** Arises from sluggish charge-transfer kinetics at the electrode-electrolyte interface. Given by the **Butler–Volmer equation** in its high-overpotential (Tafel) form: $\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln \dfrac{i}{i_0}$.

2. **Concentration overpotential ($\eta_{conc}$):** Arises when the rate of mass transport cannot keep up with the current demand, particularly near the **limiting current density** $i_L$. Given by $\eta_{conc} = \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left(1 - \dfrac{i}{i_L}\right)$.

3. **Ohmic drop ($iR_{cell}$):** Voltage loss due to the ionic resistance of the electrolyte/membrane. Given by $iR_{cell} = i \cdot \dfrac{t_m}{\kappa A_{active}}$.

4. **Membrane/crossover losses:** Small parasitic losses due to gas crossover (e.g., H₂ and O₂ mixing through the membrane), and short-circuit currents.

**Which loss is most effectively reduced by the spiral geometry?**

The **ohmic drop** is the loss most effectively reduced by the Swiss Roll geometry. Three reasons:
- The membrane thickness is small and uniform.
- The active area is maximized (large $A_{active}$ in the denominator of $R_{cell} = t_m / (\kappa A_{active})$).
- The current path is short and parallel.

Hence, $iR$ losses in a well-designed Swiss Roll Cell can be **below 50 mV at $1 \text{ kA/m}^2$**, far lower than in a comparable H-cell. The activation and concentration overpotentials are *not* directly addressed by the spiral geometry — they require **better catalysts** and **forced convection**, respectively. **(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes — Where You Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing active area formulas:** Many students write $A_{active} = wL$ instead of $A_{active} = 2wL$. The factor of **2** accounts for the *two faces* of the strip. Forgetting this loses **2 marks** instantly in any Part-B numerical.
> 2. **Units of thickness:** The membrane thickness $t_m$ is typically in **micrometres (µm)**, and forgetting to convert to metres ($183 \ \mu\text{m} = 183 \times 10^{-6}$ m) is the single most common arithmetic error.
> 3. **Mixing up which overpotential is geometrically controlled:** Students often claim that the Swiss Roll geometry reduces *all* overpotentials. The truth is: it reduces **ohmic** loss, not **activation** or **concentration** loss. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 4. **Reactor volume vs. active area:** The reactor volume is the *annular cylinder* volume, **not** the full cylinder volume. Forgetting the $(r_{out}^{2} - r_{in}^{2})$ term is a frequent error.
> 5. **Definition precision:** Saying "it is a rolled-up cell" is not enough. A full-credit answer must mention *two electrode strips + separator + spiral winding* in the definition.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following high-density bullet checklist serves as your **last-minute revision** before the KTU exam:

- The **Swiss Roll Cell** is a spiral-wound electrochemical reactor with two electrode strips + a membrane wound around a central mandrel.
- The defining metric is the **specific area** $A_{sp} = A_{active} / V_{reactor}$, which can exceed **5,000 m²/m³** in well-designed cells.
- The **active area** (per strip, both faces) is $A_{active} = 2wL$.
- The **specific area** simplifies to $A_{sp} = 2w / (h \cdot t_{total})$ — *independent of spiral radius*.
- The **reactor volume** is the annular cylinder: $V_{reactor} = \pi (r_{out}^{2} - r_{in}^{2}) h$.
- The **cell resistance** is $R_{cell} = t_m / (\kappa A_{active})$.
- The **ohmic drop** is $\Delta V_{ohm} = i R_{cell}$, which is the loss most reduced by the spiral geometry.
- The **full operating voltage** is $V = E_{cell}^{\circ} + \eta_{a} + \vert\eta_{c}\vert + \eta_{conc} + iR_{cell}$.
- The **activation overpotential** (Butler–Volmer, low-$\eta$): $\eta_{act} = (RT/\alpha nF) \ln(i/i_0)$.
- The **concentration overpotential** (mass-transport): $\eta_{conc} = (RT/nF) \ln(1 - i/i_L)$.
- **Faraday's law:** $\dot{n} = I / (nF)$; volumetric rate $\dot{V} = \dot{n} V_m$.
- **Key advantages:** high $A_{sp}$, low $iR$ drop, compactness, uniform current distribution.
- **Key disadvantages:** membrane stress, scale-up difficulty, gas trapping in inner turns, non-uniform electrolyte access.
- **Applications:** lab H₂ production, CO₂ reduction, redox-flow battery research, wastewater treatment, portable power, micro-sensors.
- **Critical constants:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $T = 298.15$ K (standard).
- **Standard metrics:** $A_{sp} > 5{,}000$ m²/m³, $V_{cell} = 1.8$–$2.5$ V, $iR < 50$ mV at $1$ kA/m² for a well-designed cell.
- **Exam tip:** When asked for the *advantage* of a Swiss Roll Cell over a plate cell, always cite (i) higher $A_{sp}$, (ii) lower ohmic drop, and (iii) greater compactness — in that order of importance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Takeaway for KTU 2024 Exam:** The Swiss Roll Cell is essentially an answer to the engineering question: *"How do we pack a large electrode area into a small volume while keeping ionic resistance low?"* The spiral geometry solves this elegantly. Memorize the **active area formula**, the **specific area formula**, and the **polarization equation** — these three are the most frequently tested items in Module 4 of PECHT417.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plug Flow Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT417 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plug Flow Cell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plug Flow Cell (PFC) / Plug Flow Electrochemical Reactor (PFER):**
> An electrochemical reactor in which the electrolyte is assumed to flow through the cell as discrete "plugs" with **uniform velocity** along the axial direction, exhibiting **zero axial (longitudinal) mixing** and **complete radial mixing**. Each fluid element entering the reactor spends an identical residence time $\tau$ inside the cell, behaving analogously to a chemical Plug Flow Reactor (PFR).

Mathematically, the axial dispersion coefficient is set to $D_{ax} \rightarrow 0$, while the radial Peclet number satisfies $Pe_r \to \infty$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Train of Fluid Plugs"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a metro train 🚆:** Imagine passengers boarding a train that travels straight from Station A (inlet) to Station B (outlet) with **no stops, no U-turns, and no passenger swapping between cars**. Each "plug" of fluid is like a single coach — it moves as a rigid body, with the front plug always ahead, the next right behind, and so on.
>
> - The first plug sees the **freshest** reactant concentration and the **highest** current demand.
> - The last plug sees **depleted** concentration and the **lowest** current demand.
> - No plug overtakes or lags behind another — this is **plug flow**.

**Contrast:** A Back-Mix (CSTR-type) cell is like a public swimming pool 🏊 — the moment new water enters, it instantly mixes with the entire pool, so every drop has the same composition.

## 1.3 Design Philosophy & Industrial Significance

| Feature | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Mixing Regime** | Ideal plug flow (no axial back-mixing) |
| **Flow Direction** | Typically parallel to electrode surface |
| **Residence Time Distribution (RTD)** | Dirac delta function: $E(\tau) = \delta(\tau - \bar{\tau})$ |
| **Conversion Behaviour** | Highest conversion per unit volume vs. CSTR |
| **Key Industrial Use** | Chlor-alkali (membrane cells), electrosynthesis, metal electrowinning |
| **Governing Parameter** | **Space velocity** $S_v$ and **current density** $j$ |

## 1.4 Real-World Applications

> [!TIP]
> - **Chlor-Alkali Industry (NaCl → Cl₂ + H₂ + NaOH):** Membrane electrolyzers approach plug-flow behaviour.
> - **Electrowinning of Zinc, Copper, Nickel:** Multi-compartment cells designed as PFRs.
> - **Electrochemical Wastewater Treatment:** COD removal in flow-through cells.
> - **Electro-organic Synthesis:** Continuous production of fine chemicals.
> - **Fuel Cell Flow Fields:** Serpentine channels mimic PFR behaviour for gas distribution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Inlet Condition:** Electrolyte enters with bulk concentration $C_{in}$ of the electroactive species, volumetric flow rate $Q$ (m³/s), and uniform inlet composition.
2. **No Back-Mixing Assumption:** Each axial slice $dz$ behaves as an independent differential reactor — what enters slice $dz$ is **only** what left slice $dz - dz$, not the average.
3. **Current-Voltage Coupling:** The local current density $j(z)$ depends on the local surface concentration $C_s(z)$ via the Butler-Volmer or mass-transfer-limited relation.
4. **Concentration Gradient Buildup:** As fluid progresses, $C_b(z)$ monotonically decreases (for cathodic deposition or anodic dissolution), and $C_s(z) \to 0$ at limiting current.
5. **Outlet Condition:** Conversion $X_{out}$ is determined by integrating the local rate over the entire reactor length.

## 2.2 Governing Differential Mass Balance

For a single electroactive species undergoing a one-electron transfer at the cathode, the **steady-state plug flow** differential balance is:

$$v_z \frac{dC_b}{dz} = -\frac{j(z)}{nF}$$

Where:
- $v_z$ = superficial axial velocity (m/s)
- $C_b$ = bulk concentration of electroactive species (mol/m³)
- $j(z)$ = local current density (A/m²)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred
- $F$ = **Faraday's constant = 96,485 C/mol (≈ 96,500 C/mol)**

## 2.3 Current Density Distribution

The local current density is determined by the **local mass transfer rate**:

$$j(z) = nF \cdot k_m \cdot C_b(z) \quad \text{(for mass-transfer-limited operation)}$$

Where $k_m$ = mass transfer coefficient (m/s), which for laminar flow in a parallel-plate cell follows the **Leveque approximation**:

$$k_m = 1.467 \cdot D^{2/3} \cdot v_z^{1/3} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{H \cdot L}\right)^{1/3}$$

- $D$ = diffusivity (m²/s)
- $H$ = channel height (m)
- $L$ = electrode length (m)

## 2.4 Conversion Equation (Limiting Current Operation)

Substituting $j = nFk_m C_b$ into the mass balance and integrating from $z = 0$ to $L$:

$$\frac{dC_b}{C_b} = -\frac{k_m}{v_z} dz$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{C_{in}}{C_{out}}\right) = \frac{k_m \cdot L}{v_z} = \frac{k_m \cdot A_e}{Q}$$

$$\boxed{X = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{k_m A_e}{Q}\right) = 1 - e^{-Da_I}}$$

Where $Da_I = \dfrac{k_m A_e}{Q}$ is the **electrochemical Damköhler number**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they are the core of Module 4 numerical problems.**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = v_z \cdot A_c$ | Volumetric flow rate | m³/s |
| 2 | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{L}{v_z}$ | Mean residence time | s |
| 3 | $v_z \dfrac{dC_b}{dz} = -\dfrac{j(z)}{nF}$ | Differential mass balance | mol/(m³·s) |
| 4 | $j_L = nF k_m C_b$ | Limiting current density (mass-transfer limited) | A/m² |
| 5 | $k_m = 1.467\, D^{2/3} v_z^{1/3} (HL)^{-1/3}$ | Leveque mass-transfer coefficient | m/s |
| 6 | $X = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{k_m A_e}{Q}\right)$ | Conversion in plug flow | dimensionless |
| 7 | $I = nFQC_{in}X$ | Total current from conversion | A |
| 8 | $\eta_I = \dfrac{I_{actual}}{I_{theoretical}} = \dfrac{nFQC_{in}X}{I_{applied}}$ | Current efficiency | dimensionless |
| 9 | $S_v = \dfrac{Q}{V}$ | Space velocity | s⁻¹ |
| 10 | $E(\tau) = \delta(\tau - \bar{\tau})$ | RTD for ideal plug flow | s⁻¹ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> The plug flow cell is the **workhorse of continuous industrial electrochemistry** because:
> - It gives **higher conversion per unit volume** than a CSTR for the same residence time (by a factor of $\frac{e^{k\tau}-1}{k\tau}$ for first-order kinetics).
> - It **suppresses unwanted back-reactions** (e.g., anodic re-oxidation of cathodic products).
> - It permits **spatial separation** of inlets/outlets for multi-stream processes.
> - It is the **basis for scaling up** from lab RDE to industrial flow cells.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion in a Plug Flow Electrochemical Cell

### Problem Setup
A parallel-plate electrochemical cell operates under **mass-transfer-limited** conditions. An electrolyte containing a single electroactive species with bulk inlet concentration $C_{in}$ enters at $z = 0$ with velocity $v_z$ and exits at $z = L$. The electrode area is $A_e = W \cdot L$ (width × length). Derive the conversion equation.

### Step 1 — Establish the Differential Mass Balance

For an elementary slice of volume $dV = A_c \, dz$ (where $A_c$ = cross-sectional area of the channel):

$$\text{Rate in} - \text{Rate out} - \text{Rate of reaction by current} = 0$$

At steady state:

$$Q C_b \big\vert_z - Q C_b \big\vert_{z+dz} - \frac{j(z) \, dA_e}{nF} = 0$$

### Step 2 — Convert to Differential Form

Since $dA_e = W \, dz$ for a flat plate:

$$-Q \, dC_b = \frac{j(z) \cdot W}{nF} \, dz$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dC_b}{dz} = -\frac{j(z) \cdot W}{nF \cdot Q}$$

Recognizing $Q = v_z A_c$ and $A_c = W \cdot H$ (where $H$ = inter-electrode gap):

$$\frac{dC_b}{dz} = -\frac{j(z)}{nF \cdot v_z} \cdot \frac{1}{H/H} = -\frac{j(z)}{nF \, v_z}$$

**Note:** The geometric factors cancel for a parallel-plate cell. ✓

### Step 3 — Apply Mass-Transfer-Limited Kinetics

At the limiting current, $j(z) = j_L(z) = nF k_m C_b(z)$:

$$\frac{dC_b}{dz} = -\frac{nF k_m C_b}{nF \, v_z} = -\frac{k_m C_b}{v_z}$$

### Step 4 — Separate Variables and Integrate

$$\frac{dC_b}{C_b} = -\frac{k_m}{v_z} \, dz$$

Integrating from $z = 0$ (where $C_b = C_{in}$) to $z = L$ (where $C_b = C_{out}$):

$$\int_{C_{in}}^{C_{out}} \frac{dC_b}{C_b} = -\frac{k_m}{v_z} \int_{0}^{L} dz$$

$$\ln C_{out} - \ln C_{in} = -\frac{k_m L}{v_z}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for Conversion

$$\ln\left(\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right) = -\frac{k_m L}{v_z}$$

Substituting $\tau = L / v_z$ and $C_{out} = C_{in}(1 - X)$:

$$\ln(1 - X) = -k_m \tau$$

$$\boxed{X = 1 - \exp(-k_m \tau) = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{k_m A_e}{Q}\right)}$$

### Step 6 — Express Total Current in Terms of Conversion

By Faraday's Law:

$$I = nF \cdot Q \cdot C_{in} \cdot X = nF \cdot Q \cdot C_{in} \left[1 - e^{-k_m \tau}\right]$$

This equation is the **cornerstone** of plug flow cell design — relate it to applied voltage, power input, and energy consumption per kg product in the exam.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison: Plug Flow vs. CSTR (Back-Mix)

For identical residence time $\tau$ and first-order (or mass-transfer limited) kinetics, define $\beta = k_m \tau = k_m A_e / Q$:

| Reactor Type | Conversion Expression | Conversion at $\beta = 1$ | Conversion at $\beta = 3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plug Flow (PFC)** | $X_{PFR} = 1 - e^{-\beta}$ | $0.632$ | $0.950$ |
| **CSTR (Back-mix)** | $X_{CSTR} = \dfrac{\beta}{1 + \beta}$ | $0.500$ | $0.750$ |

**Inference:** Plug flow is *always* superior for positive-order kinetics — this is a **favourite KTU conceptual question**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Problem:** A plug flow electrochemical cell is used to remove copper from a wastewater stream by electrodeposition. The inlet Cu²⁺ concentration is $C_{in} = 50 \,\text{mol/m}^3$, the volumetric flow rate is $Q = 2 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, and the mass transfer coefficient is $k_m = 5 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{m/s}$. The electrode area is $A_e = 0.05 \,\text{m}^2$ and the reaction is Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (n = 2). Calculate:
> (a) The conversion of Cu²⁺
> (b) The limiting current required
> (c) The mass of Cu deposited per hour (assuming 100% current efficiency)

### Solution

**(a) Conversion:**

$$\beta = \frac{k_m A_e}{Q} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-5})(0.05)}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.125$$

$$X = 1 - e^{-0.125} = 1 - 0.8825 = 0.1175 \approx 11.75\%$$

**(b) Limiting Current:**

The outlet concentration is:

$$C_{out} = C_{in}(1 - X) = 50 \times 0.8825 = 44.12 \,\text{mol/m}^3$$

The average concentration for $I_L$ calculation is typically taken at inlet (worst-case):

$$I_L = nF k_m C_{in} A_e = 2 \times 96485 \times 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 50 \times 0.05$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I_L &= 2 \times 96485 \times 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 50 \times 0.05 \\
&= 2 \times 96485 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 2 \times 12.06 \\
&= 24.12 \,\text{A}
\end{aligned}$$

**(c) Mass of Copper Deposited per Hour:**

$$m_{Cu} = \frac{M_{Cu} \cdot I_L \cdot t}{nF} = \frac{63.55 \times 24.12 \times 3600}{2 \times 96485}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{Cu} &= \frac{63.55 \times 24.12 \times 3600}{192970} \\
&= \frac{5518984.4}{192970} \\
&\approx 28.6 \,\text{g/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Plug Flow Cell — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RESERVOIR - Inlet Tank] -->|Q volumetric flow| B[PUMP or GRAVITY FEED]

    D --> E[CATHODE: Cu2+ + 2e- to Cu deposit]
    D --> F[ANODE: O2 evolution or metal dissolution]
    E --> G[LOCAL k_m and j z = nF k_m Cb z]
    F --> G
    G -->|Residence time tau = L over v_z| H[OUTLET MANIFOLD]
    H --> I[DEPLETED ELECTROLYTE - C out]
    I --> J[PRODUCT SEPARATION UNIT]
    J --> K[FINAL EFFLUENT or RECYCLE LOOP]
    K -.Optional Recycle.-> A
    G --> L[DC POWER SUPPLY - Controls E cell]
    L --> D

    style D fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px

```

## 4.2 Concentration & Current Density Profiles Along the Cell

```mermaid

```

## 4.3 Plug Flow vs. CSTR — Conceptual Reactor Comparison

```mermaid

        direction LR

        S3 --> S4[Slice dz 4: Cb near Cout]

    end
```
